Kara Swisher Calls Out Elon Musk and Big Tech’s “Petty, Angry" People | Pivot

Pivot with Kara Swisher and Scott Galloway(236.0K)·56:25·58.9K views·2026-05-08
Kara SwisherHost, Pivot Scott GallowayHost, Pivot Elon MuskCEO, Tesla and X
TL;DRThis briefing covers the passing of media visionary Ted Turner, a mixed earnings week for legacy media giants Disney, Paramount, and Warner Bros.
Discovery, and the escalating "petty" legal and corporate drama between Elon Musk and OpenAI. A Captain of Industry: Scott...

TL;DR

This briefing covers the passing of media visionary Ted Turner, a mixed earnings week for legacy media giants Disney, Paramount, and Warner Bros. Discovery, and the escalating "petty" legal and corporate drama between Elon Musk and OpenAI.

The Legacy of Ted Turner and the Evolution of News

  • A Captain of Industry: Scott Galloway and Kara Swisher reflect on the death of CNN founder Ted Turner at age 87, describing him as a "different kind of billionaire" who balanced aggressive entrepreneurship with extreme philanthropy.
      • Key Achievements: Beyond founding the first 24-hour news cycle, Turner won the America’s Cup and famously donated $1 billion to the United Nations.
      • Masculinity Role Model: Galloway highlights Turner as a model for "aspirational masculinity," noting he saw his wealth as an obligation to serve rather than a tool for seeking attention.
  • The Erosion of News Quality: The speakers trace the decline of news from a money-losing "public service" to a profit-driven "screamfest."
      • The Murdoch Factor: Galloway argues that Rupert Murdoch’s insight—that conflict-driven "point/counterpoint" segments drove ratings—eventually led to the hyper-polarized state of modern cable news.

Media Earnings: Disney Rises While Linear Bleeds

  • Disney’s Momentum: Under new CEO Josh D'Amaro, Disney reported strong revenue of $25 billion (up 7%).
      • Streaming Success: Streaming operating income jumped 88% to $582 million, with margins hitting double digits (11%) for the first time.
      • The "Iger" Halo: Galloway notes that while D’Amaro is at the helm, these results are largely the fruit of Bob Iger’s prior strategic shifts.
  • Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) Struggles: WBD reported a massive $2.9 billion net loss this quarter.
      • The "Worst Acquisition" Title: Galloway suggests Time Warner is the center of the worst value-destruction deals in history (AOL, AT&T, and now WBD).
      • Linear Decline: Domestic pay-TV subscribers dropped 10%, highlighting the continued struggle of traditional networks.
  • Paramount’s Resilience: Paramount beat expectations with $7.35 billion in revenue and added 700,000 subscribers.
      • Content Strategy: Despite rumors of "gutting" the studio, Paramount has doubled its film slate for 2026 following the Skydance deal.

The Musk vs. OpenAI Soap Opera

  • The SpaceX/Anthropic Deal: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei reports 80x growth, necessitating a massive compute deal with SpaceX AI (formerly xAI).
      • Musk’s About-Face: After previously calling Anthropic "evil," Musk now claims his "evil detector" wasn't triggered after meeting their executives.
  • The Legal Battle: Musk’s lawsuit against OpenAI continues to reveal "loathsome" interpersonal drama via court-ordered text releases.
      • Corporate Infighting: Testimony from Mira Murati and records involving Shivon Zilis suggest a culture of manipulation and lack of trust, though Swisher and Galloway dismiss most of it as "boring" corporate hijinks rather than illegal activity.
      • Contract Reality: Galloway argues Musk has "no legal leg to stand on," characterizing the lawsuit as "seller’s regret" dressed up as a legal argument.

AI in Finance and Crowdfunding Trends

  • The Limits of AI Investing: A recent *Wall Street Journal* experiment showed ChatGPT making basic math errors and providing "confident but flawed" investment advice.
      • The Institutional Gap: Galloway warns that retail investors using $20/month AI tools are bringing a "squirt gun" to a fight against institutional "howitzers" operated by PhDs at firms like Citadel.
  • Prediction: Crowdfunded Acquisitions: Galloway predicts that in the next 12–36 months, a charismatic social media figure will successfully crowdfund the acquisition of a small-to-mid-cap company.
      • Example: A TikToker recently gathered $132 million in non-binding pledges to try and save Spirit Airlines, indicating a shift in how retail investors might organize via AI and social platforms.
"These are the richest people on earth and they're so manifestly unhappy. They just can't shut the fuck up... What tiny, petty, angry, unhappy people they all are. Every single one of them."

— Kara Swisher

"Ted Turner saw his wealth and his seat as an obligation... He was optimizing for service, not for attention."

— Scott Galloway

"Every time you place a trade, you gotta keep in mind on the other side of that trade is likely someone smarter than you that has broader technology. The individual... is showing up with a very elegant squirt gun and they have a fucking howitzer."

— Scott Galloway

"These are the richest people on earth and they're so manifestly unhappy. They just can't shut the fuck up. What tiny, petty, angry, unhappy people they all are. Every single one of them." Hi everyone, this is Pivot from New York Magazine and the Vox Media Podcast Network. I'm Kara Swisher. And I'm Scott Galloway. Ted Turner, the media mogul and CNN founder who helped create the 24-hour news cycle, died at 87. Turner was also a major philanthropist, making a record $1 billion donation to establish the United Nations Foundation. He was a different kind of billionaire, although he was also called the Mouth of the South. Beyond media and philanthropy, Turner pursued countless other passions from his Montana Grill, his eco-conscious restaurant chain, to being named Yachtsman of the Year multiple times—he was Larry Ellison before Larry Ellison. He announced he had Lewy body dementia, a progressive brain disorder, in 2018. Jane Fonda, one of Turner’s ex-wives who remained a close friend, remembered him saying, "Men like Ted aren’t supposed to express need and vulnerability. That was Ted’s greatest strength, I believe." And he was. I recently interviewed her and she had told me he was quite ill. I talked to him a little bit for my book. Since AOL, he was real mad about the sale and everything else, except that when he sold the company, he had that famous quote where he said, "this deal was better than sex." Do you remember that? He always had something cokey to say at any one moment. And a lot of people are like, "oh, he’s the reason we have terrible cable." His vision of cable was not this what has happened. So I sort of push back against that because his idea was that you can get news to sort of democratize the news a little more from the big three. And so I consider his legacy very important, even though it sort of morphed into this screamfest that it has become. But his was real news. I happened to be with Christiane Amanpour, who got there pretty early, not right away at the beginning, and she had nothing but great things to say about his tenure when he was sort of doing real entrepreneurial news. He was a very generous and charitable person at the same time, and also funny and weird and interesting. Your thoughts? Yeah, this guy kind of defined the term that you don't hear anymore: a captain of industry. And the term captain, he was—something people don't know about him is, I think he may be the only billionaire who was also an elite world-class athlete. He won the America's Cup. Yeah, he did. He loved to sail. And there just aren't very many billionaires who've reached the pinnacle of a sport. Maybe Roger Staubach, is he a billionaire now? Anyways, but he built CNN from nothing. It's really interesting how it emerged. So news, when we were growing up, was a public service. That is, ABC and CBS sold a shit-ton of ads from Tang and Chevrolet running against All in the Family and M*A*S*H and The Partridge Family and The Brady Bunch. And they felt that it was a public service to have 23 minutes of news and seven minutes of commercial in this money-losing thing called the local news. And he correctly figured out that there was money in a market for 24-by-7 news. It was very scrappy, if you go back, it wasn't very well-produced. And he started the whole thing. Now where it came off the tracks was quite frankly with Rupert Murdoch recognizing that part of the news. They made an observation, and that is KABC news back in the 70s had something called Point/Counterpoint, and SNL used to mock it, you know, "Jane, you ignorant slut." That's our show pretty much. But go ahead. Anyways, so they recognized something called Point/Counterpoint and it was Bruce Herschensohn and Jim Tunney, and they would start yelling at each other. Someone did the analysis and found out that's what people were tuning in for. Yeah, I know. And that was really the key and arguably the most dangerous insight that Rupert Murdoch made. Yeah, and of course we live with it now. And that is, "no, this isn't about balanced news, it's enough news to create a halo of legitimacy." And quite frankly CNN was guilty of it too, Crossfire. And CNN now has a show like that where they all just start yelling at each other. That’s where Tucker Carlson started. That’s where Tucker Carlson started. Yeah, and Michael Kinsley. Remember Tucker and Michael just going at it? And that seemed civil compared to what we have now. Yeah, it does. It actually when you watch it, it actually is... it seems patrician. It seems intelligent. It is intelligent. It has digressed and has become something that is... I don't know, that's a longer talk show. Let's get back to Ted Turner. He gave away a billion dollars. He actually gave away a billion dollars to the UN, I would argue when the UN actually meant something. Something also, people constantly ask me for male role models for kind of a positive or aspirational form of masculinity. I think Ted Turner is a great role model. One, let's just go to the male stuff. Very aggressive. Also politically incorrect. When Time Warner was acquired by CNN, Jerry Levin introduced the whole thing and said, "and now I want to introduce my best friend, Ted Turner." And Ted stood up and said, "best friend? I've never been to your fucking house." Yeah, he was great. And then my favorite was when he did an All Hands and it was Ash Wednesday and there were a bunch of people in the audience with ashes on their forehead, he's like, "shouldn't you be working at Fox?" I mean the guy was funny. I think there's a certain charm in a world now where everything is so... I don't know. He was a different time period. He wasn't malevolent in that regard. But he was charitable, he was visionary, he was aggressive. 14 grandchildren. 14 or 12 grandchildren. Married to Jane Fonda, and she still loves him. She told me she still does. And environmentalist. And also took his seat and his privilege and his wealth as an obligation to serve and connect nations with each other. He was very serious about the United Nations and trying to promote world peace. He saw his wealth and his seat as an obligation. He was optimizing for service, not for attention. Yeah. I would urge people, if they're blaming what cable is now on him, not to. It’s not what he—that was not his vision. I was around for that, and it became that, and it was not his. He may have created the 24... and they did take advantage. Remember the thing that really sent them over the top was that girl down the well? Remember the girl down the well story? That's what Christiane was—she was like, "oh, that's the thing that really got people watching." That and the Iraq War. Yeah, but there you saw moments of that. Remember Arthur Kent? Like, it started to turn. That's when you started to sort of see the beginnings of kind of the circus acts of these cable stations. And I was thinking that as I went on Sky News and BBC and stuff like that. The constant haunt of—and I think it's a very serious—I'm worried about Hantavirus—but the constant, "let's go back to the cruise ship, let's go back to the..." and I was like, "this actually, we're in a war in Iran. I feel like perhaps we need to head over there and take a look at what's the cruising going on in the Strait of Hormuz." But he has a great legacy, and the anchors... Remember Bernard Shaw? Daniel Schorr. Oh, my dad's favorite for the wrong reasons, Bobby Battista. That was great. It was great. I grew up on CNN. Yeah, and Christiane too, and she's still there. And also, I genuinely believe a decent administration would be CNN anchors. Dana Bash should be President. She is smart, she's elegant, and she could smother you in your sleep. She's got that edge to her which every President needs if you crossed her. Anderson should be Vice President. Any problems with the Pope, he just goes over there and just talks about grief, everybody loves him, competent enough. Fareed Zakaria, Secretary of State, hands down. Michael Smerconish, he could be Secretary of Commerce or Labor. Tell me that cabinet. Do I get anything? I'm a contributor. Do I get like an ambassadorship to something? Oh, you're Ambassador to Guyana. We've already decided this here. Alright, we're going to move on because I'm saving you from yourself. I don't think Ted Turner—he didn't like the AOL Time Warner deal, that I did speak to him about. First he was like, "it was better than sex, the deal was the best thing I ever did, better than sex," and then afterwards he totally publicly decried it. One thing that Barry Diller I think told him was like, "if you sell your house and they wreck the patio, you need to shut up, you sold the house." And I didn't necessarily agree with him on that, but he definitely had much regrets. He probably wouldn't be thrilled where from the latest numbers from Warner Bros. Discovery. The company reported a net loss of 2.9 billion in the latest quarter; that's essentially a $2.8 billion termination fee tied to the Netflix deal. Paramount agreed to cover the cost, but the expense still sits on Warner's books until the deal officially closes. Nonetheless, the first quarter revenue was down 1% year-over-year and ad revenue slid 7%. Total streaming revenue did rise 9%, largely because HBO Max went international. Paramount had slightly better news to report, beating revenue and earnings expectations in the first quarter. Paramount added 700,000 subscribers during the quarter, helping streaming revenue grow by 17% year-over-year. The company's film studio did well, with revenue up 11%. And they said they're making great progress on the Warner Bros. deal with a late Q3 closing still on track. At the same time I'm going to add one more earnings, Disney's under the first new CEO Josh D'Amaro, the numbers were strong with revenue climbing 7% to 25 billion. Operating income across Disney’s entertainment, sports and experiences division beat expectations. The company says it expects earnings per share to grow 12% in the fiscal year. One weak spot: a dip in attendance at its US theme parks, probably due to fewer international travelers or repercussions from gas prices and other prices, but it's only down 1%. Maybe the next quarter is where you're going to see it over the summer. Thoughts on the Warner and everything? They're just sort of bumping along, these companies. So Warner Brothers, 117 adjusted GAAP versus estimates of nine cents, which is actually pretty good. They had a 45% beat. Operating margin was down or mostly flat. The linear networks continue to bleed, revenue fell 8% with domestic pay-TV subscribers down 10%. The Paramount deal is expected to close Q3, which will form a company with 69 billion in pro forma revenue. If there's a book called "The Worst Acquisitions in History", you could just call the book "Time Warner". Three of the five worst acquisitions in history all involved Time Warner: AT&T, AOL, right? Paramount posted a clean beat in streaming, turned its first real profit, revenue of 7.35 billion versus 7.28. It's just incredible. Companies are just blowing away their earnings. Their direct-to-consumer revenue was up 11%. They added 700,000 net subscribers to reach 79.6 million in total. TV media revenue fell 6% as cord-cutting continues. And Paramount has nearly doubled its film slate for 2026 versus 2025 since closing the Skydance deal last August. Everyone's been saying, "oh, they're going to gut Hollywood," and they're coming out swinging. The thing is, is it going to make money, right? That's the issue. Let's see what happens when the... so let's just wait until they—you can spend the money. I mean that's what rich people do, but let's see if they have some hits. And so Josh D'Amaro's first earning call as CEO reported a really strong beat. But here's the thing, 100% of this beat, the credit goes to Bob Iger. Someone doesn't impact earnings two weeks after they take the CEO job. This beat was really ringing the bell for Bob Iger. Streaming operating income jumped 88% to 582 million dollars, and Disney's streaming margins broke into the double digits at almost 11%. Their streaming revenue hit 5 and a half billion, up 13%, driven by price hikes. And Disney no longer reports subscriber numbers, which is a shame. But their experiences were up 7%, which must be an increase in prices because attendance is down. And they also have raised their buyback target, similar to what Apple did last week. I think Disney is a buy. D'Amaro came out of the parks, which grew nearly 40% in revenue under his watch. He called Disney+ the digital centerpiece of the company. He flagged fueling consumer spending. Dana Walden, who I know you know, named President and Chief Creative Officer... That was an important keep for that company, because she was a CEO—she was the top contender. Not only that, they're all—I mean quite frankly a lot of these people are probably—I mean this is the challenge when your stock is below where it was 10 years ago. You have to get the most talented people in a room and say, "I'm going to issue new options and please stick around." So what is their option? Producers. It’s harder now. You can't be as good a... you can't do those sweet, sweet deals anymore. Yeah, but if you're a talented Disney executive, you could absolutely land at... I would stay. See, now I would stay because you have power. One of the last pieces of wood floating. Oh, that's interesting, I hadn't thought of that analogy. I like that. That was from The Devil Wears Prada too, but go ahead. I thought it was from the Titanic, isn't it? It is, but she just said that. She goes, "we just got on the last..." she says... I was wondering how long it was going to be before you mentioned The Devil Wears Prada too. Just saying, it's killing it, still killing it. Yeah no, that's great. Let's go over every movie that's actually making money. It is making money. It’s making a ton. It’s going to change the world. You have to see it. Lawrence of Arabia meets The Sound of Music meets The Sixth Sense. But Disney stock is down more than 10% year-to-date despite back-to-back earnings beats. I don't know if it's because everyone's giving up on traditional media or because AI is sucking all the oxygen out of every other company. But I think Disney actually right now is a really good buy. I wouldn't be surprised if it's put in play in the next three to six months by an activist. An activist comes in and says, "you need to cut 20% of your staff or you need to put the company up for sale." Right, which they did with Iger. I mean Iger went through that, right? Several times. Yeah, but Iger had so much credibility, he was able to back—this stock is weak because this is a new CEO. Disney has this unbelievable franchise. Or it might be corporate restructuring. Spin out the parks into its own business, and... They decided not to, as you know. They were going to get rid of ABC at one point, then they decided to keep ESPN. I think that's one of D'Amaro's first decisions. And an activist will argue that their decisions have been really poor because this company has probably the greatest collection of IP in the world, a booming parks business, and yet the stock is trading below where it was in 2016. I gotta think there's a lot of activists out there. Which activists? If I had to bet on any firm that shows up forceful yet dignified, it would be Elliott. And by the way, I don't know anything, I haven't talked to them about this. But I wouldn't be surprised. Unfortunately, it'll probably be someone like Peltz. I think this merger between Netflix and Disney could never happen under an administration that didn't have an FTC or a DOJ that was in a slumber. So if there was any desire to do that, it would be now would be the time. Except Brandon is not... doesn't like Disney. It's in his sights. Brendan Carr. He specifically called out Disney, like by name, saying he doesn't like them. Yeah, but he doesn't get to decide antitrust. The bottom line is, Disney is cheap right now. And Netflix and Disney in a wind-up would be game over. If you took Netflix's IP, including Wednesday, Stranger Things, Bridgerton, and used it to create experiences in parks, I just think it would be incredible. Yeah, absolutely. I don't think it'll happen, although you're probably right. Anyway, it'll be interesting to see. It was fine, it was perfectly fine. You know when Ted Turner, when that deal happened, I mean media, that was the top for media, wasn't it? It was like they were running the fucking show, they were the kings of the road, and then they weren't. Like, it's been a downward, downward, downward slide for traditional media. The fall of traditional media for me is embodied by when I first moved to New York in 2000, and like all single people in their 30s, we would pile eight people into a house in Bridgehampton. Every weekend was a mad scramble to see who we knew at Condé Nast, who knew what magazine was throwing the most fabulous party. The masters of the universe were these creatives and the business people working at Condé Nast magazines. And in the journalism business, I have to tell you, I was like the internet person, and they treated me like shit. Like they were like, "oh, tech is boring. It's a Ponzi scheme, it's nothing, it's going to go away." The media reporters were such snotbags, I have to tell you, they just were. And I was like, "I think you're in trouble." And they were like, "you don't know, they're going to run the show." I'll never forget it. But that's what happened. Anyway, let's go on a quick break. When we come back, Anthropic gets into bed with Elon and SpaceX. Scott, we're back. Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei says the company planned for 10x growth this year, but is now seeing growth closer to 80x, creating a massive need for more computing power. Enter SpaceX, or rather SpaceX AI. SpaceX acquired xAI back in February, but Elon Musk has just revealed xAI will be dissolved and its AI products will be part of the SpaceX AI brand. Of course, because everybody left, it didn't quite have the traction that he had hoped, so he’s folding it right in. Everything's going to get folded, Tesla's next. Under the new deal, Anthropic will get compute power with the SpaceX AI data center in Memphis, the one that's polluting people, as it works to keep up with surging demand. A real turnaround for Elon, who earlier called Anthropic "evil". He's now saying he was impressed after meeting with senior executives. Again, look in the fucking mirror, dude. So is this the enemy of my enemy is my friend because of ChatGPT? Obviously Elon has not had the success he had hoped to dominate and it's all about his beef with Sam Altman. I would be careful if I was Dario, but I think he's savvy enough to deal with Elon. I'd rather talk about me in the aughts and reminisce a little bit longer. Is that okay? So I started in 2000, I had moving to New York from San Francisco, I had just been abused, ridden hard and put away wet by Sequoia Capital, kicked off the board of the company I started. I just wanted to never go back to San Francisco the rest of my life. Can I ask you a question? Did you deserve it just a little bit? What happened with Sequoia? Oh, I was an obnoxious, aggressive entrepreneur that was arrogant and reckless. But also Mike Moritz is a small, vindictive man. So yeah, I don't think there's enough blame to go around for everyone. I'm clearly over it. Anyways, I moved back to New York and I started an e-commerce incubator backed by Goldman, JP Morgan, Howard Schultz's company. And it was nearly impossible to recruit people to come to work for a technology incubator. I would take people out who were working at traditional media and I'd say, "I can only pay you 200 but I'm going to give you two million options on two million shares." And I would have to walk them through what options were. I couldn't even use a New York-based law firm because they didn't do venture. People don't realize New York in 2000 was about finance, it was about media, there was no technology whatsoever. There was one! Oh wait, the one that was bought by Yahoo. Tumblr. Oh my god, Tumblr was in New York. Tumblr, the worst acquisition in history. Within the same 60 days, Mark Zuckerberg made the best acquisition in history: for a billion he bought Instagram. And Marissa Mayer bought Tumblr for $1.1 billion, which seven years later got sold for how much? A couple million. Three million dollars. It was a collection of dirty blogs. And then when they shut down the porn, I started an online battle with... forget his name. Anyways, they told Tumblr they were no longer going to fund this bag of shit, but he convinced Marissa Mayer to buy it. I've said this, I wish I had never bet on a prediction market. Kalshi had Musk winning the case at 51 or 52%. There are no moves. He does not have a legal leg to stand on. You can go from a non-profit to a for-profit. You're allowed to do that. This is regret and a messiah complex cosplaying a legal argument. Judges in courts aren't concerned or moved by anger and indignant seller's regret. They're moved by evidence and argument, and there's none here. Though I will say, this trial, I was thinking—you know you're watching the texts go back and forth—and Mira Murati doesn't like Sam, really, even though she pretended she did. And the girlfriend who was on the board, Shivon Zilis. Isn't that his girlfriend? Well it's his partner, and then they had four children and she didn't manage to tell the OpenAI people that she had two children with him. She’s like a spy in there, right? They’re accusing her of being a spy. If you're a fiduciary for all shareholders making decisions around someone's compensation and you're having their children, that's just like, she probably just forgot to mention it. Yeah, she wasn't doing compensation. Anyway, the whole thing and they're like going back and forth in this trial and I thought, this is a soap opera, but with the most boring people in it. I don't care that whether Mira Murati likes Sam Altman or not. "Oh, he was manipulative." I don't care. Is this illegal? Is he just a jerk? Did he do anything illegal? Because otherwise your internal corporate hijinks, I don't care. I do think she should have told people she had his babies. It's just a bad soap opera that you want to turn off. It’s bad for AI, it’s bad for the companies. When you sell an asset, it doesn't matter who the bigger asshole is, the seller or the buyer. Baby daddy gave up his ownership. He sold his ownership and governance rights. And the whole media's trying to turn this into, "who would be better at running it?" It doesn't matter! Don't fall along with these people. You sell an asset, it doesn't matter who's the better person or steward of the home you sold. It is instructive to look how sloppy it is. That's why I keep saying to people, they're like, "oh, they're masters of the universe," I'm like, "they're kind of sloppy." Like Google, like everybody in the beginning of Google, they were all like fucking each other. It was a big mess and it was chaos and they all hated each other. Who cares? But the fact that it's coming out in court just makes you realize how dumb this trial is. It's about money, that's all it is. This is, "I sold something that ended up being worth $850 billion dollars." And it's, "I'm the messiah who should control autonomous in the US, and oh by the way, the one part of the technological revolution that I don't control is AI." Six years after I've decided, "oh, I should own it, I need it back." Pretty soon it's going to be, "I should own Anthropic." It's ridiculous. $80 billion to colonize Mars. Guess what Elon? You have it. Spend it and have a good time. Good luck, bye. Ron Wayne was one of the founders of Apple. He owned 10% of it. He sold his 10% for $2,300 back to I think Jobs and Wozniak. That is now worth $400 billion. And guess what? It sucks to be a grown-up. He signed the legal documents. My favorite defense is, "oh, he didn't read the fine print." He's the wealthiest man in the world with the most sophisticated legal team in history. I would love to see someone show up who sold Tesla to Musk and say, "oh I didn't realize it was going to be worth this much and I didn't read the fine print." I think this is a great buy at 38%, I'll take the other side of this. Nobody looks good. Some of the highlights of the testimony: emails from Zilis showing Musk suggested OpenAI become part of Tesla. Musk tried to recruit Sam Altman to join the lab within Tesla. Musk supposedly wanted full control of OpenAI in part to help him raise $80 billion to colonize Mars and was using OpenAI employees to do secret work for Tesla. OpenAI's former CTO Mira Murati testified saying she couldn't trust Sam Altman's words. Mira, I like you, but I don't care. If you don't like it, leave! It's not who would be a better shepherd of AI. It's contract law. When you sell an asset, it doesn't matter who the bigger asshole is. Let me make one final observation: these are the richest people on earth and they're so manifestly unhappy. They just can't shut the fuck up. What tiny, petty, angry, unhappy people they all are. And you're like, "y'all, none of you deserve what you've got here." None of you have the dignity or character to look good. I don't even trust Dario Amodei and I like him. We should be able to elect representatives we can trust to do the right thing and regulate these companies. And then we can fire them easily. Anyway, let's go on a quick break. When we come back, we'll talk about managing a stock portfolio via ChatGPT. Scott, we're back with just one more quick story. A Wall Street Journal reporter asked ChatGPT for investment advice to see if AI could realistically manage a stock portfolio. It actually makes sense; there’s a lot of interesting small AI companies being made that actually sound useful. ChatGPT generally identified market risks but also made simple math mistakes, suggested questionable market timing strategies and sometimes veered into overly complex ideas like options trading. The chatbot often told the user what they seemed to want to hear. About 30% of everyday investors report using AI for their portfolios. So apparently not so good yet, in lots of really problematic ways. It’s not there in this personalized investment advice. Huge risk for lawsuits, I would assume. Any thoughts? Well, I think it was tempting to believe Lotus 1-2-3 to beat the market by doing all sorts of analysis. You might have had an edge for a minute, but the individual thinking they can use Claude—and I'm guilty of this, I've been asking Claude like crazy to come up with hedging strategies. I enjoy it, you know it's fun for me. But here's the bottom line: Ken Griffin will hire 2,000 PhDs and he will weaponize them with AI and they will outperform the market. You believing with your $20 a month Claude weapon can compete, you are showing up with an elegant squirt gun and they have a fucking howitzer. So this is what you do: you invest in low-cost ETFs like Vanguard. The bottom line is every time you place a trade, you gotta keep in mind on the other side of that trade is likely someone smarter than you. Agree. That’s what I do. I just put it in there and then it goes away. Someone's like, "how are your stocks doing?" I'm like, "I have no idea." I'm not going to look. The more I think about it, the more upset I get, right? It's a great point because in addition to the capital you're investing, you want to talk about the emotion you're investing. I do get psychic income but I started buying stocks when I was 13. I find it fascinating. Having said that, it also takes a real emotional toll, staring at your fucking phone. The only thing I wish you wouldn't do is put so much of your medical information into these people because they're not bound by HIPAA. Same thing with legal. I just worry about that over time. What's that? Wait, who's taking my medical? No I'm just saying, whenever you put a lot of your personal info—you always sometimes you don't even realize you're saying this. I'm not sure I trust these people to have all Scott's medical... I'm very promiscuous with my information. I wonder if I should just send out a preemptive apology to Emily Ratajkowski right now for all the things I've asked AI about. Oh no. Okay, we're moving on. Alright, one more quick break. We will be back for predictions. Scott, let's hear a prediction. A 32-year-old TikToker just crowdfunded 132 million in non-binding pledges to buy a bankrupt Spirit Airlines. Did you see this? He said, "look, Spirit helps keep airline prices down, let's mutualize it." And Spirit said it was going to shut down at AM on Saturday and this guy Hunter Peterson had a TikTok up the same day, a website by the next, and more than 133,000 people have pledged totaling $132 million. It’s unlikely to close, but with in an era of compliance AI where you could spin up the legal documents and have people sign them with PayPal, I think you're going to see someone pull this off in the next 12 to 36 months. I think crowdfunding is going to happen. Also retail participation in US equities has risen by nearly 20% of average daily trading volume. Why don't you do it? Why don't we buy something? Like Chipotle or something. Chipotle is expensive. Do you know they sent me a card for free lifetime Chipotle and I lost it? But I love Chipotle. No, if they do it again I want serious cabbage. I predict the government is going to lose that idiotic case against the New York Times about the hiring, the DEI. It's ridiculous they're going after all their enemies. They attack the New York Times, then they're going to use the Justice Department to try to get E. Jean Carroll's money that Trump owes her, which is insane. And then they attack the 82-year-old woman, they raided her office who did the redistricting that kicked their ass. And then they are investigating the reporter for The Atlantic who did the story about Kash Patel. No one's having any of this nonsense that they're doing. It's just so cloddish and thuggish. I can't help it. I agree with you. Private companies get to do what they want. Let him sue the New York Times, I don't care. I will say, look, a search warrant is not proof of guilt, but it is a serious threshold and federal judges do not casually approve raids on senior state legislators. Come on! Why her? Because high-profile corruption probes often look strongest at the raid stage and weaker later once the evidence is scrutinized publicly. But to be fair, a search warrant has to pass a serious threshold. I understand that, but there's a reason they didn't pick 20 other Republican ones. This is a weaponization of the power. They complained about it happening to them, and then they're doing it. So stop it. Just cut it out. Anyway, we want to hear from you. Send us your questions about business, tech or whatever is on your mind. Scott, Prof G Markets is going on tour this month. Tell us a tiny bit about this and why people should go. Oh well thanks, Kara. It's similar to what we did with Pivot, it's a live tour. What's interesting, what sells out the quickest? New York and San Francisco. New York and San Francisco are going to sell out in the next 24 hours. We sold out San Francisco first on Pivot. We sold them all out fast, I’ll be honest. Yeah, and it'll be a bunch of people coming up to me and asking me if Ed Elson is single because their daughter is looking for a nice husband. Go to profgmarketstour.com. Great, that's fantastic. And also we're going to be doing Pivot in the fall. Another Pivot tour in the fall. You're going to be on the road, Scott Galloway. We're doing another tour? We didn't have a call about another tour. We literally discussed it. We're having a tour in the fall. You were on the call. You were listening. Anyway, that's the show. Thanks for listening to Pivot and be sure to like and subscribe to our YouTube channel. We’ll be back next week!

Candidates for governor participate in Manufacturers Association of Maine forum | WATCH

NEWS CENTER Maine(158.0K)·122:45·32 views·2026-05-08
Janet MillsDemocratic Candidate for Governor of Maine Shawn MoodyRepublican Candidate for Governor of Maine
TL;DR1.
TL;DR Candidates at the Manufacturers Association of Maine and Maine Forest Products Council forum clashed over the state's business climate, focusing on high energy costs, regulatory overreach, and educational decline. While Republican and independent candidates advocated...

1. TL;DR

Candidates at the Manufacturers Association of Maine and Maine Forest Products Council forum clashed over the state's business climate, focusing on high energy costs, regulatory overreach, and educational decline. While Republican and independent candidates advocated for aggressive tax cuts and stripping back bureaucratic regulations, Democratic candidates emphasized targeted government support, housing investments, and public education funding to revitalize Maine's economy.

Economic Recovery and Regulatory Reform

  • The Business Climate Crisis: Several candidates highlighted Maine's low rankings in national business reports, noting the state ranks 45th or 46th for business climate and has flat projected job growth through 2029.
      • State Budget Growth: Candidate Ben pointed out that Maine's state budget surged from $7 billion to $12 billion in just seven years, a $5 billion increase funded directly by taxpayers and businesses.
      • Overregulation: Candidate Bobby stated that Maine has over 135,000 state regulations, which he argued drives up operating costs and deters investment.
  • Proposed Tax and Spending Cuts:
      • Income Tax Reductions: Candidate Bobby proposed a $4 billion tax cut funded by trimming the state budget. Jonathan Bush proposed a $1 billion tax cut to stimulate private sector growth, while Owen McCarthy advocated for an immediate income tax cut phased out entirely over eight years.
      • The "Two-for-One" Rule: Owen McCarthy proposed a regulatory cap where the state must eliminate two existing rules for every new regulation introduced.
      • Opposing Retroactive Taxes: Candidates strongly condemned a state-proposed supplemental budget measure that would have eliminated the Business Equipment Tax Reimbursement (BETR) program, which would have resulted in a retroactive $4.7 million tax hike on forest product companies.
  • Streamlining State Permitting:
      • Consistency Over Chaos: Senator Rick Bennett (Independent) emphasized that businesses need long-term political predictability rather than shifting rules between changing administrations.
      • Technological Modernization: Secretary of State Shenna Bellows highlighted her success in digitizing the Bureau of Motor Vehicles (BMV) and proposed a "one-stop shop" digital platform for business licensing in Augusta.
      • Cross-State Harmonization: Nirav Shah proposed harmonizing electrical, building, and food codes with neighboring states to reduce regional compliance costs.

Energy Costs and Grid Stabilization

  • Industrial Energy Challenges: The forum highlighted the closure of long-time businesses, such as Milo's solar-heavy industrial area, due to soaring power rates. Maine experienced the fastest-growing electricity costs in the nation last year, leaving ratepayers with high bills.
  • Reforming Solar Subsidies:
      • Net Energy Billing: Multiple Republican candidates called for a repeal of net energy billing policies, with Ben noting that ratepayers shoulder $200 million annually in solar subsidies.
      • Utility Profit Caps: Shenna Bellows proposed a 6% cap on profits for international investor-owned utilities to protect residential and industrial ratepayers.
  • Diversifying the Energy Portfolio:
      • All-of-the-Above Mix: Nirav Shah advocated for a balanced energy portfolio including biomass, natural gas, wind, and solar, warning against over-relying on any single source.
      • Pipeline Expansion: Owen McCarthy and Bobby urged the expansion of natural gas pipeline capacities (such as the Algonquin pipeline) to increase fuel storage and decrease winter electricity spikes.
      • Nuclear and Biomass Alternatives: Candidates David Jones, Ben, and Bobby supported exploring small modular nuclear reactors (SMRs) and expanding biomass energy to support both loggers and grid baseload.

Workforce Scarcity and Housing Affordability

  • The Aging Population Trap: Candidates agreed that Maine’s flat population growth and aging demographic present severe labor supply issues.
  • Lowering Living Barriers:
      • Interconnected Affordability: Nirav Shah and Angus King emphasized that attracting younger workers depends on resolving four core issues: housing, healthcare, childcare, and educational quality.
      • Property Tax Reform: Shenna Bellows proposed a state property tax moratorium for primary residents, offset by doubling property taxes on out-of-state owners of vacation homes (noting that one in six Maine homes is a vacation property).
  • Entitlement and Labor Reform:
      • Work Requirements: Bobby proposed work or volunteer requirements (20 hours per week) for able-bodied adults without dependents to encourage labor participation.
      • Paid Family and Medical Leave (PFML): Rick Bennett and other candidates voiced concerns over the long-term economic sustainability of the "gold-plated" PFML program, warning it could drive employers out of state.

Educational Reform and Career Pathways

  • Addressing Declining Outcomes: Candidates raised alarms over Maine's decline in educational rankings, noting that currently only one in four fourth graders reads at grade level.
  • Mississippi-Style Science of Reading: Owen McCarthy advocated for passing a "Science of Reading" bill to mandate evidence-based reading curriculums, pointing out that 50% of current state reading programs are unproven.
  • Prioritizing Trades and Vocational Training:
      • Expanding CTE Slots: Owen McCarthy highlighted that Maine has more demand for Career and Technical Education (CTE) programs than available slots, advocating for immediate expansion.
      • Destigmatizing Blue-Collar Careers: Candidates argued for the cultural restoration of the skilled trades, encouraging middle-school students to pursue lucrative paths in welding, plumbing, and manufacturing.
      • Administrative Bloat: David Jones pointed out that Maine has 190 school superintendents for 170,000 public school students—one for every 800 children—and called for restructuring administrative spending back into classroom teaching.

Notable Quotes

"Simple beats smart, 7 days a week when you're in a crisis, and we're in one." — Jonathan Bush
"Plumbers can do everything you can't. They're going to take over the world... the surefire way to have a career is to go build something physically." — Owen McCarthy
"I think we live in a state of hostility toward business. Government in this state is overregulated, they overregulate us, they overtax us, they put us in a position where we're fighting rules and mandates and permits." — Bobby
"Business leaders need consistency, not chaos, and they need an ally in the Blaine House who knows how to get things done in government." — Rick Bennett
On behalf of the Manufacturers Association of Maine, welcome to the Maine and Maine Forest Products Council gubernatorial forum—forum, not debate, forum. I'd like to thank all of our candidates for participating today, and our partners at the Maine Forest Products Council. Manufacturing and forest products remain foundational to Maine's economy, supporting communities, driving investment, and providing high-quality jobs across the state. We felt it was important to host this forum as part of the annual summit to create a space where employers can hear directly from those seeking to lead Maine, particularly on the issues that impact our ability to grow, invest, and compete. Our goal today is simple: to provide clarity for employers on how each candidate views Maine's business climate and economic future. Oh, that's me. All right. First, I'd like to thank the Manufacturers Association of Maine for giving us the opportunity to pair up and team up for this debate. We're really grateful for the opportunity to be here and for all of the candidates who have traveled all the way as well. You'll hear about some of the challenges facing our members today. I'd first like to set the stage for this conversation with an important observation. From renewable packaging, sustainable carbon-storing building materials, and bio-based fuels and chemicals to PFAS and carbon-fixing biochar, opportunities for Maine's abundant forests to support rural economies while meeting the needs of our state and our region are endless given the right climate for investments. As you'll see in our report that's on our website, our industry is in a period of transition towards a more diverse and resilient portfolio of products. While we have contracted in recent years, we remain an $8.3 billion powerhouse that supports 29,000 jobs across all 16 counties. We welcome the opportunity to engage with each of you here today. On behalf of our members, thank you for running for office, and please keep in touch with both organizations regardless of the outcome in November. We're here to be a resource to all of you, and most of all, best of luck. No, so again, my name is John Lewis. I'm the executive director for the Manufacturers Association, and my co-moderator for this forum is Krista West with the Maine Forest Products Council. Before we begin, I'll briefly just review the format. The forum will be organized around key topics identified by Maine and MFPC members. Each candidate will have two minutes for introductions, one minute for topic responses, brief responses to the lightning round, and one minute for closing remarks. We're also going to work in a couple of audience questions in there as well. Speaking order, we are going to try to do our best to rotate, but there's a lot of you. So I'm not sure everybody's going to get a chance to go first. Time limits, hopefully we can enforce. We've got a clock over there, but please bear with us; we're going to try to do our best. And we're going to try to cover a range of topics. We appreciate everyone's cooperation in keeping the discussion moving along. We'll begin first with candidate introductions. Please introduce yourself, share what you view as the biggest challenge or opportunity for manufacturing in Maine, and what you would prioritize in your first 100 days as governor to support the industry. So, how about we start on this end? Oh, and we're going to be sharing mics, so if that hopefully is okay, we've got three up there. It's all about sharing. So I'll just start and say what an ambitious undertaking here with as many people here as are at the table. Thank you, John and Krista. My story is simple: a series of lightning strikes. I grew up in a little town of 500 called Wayne. My mother raised four kids largely on her own, and World War II vets were the distinguishing feature in my life. I worked for a couple of them. My father never finished college, but I ended up getting these lightning strikes: Dartmouth College, worked hard all the way through, Oxford University with a degree in economics, and then Columbia Law School, the Reagan White House. I did a lot of things that I never expected to do, including 10 years in Navy intelligence. When Colin Powell called and asked if I would be Assistant Secretary of State, managing most of the operations of State and also working to try to restore efficiencies, that is what I did. I have also run a company for 25 years. That company, in my view, is a platinum-level company. What it does is a small business—and 99% of Maine businesses are small. My concerns about Maine really can be collapsed very quickly: I think we live in a state of hostility toward business. Government in this state is overregulated. They overregulate us, they overtax us, and they put us in a position where we're fighting rules, mandates, and permits. This is not the state that I grew up in. I grew up in a state that was pro-business, small government, very accountable, with no crime. We were a state that really was proud to give the individual their right to prosper. So that's what I want to see back in this state. I believe we can do it; this is a "many hands makes light the load" effort. And I do also honor everybody on this stage because I think there's a good heart in everybody up here. I just think my means and experience fit like a glove to where we need to go. So, thank you. So, are we doing intros or the first 100 days? Both? Quick intro. Hi, I'm Jonathan Bush. I am a husband and father of seven. I'm a veteran, I'm an entrepreneur, and for 30 years, I was a CEO. I started a company in my basement that grew to 6 billion, shook up an industry, and created a thousand Maine jobs. I believe that Maine's government has become too big and too bloated in terms of the rules that it inflicts. We have become a no-fly zone. We're at the top of every list you don't want to be on as a state that wants to grow its economy, and the bottom of every list you need to top. Whether it's the electrical costs—where we just moved from five to three, mazel tov—or taxes, we are now the number three state in taxes in the country. It's no wonder that we are down at 46 for our business climate and 49 for economic growth at zero. Wow. So what we need to do is shrink the taxes, shred the no-fly zone type of laws, silence these performative environmental and performative social justice noises, and shrink Augusta. Then we can begin to get out our watering can and bring an economy back to the state. I'm proposing a billion-dollar tax cut, which I think can easily be returned. I distinguish myself from the other people up here in that I've done these things. I've actually built a company, built thousands of jobs in Maine, and done something that is important to Maine, which is helping small businesses survive. My company focused on small medical providers and letting them survive. So I think I'm a fit for the next governor. Thanks. Thanks, John. My name is Angus King. I am sitting here right now because I could not stand by. Too often, I think the sound and fury of our politics fans the flames of our problems instead of putting them out, and life has just gotten harder here in Maine. Housing costs have doubled, energy prices have grown at twice the national average, and our schools have gone from the best to the bottom. We need something different. We need a new and better way. The good news is my campaign offers that new and better way, one that is focused not on division but on the hopes and challenges that all Mainers face just trying to get through their day. And I know that it works. I also know with every fiber of my being that we can still accomplish great things in our state, and more than ever, we have to. To get there, we're going to need something different. We're going to need a leader who has harnessed the power of a clear vision and shared effort, someone who has put public and private sectors together, urban and rural views together, climate and energy know-how together, hell, even Republican and Democratic ideas together to make a difference in people's lives. I know it's possible because I've done it. I've built housing for thousands of people, I've built energy projects around the world, and I have done it with people who had every reason to disagree to build things nobody thought was possible. We can take that and make Maine a place where costs are lower, schools aim higher, wages are up, and discord is down. I know we can do it. I am full of optimism about what we can do here in Maine, but we really have to do something different, and it's going to come from somebody who has worked on every side of these issues and who has gotten things done. Thank you to the nice lady who told me I have 10 seconds, and I'll pass it on to my colleague Ben on the right. Thank you, sir. Has my time started yet, or? Yes, it has. It has, okay. All right, so, moving quickly, I'm not a politician, okay? I'm from outside of that world. I'm running for the governor's office to bring common-sense values and principles back to the governor's office. About me, I grew up here in Maine, my wife's from Maine, our kids grew up here, and they all went to the same high school I did—one is still in high school. Myself, I never made it out of college. I tried to, I was working a few years for that, but I couldn't afford it. Personally, I spent time on unemployment, and spent time on food stamps back in the day when they were actually stamps. In the fitness industry, which was my career, I started cleaning machines in exchange for a membership. Over 30 years, I eventually became the president of a company called Planet Fitness, which is a large national franchise. I left there and started another company called Crunch Fitness Franchising as a chief executive officer and founding partner. Over 15 years, we built that to the number one fitness franchise in the country, with 500 locations in 46 states, six countries, 3 million members, over a billion dollars in revenue, and I created 15,000 jobs across the country building that company. So, for me, I strongly believe we do need a CEO's focus in the governor's office to get things going in the right direction. What I think helps manufacturing is really what helps all businesses. I've met with over 50 different industries in the state, driven over 60,000 miles to meet with people, and nobody's really happy with the environment. There are zero jobs forecasted and zero economic growth in the job sector through 2029. That is the Mills administration's economic forecast. You can't have that. To fix that, you've got to do a lot of things. You've got to have a pro-business focus, and you've got to fix the welfare system in a way that allows people to keep their benefits as they transition off of the system—not to take them away, but to allow them the opportunity to enter the workforce. We're 4% lower on labor force participation. You've got to get rid of the high electricity costs, housing issues, insurance costs, and permitting delays. We need to cut corporate and personal taxes, use incentives to bring business back in, reduce regulations, get depreciation conformity, and do so many other things to fix the business environment. That is my intention. Thank you. Pass it back that way. Hello, my name is David Jones. I live in Falmouth, Maine. I've been a resident of Falmouth now for 25 years. I started my company when I was 17 years old. I didn't go to college; I went to the Italian University, it's called "What's-a-matter You." And I worked my way—I worked my way through everything. It was really a tough way, a tough road to hoe. But I took my company from me and a few guys to 1,100 people on my payroll, and another 4,000 or 5,000 people we kept as subcontractors. I built high-rises, malls, and shopping centers. I built the largest mall in Palm Beach, the Gardens Mall. I built the Disney Caribbean Beach Resort in Orlando. So big projects like taking on government in Augusta, I can handle it. This is not a learning experience for me. I've been married for 41 years to my wife, Nancy. I have six children: three boys, three girls. I have eight grandchildren: four boys, four girls. So the Jones family is definitely fair and balanced. Now, you know, we talk about what we want to do here. Like everybody on the stage will probably agree, I think on most everything, we are in trouble. But we need a CEO. We need a guy who really understands it and is going to toe the line for the people of the state of Maine. We have to have a business-friendly atmosphere. We don't. I have been self-employed my entire life. I've always signed the front of the check. I've been through three different recessions. I know what it's like to go through tough times. I know what we have here in the state of Maine is a lot of small businesses, and the only way you're going to get things done is to have a governor who knows and has felt your pain. I have felt your pain. So I would ask you this: you know, there are a lot of good people. Yeah, we've got choices. But who's the guy who's going to get it to the finish line? Who's the guy that can work with Republicans and Democrats and get things done? Who's the guy that's been a team builder his entire life? I've had an open-door policy my entire life. As governor, I promise you that will not stop. Thank you very much. Hi everyone, good afternoon. My name is Rick Bennett. I am running for governor as an independent. I live in the town of Oxford. Now, many of you may know me from my State Senate service, including my time as president of the Senate when we had a tied Senate, but I actually started my career in manufacturing. I've run businesses, I've met payroll, I've made tough decisions, and I've dealt with the same costs and talent issues that many of you are facing today. I understand what you're up against because I've lived it. Business leaders need consistency, not chaos, and they need an ally in the Blaine House who knows how to get things done in government as well. I'm running as an independent because I want to govern as an independent. That means I'll be accountable to you, not to a political party, and not to the special interest groups. It means I'm listening to the people on the front lines and developing solutions with the people closest to the problems that we're trying to solve. As governor, I'm going to be focused on making life affordable, growing our economy, and restoring trust in government. We will lower energy costs—and I was honored to share our plan for doing so just yesterday at Robbins Lumber in Searsmont with Jim Robbins—and we will build housing so your workers can actually live near your facilities. That's why I launched my campaign at a manufacturing plant, KBS Builders in South Paris. We'll grow our economy by aligning workforce training with the jobs needed and by creating a business climate where you can compete. It's paramount that Maine's next governor earns back the public's trust by being honest, effective, and accountable. So, I just want to say you know what this industry needs, and my job as governor will be to create the conditions for you to be successful. Thank you. My name is Owen McCarthy, and I grew up in the shadow of Mount Katahdin in a trailer on my grandfather's land, the son of a third-generation logger and a second-generation lunch lady. Growing up in the town of Patten, Maine, I had a great advantage because I learned a lot about hard work, personal responsibility, and had a great public school education. I then went on to the University of Maine and studied engineering on the Pulp and Paper scholarship, and then worked in our heritage pulp and paper industry as a supplier to the industry for many years, having been to many of the paper mills in the state. I then went on and became an entrepreneur, first starting a 3D printing electronics company, and then starting a medical device company that helps people improve walking following stroke and living with Parkinson's disease. We raised $60 million of venture capital into the state, created good-paying jobs, and proved that you could do innovation here. I'm also a husband and a father. I have two sons, Oliver and Theodore, and I don't think that their generation has the same opportunity that I had coming from here in Maine. I also don't think that if you're a working-class Mainer, it's affordable to live here anymore, and we need to turn that around. That's why I'm running for governor, and that's why I released a plan, Maine 2040: 70 pages, 130 policies, all focused on creating 50,000 foundational jobs that sell things across borders, taking advantage of the re-industrialization of America and the Fourth Industrial Revolution, playing offense in this era. We need to fix the fundamentals I talk about in the plan—which many people have covered, such as income tax, regulation, and energy—and then we need to bet on our strengths. Forest products is an industry that still has 29,000 people that work in it; we need to grow it. We can't tear out the dams in Central Maine; we need to make sure to protect it and then grow it. Maritime innovation is another; we've been building boats since the British liked our pine trees, and we can build on that more. That's the biggest national security concern in the United States. Life science is a third; we've gone from 6,000 to 10,000 people that work in the industry. I think if we bet on our strengths while fixing our fundamentals, we can create a lot of prosperity for the state of Maine and create a better future. Well, good afternoon, everyone. My name is Nirav Shah, and I'm honored to be here with everyone today. Prior to becoming and joining public service, I worked as an attorney for about eight or nine years, and practiced law at a time when a lot of businesses were still trying to sort out how they would get a foothold, particularly in the life sciences and biotech sector. I worked with a lot of those companies to help them understand how to navigate difficult and complex business environments, particularly when litigation was coming up. I then carried that same set of problem-solving skills with me as I entered public service, first as the director of the health department in Illinois, and then here in Maine as the director of the Maine CDC, particularly during a period of darkness for all of us. I then went on to the Biden administration and served as the number two official at the US CDC, a gargantuan agency with tens of thousands of employees and a multi-billion dollar budget. I then came back to Maine, and over the course of last summer saw the ways in which our business climate, as well as many of the governmental institutions that we all rely upon, were being affected. That's ultimately why, come October of last year, I decided to run and enter the race to be your next governor. Now, as it relates to the topics that we're going to be talking about today, and as we were teeing up the question, one of the questions was, "What are you aiming for in your first 100 days?" For me, what I think about, based on the conversations I've had with so many of you, including folks in this room, is what rises to the top of every single one of those conversations: predictability and stability. And so, what I'm prioritizing for my first 100 days are predictable, stable timelines, whether for permitting as well as regulation. What I'm hoping that I can do over the course of my time as governor is introduce a lot more predictability and stability so we all know the rules of the road, and thus have a pathway for you all to survive and thrive. Thanks. Well, good afternoon, and thank you so much to Krista and John for moderating. It's a pleasure to be here. My name is Shenna Bellows, and when I was 4 years old, my sister was 3, my brother was 2, and my parents bought a piece of land in Hancock, Maine. My father had just started his own carpentry business, and my mother was working nights in the light bulb filament factory in Sullivan, Elscot's. In their spare time—firstly, they pitched a tent, and in their spare time, they dug out an old cellar hole, and we lived in the basement that following winter. We didn't have electricity or indoor plumbing until I was in the fifth grade; that was a great year. But I worked my way through high school and college. I was a research associate at the Mount Desert Island Biological Laboratory. When everybody else went home at 5 o'clock, I'd go to my next job as a Subway sandwich artist, waiting tables, or working in the kitchen at a local lobster pound. After I graduated college, I went into economic consulting, and then the Peace Corps as a small business development volunteer, AmeriCorps, and then into a career in nonprofit management as the CEO of the ACLU of Maine, LearningWorks, and the Holocaust and Human Rights Center of Maine. I ran for Secretary of State five years ago on a platform of modernizing services to better serve the people of Maine. What I learned in that experience is that what you do in your first 100 days matters. So, in my first 100 days as Secretary of State, we initiated projects for appointments at the Bureau of Motor Vehicles so you can get in and out and on your way in 10 or 15 minutes. We implemented automatic voter registration so that when folks move to Maine and go to the BMV to get their license or their ID, they can register to vote at the same time. That built confidence in the legislature, so when I brought back a strategic plan for modernizing the BMV, they were on board. Now, we did what's called self-funding; we looked inside and found savings, and that's the approach I'll bring as your next governor. I think we need to modernize state government, I think we need to invest in manufacturing, and I think we need to serve and make Maine more business-friendly for a stronger economy. Thank you. Thank you. I feel like I need to lead some sort of stretching exercise here; it's been a long time. But, well, good afternoon, everybody. My name is Garrett Mason, and I'm from Lisbon Falls, the Moxie capital of the world. I'm a husband, a father, a small business owner, a former legislator, a lifelong Mainer, and I'd really like to be your next governor. You know, I didn't get my start in politics; I actually came from sports management. I used to work for the Portland Sea Dogs, and I used to work in hockey with the Lewiston Maineiacs—for those of you who remember them at the Colisée in Lewiston. It was my interactions with government in those places that led me to run for public office. When I was 25 years old, I got elected to the State Senate and served in that role for eight years. Four of those years I served as majority leader in the State Senate. While we were there, we accomplished some wonderful things. I am the only person on this stage that can say they actively lowered your income taxes, and that's something I'm very proud of. I'm the only person on this stage that has actively moved the ball forward on things like school choice, which is important for subject matters like CTE and workforce training. And I'm the only person on this stage who has been able to work with both sides of the aisle in crafting a two-thirds budget, which is so crucial in making sure that everybody has a seat at the table. You know, I think it's really important to answer the questions that John asked early on, so I'm going to take a stab at it. I believe the first 100 days start in August, and the reason for that is that is when the budget starts to be written for the next administration. So if I'm the Republican nominee for governor, I'm going to be dual-tracking a budget, and I'm going to be dual-tracking an election. That's really important. So I'm going to take the last one here: I think the greatest opportunity in manufacturing is our people. Maine people are hardworking people, and they need an opportunity to go ahead, and I'm going to make sure that happens as governor. So I'm Garrett Mason, and I appreciate your vote. Okay. So now we'll move on to the moderated discussion topics portion of the agenda, and since Garrett has the microphone, we'll start with him. These will be one-minute responses, but of course, I'm moderating so I can talk longer than that. So, I'd like to start out by talking about something that's on everybody's minds, and that is energy. Last month, longtime MFPC member, Lumber Hardwood, announced that it would be closing after 74 years of operation. When asked why, owner Steven Lumber said, quote, "Unfortunately, high operating costs and lack of saw logs due to the difficulties that the paper companies have endured are the main drivers of this decision. There seems to be a lot of hurdles lined up for businesses operating in Maine since the beginning of net energy billing and the increased costs for parts and supplies. This is the second forest industry business in Milo, a town with more than 86,000 solar panels, to close citing high electricity costs over the span of just two years." There's also been significant issues with the cost and availability of natural gas, which caused one paper mill last winter to idle for an entire month, impacting not only its employees but also the landowners and loggers during what is traditionally a peak harvest season. So, my question is, if you become governor, how will you reduce and stabilize industrial energy costs in Maine? Yeah, I mean, that's a great question and a very unfortunate circumstance for that business, but I think energy is probably one of the top items in the affordability agenda for any governor in this state. I can tell you that I am completely opposed to large-scale industrial solar and wind; it's been one of the primary drivers of increased costs in this state for a long time. We absolutely have to go after more natural gas. If you look up the ISO New England right now and what we're consuming, most of that is natural gas. We have to expand pipeline capacity, and we have to work with the administration to get a waiver for the Jones Act so we can import more liquid natural gas into Massachusetts so we can use that at a cheaper rate for people in America. I would declare on day one an energy emergency, which would give incredible latitude to be able to work towards lower energy costs on all fronts. And that's where I'm going to have to stop for now. Thank you. So, this is fun, because I completely disagree. I think that solar and wind have to be part of our future, as does biomass, but we're doing it wrong. We're balancing investments in solar on the backs of the ratepayers, and that's why we do have some of the highest electricity rates in the nation. But I think we have to go big on solar and wind. I think Maine is uniquely situated to be a high energy producer, but we also have to keep our energy in Maine for Maine, benefiting from our production and not just shipping it out to the other New England states without a profit. I think we need to invest in grid stabilization, and I think the governor needs to ensure more efficient permitting and procurement—this is something I've got experience in at the BMV with making business processes more efficient. Finally, my Economic New Deal for Maine, which you can check out on my website at bellowsformaine.com, includes capping profits at 6% for these international energy companies that are really hurting ratepayers, both industrial and residential. Thank you. Well, let me first start by acknowledging that the sky-high cost of energy is functionally a hidden tax, a phantom tax on all of the work that your corporations and businesses are doing. My approach is "all of the above," all bets on the table. I'm in favor of additional renewable investments, both solar and on- and offshore wind, because in the long term, they are zero-marginal-cost energy sources that, once we have them in place, will be part of Maine's energy future. But we also have to make sure that we are not exclusively relying upon those, because in the long run, that is a ways off. So, in the interim, I am in favor of additional sources of energy, whether that is biomass or, yes, natural gas. It will only be through a broader energy portfolio that we're actually able to bring our prices down. Parts of the country that have done so have not doubled down on one source of energy to the exclusion of others; they have introduced all of them into their energy mix. That's what I believe we should do for Maine. So, Maine had the fastest growing electricity costs in the country last year, by double anywhere else, and the fastest over the last seven years overall. If you look at the mix of electricity, that's a $2.5 billion market; $250 million of that is for solar subsidies. We have overbuilt there. And then in the winter, it's intermittent, it doesn't work as well, and you need to fire up places like Cousins Island at a really high rate, which impacts all of us. So we need to search for base-layer energy that's there when you want to turn the lights on. Where do you find that? You work to expand the Algonquin pipeline, which is a natural gas pipeline; it's a right-of-way that exists through Connecticut and Massachusetts. If you double the diameter of the pipe, or triple the diameter of the pipe, you can bring more in, and that will lower costs. Two, we've got to work to buy more hydro from Quebec. Three, explore small modular nuclear. And then four, biomass is a double whammy because it helps out with low-grade wood and energy all at the same time. So, I just announced a broad energy reduction cost affordability program, as I mentioned, at Robbins Lumber yesterday. I'd encourage you to go to my website and read the details because I can't say it all in one minute. But some of the elements relate to the stranded costs that we're seeing on ratepayers' backs. One thing is, you know, it's really easy for the legislature just to pass on costs to ratepayers rather than to absorb them in the budget, and a lot of these kind of social policies should be in the budget, but guess what, they don't show up. So the first thing I would propose is to stop doing it. Secondly, if you're going to propose costs to go to the ratepayers, we need a ratepayer fiscal note on those costs so that people know what they are. You can see other proposals I've got about creating a Maine Generation Authority, and about limiting the outrageously high return on equity that is given to our investor-owned utilities. Ideas—I'll tell you, it's simple. We have to go to the most practical sources that we can afford. You want to explore alternative fuels, I get it, but not at the taxpayers' expense. We already have ridiculously high electric costs. We've got net energy billing on our bill—did we vote for that, did we approve that? It just appeared. That's it. So we have to bring in the natural gas pipeline. Not only did we lose that factory, but we also lost Dragon Concrete; they couldn't get the natural gas pipeline in. That was a big company that invested a lot of money in the state of Maine, and they said, "Hey, we'll see you later, we're going to take our business elsewhere." And small modular nuclear reactors—it has to happen. Other states are doing it. So these are just common-sense approaches to things that we really need. The bottom line is, if you go into your house, and you flick the switch on, and the light doesn't come on, you're going to be one upset individual. We have to take a common-sense approach to energy. That will fix 90% of our problems that we have here in the state. Okay, so, for manufacturing and natural gas, you just need to create more storage. You buy it in the summer months when it's cheaper, and then the manufacturer has access to it. Electricity is not driven by natural gas prices or increases; it's half of what it was in 2022. It's all on the solar program. We have ratepayers paying $200 million in subsidy every year just for that program, and we're paying $0.30 a kilowatt hour, which is double the median average for the country, so we have to get that back in place. We'll repeal the issues of net energy billing that are hurting individuals, and then you get rid of the public policy charge, which is one of the things that is putting our businesses out of business. You've got to change the standard offer, repeal the hydro cap, and reappoint the PUC so we're doing things that are completely favorable to the folks of the state, and then you should have everything pretty much solved. Did I do that in under a minute? You did. Wow, I mean, I was like—I was waiting for the buzzer there. This is a huge challenge in Maine, and we absolutely need to do things differently. I've spent a lot of my career in the energy business, so I understand how complicated it is, but I also understand where there are real opportunities. I outlined a Ratepayers' Bill of Rights because I think it's critical that the next governor focus on how do we bring costs down for ratepayers. I think it starts with permitting and interconnection reform. Right now, other countries have figured out how to do it—protecting the environment while still allowing energy development. Other countries are developing wind and solar at a third of the cost that we are because we make it so hard on the front end. We also need to change the incentives for utilities. Right now, the only incentive is to build more things, which then costs more money. We need to give incentives—and they're doing it successfully in other parts of the country—in terms of more efficient use of the grid, more sensors, more storage, and more capacity, which ends up lowering the cost ultimately for ratepayers. And then finally, we need better deals for Maine. We have had some policies—some people have talked about them in Maine—that have added cost to Maine ratepayers. We've got to be a heck of a lot smarter so that we don't try new things on the backs of ratepayers. I know it can be done, but it's got to be done thoughtfully, and ideally by a governor who has spent a lot of his life in the renewable business. Thanks. This is a wonderful case study of what happens when you combine the recent trends in virtue signaling as a way of attaining power with one-party rule for a really long time. You get kind of a "greatest hits" of insider creep suffocating a market to the point where an entire society begins to die out. Obviously, I agree with my Republican colleagues that we need to shred the virtue-signaling solar subsidies that suffocate our bills across the board. We need to allow businesses to come in and generate power behind the grid, we need to bring back biomass and every other supply that we have suffocated, and fight for the supply we haven't suffocated. I think that we have to think about who might do this. This is complicated; it's multi-layered. So hopefully we'll be looking for somebody who is an outsider to the wall mold that has grown up, but is a changemaker at scale—someone who's built thousands of jobs in Maine, and who's helped thousands of businesses survive and thrive because I think that is the key to making each of the areas of wall mold growth, healthcare expansion at cost, energy, etc. So I have a 25-point plan, and you can go look at it.
