I actually will start by saying I love wood. I grew up in a home with no electric heat, and I cut four cords of wood every fall, and it worked very well. So we need to re-look at wood as a renewable, and then get rid of net energy billing. We need to complete the constitutional pipeline for natural gas that's cheaper, link the Iroquois, uncap the hydro, absolutely save SapPI, do not pull those four dams, take the energy office from 22 people down to two people, and sweep the PUC of political hacks and put on engineers, economists, and those who have utility expertise. And then we need to look again at reopening competition in this state, thinking in terms of pro-business, not anti-business. To agree with a lot of the other folks on this panel, we also need to look at small modular nuclear thorium reactors—very timely, it's going to take us two or three years to get there, but they're in beta tests now and could save us an enormous amount of money. We need to think about pro-business, not pro-government. Excellent. Do you guys want to stand up and stretch? Do you want to stand up and stretch? All right, you're good. Thank you for that. So the next topic is workforce and workforce development. Workforce continues to be one of the most significant challenges facing Maine manufacturers. Employers are competing for talent, managing rising costs, and adapting to new policies, including paid family and medical leave, all while trying to remain competitive. At the same time, there is a long-term need to strengthen the talent pipeline and ensure that more Maine people are aware of and prepared for careers in manufacturing and the skilled trades. If elected governor, how would you address workforce challenges in Maine, including labor availability, the impact of policies like PFML, and strengthening the pipeline into manufacturing and the trades? And we might as well just start right down here. So, I think we have suffered a cultural decline in 25 years, from hard-working—which is what everybody knew Mainers as—to not hard-working and permitting people to just sit on the couch and collect money. More than half the state is on entitlements. So the first thing we do is we think constructively about rebuilding the idea that hard work is the cornerstone of everything. Between 18 and 65, if you are able-bodied and you don't have children and you're not disabled, you will be encouraged to either work 20 hours a week, volunteer 20 hours a week, or go to school 20 hours a week, which incidentally is a win-win and it's what we're doing at the federal level but not here. In addition, you need to get back into the schools at the seventh and eighth-grade level, and start putting trades back in the schools. We had trades, I had IA, and you've got to get it done. And whether it's mandatory or whether it's available, it needs to be something the state encourages, not discourages. Then you just need to get out of the entitlement idea and start pulling the incentives. I always say I want to cut taxes, cut crime, and get the nonsense out of schools. Let us get back to outcomes being the determinant of what we're after, instead of having schools where you've got 75% of the fourth graders who can't read. We're not interested in activism—I know some folks on this panel are, but I'm not. I'm interested in a productive, prosperous future for our kids, and at the end of the day, that's where it begins. It begins with education, lower taxes, getting rid of the regulations, mandates, and all these imperatives on both the towns and the counties—which raise property taxes—and also on the businesses, and getting us back to common-sense, pro-business thinking. I remember when I opened, the state said, "Oh, you know, we've got a job training program." And I met the nice guy and I said, "Oh, please don't help me. I really don't need your help, sir. But if you could possibly fire yourself and reduce my taxes, that'd be great." When some people in our state government try to solve the decline in our economy, they think of programs that they can create. I have to say, as someone who has built a thousand jobs in this state, the freedom to move and the input costs are vastly more valuable than special programs that wrap around you like the well-meaning manacles of an octopus. It's really important that we get a governor that understands this, and that we strip back the programming and do the obvious: the electrical costs, the healthcare costs, the housing costs, and the tax costs. That's the job. Simple beats smart, 7 days a week when you're in a crisis, and we're in one. Yeah, our workforce challenge is very real. Maine's population is relatively flat, but the problem is we're all getting older, and so we end up actually shrinking our workforce every year by tens of thousands of people. To me, it's a couple of things that are really important. Number one, as has been said, we need more young people in the state, and the only way you get young people to come is by having a place they can afford. So we've got to build more housing so that they can afford to live here, we've got to work on healthcare so that they can access it, and we've got to work on childcare and all those other things that make families come here. And finally, we've got to work on education. People move for good schools, and right now Maine schools are in the low 40s, and that's a place where I think I worry a lot of people aren't going to want to move. So to me, the first piece we've got to focus on is how do we make our schools better, with accountability, with making sure the kids are starting with reading by the time they finish in third grade, and then moving on to training in middle school and high school. Right now, I think we have 300,000 people in this state who have no credential of any value. I think we can do a lot more in terms of training for people so that they can access the jobs, and make sure there's more connection especially between business and some of our colleges, like Thomas, which is doing a great job right now, so that there are people who are trained for the jobs that we have both today and tomorrow. Okay, so, trade schools are obviously incredibly important. You have to drive more folks into the trades; we have a desperate need for that, and the state can do a better job of that. But to drive the overall workforce in the state, you have to have a reason to work in Maine, and right now we're not supporting the fundamental building blocks that drive people to open businesses in Maine. You can talk to anybody who's looking at doing business in Maine or doing business in Maine, and they will struggle to tell you why this is a good state to do business. So you have to turn that around. There is not enough time to go over all the things we can do there from that standpoint, but there's a tremendous amount of things. You need a very pro-growth, pro-business based governor, and then again, you have to get Maine off of this government dependency aspect we're on. Over 400,000 people are on state services. We are 4% lower than the national labor force participation rate. Give folks the opportunity for economic freedom, get them off of government dependency, do it in a positive way, and then things are going to start to change. Well, workforce is something I've dealt with my whole life. I had a very large company with a lot of people. But you know, back then when I would have someone that didn't do their job, I could fire them. Now you've got to be worried about your unemployment compensation rate going up, you've got to be worried about paid family medical leave, and you've got all these things that are going against you as a business. And, you know, when I fired somebody back then, I could hire somebody the next day. We can't do it. We can't do it. These people don't—you know, we've got people who don't want to work. And you know, what is it that creates jobs? Businesses. Well, if we want young people to stay, you've got to have an environment that's going to allow businesses to succeed, and if the businesses can't succeed, then bottom line, the jobs won't be there. And if it's not affordable to be in the state of Maine, guess what, the businesses will not come and invest in our state. We have to change our model. We have to have affordable energy; that is the bottom-line answer to every question you're going to ask today. I think the issue with getting workers in Maine overall, and getting young people to Maine, does rely on, as Angus said, first, affordability. We all have family members who want to live in Maine, and they can't afford to live where the jobs are. You all have people that work for you that are struggling. Our housing costs are way too high, childcare is unavailable, and we need more housing, we need more available childcare—all of these issues are interrelated. Yes, we should do work promoting the trades. It used to be a dirty word to get into welding—well, guess what, you can make a lot more money as a welder than you can in a lot of white-collar jobs, and we need more welders. The paid family medical leave was a question. That was a gold-plated program coming out of the gate, and you know, I'm worried about its sustainability. I think Maine's next governor is going to have to look at that program and make sure it's sustainable, because I worry that it's going to be driving people away from Maine and businesses away from Maine too. I think we've entered into a world where manufacturing is where some of the best jobs will be in the future. I remember having a conversation with my father a couple of years back when ChatGPT came out, and I showed it to him and played around with it. He barely graduated high school, blue-collar guy, and he looks at me and he's like, "Owen, look, how's your master's degree feel right about now? This thing can do everything you can do, and plumbers can do everything you can't. They're going to take over the world." And now, where we sit right now, I think the surefire way to have a career is to go build something physically and do something physical. I think if we celebrate that, and we talk about that, and we give our people the aspiration that that is the noble thing to go and do, you're going to get more and more workforce in. And we do talk about the trades a long time out here saying, "Well, we should go have more people be in the trades, more people be in the trades." But as an engineer, I actually found a problem: we have more demand for our CTE programs than we have supply of slots. Let's open more slots. That would help solve that problem. As I've been on the campaign trail, grappling with this question, my first thought was: boy, how do we entice young people to move to Maine? But the more young folks I talk to and the more data I looked at, I realized that's not really the right question. Young folks want to move to Maine. It's not a question of how to entice them, it's how to make it easier for them to get here. And as my colleagues have mentioned, the four things that have come up over and over and over again, as I've talked to younger folks, are housing, healthcare, childcare, and education. Housing, healthcare, childcare, and education. If we can make inroads on any of them, let alone all of them, to make housing a little bit cheaper, healthcare not completely broken, and childcare just a click more affordable, then young people will find it more feasible to come here to Maine versus the other states that they can choose from. Here's one thing I'll throw in, in my last 10 seconds: we always think about workforce as a younger person's thing. There are already a lot of older Mainers here in the state who want to get back into the workforce. We have an opportunity to put them back in the workforce as well. So, my mother worked in a greenhouse for about 20 years, no benefits, no healthcare, and at age 49, she looked around and she decided she needed to do something different. So she went back to school, first through adult ed, then the University of Maine at Augusta, then the University of Maine at Orono, graduated summa cum laude with an RN, and became a home healthcare nurse during the pandemic. So, when we talk about workforce, we need to create pathways of opportunity, both for our young people and for older Mainers, to enter into manufacturing and these high-paying jobs. And that includes investment in our schools. I intend to be Maine's education governor; I think that's why the Maine teachers have endorsed me as their number-one ranked candidate. I think we need to invest in the industrial arts and trades in our high schools—that's really important. I think we need to continue the free community college program, but we need to expand it to the University of Maine system so that the young people who are poor, as I was, wanting to go into engineering, can attend. And we need to go on to think about, again, those older Mainers like my mom, because Maine needs to be a place, a destination, not just for vacationers, but a place that people can afford to live, work, and prosper. Yeah, I think the workforce development piece of our entire economy is very important, and I agree with a lot of what folks have said. That's the benefit of being the last one in the line, right? About affordability, I think if you fix that problem on all levels, then you're really going to move the ball forward. But I want to talk some specifics about education in general. I believe in school choice. I believe that charter schools can have a role to play, specifically virtual charter schools can have a role to play in partnering up with our CTEs to provide more individualized learning. I think that one of the other things I'm really concerned about is our apprenticeship programs. In the past few years, we have had apprenticeship programs be more specialized and aimed at basically, just to be honest, union-only programs that will get the reimbursement from the state. So I want to make sure that if you have an apprenticeship program, you are able to have the state help you through that program. I think if we start to solve some of these workforce issues, we're going to be able to get people to actually be able to stay here, live here, and thrive here. All right, I think we'll mix things up and maybe start with Senator Bennett and go that way this time, so that you're not always first or last. Surprise. Yeah. Okay, so next I want to talk about regulatory and tax policy. As you know, volatility in Augusta makes it exceedingly difficult to plan for large manufacturing investments that take decades to recoup. For example, this year lawmakers considered a supplemental budget that would have eliminated an important tax program utilized by 60 forest products businesses. Had it passed as presented, it would have resulted in an immediate and retroactive tax increase of $4.7 million this year alone. Our members rallied, and fortunately we were able to defend the program, at least for non-retail equipment. Another damn example is Shawmut on the Kennebec. After extensive back-and-forth with Maine DEP over the renewal of the water quality certificate, Brookfield made the understandable decision to pull its application and sell the asset to an entity with plans to remove the structure. While all sides seem committed to maintaining the water needs of Sappi's flagship mill, there is no engineering solution to do so without the impoundment created by the dam. The current uncertainty surrounding the largest wood-consuming mill in the Northeast is impacting long-term planning and investment decisions throughout the entire supply chain. These are just two examples. There are many more. So my question is: what would you do to make Maine a more predictable and business-friendly place to grow, invest, and create jobs? Thanks for the question. I have to say that the one thing that business people want, and people making investment decisions—and I've been there as a CEO—is predictability. Maine's next governor needs to prioritize consistency over chaos, and we're getting a lot of chaos from government at all levels these days. Chris is talking about the BETR program. The governor proposed, actually in a supplemental budget during a short legislative session, a major shift of decades over this program. She proposed to eliminate it. It is outrageous. We can't be making these proposals in this disorganized, dysfunctional way. And yes, part of that passed. Retail facilities who have been relying on that for years lost out. You know, all across the state I heard this thing. Hoodiemic—where Krista, you took me to Hoodiemic, hosted us there. I've heard it from Sappi, I've heard it from everyone. They're just looking for consistency because the investor is looking for as little political risk as possible, since there's so much other risk they have to deal with. So, consistency over chaos. This way? Yes, sir. It's called the pendulum of politics, right? The Democrats get in, and they make all kinds of restrictions. The Republicans get in, and what do we do? We eliminate the restrictions. We have 135,000 regulations in the state of Maine. It's ridiculous. It's just absurd that we have this many regulations, and every one of those regulations costs whatever business they affect money, right? And this is what it is: it's an affordability factor. So, we just need a common-sense approach to simplicity, right? What's right and what's wrong. You know, you can't have it where you're going to attract a company or a big corporation to the state of Maine that's going to create 500 or 1,000 jobs if they don't know what's going to happen when the next person is governor, or if the Democrats or the Republicans have control of the House and the Senate. We need consistency and it has to be made law, so the policies that I'll put into place, I will do everything I can to codify them into law. All right, so first, the Shawmut Dam deal is essentially the dumbest deal I've ever seen in my life. That never should have happened, and it has to be killed, any way you talk about it. The other issue is you have to have a pro-business focus with, you know, a combination of cutting spending. It took 200 years to get to a $7 billion budget in this state, and it only took seven more years to get to a $12 billion budget. That's $5 billion that came from all of you, came from businesses. And right now you have situations like that tax that Krista mentioned because the state is going after every tax it can possibly find, because you're increasing spending almost a billion dollars a year. So, that needs to be turned around. You need to understand the balance with pro-business policies, slightly limited government, because we're in deep now so we've got to unwind it as best we can, and then get us back to the place where people can take a breath, we support business, we support individuals, and then we go up from there. I've spent most of my life building things, and the thing that I found is successful is by bringing everybody together onto common ground and sharing a vision about how you're going to accomplish it, then everybody moves forward together. And every one of those projects took a long time to build, longer usually than a single administration. And so part of why I'm running is to think about how do we make sure that there is enough predictability so that these projects can withstand a change in administration. David just talked about Democrats doing this, then Republicans doing that. To me, what's really important is that, while I'm hoping a Democrat is the governor that brings lots of people into the conversation, we create rules and regulations and decisions that have some long-term sustainability, that have buy-in from Republicans and Democrats so that we can get things done. I think we've got a lot of common issues in terms of how do we build more housing, how do we make our schools better, and how do we lower our healthcare costs. And if we can do those things in some kind of bipartisan way, I think we'll really have an opportunity to make a real difference here in Maine, so that we don't have as much volatility and so that businesses do have the predictability that they really crave. I completely agree, the worst thing is a long "maybe," and we really need a better job of not pulling the rug out. And Maine's reputation, unfortunately, across the country is that we have earned a reputation of changing the rules midway. We've got to stop doing that, and I think the best way to do it is to find some common ground and move forward. Yeah, I don't think we're at the bring-everybody-together place. I think we're at the George Orwell bad dream turned into real-life place. And this Brookfield example is a beautiful case in point. This is a company that makes hydropower that had a bullet put in its head by the state government in the name of, wait for it, environmentalism. A hydropower company was shot in the head by the state in the name of environmentalism. When the state plays such a large role, you get to places where Orwellian moments come true. They're everywhere in Maine. You can do a crime, and the police can't take you to jail if you identify as homeless. These are things that other places, if you say them, the bartender cuts you off like you're crazy. Ten seconds? Yes, sir. It's probably enough. I'm suggesting that it's not a time for all holding hands, but getting really serious about saying we have an emergency, we've gone nuts, and we need to grow up, or our state's economy will die. So, two observations and three recommendations. First, I think it was Ralph Waldo Emerson who said, "A foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds." We don't need more foolish consistency. I will also say that we're in an odd environment in which common ground is great to find, but I find it ironic that we're talking about a Democrat legislature that literally passes budgets without any consideration whatsoever for the Republicans. So, we're not going to get—Jonathan is right—we're not going to get the common ground. Three basic observations or recommendations to get us where we belong: We need to roll back hundreds, a legislator told me recently thousands, of unfunded mandates on towns and counties that will roll back property taxes. That will allow seniors to stay on fixed incomes in their homes and young people to get that low-rent place that then saves money and gives them a real home later like we had, the American Dream. Second thing, we need to cut the income tax. And people say, "Oh Bobby, you're so unrealistic, $4 billion." No, when you go from $7 billion to almost $13 billion, I have CPAs and former senior people in the state saying, "Yeah, you can pull $4.5 billion likely over four years out of this." You need to do it. You need to cut the spending out of that 800-page budget. And the last thing you need to do is roll back regulations, and it won't take me nine seconds to roll them all back. All right, we'll go to Honorable Garret Mason. Thank you. Well, I think one of the things that I would like to say to you all is that you have fantastic representatives on either side of the stage who fought valiantly for the bill that Krista was talking about, which was LD 195. And what that would have allowed you to do is it would have allowed you to save up profits over time, and when you wanted to make some sort of infrastructure investment, you would have been able to have access to that cash over the subsequent years that you have saved for it. I think that's a fantastic plan. I think it's one that I would support, and representing a different organization, I testified in favor of that, so it's regrettable that that didn't happen. So, that's one thing I would support. And the other thing is I think we need to take a hard look at the reasons why we didn't pass tax conformity. You know, that makes it very difficult for businesses and individuals; they have to file different sets of returns anyways, but why make them harder? So that's something I'd look at as well. And as far as the dam goes, if I'm governor, I will do everything that I can to make sure that dam stays in place and fight off the removal of that. So there is an area that I agree with Republicans on, and that is that there is too much waste in the state budget, and that there is red tape in state government that we need to eliminate. And I'm the only one on this stage with experience doing so at the Department of Secretary of State, both in elections and in the Bureau of Motor Vehicles. In the Bureau of Motor Vehicles, on my first day in office, a car dealer called me and said, "You've got to do something about titles." It was a completely paper-based process, and we had months-long backlogs. I eliminated the backlog and we implemented electronic lien titling. We got rid of fax machines for the car insurance industry that was having to send everything into the BMV by fax or paper. That saved a lot of money and a lot of time. Now, we just recently rolled out a BMV branch, a mobile unit, that you can drive to rural communities. We deployed it in Belfast and Piscataquis County. And what I'm proposing as governor is a one-stop shop for businesses so that we simplify the paperwork, that we cut red tape, and that we reduce the challenging regulatory environment we have in Augusta. Thank you. As I mentioned at the top, one of the pieces that has come up in virtually every meeting with business owners—small, medium, and large—has been the need for predictability and stability. So, that is what I will prioritize as governor. I've heard from so many folks, and you and your colleagues, who tell me stories about the way that the rug was taken out from underneath them, or that the rule that they had operated on for years was changed, and now they were being asked to pay back taxes retroactively. What I will focus on is that predictability and stability going forward, whether that's around regulatory reform, permitting reform, or an area that I've worked on when I was in the Midwest, which is harmonizing regulations both across the state, as well as across the region. There's no reason why the food code in Maine needs to be different from the food code next door, the same with building and electrical codes. As for the dam, I supported the removal of the dam. There are engineering questions that will have to be answered, but we as a country and as a species have answered and figured out engineering problems that are far more difficult than this one. I have every bit of confidence that we will figure this one out as well. So Maine currently sits at 45th in business climate and 50th in new businesses per capita, according to the Maine Jobs Council report in 2024. We're sitting in a moment where I look at this both as a turnaround job for the state and a growth job. We need to grow jobs at the same time. And so as a turnaround job, you know, I don't like consistency as a word—I feel like we've got to really make some changes—but I do like predictability. That's why I released my plan, Maine 2040, to talk about how we get to the point where we turn around and we grow. Things we need to do: one, we need to make an instant cut to income tax and phase it out over eight years, and there's a plan to do that. Two, on regulations, there's 135,000 small businesses and 135,000 regulations. We're going to, for every new rule proposed in my administration, we're going to cut two, so that naturally is going to go down and be easier to operate. And then we're going to be transparent about where we spend the money in the state government so the people of Maine know where it is. We're going to fix the business climate; we need to turn it around, while we make the bets that I talked about earlier. All right, thank you very much. So this next topic is on education. I know a lot of you had alluded to education in the other questions. Educational outcomes are directly tied to Maine's long-term economic competitiveness. At the same time, many employers are struggling to find skilled workers, and too few students are being exposed to careers in manufacturing and the trades. So the question is: what is your plan to improve educational outcomes in Maine, and how would you encourage more students to pursue careers in manufacturing and the trades? So, Owen, why don't we start with you and go that way? Excellent, thank you. Back-to-back, yeah. So I have said in the plan, I've said this many times, my vision is to be the reading governor for the state of Maine. Right now, one in four fourth graders can read at grade level. We were fourth in the country when I went through the schools, we're now near last, right? The beauty in the United States is people have figured it out. Mississippi solved this problem. What did they do? It didn't take them more money to solve the problem—maybe a little bit, but not a lot more money. They passed the Science of Reading bill, which says that you must teach evidence-based curriculum. It still lets teachers pick curriculum, so you have local control, but it's got to be proven to work. According to MEPRI, which is an independent institute in Maine, 50% of the curriculum right now that is taught is proven not to work, and then we wonder why the kids can't read. I focus on reading because you learn to read up to the third grade, and you read to learn the rest of your life. You're not going to be a very good plumber, tradesperson, or engineer if you can't read. I think we can make a difference on that; we can make a difference in three years on those scores. We do that, we prove we can solve something, we'll go solve more things. Just to prove that bipartisanship is very much alive, I agree with every single thing that Mr. McCarthy said. And so I'll take it another step further. Another reason why Maine schools have also fallen significantly in the ranks, and a way that other states have really turned their education systems around—in addition to focusing on reading, I will add math as well—is chronic absenteeism, which has really become a bigger and bigger issue in Maine. It's one that Mississippi, Tennessee, and others have really addressed head-on. We've got opportunities to do so here. I know that everyone in this room is a big fan and supporter of CTE, as am I. That's another area for us to address your question about how we encourage more folks to go into the trades. I don't think we need a new special program that encourages folks to consider a career in manufacturing and the trades. The economics are very strong already. Now it's a question of making sure that those who want to go into those fields have the training opportunities available to them. I think education is one of the top three priorities of the next governor, and I rolled out an Education New Deal for Maine on my website. I'll just say this: but for Hancock Grammar School and Ellsworth High School, I wouldn't be sitting here today as Maine's first female Secretary of State and a Democratic candidate for governor. I owe everything to my public education here in Maine, so I think it's really important. However, we are funding education in the most regressive, absurd way possible: on the backs of the property taxpayers, and we're creating a neighbor-against-neighbor fight, right? Parents of kids who want better quality schools for their kids, and folks who are worried about losing their homes. So I'm proposing a statewide moratorium on property taxes for Maine residents. And, I'm proposing doubling the property taxes of out-of-staters who are gobbling up our property, because one in six Maine homes is a vacation home. And I'm proposing that in my first budget introduced in January of '27, I will introduce full funding for special education, for the free lunch program, and increasing teacher pay so we can recruit teachers in our schools. That's just a little bit of my Education New Deal, and I look forward to talking more. It's important. Yeah, so I think we're all going to hit on different subjects as we go along. Education is a big one. But, I do tend to agree on the reading piece. I mean, it is so imperative. Believe it or not, before I got a business degree, I went to school to be a kindergarten teacher. That did not work out, for a lot of reasons. But part of that is you learn really early on that if you can get a kid to read in kindergarten, they improve so quickly. One of the things I'm really concerned about in government right now is Child Development Services. The waiting lists are far too long for kids that are having difficulty with their speech and hearing, and if you can catch those problems very early, the outcomes are so much better. So I'm really focused on CDS and making sure that kids who are in preschool and before have the ability to be able to fix problems that could hurt them when they get into kindergarten. So, there's a lot of fraud, waste, and abuse there. We've seen that, and I'd like to clean that up because I think it would add to the entire education system. Maine's education system is in a complete freefall. We are in a place we've never been. In 1992, we were the best in the country. Our outcomes were what we measured, not inputs. So, we've doubled the amount of money and ended up, by one counting, 50th in the country, by another, 41st in the country. Understand, I graduated from an unaccredited high school. My whole family, by the way, are educators. My mother was a remedial reading teacher, grandmother principal, two of my siblings teach. I graduated from an unaccredited high school and survived Dartmouth College, Oxford University, Columbia Law School, and beyond. Why? Because the school system back then was awesome. Instead of doing that, we have bulked it up with administrators. They need to be fired. We need to cut in half the education department. We need to go back to empowering teachers and thinking about kids with outcomes, not a curriculum focused on activism and political—I don't know what you're going to call it—soft education, not real education. You only prosper when you know math, and reading, and science, and history, and facts, and shop. Get it back in the schools. We know the numbers: first to 41st, or by other accounts worse. We know the creep, the expansion of superintendents and administrators and ancillary programs, and we know the ball hiding—the inability to find our reading scores on state DOE websites. This is a turnaround situation. We need a narrow mission focused on one thing. As Owen said, I agree, thing one should be reading by fourth grade. If we can't get that, the school gets fired, the money goes to the families, and we can take the cap off of charter schools, we can take the cap off of homeschooling, we can take the cap off of religious schools, we can let people go to other towns. But we have got to put pressure on these schools. I don't mind providing them some resources, but the mission must be reading. To do that, we need disruption. At Athena we had a program called, "More Disruption, Please", and we would invite companies to sell to our customers. And they would compete with us, and we would lose accounts—not the whole account, but products—to competitors. But it made us a better company. We have a monopolistic insider system in education, and what we need to do is do a little more disruption, please. So my education plan is called "Back to Being the Best". You have heard all of the statistics. It starts with—and I talk about—students and teachers and community. On the student front, reading, we've beaten it to death, but unfortunately we still haven't solved it, and I think that's really important. The next piece though is also about training. We do a pretty good job in a couple of places—St. George, Gorham, Brunswick—but those are individual places, and we've got to figure out how to leverage that and do it statewide, so that every kid by the time they graduate from high school, either has a job, an apprenticeship, a training program, or a pathway to college. I know that we can do it and that should be Maine's commitment to our kids so that, again, we have the best trained, best educated workforce in the state. The next piece I would talk about though is what are we doing in our communities so that they know how the schools are doing. I think there's got to be transparency about how they're doing because then you can deploy the energy and the resources and the support so that businesses get involved, parents get involved, and community leaders get involved to help the schools that are struggling. We need, again, to copy the Mississippi path where you get tutors in, you get mentors for the teachers, and you help the people who are struggling rather than just punish them, so that everybody in our public schools succeeds as a result. Okay, so I'm still dealing with the education system day-to-day because I have kids in school. The issue we've had with the schooling is the change from academics to social-emotional learning. That is a failed experiment; it is not doing good for our kids. We've seen a 70% increase in investments, and some of the worst test scores we've ever had. So we do have to redirect the curriculum guidelines. Evidence-based high-quality instruction materials, as was mentioned, is the way to go. You do need to stop teaching ideology in the schools. I can't tell you how many assignments my daughter has come home with from English class that talked about Trump and, just the other day, one on how to be a good protester to the point that you will go to jail. Okay, we have to stop trying to teach kids what to think, we need to teach them how to think. And once you get back to the basics in this area and refocus our attention on what needs to happen, you're going to see greatly improved results, happier kids, and more people staying in Maine because they can succeed with a good education. Well, as governor, I can tell you the first thing I'll do is get rid of DEI, SEL, and CRT. I have eight grandchildren getting ready to enter the school system, and I'm telling you I'm scared. They're being taught—I had a five-year-old granddaughter come back, she said, "Papa, they read me a book about a little boy wearing a girl's dress, called A Boy in a Tangerine Dress." She says, "I didn't want to listen to it, I covered my ears but I could still hear it." She knew how upsetting it was. And then she says, "But Papa, please don't do anything because they won't like me." So here you have a child, five years old, innocent mind, there to learn, being taught things I don't want her to learn and also learning fear of retribution. We have 240,000 school-aged children in the state of Maine. 170,000 of them are attending public schools. We have 190 superintendents in the state of Maine. That's one for every 800 children. Other states, they have one superintendent per 40,000 children. Why in God's name do we have this top-heavy administration? The Mississippi Miracle? I want a Maine miracle. I know we can do it. Mainers are great and if we get together and we work together, we can have a Maine miracle. They were scared to death to implement the Mississippi miracle, and I'll tell you what: they're happy as hell that they did. Their success rate is through the roof and that's what I want for the state of Maine. I'm actually really happy to hear the focus of all the candidates on this really critical issue and a lot of the common problems we've talked about. I'd just say: teachers, kids, parents. We are hemorrhaging 1,800 teachers a year. Okay? The classroom is a different place than it was when I was in school, and there's a lot of burdens being placed on teachers right now. We need to, yes, pay them more. The minimum wage for teachers is too low, but that doesn't solve the problem. We also need to give them working places where they feel supported and they can get their job done the way they want to do it. The kids, as we've heard, are not learning; they're not learning numeracy, they're not learning literacy. They need to learn to read in order to read to learn, as you've heard. We also need to understand that when the kids are going into school they need help, and Garret touched on this. A lot of kids need some support going in, in today's world. And in terms of parents, we need to make parents welcome again in their school's education. A lot of parents are put off by the systems that have been created in our public school systems. I just have to say though, that Maine's governor under this constitution is pretty constrained as to what a governor can directly do because education is handled under our constitution at the local level. And so I, as governor, will appoint a very respected and bold commissioner who will lead and who the superintendents will follow, to move Maine in a new direction. Okay, so now we're going to transition just a little bit. Both organizations asked our members for some questions in advance that could be asked here today. To ask that question on behalf of the Maine Forest Products Council, we have Joel. Joel is the chair of our Manufacturing Committee, and he will ask that question. Thank you, Krista. Here in Maine—excuse me, here in Maine, our mills compete in a global marketplace. It doesn't matter whether you're talking about a big mill or a small mill, they all compete with mills all over the world, and a lot of them are coming from areas that are a lot more cost-competitive than what we have here in Maine. A lot of these mills are going to require modernization to be able to stay competitive in the marketplaces to come. Would you support tax incentives, financing tools, or public-private partnerships to help these mills upgrade equipment so that they can remain efficient and viable, making the products that their customers need and supporting the communities in which they work and live? And you get to pick who goes first. Mr. King, I think we'd like you to go first. The answer is yes, we need to be supporting our heritage industries, right? We have some great industries here in Maine, in our fiber, in our oceans, and we have some great skillsets in our state as well, in terms of shipbuilding, working in the forests, and serving tourists. So I think it's really important that the state focus in on where Maine has competitive advantages, including in those industries, and then what we can do. Right now FAME does a pretty good job, and I think other organizations like that, MTI, do a pretty good job in terms of supporting new businesses and supporting that kind of growth, and I think Maine's got to continue to lean in on the industries where we do have real advantage. I would want to make sure that we don't lean in on and put our thumb on the scale of something where Maine doesn't naturally compete. I think that's probably a mistake. But in terms of where we really do have a long history and long advantages, I think it's very important that the state lean in on workforce, on a lot of the other things we've talked about so far, because you're absolutely right: a lot of that modernization has to happen. I'm not a big fan of government involvement in industry in general, but in certain circumstances, again, where Maine's got an advantage, I'm supportive. I'm going to go with "hell no," and I'm as close to an industrialist as you can get. As a corporate CEO who ran a company through an IPO, I came to the state and got little tax gimmicks to get in here. We don't need more gimmickry where you have to know somebody. I can't stand hearing, particularly the Democrats running, saying, "I'll have an open door policy. I will listen to you." Well, that's because it's so complicated you need to get in the governor's door. I have an open door policy too, but you won't need the door because the rules will be clean, the taxes will be low, and it will be an attractive place to come. Every time we add a little state program, there's a whole patron-client network that builds up around it and adds to the bloat that we are now experiencing. What we need to do is be brave, shrink Augusta, cut those rules, and then not also ask for a little side setup breaks ourselves. We will do so much better than whatever those little programs the state might give us, instead. So Jonathan and I, of course, agree on everything, but I'm going to disagree on this. I'll just say: hell yes. Tax incentives and a tax code that help to recapitalize, accelerate depreciation, and do all the things that get people to reinvest in businesses—particularly when we understand that we do have labor costs, we do need to train, and we do need to get our education system back on track—are essential. We do need to recapitalize the industrial base in this state. It's fundamental. Drive anywhere in the state—and I've been to every corner of this state—and you see that what we're missing are the fundamental elements of a tax code that encourage business, and regulations that are rolled back to encourage business, not the reverse. Just distinguish between tax code and tax incentive. Tax code, yes. Little tax incentive gimmick, no. Well, I'm not going to swear in a church, so I'll just say yes. But, the bottom line on all of it is that we do compete in a global marketplace in this industry, absolutely. And you have countries like Canada, you have countries like Sweden and Finland, who are heavily subsidizing their industries beyond a tax credit. So we have to do something to make sure that you're competitive. That's why I did support LD 195, that's why I support the Pine Tree Development Zones when I was in the legislature, and it's why I support Dirigo incentives. Until we're able to lower the cost of doing business in Maine, we have to provide those incentives for you to be able to compete in a global marketplace. I totally agree with that. Regarding the Tree Growth Program, we heard some interesting stuff at the forum that we're in here at the Samoset that I learned today. So, yes, I am in favor of some of those programs. But eventually I'd like you to just be able to compete because the regulations and the taxes are so low in Maine that it's a bad deal to not compete here. Short answer: yes. And what I will say is I think we do need to increase access to capital to businesses in our state, and grow our markets, especially for forest products. And I think we made a big mistake in not putting mass timber, or laminated lumber, into the baseline budget. I would take the $50 million proposal, not bond it, but put it into my budget so that we can create a new market. With this housing boom and the needs for housing, the fact that Bowdoin College and the Portland Museum of Art are going out of state or even overseas to get their mass timber for sustainable buildings is insane, given the quality of our forest products and the lagging industry here in our state. So I think this is a role for government. I think access to capital—my economic New Deal for Maine includes the Maine Transformation Fund for startup entrepreneurs. I've been meeting with entrepreneurs at Startup Maine, and I've been meeting with Focus Maine, to talk about how we grow the economy. In addition to Maine's mills having to compete with those in other countries where there are significant government subsidies, they have to compete—as you well know better than I—with mills in other parts of the country where the tax environment is already more favorable. So, my approach is that right now we are in a world where we're going to have to run as fast as possible just to stay in the same place. So with respect to the three buckets of incentives that you laid out: investment tax credit, without a doubt. I've heard from so many business owners about the way that not only has that just rewarded decisions they were going to make, but rather actually caused them to expand their capital. A program that's just rewarding entities for decisions they were going to make anyways isn't actually generating new value. But I have heard very clearly from folks who have said, "No, this changed our strategic direction." So on that, yes. For a secured low-income interest capital pool? Maybe, maybe not. I know there was recent legislation that put that into place. Public-private partnerships, when applicable, are usually for research, but much more limited. So I talked in the beginning about forest products being one of the strategic areas we need to make a big bet on. And when I say that, I mean let's say in the Department of Economic Development, we're going to have a team of hunters that knows the industry really well, but also meets with the executives—including the governor—on a yearly basis in my Maine Means Business initiative. We can then take a lot of existing programs out there that FAME has, tailor them to the industry, and make sure the nuances work for those. We're going to continue those—you know, the new market tax credits, leaning into opportunity zones, everything that we have—and make sure everyone is fully aware of them. We're also going to make sure that the MEIF funding, which is on the research side for the forest products side, sees an increase, because I think the more innovation that we can have coming into the space, the better we're going to be able to compete while we bring energy costs down. And then finally, I have a concept of a forest products incubator, like states like Alabama have done, that we can do here to really bring things in such that we can rebuild the industry here. Great ideas here. You know, I have to say to the question: yes. But let me just elaborate. We have some devastated communities in Maine—Lincoln, Millinocket, East Millinocket—our traditional mill towns. They've got wonderful assets up in these locations, and they need help. They need help rebuilding devastated industries, and I think, in a productive way, government can—and state government particularly, and the governor very specifically—use these resources like tax credits and special rate contracts on electricity in order to attract business demand. I will be a vigorous and assertive governor as chief ambassador for the state of Maine to try to, particularly in those devastated communities, bring new businesses in. I'll use every tool at my disposal, and I will also listen. Steve Smith, the ex-CEO of L.L. Bean, used to convene meetings of CEOs for the governor every quarter. I will reinstitute that because we need to start listening, and the governor needs to listen to what the business leaders need in order to be responsive in a way that doesn't cost money for taxpayers without delivering real results in terms of jobs and opportunities for Maine people. I have a real problem with government investing in people's businesses. All right, it's too subjective. You know, I've never taken a penny in my life from government. I've earned every dollar I've ever had. I think the best thing we can do is get government the hell out of the way. I'm sick and tired of all these programs: "This is going to be the program for that, and the program for that, and we're going to do this for you." You know: "I'm from the government, I'm here to help." Run for the hills. All right, I'm sorry. You know, I will support the mills in every which way possible. I think the mills have been fighting Governor Mills and the policies that she's put into place. So, for me, I'm a common-sense guy. I've been in business my entire life, I've always balanced my checkbook, I've handled millions and millions of dollars, and I've done transactions. I own F.L. Putnam Real Estate; it's a 207-year-old company here in the state of Maine, and it's Maine's oldest continuously running company. I bring big investment into this state on a regular basis. And I'll tell you, it sickens me to see all the blockades that government has put out there. So, yes, I want to help you in any way, shape, or form that I can as governor, but I believe that it's taxpayer money and it's not mine to give. If it's a good investment, there will be plenty of private partners that would be willing to step up and invest in what you're doing. Well first, I want to thank Angus and Bobby for immediately throwing me cough drops when I was choking on my water there. I have never gotten cough drops faster! Anyways, so yes, I would set up a program similar to FAME, but increase it specifically for legacy industries because the state's going to make money if they set up a good financing pool. We've done really interesting financing pools in my industry before. So you get cheaper access to capital, the state ends up making money, we support our legacy industries, and then, in terms of forest products in general, you just have to really push innovation. The state needs to help as well because when you lose these mills, you have fewer uses for the wood, you can't harvest as much of what you would normally harvest, and then it just becomes completely unproductive. Once we have more uses for the wood, you can fill all gaps in the market, get the industry back where it needs to be, and we need to do the same for all of our legacy industries. Thank you for that. So, we also had a question submitted from members at the Manufacturers Association of Maine, and Brad Hittle, our board chair, will go ahead and read that question. Am I on? Okay. Maine has a long history of key pieces of the United States defense industrial base. In a time when the National Defense budget is likely to exceed $1 trillion next year, what can we do at the state level to ensure that Maine's industrial base—20% of the manufacturers represented by the association are in the defense industry—is ready to rise to that challenge? Do you want someone to mix... Dr. Shah. Great. Well, as you noted, much of where this has to go is not just what is uniquely within what the state or the governor can do, but what the governor can do in partnership with our federal delegation. So I don't think it's uniquely the purview of the governor. That said, there is a lot that the governor can be doing. The first is making sure that the manufacturers within that industry have the support they need from the state level. A lot of that comes down to things like infrastructure, access to available resources that they need, and making sure that they've got the capital access that they need—for example, we talked about FAME, which is just one piece of it. The other piece that I've heard repeatedly when I've spoken with the leadership of those organizations is not so much that they need help to secure the contracts. Again, our federal delegation plays a big role there, but they need help reducing the other costs that their businesses face. We've already talked about energy costs. The one we haven't zeroed in on is healthcare costs. Their healthcare costs are sky-high. In fact, in many respects, many of these big entities are healthcare companies that also happen, on the side, to make warships. The governor has a major role to play in helping to bring down their healthcare costs, both through competitive means as well as through regulatory means. Making those investments will help those industries stay competitive relative to their counterparts in other states. Well, this is a perfect setup because my second priority area is maritime innovation, and when I think about that, I think about the defense industrial base. You know, there's an experiment that a friend of mine ran—he's not in economic development but he wants to be—and he called 10 companies on the West Coast. He got two of them to visit Brunswick Landing, and both of them are signing leases to come here. One of them is in aerospace and one of them is in maritime, and that told me this is a place where we can win. So, back to the same model in forest products: have a very specialized, dedicated team of hunters go out and get that. Make sure that they know where all of our suppliers are out there in the world. Make sure that we are working closely with the federal delegation to connect them with programs, because as they build up defense demand and get really good at making it, they then go into consumer markets. We could have an ROI for a long time if we build in this area. And finally, you know, there was a shipyard that was going to be built on the West Coast that we missed out on. There's a next generation of sensing in the ocean, drones, and small vessels. It doesn't need to be built by the government, but we need to be at the table recruiting that next shipyard to come here and be built in the state of Maine. I'm just continually amazed as I travel this state about all of the innovation that Mainers and business people are doing on their own. This is an area which I've witnessed: up at Loring Air Force Base, they're bringing new businesses in there which fit this profile. Howl Labs over in Bridgton is doing some work for the US Navy indirectly through the supply chain. Steve Bassett is here with Custom Composites in Bath, and you're getting a piece of that wealth that's coming through subcontracting and getting building components. So, we have a great opportunity in this area. We've got a lot of talented people, and a lot of businesses with the engineering background already working in the defense industry in various ways. We also have capital people, like North Atlantic Capital I met with, who have a dedicated fund for this. So, what's the governor's role? Know about it, learn about it, share information, coordinate, and make sure that in Maine the left hand knows what the right hand is doing with respect to this really great opportunity that we have. Well, in order to have Maine succeed, we have to re-elect Susan Collins, we have to elect Paul LePage into our United States Congress District 2, and David Jones as governor. I mean, let's face it. You've got to have leadership, and without leadership and without investment into this state, then the industries you're talking about will not succeed. We have some really great opportunities. We have a lot of coastline here—including the most eastern point in the United States—and we've got some great deep ports further north that are underutilized. You know, you heard Trump talk about Greenland, and there was a little report that I put out on how that could benefit the great state of Maine. No, you've got to be creative. The bottom line is you've got to have leadership that cares, and you have leadership that makes good decisions and can recognize bad decisions when they're on their desk. All right, well manufacturing support is critical not just for that industry but for all of them. The state can obviously support them from a workforce standpoint in a lot of the ways we already discussed. You certainly have to help manufacturers be more efficient so you can help them lower costs in areas that are primary to them, which are electricity, insurance, housing, and regulatory issues that add to the costs. Margin management is incredibly important, as that allows manufacturers to reinvest more. And if you're not on the cutting edge of reinvestment, you're not going to get as much of that business as you need. That will make sure our manufacturers are competitive, not just on price but also on top quality, which is essential. Then you get that great need for people to have things built here. And finally, that is going to compound on itself and pull more manufacturers into the state. Then it creates a greater ecosystem where more manufacturing business comes to the state and supports everybody. Maine was built by hand, and a lot of that building was shipbuilding. It is part of our real origin story here in this state and, fortunately, we've kept it going for a long time. We have a great skillset and a great reputation around the world for what we do. The US, though, is way behind on shipbuilding, which tells me there is a lot of reason for optimism, hope, and possibility if we help focus and solve some of the problems we've already talked about: education, workforce development, training, healthcare, childcare, energy—all of those challenges we need to resolve. But as Owen described, there's Anduril, a company that could have come here, and unfortunately we lost out because I don't think they thought we were competitive. I've talked to people who were there; they still don't think we're competitive. But I think there's real opportunity to bring companies like that. And again, as Owen described, a friend of ours has managed to bring a couple of companies to Brunswick who are industrial defense companies because we have resources that they really need—namely, a workforce and access to an ocean that is a heck of a lot cheaper than California, which is where they are. So, I'm really optimistic about what's possible, but I really think it's only going to happen if we've got a governor who is able to be an ambassador, do the outreach, and help make sure that there's a single door for people to walk through—and that we start working on some of these challenges like permitting reform. Someone told me the other day we have about $7 billion worth of projects waiting for permits in Maine. If you could just solve that one piece, you'd have an enormous economic pickup here in our state. So, if you can solve some of those challenges so that people want to come to Maine—and I think the governor can help do that, both by reducing red tape and creating a single point of entry for people—I think it'll make a real difference for Maine's economy. It's the kind of work I've done, and I'm excited to do it here. The Anduril story and our impotence around marine construction are almost—they're not quite at the Brookfield being put out by the state level of irony and sickness in my heart—but it's right up there. Let me just take a minute, so I don't bore you to death, to try a different angle. We get it, right? We are in the bottom five in business climate; we're in the bottom five in growth. We're in the top five in costs—the core input costs. And in those top five costs, we have state-created monopolies that create not just waste, but impotence, inflexibility, and a lack of innovation. In picking a governor, what signal might you want to send to the investor community, the banking community, and the larger industrial community about this state? What might you want to say with the person you choose? You want somebody who has returned money to investors, who has made investments, who has employed people, trained them, and housed them. I believe we need to make a signal, fire a shot heard around the economy, not just with the policy we choose to adopt, but with the governor we choose to adopt, and I think it's urgent. How do you increase the defense industrial base? There are two methods and there are two sectors. The two methods are to recruit companies in here with incentives, the same way most states who compete with us do. And the second method is to coordinate with Susan Collins—to get the entire delegation to coordinate with Susan Collins, who happens to be one of the most powerful people in Washington as the chairperson of Appropriations. What are the two sectors? The two sectors are aviation and maritime. I ran the largest air force that the United States has outside the Pentagon. I know all the airframes, fixed-wing and rotary-wing. What you have to do is look at the incredible advantages, comparative advantages, presented by Loring and the former P-3 base at Brunswick, maximize them, and bring aviation, avionics, and SLEP programs—all of them—in here. And the second thing you have to do is look at maritime. Bath Iron Works needs to get as many possible contracts as they can, and that has to do with us working together. Yeah, so I mean, I think one of the biggest things that we can do, which I think we've talked about for almost two hours now, is making sure that we have a business climate that can support what we already have. We need to support Bath Iron Works and support Portsmouth Naval Shipyard. I've seen a lot of increased traffic recently indicating that New Hampshire wants to take that back from us, so we've got to protect ourselves from them, too. But, you know, we need to make sure that we have a great business climate. That means reducing regulations, it means reducing taxes, and it means incentives for people to come here, all of the things people said. But, I'm just going to be perfectly honest with you: these are government defense contracts. Re-electing U.S. Senator Susan Collins is the best possible option we have to keep those things. She's the chair of the Appropriations Committee, which has already been said. These are government contracts we have to compete for. That's the bottom line, and we have to have somebody who as governor can go down and make the argument as to why Maine is the best place to send these contracts, and I will do that. So that's what I think the best options are for us to get the contracts that you were talking about. So, when I got here, I visited all the exhibit tables and I said, "What's the APEX accelerator?" This is a really great program; they said it's like speed dating for small businesses in Maine with large defense contractors. That is really cool, and that is cutting edge. And I think the answer probably lies with the Maine Manufacturing Association and with the Forest Products Council when you're asking a lot of these questions. You all have the answers, and it's up to the next governor to have an open door and to actually listen. I'm one of the few Democrats in the state that's won a Trump district multiple times. In 2016, I won a Senate district that voted for Donald Trump and voted for me, and I got re-elected twice. Now, I was the former head of the ACLU and a former leader on marriage equality, and I had a high concentration of BIW workers in my district. I learned you can be pro-worker and pro-business, and if you listen, there's a lot of common ground. There's more that unites us than divides us. Now, I'm backing Sara Gideon in this U.S. Senate race. I think Maine industry is going to flourish under her leadership. We may agree to disagree on that, but I think when it comes to moving Maine's economy forward, everybody in the room can agree on that. Okay, so that concludes that portion of the agenda. Now we're going to move into a lightning round. I'll ask a few brief answers, brief responses. Wait, I do have one more question. Oh, yeah, we do have one more, sorry. I missed one. Yeah, that's okay. So, this one is around R&D and innovation. Maine manufacturers rely on innovation to stay competitive. Yet, Maine does not fully conform to the federal R&D tax credit, creating complexity and potentially limiting investment. At the same time, funding for the Maine Technology Institute, the state's primary organization supporting innovation and commercialization, is often constrained and unpredictable. If elected governor, how would you strengthen Maine's approach to R&D and innovation, including tax policy and support for commercialization, to help companies develop and bring new products and processes to market? David, why don't we go to you, and we'll go that way, to your right. So, how would I support R&D in Maine? And you said we're not conforming to federal regulations. Right. Well, how about if we make our goal to conform, right, and get back on track? You know, again, I'm not a big proponent of government, and I like to keep government the hell out of the picture as much as I can. But, you know, there are rules and regulations we all have to follow. And if anything could be done to benefit growth here in the state of Maine and encourage people to invest in the state of Maine, you know, I'm all for it. We've had so many companies that thought about coming here, and they left; they turned their backs. Energy costs are through the roof, and there are no incentives for them to create jobs. You know, that data center was almost out, and if Janet wasn't running for U.S. Senate, she would have let it pass, and it would not have gone through. But she was running at the time, and she vetoed it, thank God. So, we've got to encourage people to come to the state of Maine. We've got to encourage them to invest in our state, and we've got to do everything we can to make it grow. This is a state that's so full of opportunity. It really is. It's an opportunity for everybody. You come here, you invest, and if we get the government the hell out of the way, we can grow. We can keep our children here, we can grow our businesses here, and we can get rid of this ridiculous tax burden we have. I'm a big believer in R&D expenditures in a limited and focused way for the state. In fact, you know, it was about 25 years ago that I chaired the Joint Select Committee on R&D, recognizing that we were 51st out of 50 states plus the District of Columbia in the amount of private and public sector R&D, and we changed that. We created the Maine Technology Institute when I was co-chairing that committee. We made some limited investments through bonding that have sparked some of the things we've talked about—the Advanced Composites Center at UMaine, biosciences—and it has been tremendously successful, but we're slipping again. And MTI, as was mentioned, has been flat-funded for 20 years at $6 million. They've scrounged, they've invested wisely, and they've got some of that money back in circulation. But there's a lot of work that we can do and should do from the government and from the state to stimulate these investments because they make an actual return of $20 for every $1 invested. It's a very good deal for the people of Maine. So, when I look at this, I look at this through the lens of where we have a competitive advantage, as I talked about: maritime innovation, advanced forest products, life science, and AI horizontal. I've talked about in my plan the Katahdin Horizon Partnership, which is a recapitalization in MTI that focuses on those areas. Second, MEIF follows suit; we're going to increase funding in MEIF in those areas. We're going to restore the seed tax credit up to 50% instead of 40%. We're also going to create a tax credit for venture capital funds into the state to bring more capital into this state. We're also going to create a Builders in Residence program. One of the missing links in a lot of the R&D projects that go across the state is the actual entrepreneurs. This program will be a one-year mandate to recruit high-quality people. You bring them in, you give them free rein of all the IP that has anything to do with state money, and they're charged to go build a company—and if they don't build a company, it's not a success. We will pair them with high-quality mentors, the captains of our industries, and if we do that, I think you're going to see some really cool companies built by those people on the other side of us. I like this question a lot. And, I'm going to focus in on one area that is of primary importance to me, and that is the biotechnology sector. Not just 200 miles away from here, there is one of the most thriving biotech sectors on the planet, to say nothing of those on the East Coast. What do they have that Maine doesn't have? What are they doing that Maine has not been doing? As I've dug into this issue, this conundrum, what has emerged is that Maine has the entrepreneurial spirit and we have the scientists that are doing the work. But what we don't have is the ability for those scientists to take their ideas for new diagnostics, for new therapeutics, or for new devices, and make those into thriving companies. So, one of the things that the state can do is help invest not just in the R&D per se, but in the technology transfer—the ability to take an idea and turn it into a company. We can also do more to back that up. Where all these ideas come from fundamentally is through our universities. But sadly, funding for the UMaine system, to say nothing of the others, has fallen over the past 30 years. We need to get that back on track so we can use that as an engine for economic growth. R&D is the core of that. I was a research assistant at the Mount Desert Island Biological Laboratory. I'm a published author in acid-base transfer in the longhorn sculpin. What I saw with MDIBL and Jackson Lab in my lifetime is they've grown from small research labs to international powerhouses. They have spun off companies to monetize some of the research that's happening there, and Bigelow Laboratory is doing the same thing. So, I think there's tremendous potential. But in digging into the state budget, the Secretary of State's budget is $80 million. Would you think the Department of Economic and Community Development is more or less? Well, I'll tell you, it's less. That's insane. In order for Maine to prosper, if we are going to be a state that grows beyond the next four years, but into the next decade, 50 years, 250 years, we need to invest in research and development. We need to reinvest in economic development; it is crucial to the future of our state. I think one of the things that was mentioned in the question itself was tax conformity, and I think that's very, very important. I've said it a couple of times already, but I would fully agree with conforming to the federal government on research and development standards. The other thing I would do as governor—and this is, I think, incredibly important for any governor to remember—is that we have access to the bully pulpit. You know, it's not always about a new regulation or a new program, or repealing this or repealing that. Sometimes it's about bringing people into the room, asking them what they need, and then coming up with a solution for them or getting them to do something that's just good for the state. There's a lot of places in government where I've been able to do that, whether it be in housing or in education when I was in the legislature, and even outside of the legislature. So, I want to make sure that when we're looking at these kinds of investments, we are bringing people to the table with the assets that they have. I'd also like to make sure that we have access to private capital, and I think that lies with giving people some sort of incentive to do that. Access to capital is the biggest thing with research and development, and I want to make sure that people have access to that. So, innovation requires R&D, and R&D requires thinking long-term, not short-term. One of the things a governor can do is look sector-by-sector at how you would create the equivalent of incubators and leverage private sector investment by bringing federal funds in. There are billions of dollars of grants in federal funds, and we should look at trying to create in this state a culture of innovation and incubation. There's no reason in the world we can't do it. Companies can be literally rewarded for it. We used to have big groups like Bell Labs that did this; we don't anymore. But sector-by-sector, we can incentivize innovation with our tax code, and that will make it additionally interesting for people to come to the state of Maine. Okay, we've got some bipartisanship going here. Everybody in both parties wants to add some new programs because they believe that the state of Maine government is going to actually create enough R&D to justify such an investment while we are in a crisis—while we are 50th out of 50 states, while our taxes are the third highest, and while 76% of our fourth graders can't read. This moment right now is why I'm running for governor. No, you can't have R&D or Maine-specific VC funds. VC is fungible, money is fungible, and it flows across state lines. We need to have low energy, low healthcare costs, and high-quality education as the core tools that allow someone to be trained by an employer, so that we can live as a state. And everything else is true hubris. It's either naivety or it's cynically trying to buy your vote with a promise. Nonsense. Shrink Augusta, shred the bureaucracy, cut the taxes, and get on the road, smelling like jet fuel, inviting people back to Maine. So, yes, and I have met with both of the individuals that are working at the J in Sanford. As long as you have it off the grid with biomass, it is straightforward, it should be done, and the moratorium was the stupidest thing I've ever heard of. Okay, and we'll start at that end and come back. This one's a two-part question, so feel free to address both when you have the microphone: Would you ensure that the Shawmut Dam remains in place until a technical solution for structural removal is identified that all parties are comfortable with? And, as a follow-up question, do you view a fishway similar to the one in Howland on the Penobscot River as a potential solution for fish passage? Yes, I will preserve all four dams and Sappi, and it will be my first executive order. And fishways are fine, but people are more important, so people come first. Yes, I love the idea of the Nature Conservancy just building the freaking fishways. They can save some money, and we can still have electricity in the plants. Yes, there needs to be a solution in place. I'd want more information on how a fishway would actually work. Well, absolutely you have to make sure that dam stays in place and Sappi is taken care of fully. The fishways—there's plenty of technology on that, they work all over the place, so it should be fine. Taking out those dams is the dumbest idea I've heard in 40 years living here in the state of Maine, so it takes the prize for that. And fishways—well, if we don't take out the dam, we really don't need them, right? Yeah, now he's confused me completely. Yes, and I haven't personally traveled through that fishway, but I'm going to take your word, Christa, that it's a good one. Thanks. Yes, and more info on the fishways. Yes, and probably not on the fishways. So, I'll trust the Nature Conservancy, Sappi, and the stakeholders to come together to figure out what's right for the river and what's right for Sappi to keep Sappi going. My husband's from Skowhegan, so I know how important that mill is, and I think that the stakeholders can come to the right solution. I think fish passage is important for our fishing industry as well, and I think we can do both. I would say yes, and I'd need more information, but I do think that fish ladder was the one that Governor LePage said in 2022 he'd offer to go and manually move the fish up the ladder. So, if he's open to that, I think that could be a cost-savings measure. Okay, that way we don't have to hire Paul LePage to do that. We'll start with you, Garrett. I just have a couple more questions. Demand for pulpwood has dropped significantly in Maine since 2014. The total market loss from mill closures is 4.6 million green tons annually, or 415 truckloads every single day of the year. Will you support efforts to promote innovative uses for low-grade wood to help restore lost markets and expand existing ones? Yes. Yes, and I'm going to put funding for a mass timber facility in my first budget. Yes, without a doubt. Yes. Yes, and I support the businesses that are already doing that, using the whole tree, like Tanbark and others across the state. TimberHP is another great example. Yes. Yes. Unless this is a trick question, yes. Yes, but companies like timberlands and others that already are doing CLT want to come in. We don't need to put money into it; they'll invest. We just have to change our rules so they can come in. Yes, and incentivize things like cross-laminate timber. All right, really easy: should Maine conform to federal tax policy on tips and overtime?
