Do we see other countries pulling ahead using alternative energies and other countries benefiting from the cheaper Chinese goods and foreign goods that we decided we don't want to use so much anymore and say wait a second maybe we're making a mistake here or do we get by just fine without it? Well Steve I think history will look back on three Obama era deals that Trump tore up. One is the nuclear deal we're seeing the effects of that now. A second is the Trans-Pacific Partnership which would have created a broad trade alliance based on the most up-to-date standards that we would have then been able to use as leverage against China, also would have stimulated reformers in China. And the third is the Paris climate agreement which would have driven the clean tech industry. I mean to me it is the height of insanity that we're actually closing down wind projects in this country that are giving us these clean electrons at a time when we need all the electricity all of the above that we can get. But Steve I'll just give you one figure. It may not be exactly up to date but pretty much. So Ford Motor 40 percent of their business is outside the United States. Outside the United States the future is in electric vehicles. It was before this war, it will be massively as we go forward. And China is cycles ahead of us on that. In fact if we want to have that industry here we're going to have to have tech transfer from China in the battery realm the way we gave tech transfer to them in the 70s and 80s. So that's it's so clearly the future this war is reinforcing it. I'm going to cut you off Tom because we both lived through when the Japanese really overtook the American industry and it feels like not what's happening in America but what's happening in the rest of the world is is that same process again where we fall behind and that seems like a pretty big deal. Well you know it's clear you know clean tech broadly speaking is going to be one of the next great global industries. And please raise your hand if you think America can be a great economic power if it doesn't lead in the next great global industry. And again China's doing all of the above, that's what we should be doing. It's not it's not an either-or thing. So you know Steve, Infosys, the Indian you know outsourcing tech company, back in the early 2000s they had an ad campaign, it was called You Win In The Turns. You win in the turns. In other words, you win in the big technological terms, that's when you overtake your rivals. I think we're in such a turn right now. On the AI part of it we're clearly in the lead thank goodness but I think in some of these other realms we're going to get overtaken and we're going to look back at this moment as the moment where we we made that huge mistake. Hey Tom thanks for joining us today. Always Becky, thanks so much to you. Okay take care. That is Squawk Pod for today and for the week. Thanks for listening and thank goodness it is Friday. Squawk Box is hosted by Joe Kernen, Becky Quick and Andrew Ross Sorkin. They will be reunited next week. Tune into Squawk Box weekday mornings on CNBC at 6 Eastern or get the best of our show in an easy-to-listen-to podcast when you follow Squawk Pod. We'll meet you right back here on Monday. Have a great weekend. And we are clear, thanks guys.

What CoreWeave's Q1 earnings mean for Nvidia

Yahoo Finance(1.5M)·22:54·2.5K views·2026-05-08
Seana SmithAnchor, Yahoo Finance Madison MillsAnchor, Yahoo Finance
TL;DRThe AI boom is driving a massive surge in infrastructure demand—highlighted by CoreWeave’s $100 billion backlog—which validates continued growth for Nvidia, even as corporations aggressively leverage AI tools to justify significant layoffs and "fatten" profit margins.
The...

TL;DR

The AI boom is driving a massive surge in infrastructure demand—highlighted by CoreWeave’s $100 billion backlog—which validates continued growth for Nvidia, even as corporations aggressively leverage AI tools to justify significant layoffs and "fatten" profit margins.

The AI Paradox: Labor Market and "Agentic" Layoffs

The April jobs report showed an increase of 115,000, beating forecasts, yet significant disruption remains beneath the surface as tech companies restructure.

  • Strategic RIFs: Companies like Cloudflare (20% staff reduction) and Block (10,000 layoffs) are aggressively cutting headcount to drive fatter corporate profits and higher earnings upside.
  • AI as a Catalyst for Cuts: Ines Ferre noted the Challenger Gray report shows AI as a primary reason for layoffs for the second consecutive month.
  • The Rise of AI Agents: Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong is advocating for "agentic AI"—AI agents that work around the clock—allowing managers to replace human-led tasks with automated workflows.
  • Delayed Economic Impact: RBC Capital Markets suggests the impact of these layoffs may be delayed in official data as departing employees exhaust severance packages before re-entering the labor market.

AI Infrastructure and the "Nvidia Validation"

CoreWeave’s recent performance serves as a massive proof point for the sustained demand for AI hardware and high-performance computing.

  • CoreWeave’s Massive Backlog: The company added $40 billion in new backlog in a single quarter, bringing its total backlog to $100 billion.
  • Bull Case for Nvidia: Eric Jackson (EMJ Capital) argues this backlog is a "huge validation" for Nvidia, suggesting that skepticism regarding Nvidia's ability to maintain its growth trajectory is likely misplaced.
  • CPU Resurgence: In a shift from previous expectations, the market is seeing a "rise from the dead" for CPUs (Intel/AMD). As AI infrastructure evolves, the ratio of GPUs to CPUs may move closer to one-to-one, benefiting names like Intel and Marvell.
  • Re-rating Potential: Analysts expect a "beat and raise" from Nvidia on May 20th, potentially leading to a market re-rating as investors realize the AI boom is still in its "first inning."

Lyft’s Financial Performance and AV Transformation

Lyft CEO David Risher reported a quarter of "performing and transforming," balancing record cash flow with a shift toward autonomous vehicle (AV) logistics.

  • Record Financials: Lyft achieved nearly $5 billion in bookings and an all-time record of $1.1 billion in free cash flow.
  • The "Hybrid Network" Future: Risher dismissed the idea that AVs will fully replace human drivers. Instead, Lyft envisions a "hybrid network" where AVs provide a baseline of service and human drivers fill gaps during peak demand (e.g., after NFL games).
  • Waymo Partnership & Infrastructure: Lyft is investing $10M–$15M in an 80,000-square-foot depot in Nashville to manage Waymo’s fleet, including charging and specialized maintenance.
  • Consumer Sentiment: Despite high gas prices, Risher described a "rewards-maxing" consumer behavior, where riders are leveraging partnerships (United Airlines, DoorDash) to maintain travel habits.

Sector Winners and Emerging Trends

  • Retail and Healthcare: These sectors remain the primary drivers of job growth, contrasting with the losses seen in information and financial sectors.
  • Youth Unemployment: Brooke DiPalma highlighted a concerning tick upward in unemployment for ages 16–19, reaching 12.7% compared to 10% a year ago.
  • Premium Services: Lyft Black has seen 50% growth, as high-end consumers "trade up" for luxury services despite broader economic noise.
"Execs will say this is because of new AI tools requiring fewer people... But the fact is, companies want their C-suite, top workers, and shareholders paid very well... and one way to do that is fire people no longer deemed useful and then deliver massive earnings upside from cost cuts." — Brian Sozzi, Yahoo Finance Executive Editor
"CoreWeave basically grew their backlog to 100 billion sequentially. That is huge... it says that we've probably been too skeptical about Nvidia over these last few years." — Eric Jackson, EMJ Capital Founder and President
"Transforming means really embracing the autonomous vehicle future... we’ve got now an 80,000 square foot depot that we’re building out to help with the fleet management... Financially it’s great, it means we get paid for every ride." — David Risher, Lyft CEO
It's jobs day in America. Non-farm payrolls for April clocked in at an increase of 115,000, beating economist forecasts for a gain of 55,000. Here's a quick hot take on this report from this guy. I know you were all focused on big tech layoff announcements, but keep this in mind: there's an AI build-out boom in this country. And sure, not all jobs reports will reflect this for a variety of different reasons, like weather. But this report offers up a nice snapshot into how the AI boom is having positive effects on the labor market beyond just creating jobs on a data center construction site. And before I bring in my OB roundtable, I'm not done firing off views. Here's one I need to get off my chest. Wall Street loves fatter corporate profits. And because of that, we are getting more epic layoff announcements like the surprise 20% RIF revealed at security play Cloudflare last night. Execs will say this is because of new AI tools requiring fewer people, and that may be partially true. But the fact is, companies want their C-suite, top workers, and shareholders paid very well. No one wants to say it; I'm saying it right now. And one way to do that is fire people no longer deemed useful and then deliver massive earnings upside from the cost cuts over the next 12 months. In the process of driving this massive upside, the C-suite can go out and shop the business to private equity with a more compelling story or look to consolidate with another leaner rival. If I am wrong on this—and I don't think I am—but if I am wrong, you spin me a different tale over on X @BrianSozzi. I definitely want to hear it. Here in my Opening Bid roundtable: Eric Jackson, EMJ Capital founder and president, alongside Yahoo Finance senior reporters Brooke DiPalma and Ines Ferre. Good to see you all. Eric, coming to you. Big jobs day in this country. What did this jobs report tell you? Well, I think you're right with your opening comments. I mean, we can be fearful about AI disruption to come, but you know, that that could take a while. And in the meantime, we need people to fill key jobs to keep the momentum going. And so I think that's what the report showed. I think I think it's green lights for the markets. You know, great to see the the jobs holding in. And yet there is still disruption underneath the surface. So, you there are opportunities to find these companies that are you know, going to be doing layoffs and getting fatter profits, or the ones that are going to be too slow to adopt AI and who are setting themselves up for failure in the quarters to come. So that's what—that's where the stock pickers are going to stand out in this market. Brooke, you were looking over this report too. What stood out to you? What actually stood out to me was under the hood. If you take a look at that unemployment number, it stood out to me as staying consistent at 4.3%. But I'd like to dive into the report and look at young people across America and see what they are experiencing right now. And I did notice that ages 16 to 19, those ages actually saw unemployment tick a bit higher compared to the prior month and definitely year over year. This time last year, ages 16 to 19 saw an unemployment rate of about 10%. Now we're seeing that unemployment tick even higher to 12.7%. Just something to watch, especially heading into the summer months as maybe these kids are picking up extra work. But definitely telling, especially after we hear these stronger parts from say Starbucks, even Chipotle slightly beating expectations. But of course, big picture here, healthcare yet again a big winner in this economy. Ines, how about you? Look, I took a look at the sectors that were increasing jobs, which I thought was interesting. That was retail trade, transportation and warehousing. And then of course, the the sectors where you're losing jobs, which is information, which is financials. I mean, and that is—those are the headlines that we have been seeing. We have been seeing tech companies that have been month after month announcing layoffs. And you just had the Challenger Gray report as well saying that for the second month in a row, AI was the reason for layoffs. Ines, this has been a big week for corporate layoff announcements. Coinbase gave us a taste of what we didn't know—realize what was coming to end the week. Now we have Cloudflare cutting 20% of its humans. 100%. I mean, I took a look at Cloudflare's comments around this and it was about AI agents doing the tasks for employees. And that's really the theme that we have been seeing. And even if you listen to Brian Armstrong's earnings call as well from Coinbase, he was also talking about agentic AI and about telling managers that they should be using AI agents. And that is really the theme that we're going to be seeing going forward, that these agents are working around the clock, that they are doing tasks for employees, and so therefore they don't need as many employees. And one other note on Coinbase is that Armstrong saying that crypto has found its next catalyst and that's AI. And he's saying that there's soon going to be billions of agents transacting and they're going to need rails to keep up. Brooke, what I—I don't think folks really understand is—you know, and I got a taste of this or a reminder of it—last night I talked to Block's CFO and just trying to understand like how deeply they are going in and redoing their workforce. Not just their—it's not like GE Jack Welch days where he would go press a button and just fire everybody. These companies, these tech companies are literally redesigning, rewriting darn job descriptions. And in some cases, these jobs no longer exist because how far technology has advanced, really over the past three months. Yeah. I mean, it's been pretty incredible to watch this advancement that we're seeing within layoffs within the tech sector. And you mentioned Block, they had a reduction of about 10,000 folks on their team earlier this month. And as Ines was saying, Cloudflare saying in particularly that the roles that they let go just aren't the roles that are needed for the future. And as you can see on your screen, Challenger, Gray & Christmas putting out this report reflecting the numbers that we saw in the month of April. There was about 33,000 job cuts in April within the tech sector alone. But then you take a look at specifically how many of those announced that they were specifically tied to AI, and that was about 26% of total cuts. And so it's been incredible to see the way these companies have adopted AI and now ultimately how AI is impacting their workforce. But it's not just Cloudflare, it's not just Block, there are so many others who have also announced layoffs including Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, Salesforce, Oracle, as you can see on your screen as well. And it's just be interesting to see the way this plays out. I think it is worth noting, especially because we did have this jobs report today and we're looking within that report trying to identify the areas where this is reflected. RBC's Capital Markets Mike Reid this morning telling Yahoo Finance that ultimately though he said when these companies offer severance, this won't be reflected until months later. And these people who now got let go, who have been collecting severance, who then maybe want a job later on this year, then will be re-added to the workforce. And so this could be a delayed reaction, definitely something to watch, Brian. Eric, look, nobody likes to see people walking out of a building, even if they are well-paid tech executives holding a box with their family portraits in it and their various plants. Nobody likes to see it. But I will say this: we're suddenly now looking at a potential earnings boom from a lot of these tech companies in the back half of this year. I'm not—I'll maybe I'll discount or push aside the Mag 7 for a second, I'm looking at Cloudflare. I mean, here's a company not necessarily investing aggressively in AI infrastructure like a Meta, now laying off 20% of its workforce. I mean, like how—how won't their back-half EPS outlook look absolutely amazing? Well, I think it will. But it's interesting the reaction. I mean, you just had Cloudflare stock up there, they're down today because there's some weakness in their you know, underlying business preceding this job cut announcement. Same thing with Coinbase. Coinbase did the big RIF, but results were kind of meh, you know, with crypto trading, you know, not being so hot in the last quarter. There's a difference between these companies that might be like reacting to these AI job cut announcements defensively, versus like last quarter when Block, you know, proactively sort of announced this huge job cut out of—out of nowhere when their business was doing fine and then the stock was up like 20%. So—and last night Kaz Nejatian, the CEO of OpenDoor, really spoke about how he was thinking about AI and implementation and job cuts. He was saying like—the basically the dumb companies right now are just going to think about adopting AI to—to do the same workflows that this company has already done. And what he was trying to do at OpenDoor and what he said the smart companies were doing were totally trying to rethink their internal processes. Like, how do we use AI, which is a tool we've never had before, to sell a house in a way that, you know, we just haven't done in the past, and rethinking that process end-to-end. I think those are the companies that are going to be rewarded going forward. Eric, I —I'm going to assume you at least looked or saw some of—some of those results out of Coreweave. They're just really two ends of the spectrum. You have really people on the street that love Coreweave. Bernstein out this morning looking for reiterating their call for the stock to crash to $67. I really only care about this: what does this Coreweave quarter mean for Nvidia when it reports on May 20th? It's a huge validation of demand for AI infrastructure. I've been a skeptic of Coreweave before, but last night's results—I mean, here's the deal: 40 billion in new backlog in a quarter. And and so it—they basically grew their backlog to 100 billion, you know, sequentially. And so that—that is huge. They also announced the first investment-grade debt facility basically backed by AI infrastructure. That hasn't been done before. So, yeah, you take all those three things together, Brian, and it says that we've probably been too skeptical about Nvidia over these last few years. Everybody keeps saying like, oh, can they keep this up? Can they keep this up? And the bulls were saying, no, this is different—this time is different, AI is real, we're just in the first inning and all this. I—I think the latter argument is going to hold true. Eric, you think Nvidia beats big? I was looking at Goldman Sachs, they were out this morning on Nvidia. They hiked up their estimates and—and they do think they're going to get a—a beat and raise out of Nvidia. I don't know about beat big. I—I think the—what's been holding Nvidia back—I mean, they've always beat. They—they always sort of guide low and beat. What's been holding them back is just the skepticism and the—the market multiple that they that they received. And basically that skepticism is the market saying, we don't think you can keep this up. Yeah, you did great. Oh, another, you know, huge multi-billion dollar beat, but can you keep it up? And so I think now with what we've seen with Intel in the last week, with some of these other names sort of joining the party, I think you could be set for a re-rate in Nvidia in the weeks to come. Ines, it's days like this on Coreweave where I'm—I'm glad I'm no longer an analyst. I mean, this is a complicated company doing some really complicated stuff. It's a complicated company. You do have Wall Street analysts that are saying that just—as was just mentioned—that the revenue backlog increased 49% sequentially. This bodes well for a strong AI demand. The stock is down, but if you take a look at this stock after their earnings re-after their earnings report, every time they have a decrease but then rapidly the stock goes back up. So, even though you are seeing the stock under pressure now, that doesn't mean that it's going to stay like this because it it has had a big run this year, up more than 60% year to date. Eric, let me get back to you real quick too. You mentioned, you know, Nvidia how it's—it's lagging. Like, what do you do with an Intel? I mean, Intel's up brought almost 200% year to date. You got AMD ripped on its results too. Like, do you play the cheaper play in Nvidia or do you ride the momentum in those other two? Well, I think the biggest shocker out of Intel this last week is just the rise from the dead of the CPU. You know, we sort of thought of this as like yesterday's PC like left behind in this new rush to—to the AI gold gold mines in Nevada or whatever. And I think what we've learned now is that we're going to need the CPUs too. And maybe it's not going to be an eight-to-one GPU to CPU imbalance like we've thought it was. What if it's going to one-to-one? So, if that's the case, maybe we're just getting started with Intel. I think more interestingly are maybe some other names that have been kind of lesser followed in this sort of CPU space that where—where there's a catch-up trade to be had. I mean, Arm disappointed last night and their stock's down today, but, you know, Arm could fit the bill in—in that respect in the months to come. Some of these smaller names like Marvell, for example, are ones that I'm watching particularly. Good to see you all. Big thanks to Eric, Brooke, and Ines on this very busy Friday morning. Straight ahead, Lyft CEO David Risher is here the morning after his earnings release. More on this one next. I often say a lot that a quarter for a company is mixed. By that I mean, while there were some positives that could fuel a bull case on the stock, there were a few negatives that could be like raw meat to the bears. This is where I land on Lyft today. I liked how the company is poised to work closer with Waymo in Nashville. I liked hearing about strong new partnerships in United Airlines and I liked how prior deep restructurings have left the company lean and mean and not out here pulling a Cloudflare on earnings day and canning 20% of the workforce out of the blue because of random AI initiatives. But there were a few negatives, such as ride growth slowing and falling short of consensus. Active riders also fell shy of estimates. I want to get into all this. David Risher is Lyft CEO and he joins me now. David, good to see you again, my man. Let's start on that—that ride growth. Like, what happened there? Yeah, so it's actually a really strong quarter in a whole bunch of ways. And then, as you mentioned, rides. So, look, almost $5 billion in bookings, that's enormous. Adjusted EBITDA up 37% year-on-year, that's enormous. $1.2 billion, $1.1 billion in free cash flow, all-time record. Okay, so then the question you asked is rides. So, rides actually were super strong. We had about 237 million rides, which is a big freaking number and shows how basic this is to so many people's lives. Little bit of a—a slow start, a lot of big storms in the Northeast obviously, and our bike business, which also counts to rides, sort of went to zero. But you know what, we had our highest St. Patrick's Day ever, highest Valentine's Day ever. March was our highest week ever, accelerating in the Q2. So, I think it was more of a little thing at the beginning of the quarter rather than anything more—more substantive. Has the—has this quarter started okay? I mean, we're looking across the country, David, and we got four, five, six dollar plus gas in—in some states. I mean, this is a wild backdrop. It is, it is. And you know, and our drivers do feel it. We were the first out there to give a kind of a driver gas rebate program that really helps support them. You know what, it has been a strong beginning to the second quarter. And again, I think rideshare has moved from sort of a, you know, kind of a luxury thing off to the side to a really basic part of people's lives. Our commute business, for example, is the highest it's ever been. So, short answer is, I think we're doing okay. But, you know, you're right, the macro world's a little noisy. We saw a somewhat similar initiative over at, what was it, DoorDash. $50 million hit in their first quarter suggesting another $50 million hit this quarter because they're helping their drivers out. Like, how—how long do these incentives do you—do you think they will stay in place and how much do they add up for Lyft? So, we're kind of lucky there. So, they'll stay in place as long as drivers need them, for sure. But here's what we've done: we've partnered with other organizations, other companies, and given up to about a buck a—a gallon off at the pump. But frankly, it's a co-funded initiative. It hasn't been super material for us. And I actually think it speaks to sort of a bigger thing that's going on in the economy right now. If you sort of look at—at consumer behavior, of course people are stressed, but they're also working hard to—to rewards-max, right? You mentioned we've got a United partnership that has just been refreshed. We've got a DoorDash partnership that just extended to Canada. And all of those partnerships allow people to get points or miles or discounts by sort of shopping smarter. And I think that's part of what's—what's going on, what's kind of helping the consumer right now. I was just getting ready to book a plane for the summer, David. I might actually take one or two days off for the first time in a couple of years. I know, go me. But the prices for these tickets are pretty darn high because of higher jet fuel prices. Like, what—what will the summer travel season be for on a Lyft? You know, these drivers with their higher gas prices, does that get passed on to the consumer? So we try not to, right? And and in fact, if you look at our driver hours, which is the way that we measure driver engagement on our platform, it's actually at our highest ever. Certainly our highest in Q1 and I think our highest ever, certainly since I started. So, you know, it's interesting, the world is complex, right? On the one hand, as you say, you know, jet fuel is really expensive, that means, you know, people are going to pay more for an airline ticket. But again, then people are also figuring out ways to do you know, to sort of use their points and so forth. So, at least again from what we see, things feel pretty strong. But, you know, wait and see for sure. Rewards-maxing. I just wrote that down. I have to use that somewhere. Maybe my X account or on Instagram. Like, I don't—I don't know where yet. Let's talk—let's talk about what you are doing on—on Waymo, in Nashville. Because that seems like a very big initiative for you. It is, it is. The way I think about our company right now is we're performing and transforming, right? And you got to do both, right? So performing means quarter after quarter performance, picking people up faster, you know, financially hitting all of our metrics and so forth. Transforming means really embracing the autonomous vehicle future. And it's a real thing. When people take AVs, they tend to like them. We're bringing them onto our platform, first at a significant scale first in Nashville, as you just said. And our big initiative, aside from being able to order a Waymo on the Lyft app, is going to be that we've got now an 80,000 square foot depot that we're building out to help with the fleet management in Waymo. Financially it's great, it makes—it means we get paid for every ride, but it also means that it really shows how important the logistics and operations are going to be in this kind of AV future. I'm such a geek about that stuff. In that depot, 80,000 square feet—I mean, that's massive. Like, what—if I were to walk in there, what will you be doing in there and how big of an investment is that? You know, it's kind of $10 to $15 million worth of money in the ground. That's not a lot in the big—you know, as I mentioned, we just generated over a billion dollars in cash. But what it does do is it allows us to maintain, it allows us to charge. You know, there'll be, I don't know, tens or hundreds of charging stations in there. And funny thing happens with AVs. For example, I'll give you a tiny geeky thing: tires actually need to be changed quite regularly, right? And funnily enough, AVs pick up tires in a different way from consumer-driven because they tend to drive a little bit differently than consumers. They tend to use different parts of the road, they pick up a little bit more—more tack. It's a kind of weird thing at the edge. But we've been doing fleet management for many years with FlexDrive. Billions of miles driven on FlexDrive vehicles which are kind of owned and operated through Lyft. We have a lot of expertise here, and it's going to be even more important in the—in the AV future. In our time, do these AVs replace me using my phone and there's a human behind the car, or in the car? No, here's what's going to happen. We call it the hybrid network. And it's just—it's just—it's economics and it's also human preference. The economics are you can't have enough AVs on the ground in any one city to—to manage demand at in the afternoon or after an NFL game, and also not have there just be an economic drag on your—on your balance sheet. So, you're going to have a baseline of AVs and then drivers are going to fill in the gaps you know, at—at kind of peak demand time. And then meanwhile, a lot of riders are going to say, you know what, I want a little help with my—with my groceries or a help with my luggage or whatever it might be. So, I think it's actually one of those great things where the whole is great in the sum of the parts. I don't so much look at substitution, I look at market expansion. Since we last talked, David, and it's been a bit, your competitor Uber went out and spent some money to buy Blacklane. I mean, a black car service. I've used this business before, it works, it's a really nice service. I hope Uber doesn't mess this thing up because I'm going to be really pissed off with them. How are you focused on that next tier, that higher end person? And then secondarily, I mean, how are you focused on the folks showing up at airports? Yeah, so glad you brought it up. So copycat strategy, hate to say it, but it's true. So we bought a company called TBR a year ago. It's a global chauffeur network, very, very high lane, it's used—high end, it's used for roadshows, it's used for very high-end corporate events and and so forth. We're seeing a lot of the same sort of dynamic that you're kind of mentioning, which is people are actually trading up to Black. It's a really interesting thing. It's actually our highest rated service, Lyft Black is, on our—on our platform. And it's grown something like 50%. By the way, over a third of the cars that just joined are now I think they're Suburbans, and two-thirds are newer than three years old. So even within the Lyft app, setting aside this sort of other, you know, services that—that those guys are buying and that we've bought, Black is really exploding. And again, I think it sort of shows people are using points, people are using all sorts of ways to sort of treat themselves a little bit even as they are also trying to make their—their money go further. But that's sort of the big picture going on there. And of course, David, you're an avid Lyft user. I know you also, I mean, you ride your bike but you also drive Lyfts from time to time. Any big summer plans for you? Well, I do have a lot of big summer plans. I got to tell you a super quick Lyft driving story. Two weekends ago, I picked up a couple, they had just gotten engaged. I dropped them off at—at an event, and then I knew I was going to pick them up later because they had a scheduled ride. So, I went home, actually got a bottle of Tomorrow Cellars non-alcoholic sparkling wine—is a whole side hustle I'm doing. Anyway, brought it back to them for their next thing, brought it to their—their restaurant, and it was —we both had the time of our lives. So there's no substitute, if you're a leader, get out and—and hang out with your customers. You'll learn a lot— That's real class, David. You got to try to fix this situation with the World Cup. I mean, they're not even letting rideshares in there. It's going to be wild. We're going to be right there as long as we can. All right. That's all I can ask for. David, good to see you. Have a good weekend. Wonderful. Back at you. All right. The big interviews continue over on my Power Players podcast. This coming Monday morning, I'll drop a new episode with former X CEO Linda Yaccarino. Linda left Elon Musk's X about a year ago and is now CEO of eMed Health Population. The tech platform is focused on getting GLP-1s into the hands of more people. You can watch the episode on Yahoo Finance, YouTube, Roku, or listen in on Apple Music, Amazon, and Spotify. And if you missed this week's episodes with Chipotle CEO Scott Boatwright, which went viral, and former Secretary of Defense Mark Esper, be sure to catch them this weekend. They were really good listens. I'm biased because I created it. Julie Hyman has you on market catalysts next.

BONUS: Nebius’s Arkady Volozh | On building in the agentic era | Spotlight On | AccelVC

Accel(26.7K)·31:52·5.8K views·2026-05-08
Arkady VolozhFounder, Nebius Matt WeigandPartner, Accel
TL;DRNebius is positioning itself as a vertically integrated "AI-native hyperscaler," controlling the entire stack from land and power to agentic software to provide cost-effective, high-performance infrastructure for the next generation of AI developers.
The Five-Layer...

TL;DR

Nebius is positioning itself as a vertically integrated "AI-native hyperscaler," controlling the entire stack from land and power to agentic software to provide cost-effective, high-performance infrastructure for the next generation of AI developers.

Full-Stack Vertical Integration

  • The Five-Layer Strategy: Arkady Volozh defines Nebius’s competitive edge through its ownership of every layer of the AI value chain:
      • Layer 1: Land, Power, and Shell (LPS), including industrial permits and electrical equipment.
      • Layer 2: Proprietary hardware, specifically building their own GPU racks.
      • Layer 3: Bare metal services and Kubernetes layers for wholesale clients.
      • Layer 4: Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS) and platform services for retail cloud users.
      • Layer 5: High-level AI services, including inference (Token Factory) and agentic tools.
  • Efficiency Gains: By avoiding third-party margins for data center construction and rack building, Nebius claims superior economic and engineering efficiency, optimizing the system "down to the last bolt."

Strategic Financing via Hyperscale Partnerships

  • The Meta Commitment: Nebius recently secured a $27 billion commitment from Meta, which Volozh clarifies is structured as two distinct parts:
      • $12 Billion: Direct bare metal capacity provided to Meta.
      • $15 Billion: A "backstop" agreement that Nebius uses as collateral to secure bank financing for its own independent cloud capacity.
  • The Microsoft Precedent: A similar $17 billion+ deal with Microsoft serves as a financial engine, allowing Nebius to use long-term, credit-worthy contracts to build out "spot market" capacity for smaller AI startups.
  • NVIDIA Partnership: Beyond capital, the deepening partnership with NVIDIA involves deep engineering collaboration to optimize systems, positioning Nebius as a preferred partner for large-scale GPU deployments.

Shifting Focus: From Bare Metal to the Agentic Era

  • AI-Native Demand: Nebius is moving beyond selling "GPU hours" to selling "tokens" and agentic outcomes.
      • Inference Growth: Through its "Token Factory," Nebius serves frontier labs and open-weight model developers who are currently capacity-constrained.
      • Agentic Services: The recent acquisition of Taviely (agentic search) highlights a shift toward serving "agents" rather than human software engineers.
  • New User Profiles: Volozh notes that modern AI developers are often multi-cloud and "agentic," choosing platforms based on measurable efficiency and reliability rather than legacy ecosystem loyalty.

Scaling to Meet "Objective" Demand

  • Capacity Targets: Nebius is planning for a footprint measured in hundreds of thousands—and eventually millions—of GPUs, with a power pipeline reaching into the gigawatt range.
  • Capital Requirements: Fulfilling current programs for 3+ gigawatts of capacity will require over $100 billion in total investment.
  • Market Outlook: Volozh views the current market as a "blue ocean" rather than a "red ocean." He argues that demand is "totally objective" because AI allows industries to do things 10 times cheaper or 10 times faster, creating a structural shift in the global economy.
"Demand is so high, coming from so many directions. Whatever is built is consumed. And demand is totally objective. Everybody sees how much new value this thing generates. You can do either 10 times more things or 10 times cheaper, and it's a huge, huge industrial next step." — Arkady Volozh
"We use these big bare metal contracts not as our main line of business, but as one of the ways to finance building our own cloud for the rest of the market." — Arkady Volozh
"We are building the cloud for this new, today I would say agentic world... these new users are choosing the platform which is measurably better, more reliable, more efficient, cheaper." — Arkady Volozh
Demand is so high, coming from so many directions. Whatever is built is consumed. And demand is totally objective. Everybody sees how much new value this thing generates. You can do either 10 times more things or 10 times cheaper, and it's a huge, huge industrial next step. Welcome to Spotlight On, a podcast about how companies are built, from the people doing the building, one exhilarating decision at a time. Arkady, I am thrilled to be back together discussing Nebius with you. You and I actually set out to have this conversation one year after our first podcast, but with everything going on inside of Nebius, I think this was the first window of time we could find. Just in the last two months, you guys have announced large-scale customer commitments, rapid capacity expansion, a deepening partnership with NVIDIA, major financings, and continued build-out of your AI cloud platform. I promise you, we will hit all of those today. But I do think that there's a bigger story brewing here around what exactly it is that you're building. A story that illustrates why, when put together, all of these announcements are far more strategic than they may appear at first glance. To get at that story, I want to take us back to where we left off. It's mid-2024. Nebius has emerged on the scene as the newest entrant in the AI infrastructure race. What is it that the market maybe got wrong or didn't understand about what it was you're building? I think back then we had a clear vision. We started with a tiny 25-megawatt data center, and we had a clear vision that this is a huge build ahead of us. We knew we would need to build a lot, both in terms of scale and in terms of new functionality, new features, and new tools which need to be built for this new era. We saw our niche from the very beginning as helping people build AI. We had this experience. We were building AI in the previous 20 years, and we built a big infrastructure for that, both physical and tooling. And we realized that in this new world, there will be needed hundreds, if not thousands of times more than we have built before, and it will require a completely new set of tools. With our experience both in hardware and software, and building scale systems, we left behind hundreds of megawatts of built data centers. At the same time, we're a totally new startup on the market, and we understand very well the life of startups and the lives of enterprises trying to go into this new uncharted territory of AI. By understanding them deeply, we understand what tools they will need. When we started almost two years ago, there was this vision that we need to build something very, very big. We should own the full stack. We should build through the whole stack. We need to optimize it, which means that we need to own the data centers, we need to build our own racks, and we need to build all the software layers on top of it, from basic cloud to these all-new services. For today, we can say inference or agentic, whatever comes next. We felt that we are, by our qualification, one of the few teams in the world who can build it, both in scale and functionality. We understand what needs to be built. And I think when we started, people just didn't look at us this way. But again, there were different times. People probably didn't understand well what kind of things we're facing. Now it's two years later, and people understand that whatever we will build will be consumed. And we need to go to space for that, maybe even. And people also understand that it should be a new thing. It's not just an old classical cloud and tooling reshaped for this new era. It's a completely new set of tools for a completely new set of users. It's not just software engineers who are using it now. They need different tools. And that's what we are building: big scale, full stack, optimized, and cost-effective. Yeah, I think, you know, unpacking that a bit, I think people may underestimate just how much depth is behind the team inside of Nebius. I mean, what you just articulated requires domain expertise from land and power acquisition through to hardware development of racks, through to a large corpus of engineers going and scaling the software primitives on top of that infrastructure. Your team didn't start from zero. Could you tell us a bit about the team that you've built inside of Nebius and why it's unique for such a young company? Two years ago, as we started, we were quite an unusual startup because we came with a lot of experience. We had 20 or 30 years of experience building large-scale infrastructure for machine learning before it was called AI. We worked with NVIDIA many years before this company was created. Again, we started from a very small installation which we inherited, just 25 megawatts. Eighteen years later, we already were running 10 times of this, and this year we're building and growing again. Nebius will be this, hopefully, big, huge platform with hundreds of thousands of GPUs of capacity, and layers and layers of services tailored and built from scratch for this new AI era. We are building for developers serving the workloads which are demanded by them now, not what was demanded a decade ago. You mentioned the long-standing relationship with NVIDIA, so I want to transition us to some of these big announcements that we've seen from Nebius over the last couple weeks. Help us connect all of them, and why don't we start with NVIDIA. It's not just investment, or just money for us. It's actually much deeper work which is going on behind this investment with NVIDIA, where we together develop and optimize systems. We help them optimize their systems. It's a lot of engineering work between the teams in the background. Again, it's a sign of a deeper collaboration with NVIDIA, and we're extremely proud, of course, that they become our partner and shareholder. But again, it's not about money, just money. It's good to have $2 billion, but what we're building requires tens and hundreds of billions of dollars. Let's shift focus to another big announcement that you guys have made, which is a large commitment from Meta. The headline of that commitment is $27 billion, but really I think what's most interesting is how you architected that relationship. Can you talk a bit about what that $27 billion really means to Nebius and what it means to the overall platform that you're building for the cloud? When you see the news about this 27 or so billion dollars, you should actually understand that in this particular contract, there are actually two contracts inside. One is $12 billion when we really provide bare metal capacity to Meta for themselves to use. And for this, we are getting the second part of the contract, which is $15 billion of backstop. What it means for us is we will be building, and we will use actually Meta's readiness to offtake it, go to the bank, and finance this capacity for our own cloud. These are much higher levels of the cloud which we will be selling on-spot to thousands, or tens of thousands of smaller clients. We're all watching those charts showing GPU-per-hour rates go through the roof. And I guess what I'm hearing from you is that $15 billion contract is locked in at today's long-term rates should you not be able to sell it. But what you're saying is you're going to take that $15 billion of capacity and bring it to your core customer, that retail customer that leans on your software, services, and tooling on top of the infrastructure and pays what you call spot rates in the market—much higher rates. This effectively means that $15 billion could mean 50% or 100% more to Nebius over the life of that contract. Isn't this a similar thing to what we saw happen with Microsoft as well? Different, but a similar thing happened with our Microsoft contract last year. It was a 17-plus billion dollar contract where Microsoft actually got bare metal capacity from us and we're building bare metal capacity for Microsoft AI Lab. But it allows us to go to the banks and use this contract to finance construction of our own cloud. So we use these big bare metal contracts not as our main line of business, but as one of the ways to finance building our own cloud for the rest of the market. That's interesting. You have said that before, that these large deals with some of the big hyperscalers are a great way to finance what we do. I think getting off the blocks to be able to deliver capacity to small startups—startups that frankly don't have a contract that a bank cares to lend against—has been a big kind of chicken-and-egg problem for the ecosystem. I know you have a number of thoughts around how many GPUs are actually serving that end of the market, the core end of the market that Nebius is targeted. You mentioned how we got in there and how we're trying to find ways to finance our way through it. But talk a bit more about that core customer to us and how we serve them. So we see the main market for us, our main business model, in serving the AI-native startups and enterprises going online. And these are, again, thousands of different clients. They don't have behind them these huge piles of capital to build their own capacity or their own thousands of GPUs. We're building a cloud for them. This should be a big cloud. What we're building is actually even this year will be measured in hundreds of thousands of GPUs. So it's basically the same scale as the other big hyperscalers. It's shorter-term, smaller contracts but in large, much larger amounts of clients who actually need much more services from us, which is important. We don't just provide them with GPUs or bare metal. The big clients like Microsoft and Meta, they don't need our software stack because they have their own. These guys, they don't have their own stack, and they need a lot of help from us. And we're building tools for them. And this is our main business. This is what we're building, and we're building it at scale. To be there, you need to provide scalable, very efficient, very performant, and cost-effective systems. That's what we're building. Two thoughts on the capacity side of things. One, Nebius thinks about scaling capacity maybe slightly different from some of the other clouds or neo-clouds you might be compared against for this exact reason. Delivering a multi-tenant hosted cloud experience to the torso of the market requires a different type of capacity planning and scaling than you may see out of a large hyperscale deal on bare metal. So I'd be curious to hear a bit about capacity planning when you think about your core end customer, and how you think about whether we need a 5-gigawatt facility or we need a 30-megawatt facility, or both. Bring us inside the capacity planning of Nebius. To build what we want to build, you need to be very efficient, and you need to start building from scratch, from the land. You need to understand everything from land permits and electricity permits up to how to build data centers, how to build racks, and what software to run. If you outsource it, first of all, it will never be efficient. You pay margins to data center constructors, to rack builders, and to other software companies. We build it through the whole stack. It's a full-stack build, which gives you economic efficiency, but more importantly, it gives you technical efficiency and engineering efficiency. It is optimized to the last bolt. It's optimized on all the layers, and it works together as a system. This is what we build. Again, it should be built cheaply and optimally, performantly, and it should be built in scale. It should be big and very efficient. Looking at the capacity today for Nebius, we have both owned locations as well as co-located locations. What I'm hearing from you sounds like the direction of travel is to own more of our infrastructure from the land all the way through to the software. If you extrapolate this over the next few years, what is the footprint of Nebius between owned and co-located, and what is the purpose of co-located for us today? Well, when we started, we took everything. We took even small colocation sites although they are more expensive because we needed to start quickly. As we build, we build more and more of our own capacity. It's usually larger sites. It's hundreds of megawatts, sometimes gigawatt-plus sites, what we're building. But not only that. As I said, we will be working with any capacity we could find. So if it could be several-gigawatt-scale sites, but it also will be a range of smaller sites: 10 megawatts here, 50 megawatts there, 100 megawatts. It's a network of sites. We will be working over this network. Majority of this capacity will be, I think even this year, owned by us. But again, we will be ready to work with everything which moves. So let's pull the financing thread a bit because I know people love to talk about how you come up with tens of billions of dollars as a 30 to 40 billion dollar company. You know, two years in, you've got $47 billion of commitments from some of the most creditworthy end customers in the industry. You've found very strategic ways to fund the business to this point. I believe the CapEx plan for this year on the upper end is about $20 billion. Bring us inside the financing of Nebius. Tell us where you are today and what are some of the levers you can pull to continue to stay ahead of the pack the way you have. All capacity we're building this year is financed, fully financed. And we're now talking about—and again, if a year ago we were talking about billions of dollars—for this year we're talking about tens of billions of dollars. And we will, to fulfill our programs for three and more gigawatts, we're talking about over a hundred billion dollars already. The second question is how expensive is it. Because for a startup like us, with all our public NASDAQ status and size, we're still a young startup for the banks, working in a very risky new part of the economy. If you want to get your financing for, I don't know, 10 or 12%, you can probably get it. Again, it depends on the size. But it kills all of the economy, and actually, it's a huge difference. People maybe don't understand the difference between 6% annual interest and, I don't know, 9% annual interest or 10% annual interest. They think of it as only 4%. But on a five-year contract, it's like 20% of extra margins. It's a huge difference. So we're looking for affordable financing in pretty big volumes. Tens of billions, probably hundreds of billions of dollars. Very helpful. Very helpful. So if we just take a step back, these announcements, embedded in these announcements to have engineering partnerships to help with the software that sits on top of our infrastructure, you have financing available to us to be able to build for the vertically integrated platform we hope to build over time. You talk about the layers of infrastructure. You talk about everything from land through to software. Can you illustrate those layers for us? Because I think a lot of these announcements give us the capability to compete at every layer. And then I want to take us to the platform on top, but I first want to understand the layers. Because there's very few, maybe only the hyperscalers, that have actually stated a vision to own all of those layers the way you have. So walk us through what those layers are. It starts from LPS: Land, Power, Shell. It means land with an industrial permit, power reserved either from the grid or self-generated, and the shell—the data center itself. And it's not just shell, not the walls, it's all the electrical equipment in it. This first layer actually costs, let's say, 20% of total CapEx. And then you need to put the system inside: the racks. We build our own racks. Then there's a layer when you just, let's call it a bare metal service, when you put on, I don't know, a Kubernetes layer on top and you provide it, usually in big chunks for the long-term, to people who have their own vertical systems. It's big tech companies. Bare metal services, usually wholesale volumes, big volumes, to several customers. That's the business of a typical neo-cloud. We are not there. We are building layers on top. On top of this bare cluster software, there's a platform layer, or infrastructure as a layer—just basic services which you would find in any classical hyperscaler like AWS, Azure, GCP, or Oracle. On top of this basic IaaS level, there's a platform with higher-level services. And again, you can choose to sell bare metal at wholesale prices, pretty low prices, but big volumes, or you can go in kind of the retail on-spot market and start offering services which those companies need. They don't develop them themselves. Be it basic services like cloud services, and higher in the level it could be inference, like our Token Factory, or agentic services like agentic search with Tavily, which we recently bought. These services are completely different from what a classical cloud would provide 10 or 15 years ago. It's not just different services. What is really different is the end user of these services. What we're building, we're building it from scratch and we're not building it for the previous generation of customer. We're building it for this new generation of customers who actually have completely different requirements. They don't need a hundred of different tools to stitch together in their cloud. They don't think in these terms. They think agentic. They are usually multi-cloud, they don't care actually where compute is happening, where different services are, or from where different services are consumed, because they don't do it manually. It's the agents who do it. And the agents, they are optimizing efficiency. These new users are choosing the platform which is measurably better, more reliable, more efficient, and cheaper. Yeah, I notice you shy away from the term neo-cloud. And in many ways, you do it because of the five layers we talked about. When you actually own the land, the powered shell, the rack, the software on top of that, and you're not just delivering Kubernetes in a bare metal experience but delivering inference post-training and fine-tuning on top of that cloud, now you look like agentic services, the additional services. That's the AI-native hyperscaler that you're talking about. But I think what's underappreciated today is how you actually compete with hyperscalers. Because neo-clouds aren't your competitors; you're well beyond the scope of neo-clouds. Hyperscalers are your competitors. Hyperscalers have been building a platform for over 10 years, and they have many, many software tools and primitives that sit on top of their infrastructure already. What you're articulating with this agentic world and today's AI-native developer is potentially a window into how Nebius becomes a true competitor to today's hyperscalers. Maybe double-click on that experience for a developer today as far as why they would choose Nebius over one of these embedded, large, proven hyperscalers. Why work with Nebius? When we talk about competition here, it's not a red ocean. It's totally a blue ocean. It's a total new area. They need to move there as well. Also, when we talk about competition, it's not like we're gonna compete and kill any of them. They will continue to exist, they will be dominant. There is an opening now. We see it as an opening. There's something where they don't have any protective moat. These new requirements and these new users—it's all new to the market. And we're talking not about taking their business, we're talking about building new business together with them. The market shares will be changing. And it's, again, it's not a consumer market where the winner takes all. It's a B2B market: prices are important, efficiency is important, everything is important. And there is an opening now which I think has a market share available for players like us. I don't see too many players as of now who are building, A, at this scale, or B, at this depth or heights of the platform. It's interesting how many people in the market talk about delivering a cloud and what that has actually started to mean to the market. And I think your definition of a cloud is about as broad as anyone's in the market in terms of what you want to cover. We actually do compete, I would say, at every layer of that cake, and there are very large businesses being built at every layer of those five layers of infrastructure. And as you build higher and higher, you eat the margin of each layer. So although there are very large businesses built there, you aspire to take all of that margin all the way up to the top. Let's stop at the top for a moment because you mentioned something interesting around what has emerged as potentially one of the largest revenue opportunities in the world of AI, which is delivering inference. This is actually getting value out of all of these models we're training on these huge clusters. You have Token Factory. What is Token Factory and what is your right to win in the world of inference? There is a range of companies who provide inference services. People are training models for then these models to be used for something, to generate value in other industries. And using models is inference. Theoretically, if in the beginning most of capacity was spent on training new and new models, eventually there will be more and more usage of these models and more and more inference. There are frontier labs—we have now, what, five of them in the Western ecosystem—which provide inference services, be it Anthropic, OpenAI, or others. And there is a range of newcomers who provide inference on open-weight models. People like Together AI, Fireworks, Base Ten—there is a range of them. Frontier labs, of course, they're much bigger, and their limiting factor is capacity. They have said many times that their revenues would grow even faster if they had more capacity, and they're getting and buying capacity from everyone. These other players working with open models, their main challenge is again to get capacity. They don't build their own capacity; they're buying it on the market from hyperscalers and neo-clouds. Since the capacity becomes a more and more scarce resource today, there is not enough capacity. Everybody talks about it; we cannot build as much and as fast as needed. Their business is limited. They cannot grow as fast as they could have grown if they had this capacity. They don't have it. We build our own capacity for this, and we have our own capacity for inference. And it's not just having this capacity; also, we optimize the full stack down to the ground, to the racks, and provide more efficient inference. Again, this recent acquisition of Agent is to provide even more efficient, faster, cheaper, low-latency, and higher-throughput models. I think this is why we're growing in inference so fast and why it quickly becomes the next layer of our business. Again, it's not selling bare metal or cloud, or not selling GPU by hours. It's a different measurement: you sell tokens. And people don't care what you run it on. They're buying tokens, and tokens are different. Quality tokens at a cheap price—this is what they are interested in. This is, I would say, a business of 2025-26. The next coming business which is happening now is agentic. Agentic workloads require different services and a different environment. Our recent acquisition of Tavily is trying to provide services for this layer. And there will be more and more services coming which we build ourselves and we will be partnering with specialists in all those fields. This year we will build this next layer, the agentic layer, to our cloud. Yeah, I like talking about these services because I think it highlights a lot of what we've been talking about today. So first, as you mentioned, you control capacity. You also control engineering from hardware through to software. But the other point, which kind of encompasses your vertically integrated AI hyperscaler narrative, is margin. So if you're an independent inference provider in the market today, hypothetically, you could be procuring a GPU from a provider who doesn't own the data center or the racks. So they're leasing the data center and paying someone for the racks, and that data center operator may not own the land—they may be leasing the land. And so as you look at every layer of that cake, again, there's margin to be optimized, and where it shows up most visibly is when you can actually deliver the service on top, and then you control that whole chain. And that's in a lot of ways the summation of the uniqueness of that vertically integrated product that you guys are building. Vertical integration brings you margins, it brings you also efficiency and speed because you control the layers, and it brings you scale and size. You cannot buy a lot trying to buy it here and there; you need to have a pipeline of construction and know that the next gigawatts are coming. And if people take a step back from this conversation—the deals, the capacity, the platform—what do you think people are still missing? What do you want people to leave today understanding about Nebius and where we go from here? It's a play in two dimensions. It's a scale game and a depth or product game. In scale, we're building—again, there are just big tech companies and just a couple of neo-clouds who build at this scale. And we're talking, again, about hundreds of thousands of GPUs and potentially millions of GPUs available for public use. And then the second dimension is what you are building. And what we are building is, again, the cloud for this new, today I would say agentic, world. What it will be next year we will see, but definitely every year there's something new happening here which you need to build, which opens up a completely new, much wider range of clients. And a year ago, you and I were asking ourselves: who's going to buy this infrastructure? Who is going to buy these GPUs? Now we're demonstrating execution at quite a big scale, and we're asking ourselves: how do we bring this capacity online as fast as possible? What are we going to talk about a year from now? I think we will still be talking about capacity. I don't know what will be in 3 or 5 years. For now, demand is so high, coming from so many directions. Whatever is built is consumed. You can sell everything on bare metal or you can sell on the higher level of production somewhere. And I think in a year from now, it still will be constrained capacity—how much humanity can build to serve this new demand. And demand is totally objective. Everybody sees how much new value this thing generates. You can do either 10 times more things or 10 times cheaper, and it's a huge, huge industrial next step. Also, I think we will be talking about some new applications which emerged, which we don't know today, which will require new tools, and new audiences of users will be coming to the field. Arkady, it never ceases to amaze me when you and I get to sit down and you articulate how grand the vision is for where Nebius is going. But you and I have also talked about how hard it is to convey that to the market and to people that follow our story, and so I hope today played a small part in being able to help you to do that. So thank you, as always, for everything you and the Nebius team are doing, and we're excited to watch the progress from here. Thank you.

Iran-US War: Major Update | Hormuz War | Trump | Mojtaba Khamenei|N18G

News18 Urdu(3.8M)·23:06·2.2K views·2026-05-08
Donald TrumpPresident, United States Mojtaba KhameneiIranian Leader
TL;DRThe Middle East is on the brink of total war as Israel eliminates top Hezbollah and Hamas leadership, prompting Iran to mobilize hypersonic missiles and reject diplomatic deals with the U.S.
until blockades are lifted. Simultaneously, Russia’s "Victory Day" ceasefire has...

TL;DR

The Middle East is on the brink of total war as Israel eliminates top Hezbollah and Hamas leadership, prompting Iran to mobilize hypersonic missiles and reject diplomatic deals with the U.S. until blockades are lifted. Simultaneously, Russia’s "Victory Day" ceasefire has collapsed, with Ukraine deploying new long-range cruise missiles against Russian industrial targets.

Iran-US Tensions and Diplomatic Deadlock

  • Khamenei’s Hardline Stance: Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei held a high-level meeting with President Masoud Pezeshkian, establishing that no deal will be reached with Donald Trump unless the naval blockade of Hormuz is lifted and attacks on Lebanon cease.
  • Retaliatory Threats: The IRGC (Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps) has warned that any "forceful" tactics by the U.S. will result in the direct targeting and destruction of American warships.
  • Drone Warfare: Iran has reportedly shot down a U.S. fighter drone in the Strait of Hormuz, signaling a shift in focus toward disrupting global maritime trade routes.
  • European Intervention: France and Britain have launched a joint mission, deploying the aircraft carrier *Charles de Gaulle* toward the Strait of Hormuz to protect oil interests and pressure Iran.

Israel’s Multi-Front Offensive

  • High-Value Targets Neutralized: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu confirmed the elimination of Ahmed Ghalib Balut, Chief of Hezbollah's elite Radwan Force, along with senior commanders Mohammad Ali Bazi and Husain Hassan Romani.
  • Hamas Leadership Hit: In Gaza, Israeli strikes killed Azzam Khalil al-Hayya, the son of senior Hamas leader Khalil al-Hayya.
  • Netanyahu’s Strategic Autonomy: Despite diplomatic pressure from the U.S., Netanyahu stated that Israel will act independently to ensure Iran never achieves nuclear capability, specifically targeting Iranian uranium stockpiles.

Advanced Missile Technology and Defense

  • Iran’s Hypersonic Arsenal: Iran has placed Fattah-1 and Kheibar Shekan missiles on launch pads.
      • Fattah-1: A hypersonic missile with a 1,400km range capable of changing trajectory mid-flight to bypass radar.
      • Long-Range Threats: Kadr (2,000km) and Emad (1,700km) missiles are positioned to strike major Israeli cities like Tel Aviv and Haifa.
  • U.S. Defense Systems: The U.S. has deployed THAAD (Terminal High Altitude Area Defense) systems in Israel.
      • Capabilities: Each battery can intercept targets at an altitude of 150km, though concerns remain regarding their ability to stop 100% of a coordinated "saturation attack" by hundreds of Iranian missiles.

Russia-Ukraine: Failed Ceasefire and New Weapons

  • Victory Day Escalation: A proposed ceasefire for Russia’s May 9 "Victory Day" failed immediately. President Zelenskyy accused Russia of launching over 30 air strikes during the supposed truce, killing 27 people.
  • Ukraine’s "Flamingo" Missile: Ukraine has debuted the FP-5 Flamingo cruise missile, which reportedly has a 3,000km range—double that of the U.S. Tomahawk—targeting Russian navigation and aviation industries.
  • Moscow on High Alert: Russia claims to have intercepted 347 Ukrainian drones in a single night. Security in Moscow has been elevated to "war levels," including potential internet shutdowns, ahead of the annual military parade.
  • Force Numbers: Experts estimate Russia has 500,000 troops currently deployed in Ukraine, with a recruitment rate of 30,000 new soldiers per month to offset heavy casualties.

Notable Quotes

"Our target is to completely destroy all uranium held by Iran. President Trump may have a different view, but I have given clear instructions to our security agencies: Iran must not become a nuclear power at any cost."

— Benjamin Netanyahu, Prime Minister of Israel

"Talking about stopping the war for one day while killing our people before and after is not serious. We saw attacks in Kharkiv and Dnipro just as the ceasefire was announced. If we give them a 24-hour break, they will just start killing us again the next morning."

— Volodymyr Zelenskyy, President of Ukraine

"Trump has completely failed. His plans for a coup in Iran, or the seizure of Hormuz and our uranium, will never succeed. If he tries to use force, no one will be able to save his warships this time."

— General Jawani, IRGC Commander

ईरान और अमेरिका की डील फाइनल होने से पहले इज़रायल ने भयानक हमले शुरू कर दिए हैं। गाज़ा से लेबनान तक इज़रायल ने बमबारी की है। लेबनान में हिज़बुल्ला की रिज़वान फोर्स चीफ के साथ कई कमांडर इज़रायल ने ढेर कर दिए हैं। आपको बता दें कि इज़रायल के हमले से भड़के ईरान ने अमेरिकी ड्रोन को भी मार गिराया है। ईरान के सुप्रीम लीडर मुजतबा खामेनेई ने राष्ट्रपति के साथ तीन घंटे की मीटिंग की है और मीटिंग के बाद ट्रंप के होश उड़ाने वाला फैसला भी लिया है। रिपोर्ट आपको दिखाते हैं। खाड़ी युद्ध का कंट्रोल अब सिर्फ नेतन्याहू के हाथ में है। इज़रायल फैसला कर रहा है कि कहां पर बम फटेगा, कहां तबाही मचेगी, कहां लाशें बिछेंगी। सारे फ्रंट पर बारूदी तांडव शुरू हो चुका है, जिसके साथ अमेरिका की युद्ध से भागने की कोशिश कामयाब होती दिखाई नहीं दे रही। ईरान ने होर्मुज में उसके फाइटर ड्रोन का शिकार शुरू कर दिया है। फ्रांस भी ईरान युद्ध में अपना बैटल कैरियर ग्रुप लेकर आ धमका है। इज़रायल ने तो ट्रंप के रोकने के बाद भी गाज़ा और लेबनान को दहला दिया है। इज़रायल की बारूदी प्लानिंग ने ईरान को भड़का दिया है। ईरान ने होर्मुज से इज़रायल पर फोकस शिफ्ट करने का इशारा कर दिया है। अमेरिका और इज़रायल से नए राउंड के युद्ध से पहले ही ईरान के सुप्रीम लीडर मुस्तबा खामेनेई भी अब सीन में आ गए हैं। ईरान के राष्ट्रपति मसूद पजेश्कियान ने सुप्रीम लीडर मुस्तबा खामेनेई से ढाई घंटे की लंबी मुलाकात की। मीटिंग में मुस्तबा ने साफ कर दिया कि होर्मुज में ट्रंप की नाकेबंदी हटने तक कोई समझौता नहीं होगा। सुप्रीम लीडर ने ईरान को समझौते की टेबल पर अपनी सभी शर्तें मजबूती से रखने के निर्देश दिए हैं। जूनियर खामेनेई ने यह बात दोहराई है कि समझौते में लेबनान पर हमले बंद करने की शर्त भी शामिल है। मसूद पजेश्कियान, राष्ट्रपति, ईरान: नज़दीक दो घंटे, दो-सवा दो घंटे भी हम ख़िदमत में गुफ़्तगू में रहे। अव्वल तो मुझे ख़िदमत में कहना पड़ेगा, उनका नज़रिया, उनके गुफ़्तगू का अंदाज़ वाक़ई बड़ा हक़ीक़ी था, सादगी भरा। एक रहबर से आप ऐसे अंदाज़ की ही उम्मीद करते हैं, उनका बर्ताव वाक़ई शानदार रहा। यानी ईरान उन्हीं शर्तों पर डील करेगा जो मुस्तबा ने तय की हैं। ईरान रिवोल्यूशनरी लड़ाकों ने धमकी दी है कि ट्रंप ने अगर ज़ोर-ज़बरदस्ती की कोशिश की, तो अबकी बार उसके जंगी जहाज़ों को कोई बचा नहीं पाएगा। आईआरजीसी कमांडर जनरल जवानी ने कहा है कि ट्रंप पूरी तरह से फेल हो चुके हैं। जवानी ने धमकी दी है कि ईरान में तख्तापलट, ईरान का यूरेनियम और होर्मुज पर कब्ज़े के मंसूबे कभी कामयाब नहीं हो सकते। अमेरिका की तरफ से फिलहाल समझौते के सिवाय कोई बात नहीं हो रही है। लेकिन ट्रंप फोर्स वॉर जोन में रेडी है। ईरान पर प्रेशर बनाने के लिए फ्रांस का एयरक्राफ्ट कैरियर भी वॉर जोन में एंट्री ले रहा है। फ्रांस ने अपने कैरियर चार्ल्स डी गॉल को होर्मुज की तरफ भेज दिया है। कैरियर चार्ल्स डी गॉल मिस्र की स्वेज नहर से तेज़ी से होर्मुज की तरफ बढ़ता जा रहा है। फ्रांस के राष्ट्रपति इमैनुएल मैक्रों ने कहा कि एयरक्राफ्ट कैरियर लाल सागर की तरफ बढ़ता जा रहा है। इसके बाद उसे होर्मुज के करीब तैनात किया जाएगा। यह फ्रांस और ब्रिटेन का जॉइंट मिशन है, जिसमें फ्रांस और ब्रिटेन के जहाज़ शामिल हैं। ईरान ने फ्रांस के तेल के जहाज़ को निशाना बनाया, जिसके बाद से फ्रांस बेहद गुस्से में है। यानी ईरान अब यूरोपीय महाशक्तियों के टारगेट पर आ चुका है। लेकिन फिलहाल ईरान को इज़रायल की कोई काट नहीं मिल रही क्योंकि नेतन्याहू ने अमेरिका से अलग ट्रैक पर चलकर दुश्मनों को बेरहमी से कुचलना शुरू कर दिया है। बेरुत, नबातियेह, गाज़ा... लोकेशन अलग-अलग है लेकिन बारूदी तस्वीरें एक जैसी सामने आने लगी हैं... धमाके की, तबाही की, आग की, धुएं की। क्योंकि इज़रायल भयानक वॉर मोड में आ गया है। नेतन्याहू ने ऐलान कर दिया है कि ईरान और लेबनान में जब तक इज़रायल के दुश्मन हैं, तब तक युद्ध चलेगा। बेंजामिन नेतन्याहू, प्रधानमंत्री, इज़रायल: ईरान के पास जो भी यूरेनियम है, उसे पूरी तरह से खत्म करना हमारा टारगेट है। इसके लिए राष्ट्रपति ट्रंप का अलग नज़रिया हो सकता है, लेकिन हमने अपनी सेना और सुरक्षा एजेंसियों को साफ़ निर्देश दे दिए हैं कि किसी भी कीमत पर ईरान को परमाणु ताकत नहीं बनने देना है। गाज़ा और लेबनान को दहलाकर इज़रायल ने ईरान को फिर से उलझा दिया है। मुस्तबा की उलझन यह है कि वह ट्रंप से समझौता करते हैं, तो भी युद्ध रुकने की कोई गारंटी नहीं। अगर ईरान ट्रंप से समझौता नहीं करता, तो अमेरिका उसे धरती से मिटाने वाला हमला शुरू कर सकता है। ईरान ट्रंप से डील पर सीरियस है, बातचीत चल रही है। लेकिन जिस तरह इज़रायल ने युद्ध भड़का दिया है, उससे ईरान समझ नहीं पा रहा कि वह ट्रंप पर भरोसा करे या नहीं क्योंकि इज़रायल ने ट्रंप की सभी अपील धुआं-धुआं कर दी है। इज़रायल डिफेंस फोर्स ने बेरुत पर भयानक हमला करके हिज़बुल्ला के रिज़वान स्पेशल फोर्स के कमांडर अहमद गालिब बलूत का सफाया कर दिया है। बलूत के खात्मे का ऐलान खुद बेंजामिन नेतन्याहू ने किया है। बेंजामिन नेतन्याहू, प्रधानमंत्री, इज़रायल: रिज़वान फोर्स का कमांडर मारा गया है। वह उत्तर युद्ध जीतने का प्लान बना रहा था। उसे लगा था कि बेरुत के आतंकी मुख्यालय से वह हम पर हमले करता रहेगा। उसे लगा था कि बेरुत में उसे सुरक्षा मिली है, लेकिन कोई आतंकी सुरक्षित नहीं है। मैं अपनी सेना के वीर जवानों और खुफिया एजेंसियों को उनकी कामयाबी पर सलाम करता हूं। इज़रायल ने जानकारी दी है कि नासिर यूनिट के खुफिया विभाग के सरगना मोहम्मद अली बज़ी और हिज़बुल्ला के हवाई निगरानी कमांडर हुसैन हसन रोमानी को भी मार गिराया गया है। गाज़ा पर भीषण हमला करके इज़रायल ने हमास चीफ खलील अल-हय्या के बेटे अज़्ज़ाम खलील अल-हय्या को मार डाला है। मतलब यह कि इज़रायल सभी फ्रंट एक्टिव कर चुका है। सीज़फायर के बाद पहली बार बेरुत पर हमला करके नेतन्याहू ने सिग्नल दिए हैं कि अगला नंबर तेहरान पर धमाके का है। ईरान ने भी पूरी तैयारी के साथ इज़रायल से हिसाब बराबर करने की धमकी दी है। ईरान ने इज़रायल के लिए सबसे खतरनाक मिसाइलों को लॉन्च पैड पर तान दिया है। इसमें सबसे खतरनाक फत्ताह-1 और खैबर शिकन मिसाइल शामिल हैं। इसमें कद्र और इमाद मिसाइल भी शामिल हैं। फत्ताह हाइपरसोनिक मिसाइल की रेंज 1400 किलोमीटर है, खैबर शिकन मिसाइल की रेंज साढे 1400 किलोमीटर है। कद्र मिसाइल की रेंज 2000 किलोमीटर तक है और इमाद की रेंज 1700 किलोमीटर है। यह सभी मिसाइल इज़रायल के खिलाफ ऑपरेशन में दागी जा सकती हैं। तेल अवीव से लेकर हाइफा और अश्दोद से लेकर बीरशेबा और शेफाल जैसे छोटे शहरों को इन्हीं मिसाइलों से ईरान पहले दहला चुका है। इसीलिए ईरान के कमांडर जो वार्निंग दे रहे हैं, उसे इज़रायल बिल्कुल भी हल्के में नहीं ले सकता। ईरान के मिसाइल गोदाम लगातार एक्टिव हैं, उनके लॉन्च पैड लगातार रिलोड किए जा रहे हैं। इसमें सबसे ज्यादा खतरनाक है ईरान की फत्ताह मिसाइल। फत्ताह की खासियत है कि यह तेज़ रफ़्तार के साथ अपनी दिशा बदलती है, यानी एक ट्रेजेक्टरी में हमला नहीं करती। यह पृथ्वी के वायुमंडल के अंदर और बाहर सभी दिशाओं से गुज़र सकती है। और जब टारगेट पर हमला करती है, तो इसकी रफ़्तार इतनी ज्यादा होती है कि दुश्मन का इसे देखना भी मुश्किल है। यह बात नेतन्याहू बहुत अच्छी तरह से जानते हैं। ऐसे में इज़रायल में अगला धमाका कहां होगा, कौन सी मिसाइल कहां गिरेगी, इसे लेकर इज़रायल के मिसाइल ट्रैकिंग रडार सिस्टम काफी प्रेशर में हैं। इन सबसे खास है अमेरिका का थाड मिसाइल डिफेंस सिस्टम, जो पूरे इज़रायल को बचाने के लिए तैनात किया गया है। थाड 200 किलोमीटर की दूरी तक दुश्मन की किसी भी मिसाइल को मार सकता है। यह 150 किलोमीटर की ऊंचाई तक किसी भी मिसाइल को इंटरसेप्ट कर सकता है। अमेरिका के पास थाड की सात बैटरियां हैं। एक बैटरी में छह ट्रक माउंटेड लांचर होते हैं। दुश्मन की मिसाइल को रोकने के लिए थाड सिस्टम के एक लांचर से एक बार में आठ मिसाइल दागी जा सकती हैं। यानी पूरी बैटरी में कुल 48 इंटरसेप्टर मिसाइल होती है। इसे ऑपरेट करने के लिए 95 सैनिकों की ज़रूरत होती है। इज़रायल में ऐसे दो-दो सिस्टम तैनात हैं। लेकिन क्या यह काफी साबित हुए? बिल्कुल नहीं। हाइफा और तेल अवीव में फटी मिसाइलों ने बताया कि ईरान एक बार में सैकड़ों मिसाइल फायर करने की ताक़त रखता है और उनको रोकना न इज़रायल के बस की बात है, न ही अमेरिका इसमें 100% कामयाब रहा है। अगले 24 घंटे इज़रायल के लिए बहुत खतरनाक होने वाले हैं। अगर ट्रंप से ईरान की डील फाइनल नहीं हुई, तो ईरान लेबनान और गाज़ा का बदला इज़रायल की तबाही से ले सकता है क्योंकि रिज़वान फोर्स चीफ और हमास चीफ के बेटे की मौत ने अब सुप्रीम लीडर मुस्तबा को बहुत ज्यादा भड़का दिया है। रूस ने एक दिन के लिए 9 मई को सीज़फायर का एलान किया। लेकिन इस एलान के बाद रूस और यूक्रेन में भयानक युद्ध भड़क गया है। ज़ेलेंस्की ने दावा किया है कि एकतरफा सीज़फायर एलान के बावजूद रूस ने कीव समेत दूसरे इलाके में कई बड़े हमले किए हैं। अब रूस का यह भी दावा है कि उसने 300 से ज्यादा यूक्रेनी ड्रोन को इंटरसेप्ट किया है। रूस ने चेतावनी दी है कि 9 मई को विक्ट्री डे परेड के दिन अगर मॉस्को पर हमला हुआ तो कीव पर मिसाइलें गिरेंगी। पुतिन को कीव हर हाल में जीतना है। विक्ट्री डे से पहले कीव में मॉस्को का तिरंगा फहराना है। ईरान और अमेरिका के बीच युद्ध एक पल के लिए थम गया है, लेकिन रूस और यूक्रेन के बीच जंग डेढ़ हज़ार से ज्यादा दिनों से ऑन है। यह युद्ध अब सबसे खतरनाक मोड़ पर पहुंच गया है। रूस ने विक्ट्री डे के लिए सीज़फायर का एलान किया, लेकिन उसी बीच कीव में धमाके हुए, ड्रोन गिरे, मिसाइलें चलीं। रूस ने दावा किया कि उसने एक रात में 347 यूक्रेनी ड्रोन मार गिराए। यूक्रेन का दावा है कि उसने रूस के 90 से ज्यादा ड्रोन को इंटरसेप्ट किया। सवाल यह है कि रूस और यूक्रेन के बीच सीज़फायर का एलान क्या सिर्फ दिखावा था? क्या पुतिन 9 मई को विक्ट्री परेड से पहले यूक्रेन पर जीत को पक्का करने वाला हमला चाहते हैं? टूटा सीज़फायर, अब कीव पर फाइनल अटैक। पुतिन का ऑर्डर, कीव को कुचलो। वोलोदिमीर ज़ेलेंस्की, राष्ट्रपति, यूक्रेन: एक दिन के लिए युद्ध रोकने की बात करना लेकिन उससे पहले हमारे लोगों को मारना यह बिल्कुल ग़लत है। कल खारकीव के मरेफा में हमला हुआ, कल नीप्रो में। लोग मारे गए, कई घायल हुए, बच्चे और बड़े सब। इतना सब होने के बाद अगर कोई कहे कि परेड से एक दिन पहले मिसाइल और ड्रोन हमले रोक देते हैं और फिर 24 घंटे का युद्धविराम कर लेते हैं तो यह कोई गंभीर बात नहीं लगती। सीज़फायर या छलावा? क्या मॉस्को ने शुरू कर दिया है निर्णायक युद्ध? मॉस्को में सुरक्षा बढ़ा दी गई है, एयर डिफेंस एक्टिव है क्योंकि यूक्रेनी ड्रोन रूस के अंदर तक पहुंच चुके हैं। यूक्रेन रूस से युद्ध करने के लिए अब प्राइवेट मिलिट्री कंपनी बनाने की तैयारी कर रहा है। यह कंपनी सर्विस के बदले पैसे वाले मॉडल पर काम करेगी। ज़ेलेंस्की का इरादा ऐसे सैनिक तैयार करने का है जो पेशेवर, हाई-टेक और ताक़तवर हों। रूस के वैगनर ग्रुप की तर्ज़ पर वह ऐसे लड़ाकों की फौज तैयार करना चाहते हैं जो कॉन्ट्रैक्ट किलिंग मशीन की तरह काम करें। दूसरी तरफ रूस का दावा है कि उसने सैकड़ों ड्रोन मार
