गिराए हैं, यानी अब युद्ध सिर्फ बॉर्डर पर नहीं बल्कि कीव बनाम मॉस्को तक पहुंच चुका है। तो क्या विक्ट्री डे से पहले ही पुतिन दुनिया को एक बड़ी सैन्य जीत दिखाना चाहते हैं? या फिर रूस-यूक्रेन युद्ध पहले से और ज़्यादा ख़तरनाक होने वाला है? वोलोदिमीर ज़ेलेंस्की, राष्ट्रपति, यूक्रेन: मैं उन सभी देशों और नेताओं का धन्यवाद करता हूं जिन्होंने यूक्रेन के पूरे युद्धविराम के प्रस्ताव का समर्थन किया। रूस ने इसका जवाब सिर्फ नए हमले तेज़ करके दिया। पूरे दिन, लगभग हर घंटे यूक्रेन के अलग-अलग इलाकों से हमलों की ख़बरें आती रहीं। मॉस्को की सड़कों पर रूस के विक्ट्री डे परेड की तैयारी चल रही है। लेकिन आर्म्ड व्हीकल, मिसाइल लॉन्चर और आर्टिलरी सिस्टम वाले ट्रक क्या रूस की सीमा को पार करके कीव तक पहुंच जाएंगे? क्या विक्ट्री डे से पहले मॉस्को ब्रिगेड कीव पर सबसे बड़ा हमला करेगी? 9 मई रूस का विक्ट्री डे, जब मॉस्को जर्मनी पर जीत का जश्न मनाता है। रेड स्क्वायर पर टैंक निकलते हैं, मिसाइलें दिखाई जाती हैं। पूरी दुनिया को रूसी ताक़त का संदेश दिया जाता है। लेकिन इस बार विक्ट्री डे से पहले मॉस्को खुद डरा हुआ दिखाई दे रहा है। यह मॉस्को के डर के पीछे पुतिन का वार प्लान है। मॉस्को ने दावा किया कि यूक्रेन के ड्रोन रूस के अंदर तक पहुंच रहे हैं, जिसके बाद मॉस्को के आसपास एयर डिफेंस बढ़ा दिया गया है। रिपोर्ट में दावा किया गया है कि इंटरनेट तक बंद करने की तैयारी की गई है और परेड की सुरक्षा युद्ध स्तर तक पहुंचा दी गई है। सवाल यह है कि क्या पुतिन विक्ट्री डे से पहले एक बड़ी सैन्य जीत दिखाना चाहते हैं? हालाँकि रूस ने कहा है कि वे सीज़फायर चाहते हैं, लेकिन यूक्रेन ने आरोप लगाया कि सीज़फायर एलान के बावजूद रूस ने ड्रोन और मिसाइल हमले जारी रखे हैं। हमले की आवाज़ कीव, सूमी, नीप्रो और ज़ापोरिझिया हर तरफ सुनाई दे रही है। तो सवाल उठता है कि क्या यह सीज़फायर था या सिर्फ दुनिया को दिखाने के लिए एक राजनीतिक ड्रामा। रिपोर्ट में दावा किया गया कि 24 घंटे में रूस की सेना ने 30 से ज्यादा घातक हमलों को अंजाम दिया। रात से सुबह तक 20 से ज्यादा एयर स्ट्राइक की गई। रूस की सेना ने यूक्रेन पर मिसाइल हमले, ड्रोन अटैक किए। यूक्रेन ने दावा किया कि उसने 90 से ज्यादा ड्रोन को हवा में ही मार गिराया। जबकि इन हमलों के 24 घंटे पहले ही 9 मई को रूस ने विक्ट्री डे के लिए सीज़फायर का एलान किया था। रूस के हमलों से यूक्रेन के शहर फिर खंडहर की तरह दिखने लगे हैं। रिपोर्ट में दावा किया गया कि रूस के सिर्फ ड्रोन अटैक में 27 से ज्यादा लोग मारे गए, जबकि 100 से ज्यादा लोग घायल हुए हैं। यूक्रेन ने इसे इस साल का सबसे घातक हमला बताया। रूस के हमलों के बाद ज़ेलेंस्की ने सोशल मीडिया पर FP-5 फ्लेमिंगो क्रूज़ मिसाइल लॉन्च का वीडियो शेयर किया, जिसमें दावा किया गया कि 1500 किलोमीटर दूर रूस के शहर चेबोक्सारी को भी निशाना बनाया गया। ज़ेलेंस्की ने पलटवार करते हुए कहा कि यह रूस के हमले का जवाब है। यूक्रेन का दावा है कि उसने इस मिसाइल से रूस के उस ठिकाने को तबाह किया जहाँ से रूस की नेवी और उसके मिसाइल और एविएशन इंडस्ट्री को नेविगेशन कंपोनेंट सप्लाई होती है। हालाँकि इसमें मिसाइल लॉन्च की जगह का ज़िक्र नहीं किया गया। FP-5 फ्लेमिंगो यूक्रेन की खुद की बनाई मिसाइल है जिसे कीव की फायर पॉइंट कंपनी ने बनाया है। इसे अगस्त 2025 में सार्वजनिक रूप से पेश किया गया था। यह टॉमहॉक से दोगुनी रेंज वाली मिसाइल है। इसकी रेंज करीब 3000 किलोमीटर है। इसमें 1150 किलोग्राम वॉरहेड फिट हो सकता है। रफ़्तार 900 किलोमीटर प्रति घंटा है। इसका GPS गाइडेड सिस्टम इसे 14 मीटर के अंदर सटीक मार करने की क्षमता देता है। यूक्रेन की फ्लेमिंगो मिसाइल रूस के यूरोपीय हिस्से के लगभग पूरे इलाके को कवर करता है। रूस के पास फिलहाल मैक्सिमम 2500 किलोमीटर रेंज की ही मिसाइल है, लेकिन मॉस्को की मिसाइलों का वज़न फ्लेमिंगो की तुलना में ढाई गुना भारी है। मतलब फ्लेमिंगो से ज्यादा तबाही मचा सकती है। रूस के पास दुनिया में सबसे ज्यादा परमाणु बम हैं। वोलोदिमीर ज़ेलेंस्की, राष्ट्रपति, यूक्रेन: उन्हें किसी तरह की छुट्टी मनाने का मौका क्यों दें? हमारे लिए तो कोई छुट्टी नहीं है। हमें तो रूस के हमलों की वजह से हर समय दुख और परेशानी झेलनी पड़ रही है। अगर हम उन्हें एक दिन की राहत दे भी दें, तो 9 तारीख के बाद अगली सुबह वो फिर हमें मारना शुरू कर देंगे। इसलिए अब देखना होगा आगे क्या होता है। अब सबसे बड़ा सवाल यह है, क्या पुतिन ने कीव पर दबाव बढ़ाने का ऑर्डर दिया है? पिछले कुछ दिनों में रूस के हमले अचानक तेज़ हुए हैं। पूर्वी यूक्रेन में रूसी सेना लगातार आगे बढ़ने की कोशिश कर रही है। डोनेत्स्क और खारकीव इलाकों पर अब रूस का फोकस बढ़ता जा रहा है। एक्सपर्ट्स का मानना है कि विक्ट्री डे से पहले रूस किसी बड़े शहर पर कब्ज़ा कर लेता है तो पुतिन उसे राजनीतिक जीत की तरह पेश करेंगे। दावा है कि रूस के पास अभी भी 10 लाख से ज्यादा एक्टिव सैनिक हैं, जिसमें से करीब 5 लाख सैनिकों की पूरी तैनाती यूक्रेन में है। जंग में उसके करीब 12 लाख सैनिक या तो घायल हुए या मारे गए, लेकिन इसकी भरपाई के लिए वह हर महीने 30 हज़ार से ज्यादा सैनिकों की भर्ती कर रहा है। 2026 में सेना में आने वालों की कमी हुई है इसीलिए रूस पर आरोप लग रहा है कि वह जबरन सैनिकों की भर्ती बढ़ा रहा है। लेकिन इस जंग में यूक्रेन भी पूरी तरह टूट चुका है। उसके बिजली के ढांचे को बर्बाद कर दिया गया है, शहर के शहर खंडहर हो चुके हैं, लाखों की संख्या में लोग माइग्रेट हो गए हैं। बावजूद इसके ज़ेलेंस्की झुकने को तैयार नहीं हैं। ज़ेलेंस्की की सिर्फ एक ही डिमांड है, यूक्रेन का सिर्फ एक ही संदेश है... सीज़फायर नहीं, रक्षा चाहिए। सिर्फ 24 घंटे के युद्ध में यूक्रेन के शहरों का हाल कुछ इस तरह से है, हर तरफ हाहाकार मचा हुआ है, इमारतें तबाह हैं, गाड़ियाँ जल रही हैं। रेस्क्यू टीम हर मिनट किसी न किसी की जान बचाने में लगी है। इस पूरे युद्ध में अब सबसे खतरनाक हथियार बन चुके हैं ड्रोन। सस्ते, तेज़ और घातक ड्रोन। रूस ईरानी डिज़ाइन वाले ड्रोन इस्तेमाल कर रहा है, यूक्रेन वेस्टर्न टेक्नोलॉजी वाले ड्रोन से तबाही मचा रहा है। यानी यह सिर्फ रूस-यूक्रेन युद्ध नहीं बल्कि हथियारों की नई लैब बन चुका है जहाँ दुनिया की सबसे बड़ी ताकतें अपनी टेक्नोलॉजी टेस्ट कर रही हैं। सीज़फायर टूटा या तोड़ा गया, यह बहस चलती रहेगी, लेकिन इतना तय है रूस-यूक्रेन युद्ध अब सिर्फ ज़मीन की लड़ाई नहीं रहा, यह सम्मान और ईगो की लड़ाई बनता जा रहा है। उस संदेश की लड़ाई है जो मॉस्को दुनिया को दिखाना चाहता है। सवाल बस इतना है विक्ट्री डे पर दुनिया जीत का जश्न देखेगी या एक और भीषण युद्ध की शुरुआत।

"Unacceptable": Marco Rubio says Iran must never get nuclear weapons in briefing to media

Sky News(9.2M)·23:08·7.4K views·2026-05-08
Marco RubioSecretary of State, United States
TL;DR"The red line is clear.
Secretary of State Marco Rubio declared that Iran’s pursuit of nuclear weapons and attempts to control international waterways are "unacceptable," while affirming that the U.S. will maintain a policy of immediate military retaliation against any threats to American...

TL;DR: Secretary of State Marco Rubio declared that Iran’s pursuit of nuclear weapons and attempts to control international waterways are "unacceptable," while affirming that the U.S. will maintain a policy of immediate military retaliation against any threats to American personnel or assets.

Iran and Global Maritime Security

  • Nuclear Stance: Rubio emphasized that preventing Iran from obtaining nuclear weapons is a global imperative, noting that while many presidents have stated this, the current administration is taking "concrete" action to prevent it.
  • Negotiation Status: The U.S. is currently awaiting a response from Tehran regarding a potential negotiation process. Rubio described the Iranian system as "fractured and dysfunctional," which may be delaying the diplomatic timeline.
  • Operation Epic Fury: Rubio clarified that while the offensive phase of "Operation Epic Fury"—which targeted Iranian missile launchers, naval assets, and factories—is over, the U.S. continues to engage in defensive strikes.
      • Defensive Retaliation: Rubio dismissed calls for a ceasefire in the context of self-defense, stating that any drone or missile fired at U.S. destroyers will be met with immediate force.
  • International Waterways: The Secretary raised alarms over Iran's attempts to "normalize" control over international straits.
      • Precedent Risk: He warned that allowing Iran to control the Strait of Hormuz would set a dangerous precedent for other nations to seize international waterways.
      • UN Action: The U.S. is pushing a UN Security Council resolution to condemn the mining and seizing of the straits.

Diplomatic Relations with the Vatican

  • Cordial Meeting with the Pope: Despite past friction between the Vatican and the administration, Rubio described his meeting with Pope Leo as "productive" and "very cordial."
  • Strategic Information Exchange: The briefing focused on the Church’s unique insights into global regions where it has a deep presence, including:
      • Africa: Concerns regarding the spread of radical Islamic terrorism threatening growing Christian populations.
      • Lebanon: The plight of Christian communities and the role of the Church in facilitating peace.
      • Venezuela: Insights from Venezuelan bishops who had recently visited the Vatican.
  • Humanitarian Partnership: Rubio highlighted the Catholic Church (specifically Caritas) as a vital interlocutor and facilitator for U.S. humanitarian aid in regions where direct government-to-government cooperation is failing.

U.S. Military Posture and NATO

  • Troop Presence in Europe: No specific decisions have been made regarding the withdrawal of troops from Italy or a departure from NATO, as these remain presidential prerogatives.
  • Base Usage Concerns: Rubio expressed frustration with NATO allies (specifically citing Spain) who denied the U.S. use of bases for recent "contingencies."
      • Strategic Re-evaluation: He argued that if NATO bases cannot be used for power projection during emergencies, the U.S. must examine the cost and utility of those deployments.
  • German Deployment: He clarified that the recent withdrawal of troops from Germany (representing less than 14% of the presence there) was a pre-programmed shift that returned troop levels to 2022 status.

Regional Policy: Cuba, Lebanon, and Sudan

  • Cuba Sanctions and Aid: Rubio defended U.S. policy toward Cuba, clarifying that sanctions specifically target GAESA, a military-controlled holding company that "steals" from the Cuban people.
      • Blocked Aid: He revealed the U.S. offered $100 million in humanitarian aid to Cuba, but the regime has refused to allow its distribution.
  • Lebanon and Hezbollah: Rubio identified Hezbollah as a "100% Iranian agent" that victimizes the Lebanese people. He called on Italy to help equip the legitimate Lebanese government to confront this threat and cut off illicit financing.
  • Sudan Crisis: Rubio characterized the war in Sudan as one of the world's worst humanitarian disasters, acting as a proxy war for multiple nations. The U.S. is currently pushing for a "humanitarian ceasefire" and safe haven communities for aid distribution.
  • Taiwan Stability: Ahead of an upcoming trip to China, Rubio stated that U.S. policy remains unchanged: the U.S. opposes any forced or compelled change to the status quo and advocates for stability in the Taiwan Strait.

Notable Quotes

"The red line is clear. If they threaten Americans, they're going to get blown up... Only stupid countries don't shoot back when you're shot at. And we're not a stupid country." — Marco Rubio
"Iran now claims that they own, that they have a right to control an international waterway... if the answer is no, we don't think Iran should be able to control the Strait of Hormuz, then the next question's going to be for everyone: well, what are you going to do about it?" — Marco Rubio
"Our sanctions are against a company named GAESA... This is a holding company set up by generals in Cuba that has generated billions of dollars of revenue, none of which benefits the Cuban people." — Marco Rubio
"I think we all share the same goal, which is a strong Lebanese government that doesn't have an armed Hezbollah operating within its national territory... To do that we have to empower and equip the Lebanese government to be able to confront that threat." — Marco Rubio
Okay, let's just go to questions. You ready? Let's go. Go ahead. Hi, yeah. Thanks for doing this. I wanted to ask you first about your meeting yesterday with the Pope Leo. President Trump has had very harsh language and words for the Pope because the backdrop of the war in Iran. Did you make amends with the Pope yesterday? Well, we had a very good meeting. I mean, ultimately, it's important. First of all, there's a lot that we work together with the church on, and we talked about those areas that we're working together on in different parts of the world. I know everyone's interested in the other aspects of it, but I updated them on the situation with Iran. I expressed our point of view about why this was important and the danger that Iran poses to the world, which is largely recognized. So, obviously, the Holy Father is a spiritual leader. He's that first and foremost. I mean, that's his role to play. Obviously, the church has always interacted on behalf of a mission for peace and a respect for all humanity. But at the end, it was a very cordial and important meeting. It's important to share our points of view and an explanation and an understanding of where we're coming from. I thought it was very positive. Secretary Rubio, you also mentioned talking about the Western Hemisphere with the Pope. And of course, the Catholic Church is instrumental in delivering humanitarian aid to Cuba. I wondered if you discussed that and whether there was any disagreement on US policy towards Cuba. Well, we discussed it. I mean, we've provided $6 million of humanitarian aid—US humanitarian aid—that was distributed by Caritas, the Catholic Church agency. We're prepared to do more. In fact, we've offered the regime there $100 million of humanitarian aid that, unfortunately, so far they have not agreed to distribute to help the people of Cuba. So we did the hurricane relief, but we're offering more. And it's the regime that's not accepting it. It's the regime that's standing in the way of it. So we discussed that and we hope we can do it because we do want to help the people of Cuba who are being hurt by this incompetent regime that's destroyed the country and the economy. Secretary, can I ask about the meeting with the Pope? I mean, after this meeting with the Pope, which you described as cordial and productive, are you going to recommend to the President that he stops criticizing the Pope on social media? Why would I tell you what I'm going to recommend to the President? But beyond that, the President will always speak clearly about how he feels about the US and US policy. The President of the United States is always going to act in what's in the best interest of the United States. I think we can do that and continue to also have a very productive and fruitful and important relationship with the church because it plays an important role in the world as well. Mr. Secretary, let's talk about whether or not... No, no, I called her, sorry. Secretary Rubio, were there any themes from your conversation yesterday with Pope Leo that resonated? No. Here's the bottom line on that: the church is an important global institution. It has a presence all over the world. I know you guys are fixated on who said what about who at what time. I understand all that, okay, because you guys have headlines, you have editors, you have people that want you to post, and these are interesting stories. But at the end of the day, the Pope just returned from Africa, right? That is a very important continent with growing Christian populations, many of whom are threatened or feel threatened by the spread of radical Islamic terrorism. So we care about that. The Pope and the church have an interest in Christian communities in Lebanon, a place that we're very involved in trying to establish a peace between Israel and Lebanon. The church obviously has a very important presence in Latin America. The bishops from Venezuela had just been here a few days earlier. So we shared thoughts about those sorts of things. I think it's an opportunity to personally express our point of view from the standpoint of foreign policy in the areas we're involved in and share insights. We also want to gain insights because the church has a unique role in many of these countries in terms of the information that they're receiving. In some cases in the past, the church has been an important interlocutor, not just with governments, but with societies. And then of course on the practical level, the church plays an important role in humanitarian efforts in different parts of the world and in many places has served as a facilitator of US humanitarian assistance, such as I highlighted a moment ago when it comes to Cuba. Mr. Secretary, can you say, in your meeting with Prime Minister Meloni, whether you talked about the possibility of withdrawing US troops from Italy, as well as the possibility of the United States withdrawing from NATO altogether? No, we didn't discuss any specifics like that. That's a decision for the President to make. It's a decision every President makes. The fact of the matter though is, and I've said publicly and repeatedly, I've been a strong supporter of NATO throughout my career in the Senate and even now. One of the advantages of being in NATO is that it allows us to have forces deployed in Europe in bases that allow us a logistical ability to project power in case of contingencies. Well, we had a contingency and some countries in Europe, like Spain as an example, denied us the use of those bases for a very important contingency. In some ways, the denial of those bases actually impeded the mission. Not severely, but it had a cost and even created some unnecessary dangers. So if one of the main reasons why the US is in NATO is the ability to have forces deployed in Europe that we could project to other contingencies, and now that's no longer the case—at least when it comes to some NATO members—that's a problem and it has to be examined. But ultimately that's a decision for the President to make. His team and people like myself and others will provide him with what those potential options are, but ultimately he'll have to make that decision. He hasn't made those decisions yet. As far as recent news of deployments, like for example those in Germany, those were all already ongoing. The troops that were withdrawn represent less than 14 percent of our total troop presence there. That's already pre-programmed and in fact, all it did was take us back to where we were in 2022. So there was always a plan to do some shifting within NATO. As far as broader changes, I don't have an announcement for you on that today, but that's a decision for the President to make. Certainly, we'll present both options and perspectives, but I don't have anything new to announce on that today. Mr. Secretary, your former colleagues on the Hill, Republican senators, believe that troops' withdrawal would embolden Putin and will benefit Russia. Do you agree with that assessment? Well, in the end, the United States is always going to do what's in the national interest. We have global obligations all over the world. We have troop deployments all over the world and those are constantly shifting and changing. For example, we increased troop presence in NATO in the aftermath of the invasion of Ukraine. So, we're always making these decisions and there were some already previewed before any of this had happened in terms of the base denials. But in the end, look, here is the bottom line: while we may be the most powerful country in the world and we may have the most resources, our resources are not unlimited. We have to always assign resources around the world, including military resources, on the basis of what serves the national interest. That's a decision the President has to make, especially within the context of recent developments by some countries within NATO and the stance they've taken towards the United States. But no decision has been made on that yet. That's something the President is still working through. Have you managed to bridge differences on Iran, both with Rome and with the Vatican? Yeah, I mean, I think everybody agrees that Iran having a nuclear weapon is unacceptable. Everyone. I mean, I don't know of anyone who thinks it's a good idea for Iran to have a nuclear weapon except the Ayatollah. So, I think the question is what do you do about it. The President's actually trying to do something about it. And so, I don't think there's any disagreement that they can't have a nuclear weapon. I think the question is what you do about it. We've had every American President say, "Oh, they can't have a nuclear weapon." This is the first American President that's actually trying to do something concrete about it. I don't know who would disagree with that. It's an important thing that has to be confronted. Can we pass to, you know, officials... Can you give us an update on the status of negotiations with Iran right now? Well, we should know something today. I mean, we're expecting a response from them. We'll see what that response entails. The hope is that it's something that can put us into a serious process of negotiation. Obviously, we've seen the reporting overnight that Iran has established or is trying to establish some agency that's going to control traffic in the straits. That would be very problematic. That would actually be unacceptable. The normalizing of their control of international waterways is both illegal and just something that's unacceptable. The world has to start asking itself what it is willing to do if Iran tries to normalize control of an international waterway. I think that's unacceptable. So, we're expecting a response from them today at some point. We have not received that yet as of the last hour, but perhaps that will come. Their system is still highly fractured and a bit dysfunctional as well, so that may be serving as an impediment. I hope it's a serious offer. I really do. Can you confirm there's a resume of the talks between Israel and Lebanon next week? Do you expect that to happen? We expect talks. I don't know if we've said the exact date yet, but we expect that there'll be additional talks. Look, it's one of the areas that I think we can work with Italy on. They can play a very productive role and a constructive role in providing resources to the Lebanese government. In the end, we all share the same goal and by all I mean including the Lebanese government and the Israeli government. We want the relations between Israel and Lebanon, its legitimate government, to be very strong. The impediment to that is Hezbollah. Again, an Iranian agent, Hezbollah, is victimizing not just Israelis, but Hezbollah is also victimizing Lebanon. The reason why Lebanon faces bombings and the reason why Lebanon faces violence is because of Hezbollah. It is Hezbollah that's imposing this on them. So I think we all share the same goal, which is a strong Lebanese government that doesn't have an armed Hezbollah operating within its national territory imposing a threat to any of its neighbors. To do that, we have to empower and equip the Lebanese government to be able to confront that threat. I think Italy and other countries, but Italy in particular, can play an important role in helping equip the government and in cutting off the illicit financing that supports Hezbollah. Is it possible to get a peace deal on Lebanon without first solving the conflict with Iran? How linked are those two conflicts right now? Well, I mean, Hezbollah is an Iranian agent, 100 percent. Hezbollah only wouldn't exist without Iran's support. But I think it's possible. Hezbollah has been both weakened but still capable of inflicting damage and doing terrorist activities as we've seen. So, I think the way I would phrase it is, we're not going to negotiate with Iran over Hezbollah because Hezbollah is dangerous—unless they're willing to stop funding them and supporting them. Our negotiations are with the Lebanese; our role is with the Lebanese government. Lebanon should be governed by the Lebanese government. It should not have a terrorist group operating within its national territory that poses a threat both to its own people, including the Shia population, and to the government and to Israel and its other neighbors. So that's what we're going to focus on. Mr. Secretary, can we come back to Cuba a little bit? The United States, including yesterday, is ramping up sanctions against Cuba, the regime and all that. With the exchange with the Pope, did you feel any convergence of views on that, on US policy? Well, let me clarify something for you. Our sanctions are against a company named GAESA. Okay? This is a holding company set up by generals in Cuba that has generated billions of dollars of revenue, none of which benefits the Cuban people. Not one cent of it benefits the Cuban people. You understand this, right? I don't know if you know this, but there's the Cuban government and they have a budget, and then there's this private company that has more money than the government does. None of the money in that company goes to build a single road, a single bridge, or provide a single grain of rice to a single Cuban, other than the people that are part of GAESA. So that's what we're sanctioning. It is a company that basically is taking anything that makes money in Cuba and illegally putting it into the pockets of a few regime insiders. That's not sanctions on the Cuban people because the Cuban people don't benefit from GAESA. It's a sanction against this company that is stealing from the Cuban people to the benefit of a few. We didn't discuss those sanctions yesterday, but we imposed them yesterday and we're going to be doing more, by the way. Mr. Secretary, sorry. Many people thought that yours was a mission impossible after the Trump attacks, heavy attacks... Which mission? I'm sorry. The visit, Mission Impossible? What's your judgment at the end of the visit? Mission accomplished? We work with the Vatican on a lot of things. Our relationship between, you know, for example, our mission here and the Vatican—like I told you, I mean, we're working together on humanitarian issues and we're focused on other issues as well. The President's perspective is clear. He thinks that Iran is a threat and that it needs to be addressed. That position remains unchanged. I think we're capable of having that position and expressing that position clearly and also working cooperatively, as we have for decades, with the Vatican and with the Catholic Church who has a global presence. Did you personally ask the Pope to stop his criticism of the Iran war? I'm not going to discuss what I talked about with the Pope, but I'm not going to ask him that. That's not the purpose. I told you guys before I even came here: we have a very strong relationship with the Vatican and we work with them on a lot of things around the world. This was a trip that had been planned even before all of these things that happened. So why, just as an example, would I not want to hear the perspectives of the most important, far-reaching religious leader in the world? Why wouldn't I want his perspectives about what he saw on his trip to Africa, or what his bishops and agencies are hearing in the Western Hemisphere, or the plight of Christians in Africa and Lebanon? Of course we're going to engage with them on these things. That was the purpose of our meeting and in that realm it was very, very productive. It was a good meeting because we were able to talk about these different areas of the world where they have a presence, where they are engaged and we are as well. Of course, we find opportunities to either continue cooperating where that already exists like in Cuba, or expand our cooperation in other places where it makes sense. Is it fair to expect a phone call between the President and the Pope anytime soon? I don't know, maybe. I don't know. I mean, it could happen. Mr. Secretary, may I ask you about Iran if possible? You declared that Operation Epic Fury was over. Now, giving all these military actions we are seeing on the ground, what does "over" mean militarily? No, no. Operation Epic Fury was an offensive operation designed to destroy their missile launchers, their navy, their air force, and their factories. It achieved that. What you saw yesterday was US destroyers moving through international waters being fired upon by the Iranians and the US responded defensively to protect itself. That's what you saw. I mean, that's separate and distinct from Operation Epic Fury. If you fire a drone or a missile at our destroyer, what are we supposed to do? Let it hit it? We have to respond to it. We have to knock down the missile and we have to knock out whatever it is that launched that missile. The alternative is to let it sink one of our ships. That's crazy. So of course we responded to it. Well, yes, but the point is if there's not much ceasing in the firing because we've seen... Well, you should ask that of the Iranians. Don't ask me. We didn't fire. They fired on us. My point is, if you fire at a US Navy ship, what are we supposed to do? Say, "Oh, there's a ceasefire, we're not going to shoot down your drone"? That's a stupid question. That's a stupid position to take. Of course we fired back at them. They were shooting at us. That's what I would expect to do. Only stupid countries don't shoot back when you're shot at. And we're not a stupid country. Do you expect that there or have you conveyed any red lines to Iran where, you know, if the ceasefire or whatever you call it, if they cross certain red lines... Well, the red line is clear. If they threaten Americans, they're going to get blown up. I mean, how much clearer can you be than that? If you are a missile launching guy and you fire a missile at the United States and we saw you fire it, we're going to hit you. Of course we are. Who doesn't do that? Unless you want to get your ship sunk. I mean, we're not going to let our ships get sunk by the Iranians with their little drones that they're firing. You know, they don't have a navy anymore. But they bring out these little Boston Whaler fishing boats and they try to swarm you. We're going to blow those boats up if they're coming towards our boats. I don't know if that's a red line, but I hope they know it is by now. So if I were one of these Iranians on a fast boat and they tell you, "Hey, your mission is to go after a US destroyer," you're probably not going to survive. I think that's a red line. Can you, going back to the European reaction, you know, when we talked to... Does anybody else have any other questions? This guy back in an opera over here. No, let's go. The President has expressed disappointment in support, or lack thereof, in Europe. Was there any discussion today in your meeting with Prime Minister Meloni or Foreign Minister Tajani about them providing escorts in the Strait of Hormuz? No, we didn't get into that level of specificity about what they... Here's the bottom line, okay? Everybody says Iran is a threat. Everybody says that Iran can't have a nuclear weapon. Okay. But you got to do something about it. The President's trying to do something about it. I don't understand why anybody would not be supportive of that. And he doesn't understand—rightfully so—why somebody would not be supportive of that. But here's a more fundamental problem: Iran now claims that they have a right to control an international waterway. What is the world going to do about that? Is the world going to accept that Iran now controls an international waterway? Because if the world is prepared to accept that, then be ready. There are like ten other countries that are going to start doing the same thing in international waterways near their countries. That's an unacceptable thing that they're trying to normalize. So if the answer is no, we don't think Iran should be able to control the Strait of Hormuz, then the next question's going to be for everyone: well, what are you going to do about it? What is the world prepared to do about it? We're trying to do something about it diplomatically. We have a resolution at the United Nations that we're trying to move forward in which the world and the UN will have an opportunity through the Security Council to vote and say we are not going to allow them to mine the straits, we're not going to allow them to try to control the straits. By the way, you have a bunch of humanitarian aid that's trapped inside the Persian Gulf and needs to be released. We're going to give the world a chance to pronounce itself on it. And if Russia or China or some other country decide to veto it, then you know what the impediment is. But the fundamental question every country needs to ask themselves is: are you going to normalize a country claiming to control an international waterway? Because if you normalize that, you've set a precedent that's going to get repeated in a dozen other places. And if the answer is no, you better have something more than just strongly worded statements to back it up. That's the point I've made in all of my visits. I didn't come to Italy just to make that point. I would say that to any country that complains about the straits. Because that's what we potentially are facing unless we get a good deal from the Iranians and we're waiting to hear that today. Mr. Secretary, can I move to Taiwan actually? I'm sorry to geographically go around the world. Parliament just approved a pretty big massive defense bill today or last night. There was a little bit less than what the President of Taiwan was asking for, but nonetheless it's a big bill, including money to buy US arms. What is your reaction to that, if any, and specifically in the context of the trip you will be doing with the President next week in China? Well, all I would say in the context of the trip next week and Taiwan in specific is the United States' policy remains unchanged. You know, we don't want to see any forced or compelled change in the situation there. We think it'd be destabilizing to the world. Our position on that has been clear. We've heard obviously the Chinese position, but we've made our position clear as well. I imagine that will be discussed in the trip, but I don't think it will be a feature of our trip, though it'll certainly be an item that's discussed. We think it's to the benefit of China, the United States, and the world at large that there be stability in the straits there. That continues to be our position and that's the one we'll continue to stand by and advocate for. All right, I have time for one more and we got to leave. One last point, Ukraine before you go. You spoke with Lavrov recently and, you know, they targeted a kindergarten in Ukraine even after they advocated for ceasefire. Where does the negotiation stand? Well, right now the negotiations are... we've tried to play a mediator role in that. So far it has not led to a fruitful outcome for a variety of reasons. We remain prepared to play that role if it can be productive. We don't want to waste our time and invest time and energy in an effort that's not moving forward. But if we see an opportunity to act as a mediator that brings both sides closer to a peace deal, we'd like to see it. Our position at the end of the day is that this war is a tragedy. Both sides are paying a very heavy price for it, both economically and obviously at a human level. It's destructive, we're against it, and anytime I hear about these things, it just reminds us of why this war needs to end and why we're prepared to play whatever role we can to bring it to a peaceful diplomatic resolution. Unfortunately, right now those efforts have stagnated, but we always stand ready if those circumstances change. Okay, guys. Yeah, and then I got to go and I apologize. One on Africa. I mean, the President committed last year to personally intervening to help try to end the war in Sudan. The Pope in one of his addresses called this one of the world's greatest tragedies. Did this come out at all in your discussions and do you see any movement for... Well, we discussed Africa broadly and of course Sudan being an incredible tragedy. We've been heavily engaged through that quad process. Obviously, Sudan has become in some ways sort of a proxy engagement between multiple countries who are behind some of the elements that are fighting. There are other countries that are not directly involved but have allowed their national territories to be used as a way to ship weaponry. We recently had a pretty positive announcement for the first time on a statement of principles, but we have to give it some action. What we've really been focused on in Sudan is establishing a humanitarian ceasefire that allows us to provide aid to people that are suffering tremendously there, but not doing so at the expense of a broader deal. So we do want the humanitarian ceasefire where we can identify safe havens for the distribution of humanitarian aid for what is one of the world's worst humanitarian crises ever. But you can't do that unless you have the security to be able to provide the aid. And we don't want that to be the final outcome. In addition to the humanitarian corridors and sites, we need to bring this conflict to an end. The way to do that is for the parties who are supporting the fighting elements to force these elements toward a peace deal. As I said, we've been very involved in that quad process, invested a lot of time in it, and continue to be engaged on a daily basis. In fact, we have people in our bureau in Africa as well as our special envoy involved almost daily on this topic. Okay guys, I got to run. I apologize. Thank you. Were you surprised by your Italian origins? Well, not surprised today. I learned about it in 2017 when PBS did a "Finding Your Roots" special. That's where I found it. Okay. How about the 2028 buzz? Any comment on that, sir?

Carlos Basso alerta sobre Peter Thiel, Palantir y el nuevo poder de Silicon Valley

El Mostrador(72.8K)·28:24·804 views·2026-05-08
Carlos BassoEditor, El Mostrador Peter ThielCofounder, Palantir Technologies
TL;DRThe emergence of "Techno-fascism" and the "Dark Enlightenment" ideology, led by figures like Peter Thiel and Palantir Technologies, represents a global shift toward governance by "CEO monarchs" and AI-driven military deterrence, with active expansion efforts now reaching Chile and Argentina.

TL;DR

The emergence of "Techno-fascism" and the "Dark Enlightenment" ideology, led by figures like Peter Thiel and Palantir Technologies, represents a global shift toward governance by "CEO monarchs" and AI-driven military deterrence, with active expansion efforts now reaching Chile and Argentina.

The Ideology of the "Dark Enlightenment"

  • Rejection of Democracy: Carlos Basso describes a paradigm shift where Silicon Valley "techno-barons" view the post-Enlightenment democratic model (separation of powers, checks and balances) as obsolete.
      • The Neo-Feudal Model: Proponents like Peter Thiel advocate for a return to pre-Enlightenment structures, where states are managed like corporations by a "CEO" or "monarch" rather than an elected president.
      • Techno-fascism: This movement argues that democracy is no longer compatible with capitalism or individual liberty, favoring efficiency and personal interest over collective representation.
  • Political Influence: These figures are no longer just tech providers but ideological architects.
      • US Politics: Peter Thiel was the primary financier for current VP candidate J.D. Vance and remains a pivotal figure in the MAGA movement’s intellectual foundation.
      • The "CEO Monarch": Basso notes that Donald Trump’s governance style aligns with this corporate-state vision, treating citizens as clients and international relations as business negotiations.

Palantir and AI-Driven Warfare

  • The Palantir Manifesto: Recently published on X, the 22-point document synthesizes the views of CEO Alex Karp, emphasizing that the era of atomic deterrence has ended, replaced by AI deterrence.
  • Gotham Software: Palantir’s primary platform, named after Batman's city, is designed for defense and security.
      • Capabilities: It processes massive data sets to identify targets and make strategic battlefield decisions.
      • Operational Use: The software has been extensively used in Gaza and by various global powers.
      • Risks and Errors: Basso highlights the "muddy" ethical grounds of AI, citing reports of a software error that allegedly misidentified an Iranian girls' school as a military target, resulting in 160 deaths.

Peter Thiel’s Presence in Chile and the Region

  • South American Tour: Thiel recently visited Quito, Buenos Aires (meeting with Javier Milei), and Santiago.
      • Chilean Contacts: In Chile, Thiel met with José Piñera and allegedly with deputy Johannes Kaiser, signaling an effort to network with actors aligned with his "Dark Enlightenment" views.
  • Palantir’s Legal Footprint in Chile:
      • Trademark Status: While Palantir Technologies does not yet exist as a legal person in Chile, it successfully registered its trademark with INAPI in 2023 after a legal battle with the owners of *The Lord of the Rings* intellectual property.
      • Potential Defense Ties: Basso suggests that due to Chile's Intelligence Law, specific contracts with the Armed Forces or Police (PDI/Carabineros) would be classified, making it difficult to track the current level of Palantir’s integration into national security.

The Massive Scale of "Techno-Baron" Power

  • Financial Dominance: The wealth of these individuals now rivals or exceeds the budgets of entire nations.
      • Elon Musk: With a fortune estimated at $500 billion, his net worth represents five to six years of the total Chilean national budget.
      • Alex Karp: Palantir’s CEO holds a fortune of $18 billion, comparable to the wealthiest families in Chile, such as the Luksics.
  • Data as Power: Beyond money, Palantir acts as a "data mining" giant. The sheer volume of personal and strategic data they control gives them unprecedented influence over global policy and security.

Notable Quotes

"They believe the most efficient forms of state administration are the old pre-Enlightenment forms... it should return to the era of fiefdoms, where a feudal lord managed everything based on personal interest." — Carlos Basso
"Thiel has said it: Democracy is practically over; it is a failed experiment. He has stated that democracy is not compatible with capitalism or freedom." — Mirna Schindler
"Today, deterrence is no longer about who has the biggest atomic weapon, but basically about who has the greatest Artificial Intelligence capacity." — Carlos Basso
"These people, with that economic capacity... having the data they have, are the people who are molding the future of humanity." — Carlos Basso
Hola, ¿cómo están? Muy buenos días. Iniciamos el último capítulo de la semana aquí en Al Pan-Pan por El Mostrador.cl. Finalmente, el proyecto de reconstrucción nacional logró ser aprobado en su idea de legislar, por cierto, en la Comisión de Hacienda, con los votos del oficialismo. Contó con un PDG que no participa directamente de la votación, es decir, no entrega su apoyo, porque recordémonos lo que declaraba la directiva respecto de no haber logrado acuerdo con dos temas que son esenciales para esta colectividad, como es el tema de devolverle a las personas el IVA a los pañales y el tema de los medicamentos, una canasta de medicamentos. Pero las conversaciones están ahí y el PDG sigue siendo ahí un actor importante para La Moneda. Sin perjuicio de lo cual, el oficialismo y voces del oficialismo, pero entre ellos ni más ni menos que el presidente del Partido Republicano, Arturo Squella, están señalando que ojo con las negociaciones con un partido que, en el fondo, lo que está haciendo es subir el precio. Es más, Arturo Squella es partidario de negociar con la ex Concertación, dice, con la Democracia Cristiana y el PPD. Vamos a estar hablando precisamente con el diputado independiente por el PPD, por la región de Antofagasta, que además integra la Comisión de Seguridad, Jaime Araya. Así que hablaremos de todo lo que tienen que saber respecto de esta ley miscelánea y de cómo se ve aspectado antes de la cuenta pública del presidente, eh, José Antonio Kast. Pero ahora nos vamos a concentrar en lo que hablábamos hace algunos días, esto que dio bastante que hablar en redes sociales, que es el manifiesto de 22 puntos que fueron publicados en la red social X por parte de la empresa de Silicon Valley Palantir Technologies. Entre los fundadores de esta empresa se encuentra Peter Thiel. Peter Thiel, que estuvo en Chile y en Argentina, que se reunió con José Piñera y con el diputado Johannes Kaiser, es un personaje muy, muy relevante respecto de lo que se ha llamado la "ilustración oscura" y también el tecnofascismo. De esto y más vamos a conversar a esta hora con el editor de la Unidad de Investigación de El Mostrador, Carlos Basso. Carlos, un gusto de tenerte una vez más por acá. "Hola Mirna, ¿cómo estás?", decía él. Quería partir por el tema del manifiesto de Palantir. Es importante porque esto en Europa y en general en el hemisferio norte ha sido muy comentado. ¿Qué hay ahí? ¿Qué significa que una empresa tecnológica emita un manifiesto que tiene un fuerte contenido político? Bueno, esa es una evidencia más de cómo estamos viviendo un mundo donde están cambiando muchas cosas y muchos paradigmas. Están cambiando muchas situaciones que dábamos como objetivas o como sentadas. Y una de esas es justamente esto que mencionas tú. Si vamos un poquito más atrás, nos vamos a dar cuenta que no es un proceso que haya comenzado este año, sino que ya lleva unos buenos años. Hoy día estamos viendo compañías de un tamaño gigantesco dedicadas al tema de las tecnologías de información y datos. Estas se han convertido, por distintos motivos, en las compañías más importantes de este mundo en que vivimos. Tienen una incidencia ideológica gigantesca en esta especie de bolsillo payaso gigantesco que muchas veces denominamos ultraderecha, pero que ya tiene una cantidad de derivadas que hace que ese concepto no sea ya exacto. Hace un año y tanto, cuando asume su segunda presidencia el presidente de Estados Unidos, Donald Trump, ¿quiénes están en su ceremonia? Además de los senadores y los secretarios de Estado, están lo que se denomina los tecno-barones de Silicon Valley. Está Mark Zuckerberg, está Peter Thiel y Jeff Bezos, el dueño de Amazon. Uno se da cuenta ahí, en ese hecho, del peso que ellos tienen. Hay una foto en el acto de inauguración de la presidencia donde están todos ellos juntos. De a poco uno va conociendo otros detalles, como que Peter Thiel es el dueño de Palantir y uno de los creadores de PayPal junto con Elon Musk. No nos olvidemos que Elon Musk fue parte del gabinete de Donald Trump un tiempo. Todos estos personajes están ahí por un motivo muy importante. Peter Thiel fue el principal financista de la campaña a senador del actual vicepresidente de Estados Unidos, J.D. Vance. Es gente que viene interviniendo en política desde hace muchos años. Aquí es donde se produce este cambio de época que estamos viendo. Ellos son personas que, si tú lo ves desde una perspectiva típica sudamericana, son de lo que nosotros considerábamos antes la derecha clásica o la extrema derecha. Pero la verdad es que ellos no adhieren exactamente a esa noción. Adhieren a nociones que son incluso más extremas de lo que nosotros consideramos extremas en este continente. Por ejemplo, el concepto de la Ilustración Oscura lleva casi veinte años apareciendo en distintos manifiestos. Básicamente dice que las formas más eficientes de administración de un Estado son las antiguas formas pre-Ilustración. No nos olvidemos que la Ilustración es el periodo donde aparecen grandes pensadores como Montesquieu, Rousseau, Locke, y aparecen las nociones de separación de poderes, balance y democracia. Ellos dicen que esto no funciona así y que se debiese volver a la época de los feudos. En ese modelo había un caballero feudal que administraba todo en función de sus intereses personales y gracias a eso generaba bienestar hacia quienes vivían en su castillo. Ellos hacen el símil entre este caballero feudal y el jefe de un Estado. Dicen que sería más eficiente un CEO que un presidente. Consideran que la democracia está excedida y sobrevalorada, por lo tanto dicen que no se necesita. En ese contexto aparece este manifiesto de Palantir, que es básicamente una síntesis de un texto que publicó el año pasado el CEO de la empresa, Alex Karp. Él es un personaje bastante peculiar. Pese a que proviene de este cuño de extrema derecha, tiene un doctorado en filosofía en Alemania, donde estudió con Jürgen Habermas de la Escuela de Frankfurt. Ese texto dice básicamente: "Avanza más allá". Señala que hoy día la era de disuasión atómica ya no existe. Ya no existe aquel equilibrio que vivimos los más viejos, con la confrontación abierta entre Estados Unidos y la Unión Soviética. Hoy día la disuasión se trata básicamente de inteligencia artificial. En el caso de Palantir, uno de sus softwares principales es una plataforma que se llama Gotham. Gotham hace alusión a la ciudad de Batman. Lo mismo pasa con Palantir, que es el nombre de una piedra que ve el futuro en El Señor de los Anillos. Es un software que permite que las fuerzas que lo usan tengan absolutamente medidos todos los movimientos de sus objetivos para tomar decisiones estratégicas. Se atribuye, y aquí entramos en una zona pantanosa, la duda sobre qué capacidad de discernimiento tiene una IA que sabemos que sufre de alucinaciones. Una de las equivocaciones que se le atribuye es el ataque a una escuela de niñas en Irán, en donde fallecieron más de 160 niñas. La explicación que se ha dado a través de trascendidos es que el software se equivocó e identificó esa escuela como un sitio beligerante. Sin perjuicio de eso, softwares de Palantir han sido usados en Gaza y en otros países, como en las fuerzas de Putin o en China. Ellos dicen que la disuasión ya no tiene que ver con quién tiene el arma atómica más grande, sino con quién tiene mayor capacidad de inteligencia artificial. Plantean que hay que abandonar los prejuicios "buenistas" de Silicon Valley y concentrarse derechamente en el sector de defensa. Debe haber un trabajo conjunto entre el Estado y las capacidades de defensa creadas por estos particulares. Otro aspecto relevante es qué estaba haciendo Peter Thiel en Chile. Sabemos que se juntó con José Piñera y hay trascendidos sobre una reunión con Johannes Kaiser, aunque nadie lo ha confirmado. Todo indica que andaba en una gira sudamericana; antes de estar en Buenos Aires y Santiago, estuvo en Quito visitando a gobiernos afines a las ideas de derecha. En Buenos Aires permaneció más de una semana, se dice que compró una casa y se sacó una famosa foto con Milei. Aparentemente está intentando expandir las operaciones de su compañía hacia este continente. Hicimos un trabajo de búsqueda de huellas de Palantir en Chile y, hasta hace una semana, no existía como persona jurídica en el país, a diferencia de lo que ocurre en México y Brasil. Sin embargo, sí tiene inscrita la marca en el INAPI desde el año 2023. Esa inscripción estuvo en medio de un litigio con la compañía de marketing de El Señor de los Anillos, pero finalmente el tribunal decidió que Palantir Technologies podía quedarse con la marca. Esto indica una intención clara de establecerse acá. Este tipo de software entra dentro de la Ley de Inteligencia, por lo que costará saber si hubo contacto con las Fuerzas Armadas o las policías. Hace unos diez años hubo una filtración de una compañía italiana donde aparecía la compra de un software llamado Phantom por parte de la PDI. Cuando se les preguntó, la PDI se amparó en la Ley de Inteligencia. Este dato es importante porque, aunque hay listados públicos de proveedores de las Fuerzas Armadas, existen estas excepciones. Surge la pregunta de si se habrá reunido con José Antonio Kast, pero no hay datos objetivos que lo indiquen. Viendo la dinámica de su viaje, debiese haber sido así, pero es difícil que haya sucedido sin que se filtrara. Tiendo a pensar que fue un viaje básico para entender el ambiente de negocios acá y que muy pronto lo vamos a ver de nuevo. Esto no se trata de un grupito de excéntricos rayados. Tienen injerencia hoy en la seguridad y en la política exterior del país más poderoso del mundo. Tienen poder económico y manejan una cantidad tremenda de datos personales. Para ellos, el Estado debe ser como una empresa y los ciudadanos son clientes. Peter Thiel ha dicho que la democracia es un experimento fallido. Cuando eso entra en el corazón del gobierno más poderoso del mundo, hay que preocuparse. Nosotros tendemos a motear al gobierno de Trump como de ultraderecha, pero esa calificación está superada. Hay una ideología que está sucediendo frente a nuestros ojos y que explica muchas decisiones en Estados Unidos. La derecha más dura agrupada en el movimiento MAGA tiene diferencias con la derecha conservadora tradicional. Peter Thiel, por ejemplo, es gay y está casado con un hombre, algo incompatible con el conservadurismo decimonónico del sur de Estados Unidos. Pero para él, eso ya no es tema. En la noción del Estado de Estados Unidos hay principios que eran intransables, como la democracia. Pero estos tecno-barones poseen las mayores fortunas de la historia. Antes de Elon Musk, la familia Rockefeller tenía unos 300.000 millones de dólares; hoy, la fortuna de Musk se tasa en 500.000 millones. Alex Karp, de Palantir, tiene 18.000 millones, una cifra cercana al patrimonio de la familia Luksic en Chile. Los 500.000 millones de Musk equivalen a cinco o seis años del presupuesto completo de nuestro Estado. Estas personas están moldeando el futuro de la humanidad. Tienen conceptos que inciden en personajes como Donald Trump. Si analizas el concepto del Estado manejado como empresa y el "CEO monarca", eso es precisamente cómo Trump se ve a sí mismo. Él establece sus relaciones internacionales sobre la base de cómo negocia con sus empresas. Efectivamente, su conducta es más cercana a la de un CEO que a la de un presidente tradicional. No sé hasta qué punto lo de Trump es doctrinario o personalista, pero J.D. Vance sí es un hombre imbuido en esta ideología y él es el proyecto de continuidad. Si le va bien, veremos una profundización de estas políticas. Es un tema interesantísimo al que hay que seguirle la pista. Carlos Basso, como siempre, muy agradecidos de tenerte en el programa. Que estés muy bien. "Igualmente", respondió él. Bien, nos vamos a ir al corte, pero antes les contamos sobre "Aquí Magallanes", el nuevo newsletter regional de El Mostrador. Te contamos lo que pasa en la región del Estrecho de Magallanes, la Patagonia, Puerto Williams, Torres del Paine, Punta Arenas y la Antártica Chilena. Todo directo a tu correo cada miércoles porque Santiago no es Chile. Inscríbete gratis. Al regreso nos vamos a meter de lleno en la tramitación de la ley miscelánea. Ayer fue aprobada la idea de legislar en la Comisión de Hacienda sin los votos del Partido de la Gente. Vamos a ver si el ministro Quiroz logra llegar a la cuenta pública del 1 de junio con este proyecto aprobado. Estaremos conversando con el diputado independiente pro PPD Jaime Araya. Ya volvemos en instantes con una nueva entrevista en Al Pan-Pan con Mirna Schindler por El Mostrador Podcast. El poder de la transparencia.

U.S., Iran close to ending war? Iran war's impact on the midterms

CNBC Television(3.4M)·10:47·20.8K views·2026-05-08
Heidi HeitkampFormer U.S. Senator Mick MulvaneyFormer White House Chief of Staff
TL;DRThe ongoing conflict with Iran and sustained high energy prices present significant political risks for incumbents ahead of the midterms, while the domestic debate shifts toward "Gilded Age" inequality and the role of state intervention in the economy.
Market Resilience:...

TL;DR

The ongoing conflict with Iran and sustained high energy prices present significant political risks for incumbents ahead of the midterms, while the domestic debate shifts toward "Gilded Age" inequality and the role of state intervention in the economy.

Iran Conflict and Economic Stability

  • Market Resilience: Despite geopolitical chaos, markets have remained surprisingly stable. Heidi Heitkamp attributes this to the underlying strength and innovation of the American worker, though she warns the $40 trillion national debt remains a looming "wildcard."
  • Policy Inconsistency: Heitkamp notes a lack of consistency in war execution, which is creating anxiety in "farm country" due to skyrocketing fertilizer costs and diesel prices.
  • The "Success" Metric: Mick Mulvaney defines Republican success as a total end to the Iranian nuclear program and ensuring the Straits of Hormuz remain permanently open. He argues Trump cannot exit the conflict until these conditions are met, as a closed strait is an unacceptable economic loss.

Midterm Election Pressure

  • Gas Price Vulnerability: Both speakers agree that $4.50 per gallon gas entering the August/September window is a "tough sell" for the incumbent party.
  • Managing Expectations: President Trump has reportedly begun setting expectations that gas prices will not drop significantly before the midterms, avoiding promises he cannot keep.
  • The "Boots on the Ground" Risk: Heitkamp suggests that eliminating enriched Iranian material may require a ground invasion—a move that would trigger a massive backlash from isolationist voters.

Structural Energy Challenges

  • Slow Recovery: Experts warn that reopening the Straits will not act as a "light switch" for the economy. It could take 4–5 months to see price relief.
  • Supply Deficit: 10% to 15% of Qatari gas production is currently offline and may remain so for several years, representing structural damage that cannot be quickly repaired.
  • The "Nacho" Concept: Mulvaney dismissed the "nacho" theory (the idea that the Strait of Hormuz may never fully reopen), stating the U.S. economy simply cannot tolerate that outcome.

Domestic Political Tensions: Mamdani vs. Griffin

  • Class Warfare: The public dispute between NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani and billionaire Ken Griffin reflects a broader "Gilded Age" resentment. Heitkamp suggests billionaires must change their rhetoric from defensive to collaborative.
  • The "Communist" Label: Mulvaney characterized Mamdani’s agenda—including government-owned grocery stores—as an experiment in communism that he believes is destined to fail.
  • State-Led Services: Heitkamp defended the concept of state intervention, citing North Dakota’s state-owned bank and mill as successful examples of government filling gaps where the free enterprise system fails, distinguishing this from "Soviet-style communism."

Notable Quotes

"If you go into August to September with $4.50 gallon of gas, it doesn't bode well for the incumbent party. They need this thing to be finished sooner rather than later." — Mick Mulvaney
"We're in a Gilded Age. People are starting to be very resentful of people who aren't taking responsibility but have had tremendous success in this economy." — Heidi Heitkamp
"Maybe Zohran Mamdani is the communist who gets it right... maybe he’s smarter than Lenin and Stalin and Castro and Chavez. My guess is it doesn't [work]." — Mick Mulvaney
Right now, we're going to talk about jobs, the midterms, Iran, and the fight between New York Mayor Zoran Mamdani and billionaire Ken Griffin. Joining us right now is former Senator Heidi Heitkamp, founder of the One Country Project and a CNBC contributor, and also Mick Mulvaney, former White House Chief of Staff and co-chair of Actum Strategic Advisors. A lot to talk about. Let's just start with the situation in Iran and how close you think Heidi and Mick we really are. It feels like every day is sort of a one step forward or maybe two steps forward, one step back. The markets seem to think everything's just fine. Is it? Well, yeah, if you're going to judge what's happening with the American public by the markets, I think that's a little false kind of narrative, Andrew. I think I think that what people are seeing is something very erratic. They aren't seeing consistency in policy, and they're seeing something at the pumps that they haven't seen for a while, which is a four in front of the number. And so they're wondering why they're sacrificing and what they're sacrificing for. And that's especially true in farm country, as people see how much the fertilizer prices are going up, shortages are happening, gas prices, and especially for farmers, diesel is up. And so people are wondering, why are we doing this? And why doesn't he seem to have any kind of consistency in terms of how we're, you know, basically executing the war? Heidi, before we get to Mick though, have you been surprised at how well the market and the economy at least thus far seem to have held up despite this? You know, it's amazing. Yeah, it's amazing because one thing that we always talk about is inconsistency and chaos. You know, markets hate chaos. I think they've adapted. They've adapted to Trump chaos and they look at the underlying strength of the American economy, the innovation that's happening, the productivity, the American worker, and they're saying, "Look, we've got to ignore for a while the policy." Plus, where do we go with these dollars? But I think that the market's going to start responding to an almost $40 trillion debt. And to me, that's the big wildcard out there. Mick, in terms of the way you think the Republican Party's thinking about this right now, and what it could mean for the midterms? Yeah, they want it over. They do, and they want it won. Trump has done a really good job, I think, in the last couple of weeks, much better than the administration did at the outset of the war, defining what success looks like. And success looks like no Iranian nuclear program ever and the Straits of Hormuz open. Okay, that was not entirely clear going into the war, but it's clear now. And what Republicans want is the war over on those terms so that we can get things as close to back to normal as quickly as possible. Everybody knows, and Heidi knows this as well as anybody, if you go into August to September with, you know, a $4.50 gallon of gas, it doesn't bode well for the incumbent party. And so they need—they need this thing to be finished sooner rather than later. Now, Mick though, what do you think finished means? I don't know if you heard, you know, one of the things that some of the trading floors are now talking about is this idea of "nachos," which effectively is this idea that the Strait of Hormuz is never going to be open. And what that really represents. It can't happen. It can't happen. Trump can't leave until the straits are open and open with some some permanency. Because that first of all, it looks like a loss. And second of all, it is a loss. And he just—he can't have that. He can't have that choke point controlled by a by a hostile actor. The markets simply won't tolerate it. Our economy won't tolerate it. So no, I get the nacho, but I don't buy that one. He can't leave until the straits are open. And the other piece of this, and it's really for Heidi and Mick, is even if the strait is open, you know, you talk to different people in in the energy space, they think that it could take months, maybe as much as, you know, four, five months before you actually get prices back to where they were prior. It's not just going to be a quick drop off. We've had these sort of quick, quick drops, but you know, in terms of how the sort of energy space really works, and how you think that—what do you think that portends for how you think about the midterms? In fairness, I think a couple of months might be too short. Stuff folks I've been talking to said some place between 10 and 15 percent of Qatari gas production is offline for at least the next several years. Yes, we'll be able to fill some of the gap in in time, but not in two or three months. So there are structural damages here done that I don't think get fixed just when Trump says the war is over. This is not a light switch situation. We're not going back to $2.50 a gas here in the next three months. The question is, can you sort of try to sell to voters from a political standpoint that things are going to get better? Right now, that's a really tough sell. You know, it's interesting, Andrew, because this is a president that isn't given to, you know, like caution in terms of what he predicts, right? "I can solve that problem in a day. I'll take care of the Ukrainian war in a day." Of course, that doesn't happen, and no one really believed it could. But the president himself said, "I don't see gas prices coming down that much before the midterms." And I think he—he understands he needs to set expectations. Because if he promises $2.00, you know, a two in front of that number, he's not going to achieve that goal. And that is—it is so transparent to the American consumers. But the bottom line is, I don't think that you can achieve the goal of eliminating the the enriched material without putting boots on the ground. The minute he puts boots on the ground, that's where isolationist people, like the people I grew up with, who do not want another foreign war, that's when they go ballistic. And so that two-pronged success test that Mick just talked about, I don't think it's possible. Heidi, let me pivot just for a second to a different topic. It relates to politics, though, in a big way and and ultimately, I imagine, could become a talking point during this—the midterms, even though this particular politician is not up for up for election and isn't necessarily a national figure, per se. And that is Mayor Mamdani and this battle going on between himself and Ken Griffin. He's tried to sort of soften his words, I think, in recent days. But it's become a national story. And I just wonder how you think the Democrats are going to be thinking about him and that. You know, I've said this a couple times, what I tell people is I don't know what the mayor of New York has to do with the people of North Dakota and how they vote. But you know that this is going to be played one off the other. The one thing that I will tell you, and you're seeing it play out in a courtroom in Oakland today, or or starting again next week, you see it even in your book: we're in a Gilded Age. And people are starting to be very resentful of people who aren't taking responsibility but have had had tremendous success in this economy. And that's what Mamdani is reflecting. And so, you know, the greed and the hubris and the... you know, I—to me, my advice to billionaires out there is say, "Yeah, I don't like what you're doing, but how can I help? How can I help the American economy and the American middle class recover?" And you don't see that as a reaction. And the smartest thing they could do is actually sit down and have a conversation. Heidi, but in this particular instance—and I'm sorry, I'll go to Mick in just a second—in this particular instance, I think that he chose the wrong person to pick on. And the reason I'm specifically mentioning that is both not just the jobs that he's created in New York and the possibility of him bringing them to Miami, which unto itself is a problem if if you're a taxpayer in New York and and you want good-paying jobs there. But he's been one of the most philanthropic in New York. And not just that. By the way, he's been a Republican who's been critical—one of the few CEOs in America who's been critical of this administration. I mean, he's just sort of of all of the people that the Democrats in New York could pick on, this is the wrong guy. Well, and the other thing, Andrew, I would add to that, he didn't like what was going on in Illinois and decided to walk with his his his feet to to other places in the country. You know, and and you need to be careful. And the one thing that he did say that I think we all need to ratchet down the rhetoric. He said, "You've put a target on my back." And I—and for that, I have great sympathy. But by the same token, you know, conversation. What's whatever happened to actually having a conversation between two leaders who have different perspectives but the same goal of building the American economy and helping people? Mick, I imagine Republicans are going to use this and take advantage of it in all sorts of ways. But what do you think about this? And to the extent that Heidi is pointing up a real issue, which is there are people who are angry, and there are people who are—or rightly frustrated about inequality. It is a real issue. But how you think it should be addressed and discussed. Look, is communism the answer to that question? If you agree that that's an issue, is communism the answer? Look, I hope they—I hope they do his whole agenda. The whole soak the rich thing, the government ownership of grocery stores. I really do. It's part of that American experiment. Maybe Zohran Mamdani is the—is the communist who gets it right. Maybe he's smarter than Lenin and Stalin and Castro and Chavez. I mean, maybe maybe he will be the one who can prove that communism can last. My guess is it doesn't. It's never lasted more than two or three generations anyway. But the great part about being in America is you can try different things. Even if I share Heidi's concerns about the the nature of the status of the of the country. And I do to a certain extent. I'm not sure communism is the right answer. But we get to find out. And hopefully the message will go out that either it really works, and then, you know, Heidi can go from state to state and say what a great idea it is to to spread that doctrine, or it doesn't work. Like it's never worked before. And then we could try something else. Andrew, I just want to point out I come from a state that owns a bank and uses that bank very effectively to grow our economy. We own a mill and elevator. And so when you talk about communism, what does that look like? Is state ownership or stepping in to provide services to respond to a question when the free enterprise system hasn't adequately responded? That's why I got elected. So Mick, I agree with you. I don't think we should head down that direction. But let's not—let's not be too harsh in terms of saying you're going to have Soviet-style communism in New York City. We get to find out. That's the beauty of the system. So sadly, New York, I think is going to pay the price. But as Ed Koch famously said, the voters have spoken and now they must be punished.

Elon’s Anthropic Deal, The Next AI Monopoly?, “FDA for AI” Panic, Trading the AI Boom

All-In Podcast(1.1M)·82:02·359.7K views·2026-05-08
Chamath PalihapitiyaCo-host, All-In Podcast Jason CalacanisCo-host, All-In Podcast David SacksCo-host, All-In Podcast
TL;DRThe emergence of "Elon Web Services" (EWS) via a massive data center deal with Anthropic has fundamentally shifted the AI landscape, fueling a debate over whether Anthropic is destined to become the world’s most powerful monopoly while the industry fights off "FDA-style" federal regulation.

TL;DR

The emergence of "Elon Web Services" (EWS) via a massive data center deal with Anthropic has fundamentally shifted the AI landscape, fueling a debate over whether Anthropic is destined to become the world’s most powerful monopoly while the industry fights off "FDA-style" federal regulation.

The SpaceX-Anthropic Deal: The Birth of "EWS"

  • Infrastructure Synergy: Elon Musk has leased the "Colossus 1" data center capacity (over 220,000 Nvidia GPUs) to Anthropic, effectively solving Anthropic's compute constraints and launching "Elon Web Services" (EWS) as a legitimate hyperscaler competitor to AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.
      • SpaceX Valuation: The deal provides a "structural core business" for SpaceX that de-risks the valuation of its future orbital data centers.
      • Revenue Generation: Estimates suggest this could generate an incremental $4–$5 billion in annual revenue for Musk’s ventures, subsidizing the training of xAI’s Grok models.
  • Anthropic’s Growth Trajectory: Anthropic is currently experiencing unprecedented exponential growth, tripling its ARR from $10 billion to $30 billion in Q1, and reaching $44 billion by April.
      • Market Forecast: At current rates, the company is on track to exit the year at $100 billion ARR, leading to projections of a $1 trillion ARR by 2027.

The Monopoly Debate and "Regulatory Capture"

  • The "Safe Oil" Analogy: David Sacks compared Anthropic’s "safety-first" branding to a hypothetical JD Rockefeller calling Standard Oil "Safe Oil" to invite regulation that would crush smaller competitors.
      • Monopoly Signals: Sacks argues that Anthropic and OpenAI are forming a duopoly, with Anthropic potentially becoming the most powerful monopoly in history.
      • Anti-Competitive Behavior: The "Besties" debated whether Anthropic’s refusal to let certain competitors use their models is a precursor to anti-competitive market control.
  • Safety as a Moat: There is a growing concern that "safetyism" is being used as a tool for regulatory capture, creating high barriers to entry that prevent new startups from competing with established labs.

"FDA for AI" and the Regulatory Outlook

  • White House Rumors: Recent reports suggested the White House is considering an FDA-like vetting process for AI models.
      • "Fake News" Clarification: Sacks and Gerstner reported conversations with senior officials (including Kevin Hassett) clarifying that there is no intention to create a pre-approval regime, though cyber-vulnerability reviews are on the table.
      • Cybersecurity Focus: The catalyst for the "freakout" was the capabilities of models like Anthropic’s Mythos, which display significant cyber-offensive potential.
  • Proposed Guardrails:
      • KYC (Know Your Customer): Chamath and Sacks suggested a "KYC wrapper" for powerful APIs to ensure state-sponsored actors cannot access frontier models.
      • Self-Policing: The labs have largely self-regulated by not releasing "Mythos-class" models to the general public, proving that the industry understands the stakes.

The AI Economy: "Goldilocks" or Financial Engineering?

  • Market Bullishness: Brad Gerstner characterizes the current US economy as a "Goldilocks" situation—accelerating GDP, controlled inflation, and a massive lead in AI over the rest of the world.
      • Hyperscaler Boom: Google Cloud (63% growth) and Azure (39% growth) are seeing massive AI-driven reacceleration.
  • The Margin Question: Chamath remains skeptical that AI is currently driving S&P 500 margins, suggesting current profitability is a result of "financial engineering" and post-COVID "fitness" (cost-cutting) rather than AI-driven productivity.
      • The 500-Day Window: Chamath predicts a "reckoning moment" in roughly 500 days when enterprises must prove that the money spent on AI tokens has yielded a measurable ROI.
  • Societal "Antibodies": To counter the negative public perception of AI, Jason Calacanis proposed that tech leaders should champion "giving back" via:
      • Invest America: Allocating a percentage of AI IPOs to investment accounts for every American child.
      • Vertical Innovation: Using AI to aggressively lower the costs of healthcare and education.

Notable Quotes

David Sacks: "Imagine if JD Rockefeller was way better at public relations and instead of calling his company Standard Oil, he called it 'Safe Oil.' He would call for a new government agency to regulate the safety of his product... people would have gotten so wrapped up in the safety debate they would have missed that he was building the most powerful monopoly of all time."
Chamath Palihapitiya: "We have horrible messaging. The idea that there’s going to be a few winners and many, many potential losers is disconcerting to everybody... the tech community is doing a horrible job avoiding the buildup of societal antibodies. I'd give them a D-minus trending to an F."
Brad Gerstner: "The policies of this administration on the economy are working in spades. Our gap on the rest of the world in AI is growing... there’s not a country in the world that wouldn’t trade all of its fortune for the United States' fortune today."
Jason Calacanis: "We need to look at why Americans are scared. It's income, healthcare, and their kids' education. If we don't let founders 'cook' in those verticals and get rid of the regulation, the black eye on AI will only get worse."
How do I sound? You sound perfect. You sound great. How do I look? Yeah, you sound great. Better than you look. Got the face made for radio. You don't look as tired as you have in recent weeks. That's true. Oh really? Oh yeah, somebody was slagging me for the bags under my eyes. I mean, this audience is brutal. They're brutal. They're brutal. It's a good thing I'm rich. All right everybody, welcome back. It's the number one podcast in the world. It's the All-In Podcast. With us today: Chamath Palihapitiya, David Sacks, and our fifth bestie, Mr. Brad Gerstner is here. I think David Friedberg is suffering from some socialist-related flu. He's very sick of reading about socialism, but he'll be back next week with two incredible, incredible interviews. You guys see those Spencer Pratt ads? Wow. It's one of the best political ads I've ever seen. Oh, there's like three or four of them. There's multiples. Whoever that social media team is, is on fire. If you get a good social media team and you get a good ad production team, I think it's next-gen because these things go crazy. And Spencer Pratt, if he wins this election—which I think he's going to in Los Angeles—the reason is what Brad said. Those ads are incredible. Well, he's also quite a good debater. Did you see clips from this debate? Incredible. He's so funny. He's so chill. Yeah, well he's up against Karen Bass who's the mayor, who is basically extremely left-wing. And then there's someone who's a city councilwoman who's even further to the left of Karen Bass. I mean she's often like Fidel Castro territory. She's an Indian Fidel Castro, Raman. So she was basically, I guess, criticizing the mayor for the homelessness problem and then Pratt pointed out that this councilwoman is actually in charge of all these homeless programs already. He eviscerated her. He eviscerated her. He destroyed her. And he basically made the key point, which is look, the problem here is not lack of housing; it's an addiction issue and it's a mental illness issue. And he said, look, he said if she went to the street she'd get stabbed in the neck. Which is pretty accurate if you've been to Skid Row. I mean, you would not want to walk through there. It was like the Spider-Man photo... Wait, Nick's got the clip. Play the clip. Oh god, this clip is brutal. This is a different clip but this went super viral. It reminded me of Trump a little bit. Let's see. "I'm not sure how to respond to that vision of Los Angeles. This is a MAGA Republican's idea of what Los Angeles looks like. This is... this is really not..." Unbelievable. For those of you listening, he put his hands up and wiggled his thumb like "oh my god." Hey, hey Sacks, you know, Spencer Pratt wins Mayor and then the ballot initiative, the Retirement Protection and Savings Act, right, is going to pass. It's going to pass with big numbers. This is the referendum that effectively is going to knock out the wealth tax. Can you imagine if California effectively passes a constitutional amendment protecting retirement savings and personal assets and banning the wealth tax and Pratt gets elected? The message that that would send to the country? That's a very non-consensus view that I'm becoming increasingly optimistic about. Well, from your lips to God's ears. But until that message actually is sent, I think I'm going to be... in Texas. In Texas. Yeehaw!
