  • Vay (Remote Mobility): CEO Thomas von der Ohe is utilizing "teledriving," where remote drivers deliver EVs to customers.
      • Targeting Ownership: Vay aims to replace private car ownership rather than just ride-hailing, targeting a market 10x the size of the current taxi industry.
  • Verne (European Robo-taxis): Launched Europe’s first commercial robo-taxi service in Zagreb, Croatia, using software from China’s Pony.ai.

Global Competition and Vertical Integration

  • BYD’s Integration Power: Executive VP Stella Li highlights BYD’s unique position as a vertically integrated manufacturer that controls both the "heart" (EV hardware) and the "brain" (software).
      • Internal R&D: The company has invested in 5,000 software engineers to keep autonomous development in-house while partnering with NVIDIA for advanced chipsets.
  • Regional Regulatory Shifts:
      • United States: Currently holds the lead in regulatory frameworks and large-scale robo-taxi rollouts.
      • China: Recently paused new autonomous licenses following safety concerns in the robo-taxi sector (specifically regarding Baidu’s Apollo Go fleet).
      • Europe: Catching up in the freight sector, with regulators increasingly "leaning in" to autonomous trucking frameworks.

Notable Quotes

"Instead [of a detector for every object], we don't tell the car how to behave. We simply say, 'Hey, here's the end outcome you need, let the data speak for itself, learn how to drive in this way.'" — Alex Kendall, CEO of Wayve
"It's the driver, the simulator and the critic: onboard components that prioritize safety... that’s what it takes to drive." — Srikanth Thirumalai, Waymo
"What we are doing different is... the brain is autonomous driving software, but then you have the heart which is hardware. BYD is the only company where we integrate software and heart and the hardware together." — Stella Li, EVP of BYD
"We've taken the cab out from day one and built the vehicle to be autonomous from the get-go... there's no safety driver in the vehicle." — Roozbeh Charli, CEO of Einride
You are watching Bloomberg Tech Europe. Coming up, the self-driving race is back on, but the industry is split on the road ahead. There are three competing approaches: systems that use mapping and lidar, those that rely on end-to-end AI, and hybrid setups that combine both. Will it be winner take all, or is there space for multiple players? Wayve versus Waymo: a David and Goliath battle of two visions fighting for the future of autonomous driving. Our tech stack focuses not just on the driver, but also ensuring that all of the plans the driver generates—it's Gen AI of course, so it generates plans—but you have to make sure that those plans that it generates are safe. Building an end-to-end AI system for driving requires a completely different approach, and this is something that we've spent the last decade building from the ground up. We speak to Srikanth Thirumalai at Waymo and Alex Kendall at Wayve and Stella Li of BYD, plus two more companies rewriting the rules of self-driving. Welcome to Bloomberg Tech Europe, I'm Tom Mackenzie. Two companies then, two very different bets on autonomous driving. That's where we start today. Waymo is leading today; Wayve AI is banking on what comes next. Waymo is combining multiple sensors then—lidar, radar, cameras, and detailed maps—and a step-by-step driving system. Wayve AI, by the way, uses just cameras and a single AI model trained to drive from data. That means that Waymo is already on the road today but needs maps and higher-cost hardware and rolls out city by city. Wayve is still testing but doesn't need maps and uses cheaper hardware and could therefore scale much faster. Both will launch in London later this year going head-to-head. Waymo works now, Wayve could work everywhere. The future may lie somewhere in between. So we begin with Wayve, the UK-based company set to launch public road trials of fully autonomous vehicles in the UK this year in partnership with Uber. I spoke to founder and CEO Alex Kendall about why he believes Wayve's approach will define the next era of autonomy. Building an end-to-end AI system for driving requires a completely different approach to thinking about safety, to thinking about infrastructure, simulation, data, even the embedded architecture you put on the car. And this is something that we've spent the last decade building from the ground up. And what we're really excited about now is that we've got, it feels like, the entire weight of the industry behind this. And it's really interesting because there are those different approaches to autonomous driving and partially autonomous driving. There's Waymo which is more reliant, as you said, on mapping and on lidar and radar as well as the sensors. And then there's Tesla with its own approach which seems at least from the outside slightly closer to the approach that Wayve AI is taking. And then there are the Chinese competitors as well, like Baidu and Pony AI, which may be arguably slightly closer to Wayve. Do you think ultimately this is a winner takes all market, or is there room and space for different approaches? You can sell your own cars, that's what Tesla's doing, but that limits you to only your brand and your fleet. And of course, there's so much of a variety in consumer choice. The second approach which Waymo's taking is to build your own fleets. I mean, that requires a very capital-intensive operation to go and grow out city by city. We've chosen a third business model, and that's to license this technology to fleets and manufacturers out there. Now sure, these three business models will coexist, but we've chosen a business model that we believe is going to be the largest because the majority of fleets or manufacturers of robots, whether that's vehicles or in other physical industries beyond cars, are going to find a more efficient and a more effective way to partner with an embodied AI platform. The AI we build can scale and have the economic and safety leverage of the entire industry that we work with. The data that we're able to absorb and provide, I think, provide a safer and more cost-effective solution, a more performance solution than what any manufacturer can build on their own. When I spoke to Waymo about their approach, they said that what lidar and what radar gives them along with the other capabilities they have are redundancies and that that makes their system safer. Are they right? I think that's a complex and nuanced question because it really depends on the product you're building. Some products will be better maybe camera-only, some radar, some lidar. It really depends. We believe that different sensor configurations are going to be beneficial for different products. Because we want to deploy autonomy in any vehicle anywhere, therefore we need to support them all. And that's the foundation model we've built, a physical AI model that can drive with camera-only systems like some of these cars, some of them have radar, or some of the products that we're building have lidar. And it really depends. But the key thing in all of these products is that it's built for scale. And your business approach is quite different to Tesla's business approach, but in terms of the technology it seems at least from the outside that they're quite similar. What are the key technological differences between what you're offering and what Tesla's offering? Today ourselves and Tesla have a similar benchmark in performance of safety, but we've built that with a fraction of the data and compute that we see in that solution. And now as we scale up data and compute, as we start receiving data from large-scale consumer fleets from our partners around the world, we are going to scale up. Alex, you're a Kiwi running a global company out of London. Do you feel the weight of pressure of building a globally scalable successful tech company out of the UK, out of Europe? You know, actually sometimes I feel like there's a bit of a "hold it close" mentality and what I want to be able to do is actually break out and build a global success story. I don't just want to be a success in the local market. I really think of Wayve as a global company and I want to compete and lead the frontier on a global setting. And going back to what motivates me, this is an adventure. This is the chance to push the frontier both technically and now commercially. So I think it's a huge privilege to be where we are today and to have the opportunity to go do this. Speaking of opportunities, we got to experience a Wayve-powered car first-hand. Right, welcome to our robo-taxi. There is something unique to London's pretty ancient road system. To what extent does that challenge the way that your models are trained or give you an advantage? Starting in London, we were out of the thought bubble of Silicon Valley where you saw dozens of autonomy companies all spurning from the DARPA Grand Challenge and the early Google project, that were all building with the same technology strategy. And to be out of that ecosystem gave us the space to go build something contrarian and build a different approach that wouldn't have been rejected by the industry there. And then secondly, London is a driving environment that is so complex that almost forced us to take this more scalable approach because mapping and driving with remote systems where you've got challenges on your connectivity infrastructure, or London is a 2000-year-old city, so you've got way more roadworks than you have in San Francisco. I think about 20 times more roadworks. You've got about 10 times more cyclists and pedestrians than San Francisco, and then of course a lot rather than protected traffic lights, a lot more merging roundabout interaction scenes. And so all of this adds up to a much more complex autonomy challenge that requires a rethink and a scalable approach. Am I right in thinking our safety driver hasn't yet touched the wheel? Oh, they won't on this drive. So you've got the model—the model is actually on the chip stack in the car. The computer is actually on the device. Yeah, that's right. So we have an AI onboard the vehicle that's currently driving this car with six cameras and one radar. It is a very low cost, hundreds of dollars compute and sensor stack. This is the kind of stack that can go on mass-market vehicles. Hundreds of dollars? Yeah, and we're driving around the complex streets of King's Cross in North London. We've got some cyclists on our left, we've got traffic in front of us, we've got a diversion in front of us, and it's driving with all the everything decisions made onboard using onboard intelligence. Is it—the cameras are picking up on indications like whether a car is indicating or not, how does a scenario like that work? This is the transformative approach that we've taken. Traditional autonomy companies would have built an indicator detector, a car detector, a traffic light detector, and then had some logic or learned systems that say how to reason about all of these things. In scenario A do scenario B. Exactly. And you can deploy billions of dollars and create such a system but it's very complex and unwieldy. Instead, we don't tell the car how to behave. We simply say, "Hey, here's the end outcome you need, let the data speak for itself, learn how to drive in this way and figure out what's the right way to represent the world." And we do this by learning a world model and that world model is able to reason and predict how things are going to unfold, but we don't tell it what features to look out for, it just learns. Turning in, no mishaps, very smooth. Fantastic, yeah, very smooth. I felt there was—this was an even more confident version to the version that I joined you in maybe two years ago, a year and a half ago. Good stuff. I mean it just keeps getting better by orders of magnitude every year. Wayve CEO Alex Kendall. Now the counterpoint then: Waymo and its full stack approach. A rules-based system using mapping, lidar, radar and cameras. A platform they claim is safer. I spoke to Srikanth Thirumalai about how that approach is already delivering on the road. There is the obvious which is the driver, the software that runs the car. But there are two other very important pieces of the tech stack. One of course is the simulator, which is this virtual environment where you can test the car before you deploy it on the roads. And then there is a critic, which is a piece of software that's able to detect suboptimal performance in the simulator or in the real world. Because then you can hone in on those suboptimalities and then improve the driver and then verify that those fixes have indeed worked in the simulator. So it's really the driver, the simulator and the critic: that triad in our tech stack that allows us to scale. You have the full stack when it comes to autonomous vehicles at Waymo. That's right. But some of your competitors are going for a very different approach, the kind of "brain only" approach, the end-to-end machine learning approach without as much hardware, without necessarily those three different components. What gives you conviction that your approach, that the Waymo approach, ends up winning out? Our tech stack focuses not just on the driver, but also ensuring that all of the plans the driver generates—it's Gen AI of course so it generates plans—but you have to make sure that those plans that it generates are safe. Right, so you need to build in safety checks in real time so you can make sure the driver is prioritizing safety. And so it's the driver, the simulator and the critic: onboard components that prioritize safety, which is an onboard validation layer, an independent validation layer to ensure that the driver does the right thing. And when you look at all of that, that's what it takes to drive. Because there will be some who say that Waymo is winning the present, but Wayve AI, Tesla, maybe others, end up winning the future. I think for us, what we have seen is that the system allows us to learn fast and learn the right solutions to some of these problems. We call—we talk about this Waymo flywheel inside the company, which is this continuous learning loop where we take all of the experience that we've gained and every additional mile of experience, whether it's on the real road or in the simulator. And we take all of those learnings: we discover, we learn, we train our models, we simulate them, we validate it, we deploy it, and then we collect more miles which is more experience. It's that continuous learning loop, that flywheel, that we build around these three crucial components that allows us to scale. Do you think ultimately the two approaches converge? Those pursuing the end-to-end machine learning approach end up putting some lidar, some radar onto their systems to have that backup when it comes to safety as you start to pair back some of the hardware to meet those cost differentials. Do the two systems start to converge at some point? I can't predict the future, but I think so. Right, because in the end we're all solving the same problem. We want to drive safely and we want to have good solutions to the challenges that we face along the way. If Tesla cracks L4, fully autonomous, and then they ship across that fleet that they have, how much of a competitive challenge would that be? We're always looking at competitors and Tesla is a formidable competitor. And so, you know, I think competition is good. But if there is that breakthrough moment, we'll be right there with it. The Waymo approach of course being explained, and it is something of a David and Goliath. Head to head, this is going to be taking place here in London between Waymo and Wayve AI. Coming up, from autonomous trucks to remotely driven cars, we're going to spotlight more of the novel tech being harnessed to improve mobility. We're going to hear from Vay CEO Thomas von der Ohe and Einride's Roozbeh Charli. Two very different approaches. That's coming up next, this is Bloomberg. Welcome back to Bloomberg Tech Europe. A quick snapshot now of some of the recent developments in autonomous driving. Europe's first commercial robo-taxi service has started in Croatia, with customers able to book rides for a fraction of the cost of a regular taxi. Local startup Verne operates a fleet of 10 electric vehicles in Zagreb, the country's capital. The cars are equipped with autonomous driving software from China's Pony.ai. And Bloomberg understands China has paused new autonomous vehicle licenses following safety concerns in its robo-taxi sector. The move follows a disruption involving Baidu's Apollo Go fleet in Wuhan. Regulators are now calling for tighter oversight and a full review of existing pilot programs. A Baidu representative didn't respond to a request for comment. And staying with China, in just a few years BYD has gone from upstart to the world's leading EV maker outselling even Tesla. Now it's targeting its next frontier: autonomous driving. Executive Vice President Stella Li told me the company is well positioned to replicate its success. We have the integration. So what we are doing different is if you are thinking about autonomous driving, the EV vehicle is the human being. You have the brain which is autonomous driving software, but then you have the heart which is hardware. BYD is the only company where we integrate software and the heart and the hardware together. So then if we want to do more autonomous driving, then it's one control center to going to to tell you how to move the finger, how to move your feet. Then we can dance well. But a lot of people, they ask to do the hardware for autonomous driving from one company, but the intelligent driving experience from another company. They need to integrate together. And then software from other people. But we do everything in-house, we integrate everything in-house. So this is the power for BYD. Then at the same time we have, like, we invest 5,000 software engineers to doing all the autonomous driving software. And then at the same time we are an open platform. We can work with the best computing company, which is software, and we can work with the best chipset company. For example we use NVIDIA, the most advanced chipset they just announced like one month ago, and then integrate into our system. And then we can also always use the best intelligent driving experience company with voice control, everything into our platform. BYD Executive Vice President Stella Li. Meanwhile, Swedish autonomy freight firm Einride is preparing for a US listing in the first half of 2026. It is expanding rapidly, deploying 75 electric trucks with Amazon across five US states. Here's one of them, you might notice something unusual: no cab. What you see here is our generation two. It's a rigid design to carry pallets for our customers. As you can also see in the front here, as opposed to what you would be used to seeing at a truck which would be a cab and a seat for drivers—there is no cab. Exactly. So we've taken the cab out from day one and built the vehicle to be autonomous from the get-go. To be clear, there is no safety driver here. There's no safety driver in the vehicle. I spoke to CEO Roozbeh Charli about the future of autonomous trucking and what it could mean for jobs. Einride is at the core a freight technology company that helps our customers, who are some of the world's largest shippers, to sort of transition their fleets from where they are today with diesel manual-based transportation into electric and autonomous. And we do that through the deployment of our platform model which sort of sits in the middle and orchestrates all the different parts and the different technologies required to drive that transition and help them throughout that journey. We deploy our sort of electric manual trucks for the first step in terms of electrification and our autonomous vehicles for the automation of their of their transportation. Where does Europe stand when it comes to autonomy in the freight sector? Is Europe leading, and if so in what parts of the space is Europe leading, and where is it challenged, where is it falling behind? I mean if you look at what's happening on the autonomous side in general, freight and robo-taxis, I think what you've seen over the past 18, 24 months is the US taking a clear leadership position in terms of the regulatory environment. You see mass at-scale rollouts of robo-taxis in US cities, and regulatory frameworks leaning in especially on the state legislature side. So I think you're seeing that, but that's also creating a pull effect I would say with Europe. We're working across a number of European countries with the regulators in terms of autonomous regulation and deployment and we're seeing the same inward-leaning sort of attitude in a European context as well. So I think Europe is sort of seeing the opportunity that exists within freight and robo-taxis, but specifically on the freight side. So I think we'll see continued success and Europe sort of catching up to the US in terms of that regulatory environment. Big questions right now in terms of AI disruption and AI displacement of jobs. Do you face pushback, do you face political pressure in terms of the displacement of jobs in this sector? I would say the way that we've thought about our autonomous technology is that you're always going to need to have a human in the loop for edge cases, for interaction at a loading bay for example or other places. So I think when you think about automating freight, you only think about the autonomous technology. But automating freight is about so much more that comes into it: setting up the entire operational workflow and operational flow. And so if you look at how we're deploying autonomous technology today, our vehicles are fully autonomous and are driving autonomously when they're driving, but they're being supervised by a human in the loop sitting at a remote station far away from the vehicle in an office setting. They are supervising that transportation, helping the vehicle take tactical decisions for example in edge cases or interact with humans as required. So I think it's once again that gradual process and actually transitioning jobs into a different set of jobs is how we think about this transition to autonomous. Einride chief executive Roozbeh Charli. Meanwhile, German startup Vay is taking a different route to mobility. Its model: remote drivers deliver EVs booked via an app. Customers take the wheel, then a remote driver retrieves the car at the end of the trip. CEO Thomas von der Ohe says the approach could reshape urban transport and even challenge private car ownership. Why would you want to own a car that you have to pay insurance for, that you have to charge, and that you have to take maintenance of, if instead you can just click a button and get an electric car that you don't have to charge and it's similarly priced down the line as your private personal car. And that's where the massive business opportunity comes from a business perspective because we're not only going after the ride-hailing rides which can be 3, 4, 5 percent of trips in cities, but we actually really want to be seen as the company that replaced private car ownership. That is the vast, vast majority of trips in the cities. And hence if you think about the market opportunity, we believe this to be approximately 10x the size of ride-hailing at full scale. Vay CEO Thomas von der Ohe then on potentially replacing private car ownership with their model of autonomy. Now don't miss the next episode of Bloomberg Tech Europe, it's going to air on June the 12th here on Bloomberg Television. You can also watch on the terminal, on the YouTube platform as well, and of course on bloomberg.com/video. That is it for Bloomberg Tech Europe. The Pulse is up next. This is Bloomberg.

Watch CNBC's full interview with Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee

CNBC Television(3.4M)·10:12·5.9K views·2026-05-08
Austan GoolsbeePresident, Chicago Fed
TL;DRChicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee warns that while the labor market remains stable, persistent and rising inflation—particularly in services—is a major concern that may require the Federal Reserve to maintain a restrictive stance and re-evaluate forward guidance.

TL;DR

Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee warns that while the labor market remains stable, persistent and rising inflation—particularly in services—is a major concern that may require the Federal Reserve to maintain a restrictive stance and re-evaluate forward guidance.

Labor Market: "Stable Without Being Good"

  • Low-Hire, Low-Fire Environment: Goolsbee describes the current job market as stagnant rather than deteriorating.
      • Hiring Rates: Current hiring levels are comparable to the depths of a recession.
      • Layoff Rates: Conversely, layoff rates remain at historic lows typical of an economic boom.
  • Overall Stability: Despite the "cross-current" in data, Goolsbee notes that unemployment (4.3%) and vacancy rates have remained steady for over a year, suggesting the market is not "falling apart."

Inflation Concerns and the Dual Mandate

  • The "Wrong Way" Trend: Goolsbee expressed significant concern that inflation has moved away from the Fed's 2% target over the last three months after five years of being elevated.
  • Service Sector Pressure: While energy and tariffs contribute to price hikes, Goolsbee highlighted that services inflation is uncomfortably high and not driven by temporary external shocks.
  • Addressing Stagflationary Risks:
      • Framework: In the event of threats to both employment and price stability, the Fed must determine which side is deviating more and the projected duration of that deviation.
      • Policy Options: Goolsbee emphasized that "everything should always be on the table," suggesting a stern response might be necessary if inflation expectations become unanchored.

Monetary Policy and Forward Guidance

  • Skepticism of "Jawboning": Goolsbee remains skeptical of using forward guidance (words) to influence markets when interest rates are not at the "zero lower bound."
  • The "Data Dog" Approach: He advocates for a comprehensive review of all economic indicators—including food, energy, and private sector data—rather than focusing strictly on core inflation.
      • Quote Reference: He expressed agreement with Chair-designate Kevin Warsh’s view on utilizing a diverse set of inflation measures.

AI and Productivity Risks

  • Short-Term Overheating: While AI promises long-term productivity gains, Goolsbee warns against "counting chickens before they hatch."
  • The Wealth Effect: High expectations for AI can drive up stock market wealth, leading high-end consumers to increase spending before actual economic potential is realized.
  • Supply Chain Bottlenecks: Massive investment in AI infrastructure is already creating "crowding out" effects, such as:
      • Skyrocketing land prices.
      • Shortages of skilled labor (e.g., electricians).
      • Increased competition for computer chips.

Notable Quotes

"I'd characterize it as we've been stable without being good... The hiring rate is low enough it would correspond to kind of the depths of a recession, but at the same time, the layoff rate is as low as would be at the peak of a boom." — Austan Goolsbee
"I want to be one of the data dogs, and one of the main rules of the data dogs is sniff every piece of data that hits the floor because it might be food." — Austan Goolsbee
"Expectations of future productivity growth, the more hyped they are, the more they can contribute to inflation in the short run and they can force the hand of the central bank." — Austan Goolsbee
We're joined from California by Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee. Austan, thanks for joining us this morning. Just so much to talk about. Yeah, great to see you, Steve. Hey, let's start off with the jobs report. It came in quite above expectations, unemployment rate remaining stable at 4.3%. What's your take, overall take on the job market? Yeah, pretty much stable for a year, year and a half. I characterize it that we've been stable without being good. It's if you look at the rates, the unemployment rate's been stable, the hiring rate's been stable, the layoff rate's been stable, the vacancy rate's been stable. So, I still think there's not a lot of evidence that the job market is falling apart. And on the other side of the Fed's mandate, inflation hasn't been great and it's been going the wrong way lately. I want to get to inflation more specifically, but what's not good about the job market? You've been above 100,000 for a couple months in a row. You're at a relatively low unemployment rate. The part that's not good, go ahead, is the hiring rate. I mean, the hiring rate is—we're in a low-hire, low-fire environment, as they say. The hiring rate is low enough, it would correspond to kind of the depths of a recession. But at the same time, the layoff rate is as low as would be at the peak of a boom. So, we're getting a little bit of a cross-current in the data. But it's been stable. That's the thing that I want to emphasize. It's not, to me, there's not a lot of evidence that the job market is deteriorating any time on a rapid basis. All right, let's go to the other side of the mandate now, the inflation side. It has been elevated. Is it all energy and are you willing just to look through it, or is there a more stern response from the central bank required here? Well, that's two questions. The second one let's hold off for a second. I don't think it's just energy. Energy's adding onto it. It's more than just that inflation is elevated. It was already elevated before the war, then the war increased oil prices, now it's even more elevated. I think for me, the most concerning part is that if you look at services inflation, that's not coming from tariffs, that's not coming from oil prices. It's for several months in a row now been higher than is comfortable and going the wrong way. And to pile that on, we've been above the 2% Fed target for five years now. We stopped making progress last year and now in the last three months it's going up instead of down. We gotta just keep an eye on this because if everybody starts presuming that inflation rates are going back to something like what they were a few years ago, we would be in a bit of a pickle as a central bank. Fed Chair nominee says, as repeated several times, inflation is a choice. And I'm assuming by that metric that you guys are making the wrong choice if inflation is continuously above target. Are you thinking about whether or not you need to make a choice to address that inflation? Yeah, look, as I say, and Steve, you've been there to the room where the FOMC meeting takes place. It's literally the biggest table that I've ever seen in my life. So there's plenty of room on the table. Everything should always be on the table. That said, we outlined as a committee—if we faced threats to both sides of the mandate at the same time, we voted unanimously on a monetary policy framework. That said if we start facing that kind of stagflationary shock, we're going to look at which side is deviating more and how long we think the deviations are going to last. And you're seeing problems on the inflation side, but there is an argument that these should be one-time increases in prices and not lasting inflation shocks. That's what we're trying to figure out. That we're piling an oil one on to the tariff one before the tariff one went away, I'm a little concerned about. But for me, that idea that let's figure out not just which one is getting worse but how long do we think it's going to last, that's a core of what I'm trying to figure out. Austan, do you think that the Fed ought to remove that guidance which people say is the guidance that suggests the next move is a rate cut, and will it matter if you move that guidance? Is that essentially—should it be seen in your mind as a tightening of policy? And Steve, as you know, before I ever got to the Fed a little over three years ago, I was a little skeptical about the use of forward guidance in the statement as a way of making policy. I can understand it when you're at the zero lower bound so you really can't make policy. But we're not at the zero lower bound by any means. So I'm not—I've never been that big of a fan of trying to use words to jawbone policy decisions. Hi, President Goolsbee, it's Sarah. How are you guys judging inflation right now? Is—are you looking only at core? Are you stripping out food and energy and can you just talk us through that? We're going to get CPI on Tuesday. I mean, what people pay for is food and energy. So I am curious how you're thinking about it. Yeah, no, look, as I say, I understand why in general the Fed tends to look at core because it gives you a—it's not as variable and it gives you a little bit of a better insight into what's a through line, and that's the most important thing is what's a through line. But the fact that that is mostly what we look at drives my mom crazy, drives a bunch—what do you mean you don't look at energy and food prices? That's all we look at. I think you're never going to hear me say throw out any data. We should look at food and energy and core. We should within core look at services separate from housing separate from goods. And we should be incorporating all the private sector information that we can about prices. I'm just—I want to be one of the data dogs and one of the main rules of the data dogs is sniff every piece of data that hits the floor because it might be food. And I think that's what we should do. And I thought in his hearing, Chair designate Warsh said something that sounded like that—that let's look at a lot of different measures of inflation rather than just key on one and I strongly agree with that. I'm also curious how you think the massive amount of spending into AI and building out the AI infrastructure is influencing inflation. Some people say it's deflationary, it's boosting productivity, but right now, I mean there's a lot of money going into building this all out and it's not just, you know, in high tech, it's in the industrial part of the economy as well. Yeah, you're not wrong about that. And just look, on your previous segment, there was that lovely phrase, "Is the AI trade superseding the economy trade?" I want us to just be a little careful at moments like that. If what's happening with AI is there's going to be a huge increase in productivity, but it's not here yet, the more people expect it and hype it, the bigger the threat that we could overheat the economy in the short run. As everybody's trying to pull forward where we get a wealth effect—that consumers are going to spend out of this, high-end consumers who have a big increase in stock market wealth are going to increase their consumption and their consumer spending before the economic potential and the bounty of this thing has arrived in the economy. We gotta be—we have to be careful about that. Yeah, so Austan, you just wrote a paper on this and I was trying to spare viewers from having to talk about it but—thanks a lot Steve. Yeah, yeah, well, it's an interesting academic paper if you're into that sort of thing. But if I could if I could sum it up, it seems like when it—what you're saying is when it comes to monetary policy, the thing you're saying is don't count your chickens before they hatch. And so you sound like you are—would be reluctant to incorporate potential productivity gains on the front end of monetary policy now, but not necessarily on the back end if they show up. And it's also important how they show up. Yes, I think all of those statements are—I agree with. If what happens is like the mid-1990s where we get productivity growth and it wasn't expected and people didn't change their behavior in the expectation that they were going to get these productivity gains, I think inflation goes down and the natural thing is for rates to go down. What matters is how much does the knowledge of what's coming affect our behavior. We consumers, we businesses making these investments. And the more you see this kind of crowding out—like AI data center investment driving all the non-AI industries crazy—we can't hire an electrician, we can't buy computer chips, the price of land is going through the roof—those are kind of the embodiment of the people getting excited, equity values getting ahead of getting into the counting of the chickens, and we just gotta be mindful whether that is going to lead to overheating in the short run. Expectations of future productivity growth, the more hyped they are, the more they can contribute to inflation in the short run and they can force the hand of the central bank.

Thin Harness, Fat Skills: The New Way To Build Software

Y Combinator(2.2M)·41:30·30.3K views·2026-05-08
Garry TanPresident & CEO, Y Combinator Harj TaggarGroup Partner, Y Combinator Jared FriedmanGroup Partner, Y Combinator
TL;DRAI agents have transitioned software development from a manual "writing" task to a high-leverage "director" role, enabling founders to achieve a 400x increase in output by "tokenmaxxing" and utilizing agentic workflows.
The Comeback: After a 13-year hiatus from active...

TL;DR

AI agents have transitioned software development from a manual "writing" task to a high-leverage "director" role, enabling founders to achieve a 400x increase in output by "tokenmaxxing" and utilizing agentic workflows.

The Return of the Builder: Garry Tan’s 400x Productivity Leap

  • The Comeback: After a 13-year hiatus from active coding, YC CEO Garry Tan returned to building software, shipping hundreds of thousands of lines of code and launching viral open-source projects like *gstack*.
  • Cost and Velocity Collapse:
      • In 2008, building a blogging platform (Posterous) took $4M and 1.5 years.
      • In 2024, rebuilding a more advanced version with agentic research capabilities took $200 in tokens and 5 days.
  • The 400x Output Metric: Tan calculated that by directing 15 agents simultaneously, his logical lines of code (LLOC) production is 400 times higher than his peak output in 2013.
  • The Ferrari Analogy: Tan describes using modern AI agents as "driving a Ferrari"—exhilarating and incredibly fast, but requiring the user to be a "mechanic" who can pop the hood and fix the system when it breaks down.

"Tokenmaxxing" and Agentic Engineering

  • Defining Tokenmaxxing: The philosophy of intentionally spending heavily on high-quality model calls (e.g., $500/day) to "boil the ocean" of research, context, and testing.
  • Journalistic Software: Tan's project *Garry’s List* acts as an investigative journalist rather than just a CMS. It uses APIs (Perplexity, X, Grok) to ingest dozens of sources, cross-reference arguments, and produce sourced reports.
  • Latent Space vs. Deterministic Code: Success in AI engineering depends on knowing which tasks belong in "LLM land" (handling generic cases, user intent) and which belong in "code land" (deterministic logic).
  • The "Rent" Mental Model: Spending on tokens should be viewed like San Francisco rent for a startup—it is an expensive but necessary investment to create the serendipity and utility required for high-speed growth.

The gstack Workflow: Thin Harness, Fat Skills

  • Thin Harness: The core loop that manages LLM inputs/outputs and tool calls (e.g., Claude Code).
  • Fat Skills: The high-quality markdown, prompts, and logical checklists supplied by the human "director" to guide the agent.
  • The Multi-Stage Pipeline: Tan uses a specific sequence of "skills" to ensure code quality:
      • ASCII Visualization: Forcing the AI to create data flow diagrams before coding to ensure it understands the architecture.
      • The CEO Skill: A meta-prompt inspired by Brian Chesky’s "10-star experience" that asks the AI to find a path to 10x value for only 2x effort.
      • The Eng Review: Automating the creation of unit and integration tests, reaching 80-90% coverage—a task human developers often neglect.
      • Codex/QA: Using high-IQ models to find bugs and Playwright-wrapped agents to perform automated browser-based testing of UIs.

The Future of Personal AI and Human Agency

  • Personal vs. Corporate AI: Tan argues the defining question of the era is whether individuals will control their own tools and data or be controlled by corporate-hosted algorithms (similar to a Facebook feed).
  • Open Source Golden Age: Using tools like *Conductor* and *G Brain*, developers can now "extract" logic from existing repos and use AI to build complex RAG systems in a single weekend.
  • The AI Time Dividend: AI allows busy executives to "buy back time" by utilizing machine consciousness to handle the "boring" parts of engineering (QA, boilerplate, research), leaving humans to provide taste and product intuition.
"Using OpenClaude these days is like driving a Ferrari... it’s exhilarating. It figures things out you would never think a machine could figure out... But then it's also like a Ferrari in that you better be a mechanic. You’re going to have to fix it yourself." — Garry Tan
"If you can tokenmax, it’s like... you could buy millions of years of consciousness, of machine consciousness. Now I can be a time billionaire. It’s not my own time; it’s the time of a machine doing work for me." — Garry Tan
"Markdown is actually code; it’s just compiled in a different way. You can get the computer to do really astonishing things... I don’t use Visual Studio at all. There’s no reason to when I can talk to my agent." — Garry Tan
I think that's like the defining question: will you have control over your own tools, or will your tools have control over you? Using OpenClaude these days is like driving a Ferrari, and it's like exhilarating. It's insane. Like, you get to do things... it figures things out you would never think a machine could figure out, and it does it so quickly. But then it's also like a Ferrari in that you better be a mechanic. Like, it's a Ferrari that will break down on the side of the road, you know, when you most need it, and you need to get out with your wrench and pop the hood and like fix it, you know? You're going to have to fix it yourself. And so this is a very exciting time in computer science and technology. Welcome back to a special episode of The Lightcone. In this episode, we're going to talk about how Garry Tan got back to building. If you follow us on Twitter, you'll know that after a multi-year hiatus to become an investor, Garry Tan is back to being a builder. In the last couple months, he's shipped hundreds of thousands of lines of code and built popular open-source projects that have gone from nothing to more than 100,000 stars on GitHub. And he did all of this while having a very demanding job running YC full-time. A lot of people on the internet don't even think that this is possible and are somewhat like in disbelief, but it actually happened. We know because we were here to see the whole thing. And so today, we're going to talk about how he did it. Well, I'm relatively shocked myself. I'm amazed as well. It was 13 years of not coding and then suddenly boom, I'm doing about 400x the amount of work that I was that year—the last time I was even sort of like two-thirds of the time writing code. Maybe to start things off, how about we go back to the project that started it all off, which was Garry's List, and just like talk about a few months ago how you powered up Claude Code and like started to get back to coding? And it was right after one of the Lightcone episodes, right? Oh yeah, definitely. I realized that I wanted to bring together all the people who believed what I believed, particularly for California. And so I started a 501(c)(4), and now it's a C3 and a PAC, which is sort of what a lot of political groups do. It's a very common way to bring people together. You know, everyone focuses on the money, but we're trying to bring together smart people. You know, what I learned in the years of working in San Francisco politics is that bringing together people is so powerful and that's what a mass social movement is. And I said, okay, well, why don't I just make a website where we start doing that? And it would just start with... why don't I start writing about the issues that I'm worried about? It's like, I want children in school... you know, people watching this from all around the world might find it very, very strange, like I find it strange, that it was not possible and still very, very hard for a seventh grader or eighth grader in middle school in San Francisco public schools to be able to take algebra. And that was a math education thing... like, you know, if I didn't get to do that when I was in public schools in the East Bay of the Bay Area, there's no way I would have studied engineering at Stanford, I never would have written code, I never would have been able to do any of these things. So it was close to my heart and I realized like, hey, it's time to write code and I ended up building Posterous, my first YC startup from 2008. What was Posterous for people who don't remember it? Yeah, Posterous was dead-simple blogs by email. It grew to be a top 200 website on the internet and then Twitter ended up buying it for about $20 million. So that was sort of like my first bag, really. I actually built it again as Posthaven when Twitter, you know, bought it for the amazing people that we had hired and they shut down the startup. It would have cost a couple million dollars to buy it back from Twitter and at the time I had no money in the world, so the next best thing was why don't I write it again? And then in January of this year, I ended up writing it a third time. Only... you know, the first time it took about, you know, $4 million and you know, six or seven people and about a year and a half. And then the second time it you know took about, I don't know, $100k and two people, me and my co-founder Brett Gibson, who now runs Initialized, and maybe like three months or so. And then in this case, it took about $200, which was my Claude Code Max account, and probably five days. Full-featured blog platform, does everything you want and then on top of that, like full RAG, full agentic retrieval, like be able to you know sort of go out and read all of the internet, like every tweet I've ever done, recursive crawl, deep research of any topic. The algebra thing is just one of a whole lot of different issues that we really, really care about and to be able to go ingest the internet, you know, see all the arguments for and against, and then to craft incredibly detailed reports on the back end about what are all the quotables... I think people who are big followers of the Lightcone might remember one of our first episodes about agentic systems with Jake Heller, actually. So Jake created CaseText and he described exactly what I ended up building for basically journalistic long-form articles about any you know sort of issue or piece of news that was happening. And so you know anyone can go to garryslist.org today and you know we do about two or three relatively, you know, researched, all fully sourced articles about what's going on in California, in San Francisco, in LA, and like how do we build a better government? This is the thing I feel like people missed about Garry's List or don't fully get is that it's like the classic thing we've been talking about here which is like, software was you built software to let people use it. So it was like you built a blogging platform and people like write blogs and maybe like they'd start their own Substack eventually or they like write articles, but Garry's List is both a blogging platform and it actually does the work of a high-quality investigative journalist. It's not just something that a journalist uses to publish their articles. Yeah, I mean basically for the equivalent of like $5 or $10 of Opus calls, I mean I would estimate that it does the work of like you know a real human being that would have to like go painstakingly through dozens of articles, read entire books about certain subjects, annotate them. I mean going back to the CaseText example, like the thing that Jake taught me was that you need to think about what a human would do with the context given. Like what would it retrieve, like does it go to the library, what kind of book would it look for, what does it search on for... search you know on the web? I mean the great thing now is like you don't have to just do that, like you can get Perplexity's API and you can do deep research there. You have X's API, you can do deep research there. You know, Grok's API, if you need to like do research on X, using the Grok API is actually very, very good. And you can just grab all of the context. This is sort of going back to the philosophy of boil the ocean, which is one of my essays. It's like, particularly when building agentic software now, you don't have to settle for what we did when we were humans writing the code. Like and that goes for research as well. What if you absolutely boiled the ocean? Like what is you know the total completionist... like if you were a human, this would take you about a month to do this research. You can just you know zap the rocks harder. You know, you pay more money and you might be token maxing, but you should token max. Basically if there is incremental work that makes something more complete, more awesome, more... you know, in the case of this type of writing, like we want it to be more representative of reality. Like how... you know, we don't just settle for one source when we can get 20 sources and we can cross-reference them. We can figure out like, well these 13 sources say this and the seven sources disagree with that. And then you know, you want to feed all of that context into like your core prompt and then you can basically make a better decision than what you would like just, you know, a human being clicking on a link, reading a headline and that's all you understand. And I think if you token max, like that's actually the coolest thing you can do now and it's not just in you know generating articles, it's not... you know, it's clearly in writing code, right? I think now it's... it's going to permeate every part of society. Like every thing that we would call knowledge work could be token maxed and I don't think that it means that we're going to get rid of people. I think it means that people need to still supply the agency, like I need this. Like I'm the one who's sitting here caring about algebra. Like I want kids like me who couldn't afford private school... you know, San Francisco is the one city in the world that has the highest rate of private school attendance, probably in the entire country, actually. And that's not okay. Like you shouldn't have to be rich to have a good education and you know I don't know why that's controversial. And so for me it's like this you know mass sort of shift in technology was happening and then I had a need and a want and a desire and it was a burning desire. Like it hurts me and pains me to think about 10, 12, 13-year-old kids who don't know algebra and like could have, but some bureaucrat or you know some virtue signaling person in power says like, actually I don't want that kid who wants to learn algebra to learn it. So I think in this process of basically solving your own pain and need from the young Garry and building Garry's List, you sort of discover a lot of patterns on token maxing and this new way of building that led you to the next project, which was gstack. Like I actually did not plan to make gstack. All I did was like I realized that I was doing the same things over and over again and then I got sick of typing the same thing, so I went into my Apple Notes. I typed in all the things that I found myself writing over and over again into Claude Code and it was pretty simple stuff. It's like, here's the plan review. One of the things I started doing is I really love asking Claude to make ASCII art diagrams. One of the things I discovered is sometimes Claude would just get confused and like write bugs or not be complete. But once I started saying, actually before you start your work, make an ASCII diagram of all the data flows, all the inputs and outputs, what are the user flows, what are the error messages... and you can see this. It's like data flow, state machines, dependency graphs, processing pipelines, decision trees. Once it did that, it loaded all of the context in and then it just did the work more completely. Like it boiled the ocean better. And it broke down into a bunch of different sections, like here's architecture review, code quality, test... I mean one of the things I learned building Garry's List was that when I was writing the code myself, I would always do the minimum amount of testing because it was just like not very fun. I knew I needed to have it, but I'm here to write you know fun new code, I you know did not like to write tests. And then honestly like I hit all the things that everyone else hits when they start vibe coding which is like, this is slop, it's not working that well. Like it works fine for the 80% case, but if any users actually touch it, it starts falling over. And then that's when I realized, oh I can get to 100% test coverage. I've since learned that 100% is probably too much, like hitting 80% to 90% is usually the best practice at this point. but yeah, this is basically the first version of plan-eng-review. I know everyone knows the office hour skill, which is you know what people can use and I still use when I'm trying to make a brand new product or a brand new feature. It simulates what a... what we do when we're working with a company. It's like how do you know that people want this? You know, who's it for, what does it do and what's the impact, right? But this is like the proto-skill, like this is... I didn't even know skills existed and I posted this and it went viral, like you know 200,000 people saw that. And then I made another version of it that was a much more expansive version, I called it the Mega Plan, and then I ended up renaming it to the CEO Plan. We've probably talked about meta-prompting before, I used meta-prompting here. I took the other review plan that we had and then I said okay, well let's do a version of this but like imagine Brian Chesky sitting with you, right? Like Brian Chesky has this great line about what is a 10-star experience? So and you know the point of it is everyone thinks about hotels in terms of like three... this is a two, three-star experience, this is a four-star experience and he like goes you know through the list like five stars, it's like everyone you know yeah cool, like he's like what's a six star? And what's a seven star? And what's an eight star? And like he goes all through that entire list. And that's one of my favorite like product and design exercises to go through like as a mental exercise. And then the cool thing is like you can do that every single time now. And so that's what this is. You know, this prompt basically tries to figure out what is the platonic ideal of what this is. These are sort of like the three... the two things that are pretty awesome. One is what is the 10x check? What is more ambitious and delivers 10x more value for only 2x the effort? Right? And so for whatever reason coming out of latent space, it helps the model like really visualize... like so I'm Plan CEO skill I actually really enjoy because I'm an ADHD CEO and I love potential, like pure potential. And so this is like the one... like I can't believe this is just literally two little sentences, but like this unlocks an incredible amount. And so that's how gstack started actually not as you know I didn't want it to be anything other than well I just need to make some skills and I had heard that people were making like skill repos. So one of the cool things that I actually learned from YC alum, I came to an event and brain totally frazzled, but you know went to one of our batch events and we're just you know shooting the shit about what's going on with Claude Code versus Codex. At the time I was a total Claude Code only guy and I realized oh a lot of people actually prefer Codex. Why is that? I discovered that Claude Code is ideal for the ADHD CEO, but once in a while there's a... you know, Claude Code will just BS a bunch of stuff. Like Claude models are very, very good, but like they are not the smartest, it turns out. And so a lot of people you know explained to me that if you have a problem that's much crazier, you need the 200 IQ nearly non-verbal CTO. So you can just call in a friend and then that's what like /codex is. It's a gstack skill that takes whatever plan is your plan is or if you're out of plan mode and you already implemented, it'll take your repo and it'll run Codex in a command line prompt with the prompt that says find all the problems and all the bugs and it reports it back to Claude Code and then you and Claude Code can work through those feed... that feedback. And then I have since added if you use Codex as your main coding agent you can actually go and type /claude and have Claude come and be the CEO briefly if you want as well. Yeah, I mean some of it came out of being trolled on the internet relentlessly about markdown and like I you know I'm just like peddling a markdown set of markdown. And it's like you know I guess my lived experience at this point is that markdown is actually code, it's just like this compiled in a different way. But like you can get the computer to do really astonishing things. Like I mean even this, it's like could we have imagined that I would be talking to something that has replaced Visual Studio? For like for I don't use Visual Studio at all, like there's no reason to like when I can talk to my agent and my agent can do this, right? The article actually name—the name actually came from our partner Pete Koomen. We have had to build an internal agent and you know we call that the Harness over and over again. And then at some point using Claude Code all day we realized like you know why should we rewrite a version of that over and over again. Like you know we should just use the things that are really awesome as you know harnesses. Like a harness is the core loop that takes the user input, gives it to the LLM, runs what the LLM does, like it can do tool calls and things like that. I mean why would we build that? Like what we should be spending all our time doing is thinking about what markdown should there be and the way to think about markdown is if you were an event planner and throwing a wedding and you were trying to write down a checklist of how to throw a wedding again, like what would you what would you write in plain English to teach the next person who had to do it what to do. All of that should be in the markdown. Whereas all the things that should be deterministic—like I mean or is a real action. Like a wedding planner might have to call like 20 venues, right? But you wouldn't use markdown for that, like you would make a you know a call to Twilio for instance, right? There's like a lot you know sort of all of the difficulty in agentic engineering today is when people try to do things that should be in markdown in code and it fails because code is brittle, it doesn't understand special cases, it doesn't actually you know code literally doesn't understand what you want or who you are. It is like you know executing deterministic zeros and ones in a Turing-complete loop, right? Like it doesn't know. But then now we have LLMs that have latent space and they know who you are and it knows what your motivations are and it can handle generic cases. And then you know a lot of the the magic right now as an engineer is like figuring out okay how much of it is over here in LLM land and how much of it is over there in code land. And then you know if you combine that with the other thing I learned which is like get to 80% to 90% tests. Like if it's not tested and you're just throwing users in there, like it's slop, you know 10x worse than like human-written code because like you just have no idea what's going to happen. and so that's like one of the things that people have to do. It's like all right, not only do you need to figure out what's going on in latent space and deterministic space, you also have to make sure that like it's you know unit individually tested and then the integration is tested. And then going back to boil the ocean, like the machine doesn't care, it'll just do it. It's amazing. Like just zap the rocks more and you can get to 90% test coverage and then you can have a system that you know is not quite perfect like you know OpenClaude right now there are lots of like failure cases, but it's 95% there. Like you get to do things... it figures things out you would never think a machine could figure out and it does it so quickly. But then it's also like a Ferrari in that you better be a mechanic. And so this is a very exciting time in computer science and technology because it's like this is Homebrew Computer Club, you know the moment when the Apple I came out. Like the Apple I created by Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak was a breadboard inside like literally a wooden case hammered together with like nails and duct tape, you know? Yeah this might be like a good quick aside to talk about like this this idea of lines of code being an important measure has been like controversial on the internet. There's obviously the counterargument like oh lines of code doesn't like measure developer productivity but why do you think it... It doesn't, right? But it also does. It also kind of does, right? Yeah, like it doesn't... it's clearly and you know what's interesting is you can actually there are well-published git repos out there that you can run to strip away and like standardize what is actual logical lines of code. And so I actually did go ahead and do that you know and I got into trouble for saying like oh I'm coding at like 100x the rate that I was in 2013. And then after I did the logical lines of code strip down it actually went up. It actually went up. So it turns out that I was actually doing 400x the amount of code. But you know obviously I wasn't writing it, I was directing you know 15 agents at a time to do so and then by the numbers like it was not that it did like knock down my lines of code from Claude Code a little bit. But the surprising thing to me was that it knocked down the amount of lines of code that I was writing in 2013 by like 70%. And so I think that that's sort of the mismatch here, like people get very upset because it's easy to like pad the lines of code if you're a human writing code. Whereas like unless you direct Claude Code to literally like pad the lines of code, it doesn't necessarily do that. Like it'll maybe build the wrong thing, like you might not steer it very well, it might not do the right thing, but like it's not trying to optimize for lines of code the way a human working a job would, right? Which is you know that's just life. And then I guess the really surprising thing is if you look at the literature about software engineering going back to like 2000, 1990, I mean it's pretty clear that the average number of lines of code that a professional software engineer that's like tested and production ready, it's not like 100 lines of code. It's like 50. It's like 30. Like a day. Yeah, a day, right? Like for me it was like 14 but I was like part-time. I don't know. It's so that's where the 400x actually came from. Like that's one of the things we learned from him too. It's like unless you have your own prompts and you can write it for yourself, like you are you know below the API line for some PM or developer that is not you who like will not understand you, will not understand your needs, will not understand what you uniquely care about. And I think that's like the defining question, like, will you have control over your own tools, or will your tools have control over you? And I think this is the one of the disconnects that the public has, I think, is a lot of these capabilities you have to be on the latest and greatest models and it's actually quite expensive to use them and burn all the tokens for now. It's coming down, but I think maybe people are just trying like Sonnet or the free model or having the basic Claude Pro subscription only. Yeah and part of it is maybe we have to address that this new way of really getting all these almost ASI AGI moment for for building is you have to be burning lots of the token, the whole token maxing paradigm. It actually reminds me of rent, San Francisco rent. Like one of the things that I feel like we always have to do with YC founders is that it's just like a general thing. It's like, oh like I don't want to move to San Francisco because it's like so expensive to live there but it's like... It's so expensive to not live there. Yeah, exactly. That's the whole point, right? Like early on in a YC batch like I'm just used to like a founder being like, like this apartment is like thousands of dollars a month in rent like seems ridiculous like should I like pay it or not? And it's like, no, you should absolutely pay it and if anything you should pay more to not just be in San Francisco, but being in like the Dogpatch and just like being in neighborhoods where you create this serendipity. Token maxing is going to be one of those things for founders that we sort of have to teach them where it's not immediately obvious that you shouldn't... this is actually like rent. Like this is one of the things where you should like spend as much as you can to like get the like most utility out of it versus treating it like the office desk or something. Like sure, you can economize on that or you don't need like a super expensive like couch but like when it comes to like actually using the models and your token spend, you should probably be like pushing pretty hard on that. Yeah, one of the key maxims for YC is you know how do you find good startup ideas: live in the future and build what's missing, right? And so this is a profound version of that where all you have to do is commit your brain to look at you know spending $500 in a single day on tokens and say actually like you know as long as I'm building something that's actually of great value to me or you know and I'm building the right thing I'm going to do that. Hey Garry, I have a weird question. Do you think that in some ways the fact that you tried to build all of this while also being the CEO of Y Combinator actually helped you? Because like your time is so scarce, you had to like try to figure out how to write hundreds of thousands of lines of code with just like spare minutes in between meetings unlike a a full a full-time software engineer that could you know just take the time to like open the website and like click around it like to test it. Like those minutes were like insanely, insanely scarce for you and so you were constantly pushing yourself to figure out how to automate everything. Yeah, I envy time billionaires, you know, sometimes look at I mean I look at my kids and it's like these kids are time billionaires right now, man. Like you know you know you run across people at Startup School all the time and it's like, you're a time billionaire right now, like this is incredible, like you could just do any you like learn about anything, this is so great. So yeah, I mean you know personally like I think my philosophy is I am in a crazy rush. In my brain I'm like probably live 10 billion lifetimes in this body right now and I need every single moment to count. and then if you can token max, it's like I mean you could buy millions of years of consciousness, of machine consciousness. Now I can be a time billionaire. It's not, you know, my own time. It's the time of a machine like doing work for me and like the human entities that I care about working on the causes that I care about, right? I care about YC, I care about builders being able to build. Even in a lot of our internal meetings last year, remember in our offsites, we would talk about like how do we teach the next generation how to use these tools. And so, you know, I'd like to I wish that I could say like that was all a part of the grand plan and that's how it started. It's not, like but you know, subconsciously I actually think it was. Like I think subconsciously from doing Lightcone and like talking about this stuff, like sitting side-by-side with Boris Cherny right here was a very powerful moment for me because I realized like he he started saying things that like I could do myself. It's like he said our team doesn't write a single line of code. I'm like oh actually like I can do that. And like the people who are watching right now, it's like you and I are not different, right? We're the same. Like we started in the same place. I don't think of myself as like you know in the sky yet, even though people seem to talk like I am, you know? Like I'm just a person trying to do a thing and if I sit next to Boris, I'm like you know this guy's one of the best engineers I've ever met. But also like if I just open a prompt, we have the same prompt. We have the same MacBook Pro and you know there's nothing that stands between like me or you or any of us from like drawing on millions of years potentially of like tokens to like serve humanity. Well, Garry, I think that was a beautiful quote that should be retweetable. It's just... got to get it on X right away. You could have infinite time by borrowing the time from the machines. Yeah, what a time to be alive. That's beautiful. That's a beautiful thought to end on. Thanks Garry for showing us the future. Thanks guys. All right, thanks for watching and we'll see you on the next episode of The Lightcone.

Why Private Credit Is Facing Its Biggest Test Yet

Bloomberg Originals(5.0M)·10:51·40.7K views·2026-05-08
Michael BloombergFounder, Bloomberg L.P.
TL;DRThe $1.8 trillion private credit market is facing an unprecedented "crisis of confidence" as rapid expansion into the software sector is threatened by AI disruption, leading to record redemption requests from retail investors and growing concerns over opaque valuations.

TL;DR

The $1.8 trillion private credit market is facing an unprecedented "crisis of confidence" as rapid expansion into the software sector is threatened by AI disruption, leading to record redemption requests from retail investors and growing concerns over opaque valuations.

Market Expansion and Regulatory Shift

  • Rapid Growth: The private credit market has ballooned from roughly $500 billion in 2015 to $1.8 trillion today, with some managers estimating a total addressable opportunity of $40 trillion across infrastructure, music royalties, and railcar financing.
  • Post-2008 Landscape: Growth was fueled by post-financial crisis regulations that forced traditional banks to reduce risky lending. Private credit firms stepped in to provide speed and certainty that banks could no longer offer.
  • Dominant Players: The sector is led by major alternative asset managers including Blackstone, Apollo, Blue Owl, KKR, and Ares.
  • Geographic Concentration: Approximately two-thirds of the private debt market is concentrated in North America, followed by Europe and the Asia-Pacific region.

The AI "Existential Risk" to Software Lending

  • Sector Exposure: Private credit firms became major lenders to software companies—often backed by private equity—due to their strong revenue growth, even if they lacked profitability.
  • Disruptive Impact: The rise of generative AI (OpenAI, Anthropic) has fundamentally challenged the long-term viability of legacy software business models, turning what were considered "durable" loans into high-risk exposures.
  • Underwriting Scrutiny: Investors are now questioning if these loans were underwritten based on "overly optimistic" growth trajectories that failed to account for rapid technological shifts.
  • Wider Credit Failures: Recent collapses in adjacent sectors, such as subprime auto lender Tricolor and First Brands, have fueled fears of hidden leverage and poor underwriting standards across the broader credit ecosystem.

Liquidity Stress and the Retail Exodus

  • Redemption Requests: High-net-worth retail investors, who recently flooded into tailored private credit products, are now seeking the exits at an "unprecedented" rate.
  • Liquidity Gating: To preserve fund stability, firms including Blackstone, Blue Owl, and Apollo have been forced to enforce withdrawal caps, denying billions of dollars in redemption requests.
  • Corporate Response:
      • Blue Owl allowed investors to withdraw 15% of shares from a specific fund and sold assets to return capital.
      • Blackstone employees reportedly contributed personal capital to help meet redemption demands and stabilize investor sentiment.
  • Market Performance: Share prices for major firms like KKR and Blue Owl fell by as much as 10% earlier this year as the crisis intensified.

Transparency and Systemic Risk

  • Opaque Valuations: Unlike public bonds, private loans are bespoke and do not trade on open markets. Critics argue this lack of transparency allows firms to hide declining asset values until they are forced to slash them to zero.
  • The "Cockroach" Theory: JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon has warned that isolated failures in the credit market often signal deeper, systemic issues that have yet to surface.
  • Comparison to 2008: While some observers see parallels to the subprime mortgage crisis, proponents argue the risk is better contained today because loans are funded by institutional and private investors rather than sensitive bank deposits.
"Credit quality is excellent in our book and that doesn't mean no defaults. Everyone's gonna have defaults now and then. You just can't have many and you have to get good recoveries and that’s exactly what we do." — Marc Lipschultz, Co-CEO of Blue Owl
"My antenna goes up when things like that happen. And I probably shouldn't say this but when you see one cockroach there are probably more." — Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase
"Private credit's been around for decades... but people have been talking for years about how private credit has been untested. This is the first time that they’ve really been under this amount of pressure." — Bloomberg Narrator/Analyst
It’s been a tough few months for the 1.8 trillion dollar private credit market. The market is going through its biggest crisis of confidence in its history. Private credit firms make loans directly to companies, giving them a way to borrow outside of banks and bank-led channels. They use money predominantly from institutional investors, but recently from some wealthy retail investors too. They became major lenders to software companies, especially those backed by private equity firms, which by and large had seemed to be going pretty well. Now you just have this existential risk in the form of AI that can change everything, and private credit's just at the center of that now. Late last year, concerned retail investors started trying to pull their money out. Blue Owl allowed investors to take 15 percent of shares out of one of its private credit funds. It also sold assets to return capital to investors and reduce debt. Others got more creative. We did see some Blackstone employees basically pitch in some of their own money to help meet redemption requests. But this wasn't enough to calm investors. Blue Owl’s shares have fallen sharply since the start of the year. Industry-wide concern, though, meant it was far from a unique case. People have been talking for years about how private credit has been untested. This is the first time that they’ve really been under this amount of pressure. Some observers have drawn parallels with the lead-up to the 2008 financial crisis. It has the same trappings as subprime mortgage repackaging had. While others say that fear is overblown, especially since private credit firms don’t use your bank deposits to make loans. It’s just really hard to assess because these loans are private. Private credit lenders will tell you that they made really smart investments. But the concern is that, what if they didn't? Good news, everyone. Today you’re going to learn the connection between one of these, lots of these, and even this: Private credit. You can think of private credit as lending that’s not arranged by banks. It could be an insurance company or, in many cases these days, it could be a private credit fund. These deals are managed by some of the biggest names in private markets, like Blackstone, BlackRock, Blue Owl, and even some not named after colors—Greek gods, for instance. But what is it? It’s really a very broad umbrella term that refers to multiple forms of lending. The type that’s in the news most these days is what’s called direct lending. And that’s when basically these institutions lend money directly to companies. If you’re a company and you need to borrow money, you might go to a bank. But if you’re really, really big, a bank can’t provide that much of a loan to a single company. So instead, you go to the debt capital markets, where a bank arranges a bond or loan and sells it on to multiple investors. For a fee, of course. Private credit is a way to accomplish the same type of transaction, but just directly between the borrower and the ultimate buyer of that debt, cutting out all those middle stages. And it became central to the global financial system. Back in 2015, I think this market was around 500 billion dollars. The overall assets under management for private credit is roughly 1.8 trillion as of today. The 1.8 trillion market that most people are talking about is just a subset of what some managers see as a total opportunity of as big as 40 trillion dollars. In other words, the current market’s just the tip of the iceberg for private credit. Far bigger opportunities lurk underneath. There’s supply chain finance, there is consumer, auto loans, mortgages, there is railcar financing, there is different kinds of trade financing, and music royalties. We did a story last year about them getting into things that would have normally been financed by municipal bonds. A lot of lenders, especially places like Apollo, feel like the sky’s the limit. About two thirds of the private debt market is in North America. Europe takes the next biggest slice, followed by Asia-Pacific, and the rest of the world. And you could say private credit’s been around for decades really. I think the 1980s is probably when a lot of this started. But really back then it looked nothing like it does today. One event in particular didn’t so much grease the wheels as crash the car and set fire to it. The 2008 financial crisis led to numerous regulations to try to keep that from happening again. And one of the key things regulators essentially said was they wanted banks to do less risky lending. Regulators didn't want banks to make a lot of loans with their own money. And that's what allowed private credit to sort of step in and start doing a lot of this stuff. It can also help out when things get choppy in other debt markets. For example, in 2022, the Federal Reserve had to start raising interest rates. It really caused some problems in the high-yield bond and leveraged loan markets and made those much harder to borrow from. And private credit lenders really stepped up. And they saw it as a massive way to grow their market share.
