Now their loans can be more expensive than traditional financing, but borrowers often accept that for speed, certainty, and longer-term relationships with lenders. And software has been one of the biggest targets for direct lenders in recent years. A lot of these companies have very strong revenue growth, but they're not making money. That’s something that banks have a hard time financing, but private credit can do quite easily. This is the S&P 500. And since 2022, firms like Ares, Apollo, Blackstone, and Blue Owl have seen their share prices generally outperform it as they expanded their credit strategies and grew their assets under management. Over that period, Blue Owl’s AUM flew up from around 100 billion dollars to more than 300 billion. But something else happened in those four years. OpenAI is now valued at 852 billion dollars. Anthropic’s been making headlines almost on a weekly basis. The rise of AI has completely upended the private credit market. So now the spotlight is on how much exposure private credit firms have to software. And so retail investors in particular have been pulling their money. Shares of KKR and Blue Owl were down as much as 10 percent. Blue Owl is one of the most exposed. And it’s why investors are trying to figure out if those loans were underwritten with sound principles or based on overly optimistic growth trajectories that the AI revolution could now put in jeopardy. Towards the end of 2025, Blue Owl co-CEO Marc Lipschultz was pretty confident. "Credit quality is excellent in our book and that doesn't mean no defaults. Everyone's gonna have defaults now and then. You just can't have many and you have to get good recoveries and that’s exactly what we do at Blue Owl, and by the way so do our peers and so do the banks." In November though, it called off a merger of two of its funds amid scrutiny over potential investor losses. It coincided with redemption requests—that is people asking for their money back—climbing sharply across the industry, including at Blackstone, Blue Owl, and Apollo. There’s really not a fund that’s been spared over the last few months here. We’ve seen huge funds receive massive redemption requests that are unprecedented in the history of private credit markets. A lot of the firms that had made a big push into the retail market have had to deal with these redemption requests in an amount that was unprecedented. And to preserve the value of the funds, they’ve had to limit in many cases the amount of money that investors could take out. In the first quarter, funds were enforcing caps and denying billions of dollars of withdrawal requests. Predominantly from wealthy retail investors who’d sunk capital into special products tailored for them by private credit firms. In some cases, their unmet requests were tens of percentage points higher than the money actually allowed to be taken out. This all comes years after heavy inflows from institutional investors and retail focused funds. Much of that money going into software businesses that were seen as durable. Now AI is starting to challenge that assumption. And all of this is harder to judge because the market is so opaque. Some of the main drawbacks of private credit is there’s much less transparency. Private credit are bespoke loans between sophisticated parties and it’s all private. Loans are not securities. And crucially, that privacy means they don’t really trade, so there isn’t a market deciding what they’re worth. Skeptics have begun questioning the accuracy of private loan valuations, particularly after a string of investments recently had their values slashed to zero with little to no warning. It follows the collapse of subprime auto lender Tricolor and auto parts supplier First Brands last year. While only tangentially related to private credit, their demise raised broader questions about underwriting and hidden leverage across all credit markets. Jamie Dimon has been sort of a vocal critic of the private credit market, as how I would say it. The JPMorgan CEO was among the bankers facing losses tied to Tricolor and he went full insect in his assessment. "My antenna goes up when things like that happen. And I probably shouldn't say this but when you see one cockroach there are probably more." It’s a very visual image of hidden problems coming to the surface as cockroaches tend to do when lights go off. But the bigger point that he was making is there could be more problems and this could be just the beginning. And no one is really safe. This is what led some observers to draw comparisons to what happened in 2008. I think the counterargument to that is that the regulation following the 2008 financial crisis intentionally pushed risky lending off of bank balance sheets and instead ended up with investors. And so they can afford to lose that; they may not like to lose that, but they can afford to lose that. We're not really seeing that sort of widespread distress just yet. But definitely, I think we can say that the best days for private credit are probably behind us and the next few quarters, years, are going to be more challenging.

Raising Cane's CEO Todd Graves: Building a billion-dollar brand on one menu item | Masters of Scale

Masters of Scale(155.0K)·35:45·36 views·2026-05-08
Todd GravesCEO, Raising Cane's
TL;DRTodd Graves built Raising Cane’s into a multi-billion-dollar empire by defying industry norms, maintaining a strict focus on a limited menu, and prioritizing long-term cultural integrity over short-term private equity gains.
His journey from working as an Alaskan fisherman...

TL;DR

Todd Graves built Raising Cane’s into a multi-billion-dollar empire by defying industry norms, maintaining a strict focus on a limited menu, and prioritizing long-term cultural integrity over short-term private equity gains. His journey from working as an Alaskan fisherman to fund his first location highlights a "never quit" mentality that remains the brand’s core driver.

The Founding Philosophy: Simplicity as a Competitive Edge

  • The "B-Minus" Business Plan: While in college ('90–'94), Graves wrote a business plan for a chicken finger restaurant that received the lowest grade in the class.
      • Industry Pushback: The professor argued the plan ignored the industry trend toward variety (e.g., McDonald’s adding salads and wraps) to avoid the "veto vote."
      • Graves’ Counter-Intuition: He believed that executing one thing better than anyone else would create a "craveable" destination. He cited In-N-Out Burger (founded in 1948) as an eventual model for this focused approach.
  • Operational Efficiency: The limited menu allows for "scratch cooking" and high-speed service.
      • Speed Factors: Customers don’t need to study the menu; they often order by name (e.g., "Box Combo, no slaw, extra toast").
      • Consistency: By refusing to add items like spicy chicken or barbecue sauce, the kitchens maintain specialized, high-speed cooking procedures that "seconds matter" in the drive-thru.

Funding Through Grit and Manual Labor

  • Bootstrapping the Vision: After being rejected by every bank in town, Graves committed to funding the restaurant himself through grueling manual labor.
      • Boilermaker Work: He worked 90-hour weeks in California oil refineries (El Segundo and Torrance) to accumulate capital.
      • Alaskan Fishing: On the advice of a coworker, "Wild Bill" Tolar, Graves spent a summer in Naknek, Alaska, gillnetting sockeye salmon. He worked 20-hour days with only four hours of fragmented sleep to save the final $40,000–$60,000 needed.
  • The First Location: Graves opened the first Raising Cane’s at the north gates of LSU in a dilapidated building he renovated himself to save costs, learning plumbing and contracting on the job.

Ownership Strategy: Eschewing Franchises and Private Equity

  • Shift to Company-Owned: Graves originally planned for a 60/40 split between company-owned and franchised locations but eventually bought back his franchisees.
      • The Excellence Gap: He noted that while his company-owned stores operated at a 95/100 level, even good franchisees hovered around 85/100. This 10% difference in quality and culture drove him to move to a 100% company-operated model.
  • The "Death by a Thousand Cuts" Warning: Graves strongly advises founders against selling to private equity firms that prioritize three-to-five-year exit strategies.
      • Boardroom Erosion: He argues that PE-backed companies often make small, incremental cuts—like cheaper music systems or commissary-made sauce—that destroy crew morale and product quality over time.
      • Purpose-Driven Growth: Graves views his success as a platform to help his crew and community, a mission he believes is often lost when shareholders prioritize quarterly financial returns.

Marketing through "Caneiac" Culture

  • Top-of-Mind Awareness: Graves emphasizes the need to be visible the moment a customer asks, "What do we want for lunch?" This is achieved through heavy investment in billboards, radio, and timely TV placements.
  • Authentic Celebrity Partnerships: The brand utilizes "celebrity Caneiacs" who genuinely love the food, such as Snoop Dogg, Saquon Barkley, and Cynthia Erivo.
      • Humanizing the Brand: Marketing stunts, such as Snoop Dogg working a drive-thru window or hosting a fashion show during Fashion Week, keep the brand relevant and "cool" without taking itself too seriously.
  • Personal Leadership: Graves models himself after founders like Dave Thomas (Wendy’s), believing that a visible founder conveys a commitment to food safety and quality that a faceless corporation cannot.

Future Outlook: AI and Human Connection

  • AI for Prediction, Not Replacement: Raising Cane’s uses AI for data compilation and drive-thru speed optimization but Graves is hesitant to replace staff with robotics.
  • The Human Advantage: Graves believes that as competitors move toward fully robotic restaurants, Raising Cane’s "human touch"—friendly crew and handmade food—will become an even stronger competitive differentiator.

***

"The subconscious believes what you tell it... You can get up in the morning and say, 'Hey, I'm going to have a great day,' or you can get up and go, 'Oh my God, it's just going to be a tough day.' And whatever you say is pretty much right." — Todd Graves
"If you're sitting in a boardroom and the CFO says, 'Here's 15 things I can cut to make us more profitable'... it’s death by a thousand cuts. You implement it from a boardroom without being in the kitchens, and you lose the crew's attitude and the customer service." — Todd Graves
"If you try to be all things to all people, you're not serving any of them well." — Todd Graves
Coming up... I'm going to open my restaurant. I'm going to do it. I'm going to... I'm not going to stop until I do. Nothing's going to ever stop me for this. I'll never quit. I'll never give up. And when I tell myself that—and it's a human condition, right?—the subconscious believes what you tell it, right? The brain believes it, and that's where I could get that fuel to keep working hard. You know, you can choose your... you can choose your mood during the day. You get up in the morning and say, "Hey, I'm going to have a great day," or you can get up and go, "Oh my God, it's just going to be a tough day, right?" And whatever you say is pretty much right. This is Masters of Scale. I'm Jeff Berman, your host. This week on the show, Todd Graves. Todd is the founder and co-CEO of Raising Cane's Chicken Fingers. He blitzscaled his fast food concept into a brand worth billions, and is well-known both for its deliciously simple menu and its refreshingly friendly staff. We talk about how he perfected the recipe for success, why he thinks restaurants should be wary of private equity, and much, much more. It is a tasty conversation. Stay tuned. Todd, welcome to Masters of Scale. "Thank you for having me here." I'm thrilled to have you. I discovered Raising Cane's in, I want to say, 2022 after you opened in El Segundo in LA. And for my now 16-year-old and I, it had absolutely changed our chicken eating habits. So thank you. Fired up to talk to you and get into the story. Let's start at the start. Where did the inspiration for Raising Cane's come from? "Well, it really started as a kid. You know, I was always entrepreneurial. I was the kid that did the lemonade stands in the neighborhood, and then cut people's grass for five dollars, and just always thought around business. And when I started working, you know, high school years, I worked in the restaurant business. And I did that because I liked to cook with my mother. Like, food was, you know, for me, an expression of love. You know, spending time with my mother, cooking Cajun food, like let's say a gumbo, right? Or my grandmother made me a pie. And so I'm like, you know, it was my first job, go work in the restaurants. And I liked the pace. I liked the immediate gratification of cooking something and then serving it to somebody and them being happy, right? Working with teams, things like that. So for me, during college, you know, I was trying to consider what I was going to do. And that entrepreneurial streak just went back in me and I'm like, you know what? I want to open a restaurant. And I want to open it when I get out of college. I had no idea how hard that would be at that point, but that was the dream. It was just a natural thing for me to go into to do the food business. And when I was in college, and if you remember back in these days, this is '90, '94. Back then it was like ground meat was bad for you. Beef was bad and chicken and fish was good for you, but chicken became the number one protein. And I could see, and I grew up in Louisiana on fried chicken, right? But it was all bone-in fried chicken. During that time, while I was in college, people started eating the boneless chicken, whether it was chains like Chili's having that sauce, or it was going to mom and pops. And I was like, 'Man, this is gonna be the next thing.'" So, okay. So you're a college student in the '90s. You have this idea. Start working on a business plan back then? "I had a partner when I started the business and he had one more year of college and he enrolled in business classes, and we took a business planning class. I couldn't actually go to the class, but I actually wrote that business plan with him for that class. And man, it was good because I knew the business, right? I mean, it was a well-done business plan that he presented, but it ended up getting the worst grade in the class." Wait, why? What was the response? "And actually I was really glad it was the worst grade, because it motivated me. But the professor said, 'The plan's exceptional.' I mean, I knew details. I knew exactly, I knew what our aprons would cost to get washed, right? And so because I worked in the business. But he said, 'You know, you didn't follow the concept and understand the industry.' At that point, quick-service restaurants like McDonald's were adding menu items. They were worried about a veto vote. One person in the car doesn't want something from that restaurant, then they're going to go to another restaurant that has more variety. So they were adding variety and they were also adding healthy menu items. They were adding salads and wraps and things like that. So he said, 'Overall the plan was good, but you didn't hit with what the industry's doing.' So that's why I did it. And, you know, I was like, 'You know what? I'm gonna use that as motivation.' Because I knew that point, you go to a place because it's craveable food. And you're going to go there, and even though it has a hundred different menu items, you're going to get what you really like. And I'm like, being able to execute on one thing and do it better than anybody else is going to be a recipe for success. And it ended up being there. Look, In-N-Out Burger's done it since 1948. And I didn't have that as a model then, but I just had that intuitive sense that serving something craveable, people will come back for." We've had so many entrepreneurs on Masters of Scale who hear a hundred no's before they get their first yes. The first no sounds like it was a professor saying like, "This is the worst grade in the class for this reason." I appreciate the instinct was there, but to go launch a business takes... takes more than instinct. I mean, there's... there's a lot that goes into that. Where do you think that conviction came from, not just to believe it and feel it, but to follow through on it? "Yeah, so I was like one of these nerds in college. I was part of the entrepreneurial club and would... would read business magazines, you know, reading the paper. And what I saw was a lot of people that had really good ideas that they're passionate about never go and get it done because it's so hard to start a business, right? And so I was like, 'If I want to do this and this is my dream to go own my own restaurant, I gotta be committed, like to success or eventually failure, whatever it is, but never stop till I get it done.' Took that business plan to every bank in town. You know, bought a cheap suit and cheap briefcase and went in and presented the plan. And, you know, no after no after no. Nice enough, right? But then they also give me some advice like, 'Hey, why don't you... you don't have any money. Why don't you... you got a college degree, why don't you work in the industry for 10 years and then you can do that.' And with every 'no', you know, it's the best thing for an entrepreneur to be told, 'Hey, I don't think this is going to work,' because you just use that as fuel. You want to get a chip on your shoulder. You're like, 'I believe in this and I'm going to do this.' And, you know, to back that up, you know, when I couldn't get any money, I'm like, 'I'm going to go work and work as long as it takes for me to fund the restaurant myself.' And so I got a job through a friend of a friend working in... as a boilermaker, which is turnaround shift working in refineries. One in El Segundo, LA, and the other one in Torrance in LA. And that's when I came out to LA and worked hard. 90-hour weeks. California you get some double time in there. It's a great way to make money if you want to work hard. And met a guy there, interesting name, Wild Bill Tolar. Okay, Wild Bill. Everybody had nicknames there, and mine was Hollywood, which is a whole other story. But Wild Bill's like, 'Man, you're... you're not afraid of hard work and I know you have this chicken finger dream.' He's like, 'You should come with me, come up to Alaska and I'll introduce you, show you around, and you can work the Alaskan fishing trade catching sockeye salmon with gillnetting.' And he says, 'You'll get a job on the boat. You're determined.' He says, 'Actually you're going to make some real money.' He said, 'Now it's dangerous. The hours are incredibly hard, but you can make some good money.' So sure enough, caught a plane to Anchorage, float plane to King Salmon, hitchhiked to Naknek, and spent that summer commercial fishing in Alaska." So, a guy named Hollywood, nicknamed Hollywood, goes up to Alaska to start doing commercial fishing, which, to say it's dangerous and to say that the hours are tough and the working conditions are rough is an understatement. I mean, this is one of the toughest jobs in the world. "It is, yeah." How did you manage it? "You get about four hours of sleep a night. And that's 30 minutes here, an hour here. 20-hour days you're just like working hard, hard manual labor. The boats are filling up with fish and captains don't want to go to the tender boat and you might sink and boats are ramming each other, National Geographic helicopters going over, medical evac helicopters going. It was just insane deal. And I was just like, 'I'm doing this. I'm fueling my chicken finger dream. Nothing's going to stop me.' But that resolve, having that resolve is what kept me going and then eventually opening up." And you were telling everyone your why. You were telling, "I'm here because I'm going to make enough money to go open my restaurant." "That's right, that's right." Wow. And do you think that there's something psychological when you say it out loud that commits yourself to it? Is there a psychological insight there? "It absolutely is for me. And so being able to say, 'I'm going to open my restaurant. I'm going to do it. I'm going to... I'm not going to stop until I do. Nothing's going to ever stop me for this. I'll never quit. I'll never give up.' And when I tell myself that, and it's a human condition, right? The subconscious believes what you tell it, right? And the brain believes it. And that's where I could get that fuel to keep working hard and do it. And it still does today too, right? So, you know, you can choose your... you can choose your mood during the day. You get up in the morning and say, 'Hey, I'm going to have a great day,' or you can get up and go, 'Oh my God, it's just going to be a tough day, right?' And whatever you say is pretty much right." By the end of that Alaskan summer, how much had you saved up? "That point about maybe like 40 to 60 grand at that point, which was significant money and enough for me to get the project rolling. And also this point too is I was able to get a small group of preferred investors because they had seen, you know, it had been roughly two years to where when I opened it up, they're like, 'You know what? You are committed to this.' You know, little five thousand dollars, ten thousand dollars. Some of them boilermakers, some of them Alaskan fisher people, you know, my bookie who invested in cash. But it's just little five, ten thousand dollar increments. And then got a small SBA loan. And since I had that equity side of it, and I had to live off it too—I mean, because the money I'd made, I had to live off that. We were paying ourselves like five bucks an hour, whatever we were doing. And was able to open up. Secured a place in the north gates of LSU, right? This old dilapidated building. The broker was a great guy, Red Reynolds, and he said, 'I believe...' He actually like told the landlord, Tallulah Harper, she was like 96 years old, and said, 'Todd will... it was all kind of college concept.' It just was in a year, out a year. He's like, 'He will make a long-term thing and we can just wait for him.' So let's just wait for it. But going in, I wanted to be prudent, right? And I didn't have a ton of money going in and renovate this place. So I just learned what to do, right? Learned how to do plumbing. I learned how to do contracting work, things like that, and then bring in the experts when we really needed—you know, electrician, things I couldn't do—so figured that out. Same with going to the restaurant. I knew the restaurant business, but I'd never gone out to hire people, never done these things before so I'd learn it." Was there a piece of advice you got from someone who'd been there, done that before that was particularly instrumental in this phase of the process? "You know, not at that point because, to be honest with you, I had a lot of bad examples. So when, you know, working in restaurants in high school and college, the restaurant business was... it was more militant. It was 'do this, do that,' no music going in the kitchens. There wasn't a semblance of having fun or teamwork. It was, you know, it's the 'yes chef' type of a deal. And so I knew what I didn't want, you know what I mean? I knew I wanted people to wear casual uniforms, just Raising Cane's shirt and jeans, shorts, whatever. I wanted people to listen to music. I wanted them to have fun, but taking pride while they worked and did those things. So those types of things helped me on doing that. So like a lot of these restaurateurs were just... just not good people people. They weren't good culture people. So, but look, National Restaurant Association gave us a lot of good things on like actually the formats on how to do things, how to do templates for scheduling and things like that. And then I reached out to the restaurateurs I did respect in Baton Rouge and be like, 'Hey man, how are you getting good people? And what are some incentives you're doing?' My wife, who I started dating back then, was a McDonald's franchisee. So we spent a lot of our time just talking about, you know, 'How do you motivate the crew? How do you salt your fries?' things like that." Was there a point where there was an inflection where you said, "Oh, we've got this. We really have this right now"? "Luckily we made money from the first month, right? I saved thirty bucks, then we started doing well. And actually we had so much success that I opened my second location 18 months later. Got a full SBA loan, 18 months on that, and construction from the ground up on this building. And so I was always fortunate that we were making money and doing it. So I never had the... had the, 'It's not going to work' moment. I had the, 'Look, I just hope I can keep this energy going,' because I was working non-stop and doing it and doing that and I need to get other people to come help me and do this. But the thing that was the inflection point when I knew it worked... I thought this was just a college concept, right? Opened my first location one side of campus, the second the other side. But that other location had traffic flow of not just students, right? So now I'm having mom and dad pick up food on the way home from work. I got T-ball teams on Saturday, church groups on Sunday, and business people coming in for lunch. And then I'm like, 'Wait a minute. This isn't just a college concept. I think this can work.' And that's when I got the bug, man. I was like, 'I'm enjoying this. I'm figuring things out. I'm enjoying having teams build. And I'm enjoying this, man. I'm like, I want to go. I love serving customers.' And I was able to start doing a few things in the LSU community. And I got that bug, man. I wanted to grow. And I never thought that would be that. But at that point I was like—and that's where the vision was from that point was—as many Raising Cane's I can do in the world, that's what I want to do." I know you have eschewed a franchise model and I'm keen to hear about that theory of growth and scale. Maybe we can enter some of that through how do you come into a community and really get to know it so you're serving their needs and not serving your own objectives first? "Yeah, you know, so for us it's hiring local people. And so hiring local leaders and being there. Now we send our crews to go help get this, you know, established, bring in the culture things, the training and things like that, but hiring local leaders and people that are intrinsically motivated. And meaning, you know, they want to take care of the crew and they want to create great opportunities for them and they want to give back to the community. And it's actually seen, you can see it all the time in the restaurant business, in most restaurant business chains are into doing community things. You can tell the ones that are into it and the ones that are not into it. You can tell the ones that are into their crew or really not into the crew." Why didn't you go the franchise route as you looked to start scaling outside of either side of the LSU campus? "Well, when I started I did start with franchisees. And my idea was going to be that I'd be mostly company, maybe 60% company and about 40% franchised, right? And that was going with strong restaurant operators that have the same, you know, intrinsic values, crew, people, community, and those sorts of things. And we did well with them. They were great franchisees and they cared and they did. They just didn't operate as well as us. And so let's say we're rated at our company restaurants 95 out of 100. They'd run it like 85 out of 100, which is really good because most franchisees are 65 out of 100, I would say. That 5-10% difference drove me crazy, you know? And it's also the efficiency. So with working with those franchisees, it's their business and they care about it, but like maybe we have a new training program, a way to get faster in drive-thru things. It was such a talk into certain things—'No, we like to do it this way,' and did-da-da-da—that I spent more time on convincing versus moving forward on things that I knew that worked, right? But for me it's an expression of my family, you know what I mean? We want to do a good job and do that. So we bought them back. They're happy to, and I'm like 100% company restaurant operations." How many restaurants now? "We're almost at a thousand right now." You know, we're in an era where, whether it's private equity or it is, you know, simply like squeezing out every last penny to achieve the best returns for that quarter and appease the market or appease investors. And there are a lot of companies, a lot of companies that are doing this to the detriment of the well-being of their team members or their crew members. You've taken a bit of a different approach. Just talk a little bit about how you've approached building a team at a thousand and growing number of locations around the world. "I want to be the brand known for not just craveable chicken finger meals, great crew, cool culture, active community involvement, right? Guides everything I do. And it's timeless. It's like, those are the basics, right? If you want a great crew, you treat your crew right. You do those things. And you want the culture, you power the people that can simulate that culture, which is a culture of appreciation. So for me is look, I feel like God made me good at chicken fingers to help people. It's my purpose, right? So I have purpose, that's why I continue to grow this. And if you have that purpose, then you're going to take care of your crew, you're going to take care of your communities. You're going to do that. And if you can't execute on that, then you're not executing on vision, then the whole idea is over. The whole dream is over. And the reason why I try to talk these business founders into not selling to private equity is... and look, they're not all bad. And so I'm not talking about all, but a lot of them are. And look, if you have investors, if you have shareholders, that their concern is, 'What financial return I'm going to get?' then things go bad, because people are not, they're caught in that deal. If you're private equity and you need to be out of this deal in three to five years, that's your goal. When you're out and sold major money, there's still people that are running these restaurants whose lives are just as important as yours, their families are just as important, and now you're gone and the next people come in, might come in and hack them down again and it's just, you see when it happens, you know? And so there's a few out there that I've seen in our space, restaurant space, that do a good job and they take care of the people. Most of the time these other ones don't because it's not what they're talking about in the boardroom, right? So let me give you one example. Different CFO types and different people and different advice. Something as simple as this. If you're sitting in a boardroom and you're talking about, and the CFO is in there like, 'Here's these 15 things I can cut to make it cheaper, right?' I mean, to make us more profitable. 'The music system we have. It's number one top music system has all these choices. This one we can say, we can do half that amount for the music system and it has the basics.' Well, then they implement that. And they implement it from a boardroom not being in the kitchens. And the reason why the crew members love it is they have the newest music and they can jam where they go and they're happier in doing the deal. Now they don't have that music, they're listening to old music they don't like. Their attitude goes down, they're not quite as happy, customer service not as good, speed goes down. All those little cuts, like those 15 things that go right. 'We can get our sauce made at a commissary. Commissary keeps up quality, we can save 50%.' Another slash. You know, it's death by a thousand cuts in that deal. It happens over and over again. Most of the time that's what happens." Todd, you all are known for your marketing. It's exceptional. The product is phenomenal. My youngest and I especially are massive fans of the product. In this world of, you know, we are deluged with marketing messages non-stop. How are you generating the ideas and having the courage of conviction to go after non-obvious plays that actually break through? "Starting off being an entrepreneur, another great thing is you learn marketing and you learn it how you get great impressions, cost per impression, right? Because it's like you don't have the money to do it. So around LSU I just started doing the LSU radio, and getting them to do that, but I'd meet the DJs and be like, bringing them food and they talk about it and got it good in with them. And then when I was able to afford billboards, TV, things like that, I added. So in my business, quick-service restaurants, you have to be top of mind, right? You need to see the billboard on the way to lunch because at lunch everybody says, 'Hey, what do we want today? Oh, Cane's, right?' It's that speaking it, like I'm speaking it or hear it on the radio or see the television. That's a really important part of the deal, right? But there's so many of those messages and that. So for me it's being literally in the moment when something is going to happen, we want to be a part of it, right? So Cynthia Erivo, she's at the Oscars going for the Triple Crown, she goes to Cane's afterwards. She wins, she goes there, she gets this amazing shot. And she's a Caneiac and she's a friend, right? She gets that shot, and it's like after this Super Bowl, it's going to be having the quarterback from either team, with the winning team but doing it immediately after. Like doing Saquon two days after the Super Bowl last year. Going and doing it, it's timely. I like tapping into celebrities' fan bases, right? And so obviously that's an obvious thing and then customers become loyal because, 'Hey, Saquon loves it and he's friends with Todd.' But having that in real time is to me so important when it's the top of people's minds, right? And it's in the press and it's going and doing it. So just anything. You can be creative, right? So fashion week. Teams like, not my idea, teams like, 'Hey, let's do a Cane's fashion show. We're not taking ourselves too seriously, but we like this design or we can do Cane's sauce dress and-da-da-da-da.' And we did it the day before, like Wednesday or Thursday, cranked up, and we had all the press come because it was something different, right? And so we were also part of that and hit a different group. Hit the fashion world group and got that different deal. So for me is like, if I think about I'm doing my job for my crew, right? It's like how do I get them more sales and we stay relevant and do things, because it means their bonuses and what people can make and, you know, all that good stuff that goes with that. So I just push myself constantly to do that. So I run our marketing. Marketing for a founder, obviously a very personal thing. It's also a slippery slope being top of mind all the time and doing so much, because you can flip and not be cool, right? You could pick the wrong person, you know, it's somebody that might not be a genuine Caneiac. It might be somebody that later gets in trouble for things. So it's that deal. It just takes quite a bit to do it, but the activations are great." Well, and celebrities are so often misused, and it does strike me that the celebrities you work with truly are Caneiacs. I mean, they love... you can feel that from them. It's fun. It's not so serious. It's a good time, right? Snoop working at a drive-thru, like, I mean, who doesn't want to hang out with that? That's a good time. "It is, and people want to see their heroes doing something like that, work a drive-thru. It's just the humanization of it. And then like if someone is so funny with doing it and he's just so clever and the crew loves it, the customers love it. It's a really fun way. So actually Snoop's the one that I broke into doing the celebrity drive-thru with. And, you know, we helped each other's business. We've been friends for a long time. And he literally called, 'Hey man, what can we do? What do you want to do? Let's go. Where are you?' He's like, 'I'm about to be in Little Rock and do a show.' I was like, 'Man, you know what? I've been wanting to do someone in the drive-thru. If you do it, I know I can get everybody after it.' And he said, 'What are you talking about?' I said, 'Like just go in and work Cane's, man, and put secret cameras out.' He had a blast doing it. And then after he did it, I was able to say, 'Hey,' with different people, I'm like, 'Hey, Snoop did it, you gotta do it now.' But you hit on a really good point is just the whole being a brand ambassador, they have to be Caneiacs. And so it starts with that. And, you know, I think a lot of brands do it really, really well. Some don't though. They end up getting somebody then you can see through... people have so many messages now, marketing messages, they can see through things that aren't genuine in two seconds, you know? It's like, then it hurts the brand, right?" I want to go back to the professor who gave the business plan a B-minus because the other element here beyond customers seeking, you know, a range of options and what have you, is supply chain risk, right? I mean, you know, God forbid, knock wood, we are, you know, one bad bird flu away from a really bad quarter or year for you and for your customers. How do you think about managing for that kind of risk when you've got such a limited menu? "Yeah, so for me, well, one is just redundancy within the products you have, right? Let's take out bird flu, anything like that. You just look at it. So it's, you know, use most of the poultry providers. Everybody can do it, right? Like fries. We get fries made to our specs by several different fry companies. In the beginning, when I couldn't get that redundancy, look, if they had a fire at the plant and couldn't give me potatoes, I'd have to change the product that I was doing, but still get fries and then do that. So it was redundancy in all our suppliers. Plus it keeps everybody honest. We have really good vendor partners, so it's not that they don't, but it's, you know, good competition, healthy competition's always a good thing. As far as like the protein, let's take bird flu. That sort of thing is chicken is an extraordinarily resilient protein, right? It's a very... bird. The poultry houses are on and stay up, up to date and do it. I just don't see that there'd be, you know, something that would go in. When the first bird flu happened in China, I think KFC sales went down maybe 20% for a period of time, and then bounced right back. And it was studying those different things as... so for me is you have, you know, we do fresh chicken, but we have frozen supply to get us through any kind of outbreak. So we still have that going, so long-term lead times on a frozen supply on that deal is. But ultimately if people just stop eating chicken, then I'd be extinct. I would absolutely be extinct. I wouldn't start serving steak fingers or something like that. I'd go, 'Hey look, it's time for me to hang this up.'" I think we're at low risk there. I love the simplicity of your menu. I have to imagine that certainly outsiders have advised to diversify the menu. Has there been a point where you've really questioned this and thought about adding a new item that goes beyond your core? "You know, thank God that I'm just... just hard-headed. You know what I mean? And not listen. And so from the get-go I just do this product, this, you know, serving that one thing, right? If you try to be all things to all people, you're not serving any of them well, right? So doing one thing. And so if you break down our concept, it's craveable food served with fast food speed and convenience, right? And so those things together. So if you have to do it with fast food speed and convenience, then you gotta be fast. And if you want to serve craveable food, you have to do it homemade style and do that. It comes down to the simplest procedure to get that food through, right? And so I can do it all basically scratch cooking because I have just that menu. Outside of that, it's the customers coming in. And if the customers come to the order window, I mean, look, they don't look at the menu board. They pull up to the speaker, 'I'll take a box combo, no slaw, extra toast, da-da-da-da.' Everybody's got their order. It doesn't take extra time to do it, right? So people are like, 'Man, you know, Texas gotta add barbecue sauce, cream gravy.' And I'm like, 'I hear you, but it would come up to the window and they go, 'you know what? I don't know if I want that cream gravy or I want that.' Look, you're getting Cane's sauce. And you're going to like it.' You know, you're gonna love it and you're gonna do that. And you train people to go not on those extra choices. If you had spicy, you know, people are like, 'You should do hot chicken and that deal is.' Once again, it would be slower from the cooking procedure because we don't hold food, so how much are you going to estimate for each one of those types of orders? Then they're gonna come up and go, 'you know what? I'm gonna change that to spicy,' even at the window, you know, come up and do that stuff. All those seconds matter. I knew the concept. But, yeah, I've had quite a few suggestions. Not as many now, not as many now. But even when we went to the Middle East, we kept the product exactly the same. We kept the same thing. And there was a lot of talk there about there's another sauce they have there. It's like a mayo base and garlic and all this stuff. It's the most popular sauce. So I'm like, 'They will come to love Cane's sauce and we gotta stick to our guns.' And sure enough, they love Cane's sauce as much as that other sauce they got." Because it's awesome. "Because it's good, yeah." You are now Shark Tank famous. Not having pitched your business there, but having... having the role of a shark. How... how do you think for you as a CEO about your personal brand and... and how that helps build the business globally? "Yes, so I... I grew up seeing Dave Thomas on the commercials for Wendy's. And for me, it was like, 'Wow, there's really a person. It's not a corporation. And that guy cares about the quality of the food and that guy's making those decisions.' So from a young kid seeing that. Right now it's even more important because there's a lack of founders in the restaurant space, and that's because they sell to private equity. And so it is something different. So I believe that when people see, 'Hey, this guy and his family care about quality, they care about food safety, they care about those things,' it just gives me another advantage over other competitors that just don't have that." It strikes me you seem to really enjoy doing it also. "I do. It's just something when you're... when you're happy about it and do it. And I can actually... I can actually do it okay. A little bit acting, but like it's I get to have fun with people like Peter Billingsley—you know, the Ralphie—and like to actually do a commercial with him. And, you know, he wrote them and all that stuff, but I can actually be a little bit of that part of that deal which was really fun, right? Like I'm acting with Peter Billingsley, you know, one of my heroes and same with everybody else in that space. Doing stuff with Snoop Dogg and actually having him do that. It's like fun. It's like when I did Archie Manning and Usmi, but like Cooper Manning wrote the scripts, you know, and he's like, 'Todd, you're going to do this.' And Archie is one of my heroes. It... it's a blast. And for me is I do take time off, so it's not like I don't have fun and do things like that. But for me it's like work productivity. So I can actually go goof off doing that and have fun and I know it's productive so I can spend half the day with Kirk Herbstreit shooting commercials with our dogs, because I'm like I don't feel guilty about like, 'I should be answering those questions, you know, I should be in a meeting right now.'" Speaking of productivity, I can't manage... I mean, my team will tell you like I will not use some of the services that they use for communication because I don't have time to get work done. I mean, it's all you're doing is being reactive and responsive and not being proactive and thoughtful. How are you managing your time to maximize productivity and also to keep enjoying what you're doing? "Yeah, I'm pretty erratic and so I'm not a good example of this. So for me is whatever needs to get done is going to get done, it doesn't matter when it is, doesn't matter if I don't sleep, doesn't matter these things. And so with having that type of erratic schedule... I might be up working till 4 AM, right? Some mornings I have to get up early because I got meetings and what to do that deal. Other times I need to sleep and I will sleep. I'll go several nights without getting a lot of sleep and then I'll go sleep 11 hours. The team just knows like, 'Hey, he's... he's conked out and he's catching back up and doing those things.' So what I do is surround myself with very, very good people that are very detailed, disciplined, and scheduled. Kind of like you're talking about your team and doing that stuff. And then they know how to manage up to like, 'Hey, Todd, I will need this tonight. We need it in the morning.' My daughter's in the business with me, like she... she's like, 'Dad, we have to get this tonight.' I'm like, 'I know, maybe something marketing.' I'm like, 'Hey, I gotta approve all these sites first.' She's like, 'It's okay, call me up, too, because I'm gonna go to bed at 10 and I'll get those answers done in the morning and things like that.' So me it's just getting it done in what... in whatever it takes. I do take time, but I use modern things I didn't have when I started. Like I can go to the beach with the family and I can actually have dual monitors set up, work remote there, do Zooms. Oh my God, Zooms. So great because you can get the interaction. It's always better in person, right? But at least I can go on that trip and the family can go hike or go out to the beach and I can do that, be with them and I'll get five hours that day and I'll go to dinner with them, or work later that night. So stretching that out and doing it. But my thing is just whatever, I made this commitment so whatever the commitment I make for the crew is I have to just get it done and... and do that. So I'm probably not the best example of that. But discipline. Discipline to get it done. I have friends like... like a Tom Brady. He's like, he's... he's so disciplined and it's so great. Like tonight he's coming in, 'Hey, what are you doing tonight?' I said, 'Well, I'm going to NFL Honors and I'm gonna go to this party.' and I'm like, 'Hopefully not stay up,' because I'll end up getting a couple drinks and I'll see friends and do it. He goes, 'Yeah, I definitely won't be up late.' because he's going to get up and work out at 5 AM and he's going to knock out his day and do that and he keeps that schedule and does it. I'll dry up in a schedule like that. I gotta have different things." But also most of us aren't wired like Tom Brady. I mean, most of us don't have that level of discipline. Very few of us have that level of discipline, and so I appreciate the just the humanness of the reality of the answer. As we sit here in the first quarter of 2024, there is not a company in the world that isn't thinking about artificial intelligence. How are you approaching the integration of new technologies into the business? "Yeah, so for us, one is just starting off on how do we get information quicker and get it compiled quicker and doing it, also keeping the people thinking and saying, 'Hey look, I could've... I could've ChatGPT'd that myself.' You know what I mean? Like let's use that stuff, but let's use it to make our decisions, but we're doing that to get better decisions done. We use it right now too as just like to get prediction for the future, right? So right now we use all the technology you can in the drive-thrus, right? Handhelds, great systems, all this stuff to go faster. What's coming up with robotics? How quickly is it going to come up? And so I want to keep the human touch as we go but I do believe, you know, someday you will have all robot restaurants serving food, doing it. And I'm going to use that as competitive advantage. So one, my... a lot of my competitors—and I would never mention any—but they don't have the friendliest crew, right? They don't have the best experience when you go there because they're not being treated right, by the way. It's really not their fault, it's they're not being treated right. But in the future when you have robots doing that, robot's going to be friendly every time, right? So you could look at this from Cane's going, 'Oh man, we're going to lose that friendliness component with my... our crew, that competitive advantage.' But no, I think people are going to go, 'Hey look, there's a real person back in that kitchen cooking, there's a real person at the counter doing it.' So I'm not planning to... to replace crew members with... with robots." It'll be a while, if ever. "Exactly, huh." We're in a moment that is incredibly exciting and also daunting for young people as they are coming out of college or otherwise entering the workforce. If there's a 21-year-old soon-to-be Georgia graduate who bumps into you and says, "You know, what's one piece of advice? What... what should I be thinking about? What should I take away from a conversation with you?" What are you telling them? "Yeah, go for your dream, go for your passion, right? You have to be good at what you're doing, right? I was good at the restaurant business. Now I couldn't have been a professional quarterback and things like that, so that was not my dream to do that, but I knew I was good at the restaurant. But whatever you want to go into, be good at it and make sure you're passionate about it. Then it's going to be so hard you can't even believe it's going to go so be committed to do it. Then go in and when you start a business or whatever field you go into, treat people well. If you treat people well, then things will always do well. And I think this generation has even more opportunity than we've had in recent years because if you're a go-getter—I love the younger generation—but sometimes in some areas people are getting more soft, you know, and thinking different things. The old work ethic, you know, we had to work, you know, they're like you had to do these things. For people that want to go out there and get after it, I think they're going to just smoke it and people will follow. They will... they will follow good leaders, being aggressive for what you want to do, working hard, doing those great values while you're taking care of people and doing those things. I think that's a great opportunity for young ones out there right now. I tell my kids right now too—my kids, one's my daughter graduated two years ago from LSU, she works in the business, my son's graduating SMU this year, he did business to understand they're coming to the business—I'm like, 'You can come in immediately and start adding value, right? And come in and like go for it.'" Perfect place to wrap. Thanks for being with us. "I appreciated it. Enjoyed the conversation. Thank you." Thanks again to Todd Graves for joining us. Todd's tenacity is inspiring. His story is a testament to what's possible if you live by your principles, take care of your people, and stay incredibly focused on your goals. It also doesn't hurt if your signature dipping sauce is darn delicious. Every drop is worth a dime. Todd, take my money, please. You already have. I'm Jeff Berman, thanks for listening.

FULL GAGGLE: Trump Takes Questions About Iran War During Visit To The Lincoln Memorial

Forbes Breaking News(5.5M)·31:10·22.3K views·2026-05-08
Donald TrumpPresident of the United States
TL;DRPresident Trump highlighted the rapid, cost-effective renovation of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool while maintaining a "zero-tolerance" policy regarding Iran’s nuclear ambitions following recent naval skirmishes.
Reflecting Pool Renovation: Trump emphasized a new,...

TL;DR

President Trump highlighted the rapid, cost-effective renovation of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool while maintaining a "zero-tolerance" policy regarding Iran’s nuclear ambitions following recent naval skirmishes.

National Mall Infrastructure & Revitalization

  • Reflecting Pool Renovation: Trump emphasized a new, industrial-strength "American Flag Blue" coating for the pool.
      • Cost Efficiency: Claimed the project costs $1.8 million and will take roughly two weeks, contrasting it with a previous $38 million Obama-era project that "didn't work."
      • Technical Details: Workers removed 12 truckloads of filth; the new surface is designed to last 50 years.
  • Undercroft Museum: Interior Secretary Doug Burgum announced a new 15,000-square-foot museum located in the 80-foot-high "cavern" beneath the Lincoln Memorial.
      • Timeline: Scheduled to open June 23 as part of the "Freedom 250" anniversary.
  • Triumphal Arch: Trump proposed building the world’s 60th triumphal arch near the bridge by the river, claiming it will be "more grand" than the Arc de Triomphe in Paris.
  • City Beautification: Secretary Burgum noted the repair of over 19 fountains and the removal of over 1,000 graffiti sites across D.C.

Iran Conflict & Nuclear Diplomacy

  • Naval Engagement: Trump reported that three U.S. destroyers successfully repelled Iranian "fast boats" that were firing at them.
      • Casualties: Trump stated the "people that shot [at us] are no longer with us" and claimed the Iranian Navy's small boat fleet is being systematically depleted.
  • Nuclear Red Line: Reiterated that Iran will never be permitted to possess a nuclear weapon.
      • Negotiation Status: Trump claimed a deal "could happen any day" but warned of "one big glow coming out of Iran" if a ceasefire and agreement are not signed.
  • Regime Change: Trump claimed his administration has effectively "got rid of" three successive regimes through his policies.

Public Safety & Urban Policy

  • Crime Reduction Claims: Trump asserted that crime in Washington D.C. is down roughly 88% due to the removal of 5,000 "career criminals" and stricter enforcement.
      • Other Cities: Noted significant crime drops in Memphis and New Orleans following federal interventions.
  • Homelessness: Secretary Burgum stated that 82 homeless camps have been eliminated in D.C. to make the city "safe and beautiful."
  • Border Link: Trump linked urban crime to the "open border," claiming thousands of murderers were allowed into the country but are now being deported.

Economy, Trade, & Global Relations

  • Market Performance: Trump celebrated the stock market hitting its 59th all-time high during his presidency, specifically noting the benefit to citizens' 401(k) plans.
  • Energy Prices: Predicted that gas prices will drop to historic lows (potentially below $2.00/gallon) once Middle East tensions subside.
  • Foreign Relations:
      • Brazil: Reported a positive meeting with President Lula regarding increased trade and tariff relief.
      • China: Affirmed a strong relationship with President Xi and expressed a willingness to lobby for the release of an imprisoned Christian pastor, Ezra Jin.
  • Tariffs: Defended the 10% across-the-board tariffs despite recent court rulings, stating the U.S. is "taking in hundreds of billions" from countries that previously "ripped us off."

Notable Quotes

"The plan is very simple: Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon, it cannot have a nuclear bomb and it's not going to have... If there's no ceasefire, you're not going to have to know, you're just going to have to look at one big glow coming out of Iran." — President Donald Trump
"We had estimates to fix it [the reflecting pool] of about 355 million dollars and it was gonna take three and a half years... we're gonna be able to do it for about a million eight, 1.8 million, and it's gonna take one week." — President Donald Trump
"Under President Trump's leadership we've taken care of 82 homeless camps that were in the city when he was elected, making DC safe and beautiful. Crime is down, beauty is up." — Doug Burgum, Secretary of the Interior
"I hope not [on the virus spreading]. We'll do the best we can... A lot of great people are studying it. It should be fine." — President Donald Trump on COVID-19
Hello everybody. Hey. Good to see you. Making progress, huh? Yes sir. Good to see you guys. Hey, great idea, right? It is. Great idea. Everybody thinking that idea? I think it's gonna be good. It's gonna be good. I want to come and go. Doing a job on the reflecting pond. It's really beautiful and it's gonna be fantastic. Hundreds of millions of dollars has been spent over the years foolishly. Hello. Foolishly. And we're doing a job that nobody's ever seen anything like it. It was a good idea we came up with and we're—we're doing a sealer and then some very intricate work with modern materials and it's gonna look better than it did in 1922 when it was built. It was built in 1922 and it had granite pavers; it was granite, various materials on top. The size is very—it's almost—I guess it's over 2,000 feet, 2,400 feet long. That's taller than any building in the world if you lay it on its side. And very big. The surface was bad, the leaking was bad. These workers are the most fantastic people. You know Doug Burgum? He's in charge of it, he's the head of interior and done a great job. And I was getting complaints from people saying that it wasn't good, this is a number of months ago, and I called Doug and he agreed. They spent a fortune. President Obama spent 38 million dollars and closed it for two and a half years. It was a construction nightmare, construction mess. When they opened it, it didn't work and it leaked. It was a disaster. But I know how to build and we worked with Doug and Interior and Parks, and these guys have been fantastic. So we're putting a beautiful surface on. It's called "the coloring." It's like a swimming pool but industrial strength, much stronger. And I think you're gonna see something that's amazing. Very interesting is we took all of the—they took 12 garbage trucks full of garbage that was under the water that people didn't see but they knew it was there. It was a real mess. And you're gonna have basically industrial strength. It's gonna last, I would say 50 years it'll last. It's gonna last a long time and you'll have no leak, guaranteed no leak, and it's gonna be something very special and it's gonna be a beautiful color. The problem was the color was never good because basically it had a gray stone underneath. Whether it was the concrete or the granite, it was a gray stone. Now we actually picked the color; it's called American Flag Blue. And so you can't do better than that. We had estimates to fix it of about 355 million dollars and it was gonna take three and a half years, you can see by the size of it. And so we're gonna be able to do it for about a million eight, 1.8 million, and it's gonna take one week. And we'll be finished—I guess I'm gonna give them a little more time. Let's give it two weeks, but we're pretty much—a lot of the hard work is done. The beautiful work is now. The application is really the fun. Yes sir. The sidewalk surfaces—they're fixing that too, they're having it repointed, refixed. It's gonna be like actually much better than new. It’s much more beautiful than it was new because it never had the color people wanted, but now it's gonna have the great color. So it’s gonna be good. It's gonna last many, many years and it's gonna have no leaks, gonna have no problems, gonna be clean and beautiful. I want to thank Doug Burgum for the job he's done. We're working on some other jobs that are great. We're working on—you know the back terrace of the Lincoln Memorial? That was supposed to be the main entrance, but they built two roadways behind it so it never had access to the Potomac and we're working on that. We have a beautiful plan for that, the Lincoln Memorial, and we have other things. Also, I guess you want to talk about what you're most proud of, which is underneath the Lincoln Memorial, if you want to discuss that. Well, underneath the Lincoln Memorial, it's built on essentially arches or stilts like a cathedral, so there's an undercroft 80 feet high that's only been filled with junk and graffiti from back when it was built in 1922. This summer there's been a long vision, but with President Trump's leadership and support of donors, they're opening an undercroft museum. It will tell a great story and hold great documents coming as part of the 250th anniversary. So 15,000 of the 50,000—there's a 50,000 foot cavern underneath the Lincoln—15,000 will be opening as a new museum on June 23rd of this year as part of Freedom 250. And again, we've never had a president like President Trump who's cared more, invested more, or put more time and attention into everything about the mall. When he drives around, if he sees something he calls us, we call the amazing National Park Service, they're on it, they fix it up. That's why we're fixing over 19 fountains around the city. That's why we've eliminated over a thousand graffiti sites. Under President Trump's leadership, we've taken care of 82 homeless camps that were in the city when he was elected, making DC safe and beautiful. Crime is down, beauty is up, all because of President Trump's leadership. So very big, thank you Doug and great job. Your people are fantastic. The whole Department of Interior, the parks, just amazing. And the police. The police have been incredible. You know, we have a very safe city now. We didn't have a safe city when I first took second term; it was a disaster what happened. And we now have a very safe city, people walk around, they love it. Frankly, doing this would be no good if we didn't have a safe city, but the crime, I think it's down about 88%, something like that. And Memphis too, and if you go down to New Orleans, we spent—I guess we're in the fourth month, fifth month—and we have it down about 78%. It's going to go down to 90%. Memphis is way down. But DC, I don't know, it's my—it's the capital and we had a very, very terrible crime right here and it's down now. We're gonna have it down, I think we'll get it above 92% down. We took 5,000 career criminals, people that were really, really bad. They came through the open border—I'm sure you guys love open borders, I don't think so. But they came through in the open borders, they came out of prisons and mental institutions and they came all over the place. It is horrible what happened, that they allowed this to happen to our country. But we got them out, and we had 11,888 murderers allowed into our country. We got them out; we got many of them out. We're looking for quite a few but we got many of them out. And the crime rate is—as you know, the murder rate in the country is down to the lowest it's ever been. It's down to in 1900, 125 years, lowest in recorded history and that's good. So we're very happy about that. And that goes hand in hand with fixing things up. So as Doug said, I think 19 or a lot of fountains, about 19 maybe more than that. We got Lafayette Park is beautiful, I made that contribution, it's pretty significant contribution and Lafayette Park is being rebuilt. We have tremendous amounts of things going on around Lincoln. I think to me the most exciting of all is gonna be the triumphal arch. We're gonna build one of the most beautiful arches in the world. If you look at Paris, in Paris they have the Arc de Triomphe; it's their number one tourist attraction, even more so than the Eiffel Tower. And we're gonna have one that's more grand, even more grand because that's one of—I would say that's the best right now. There are 59 triumphal arches throughout the world and this will be number 60. Many of them are very old, some are six, seven hundred years old. And they represented triumph, usually a victory, a military victory. But we are gonna have one that's gonna be, I think, superior to anybody else's. It's gonna be a little bit larger and I think a lot more beautiful. And that's gonna start, I would say, very soon. It was supposed to be built, you know, the circle by the bridge, by the—by the river. It's gonna be, I think it's gonna be something that will be so special. It's been there for—it's been there like that, it used to be a dirt path and it wasn't built because of the Civil War. Civil War got in the way. The eagles are up, that they were built later, but the eagles are up but it hasn't been built and people a lot of times they'll be going over the river, the bridge, and they'll be saying "why isn't this built?" And they've been saying that for way over 100 years. So you're gonna have something built there. So we're really fixing up Washington DC on a bigger front, even bigger front. Worldwide our military is unbelievable, they're doing an unbelievable job. We're negotiating with the Iranians. We have—you probably heard—we took our three destroyers and we rammed them through some pretty big stuff today and we knocked the hell out of them. The destroyers weren't hurt in any way. We—the people weren't hurt but they were firing at us and we were firing back at them and our firepower was a hell of a lot stronger than theirs and they knocked the hell out of them. They took down a lot of small boats—you know we call them small boats or fast boats, they're both small and they're fast with some weaponry on the front. You know this is what—so they—this is now replacing the Navy. They had a Navy 159 boats, they have none, okay? They're all at the bottom of the sea. So they replace that with what they call the fast boat. It's a fast boat, big deal, it's fast, got a machine gun in front. They've been knocked down a—they knocked down quite a few of them today. Yesterday they knocked down eight; they average eight a day it's to some group of people we're dealing with. So you know it's very simple, somebody said what's your plan? I said the plan is very simple: Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon, it cannot have a nuclear bomb and it's not going to have. And they're really at the end of the line, they really are at the end of the line. Now unless you guys feel differently, now these guys I think will say that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon, would you say? Yes sir. We wouldn't feel very good if they did because this what you're doing and all the work that you do and that we do it wouldn't look too very good after a while with these characters. We're not going to give them the right to have a nuclear weapon, there is zero chance and they know that and they've agreed to that. Let's see if they are willing to sign it. So do you have any questions, please? Mr. President, talk about your meeting with the President Lula earlier, was there anything agreed on? Yeah, we'll talk about it, but you know you have some of these construction workers that I love. I love them because they all voted for me. I would say 100 percent or 99, I think about 100. 100 percent. Yes sir. Can we get those guys over here so they're not standing there? We're on—oh there they are. I saw you over there, there's no way I'm leaving you out there fellas. So who didn't vote for Trump, anybody? Huh? Thank you. I would say that's right. We're from Oklahoma. We love you guys. We just said you agree: Iran cannot have a—I love that guy. Are you from Oklahoma? Yes sir. So, so, so Trump won Oklahoma 77 counties. Yes sir. I won all 77, right? I won it in a landslide like the election. But you know who second was? Ronald Reagan was at 56, I was at 77. And by the way, you have a very good basketball team. Yes sir. Yes sir. I was watching them the other night and I may try and come to one of those games. I like your basketball team, these guys are fantastic so they're doing a job, they're working hard. And they do—anybody think Iran should have a nuclear weapon so they can blow you up? No! No. It's a very important thing that we're doing, right? Yes sir. And oil is coming down and the stock market today just hit an all-time high. You know I was reading about doom and gloom. We had to do what we did. We took a little U-turn. We just hit the highest number in the history of our country, the stock market, and I said to my people we're gonna have to take a little U-turn and go to the lovely country of Iran and blow them up a little bit because they're not going to have a nuclear weapon and they're not. And we're not going to ruin your—these guys work so hard to make things beautiful, we're not going to let a nuclear weapon ruin it. So thank you guys. Are you all from Oklahoma? Yes sir. How did that work out? Well he's from big—they wanted super quality, right? That's right. Yes sir. We bring quality, Oklahoma brings quality. We love him, he's doing a good job Markwayne. You know Markwayne could have been a senator for the rest of his life, maybe could have been president, who the hell knows? I don't recommend it. But but he was your senator and he wanted to do this, he wanted to work with a man named Tom Homan who I'm sure you never heard of. But he showed up. So Tom, how good a president is Trump? Greatest president of my lifetime. Are you all legal? Yes sir. Yes. From Oklahoma the answer is probably Indian. Choctaw. Native. So you here pretty much until this gets finished? Yes sir, absolutely. Are you with the company, with the contract? I'm with those two men behind you, yes sir. That's good, that's good. You got a good looking crew back there fellas, huh? Yep. Thank you sir. Oklahoma. Mr. President, against the backdrop of the war in Iran, why focus on all these projects right now on the National Mall? You know why? Because I want to keep our country beautiful and safe. Beautiful also. This place was a disgusting place. It was Washington Monument, the Lincoln Memorial, and we had a—a terrible disgusting—I don't—you—you probably don't see dirt but I do. And you walk down this—this pond, if you would have walked down they'll tell you better than anybody, they had to take 11 or 12 truckloads of filth out of the water in front of the Lincoln Memorial. And it sat there for years like that. And that's not what our country is about. Our country is about beauty, cleanliness, safety, great people, not a filthy capital. That's such a stupid question to ask. You're—we're fixing up the reflecting pond to the Lincoln Memorial, the Washington Monument, and you say why are you fixing it up? Because you can understand dirt maybe better than I can but I don't allow it. This is one of the worst reporters, she's with ABC fake news and she's a horror show. She's saying why would you bother fixing this up? Why would I bother taking 11 or 12 truckloads of filth out of the water in front of the Lincoln Memorial? That's what made our country great. Beauty made our country. People made our country great. A question like that is a disgrace to our country. Any other questions? Mr. President, I'm also with ABC News, can I ask you—Fake news—About the virus, have you been briefed on the virus? Yes, I have. Can you tell us what you learned in these briefings? Should Americans be concerned? Well, I think you're gonna be told everything and you already have. It's very much—we hope under control. There was the ship and I think we're going to make a full report about it tomorrow. We have a lot of people, a lot of great people are studying it. It should be fine. We hope. Should Americans—should Americans be concerned that it's going to spread and how are you going to get Americans back? I hope not. I mean, I hope not. We'll do the best we can, yeah, to get the Americans that are on it back. Go ahead, please. Mr. President, detail your meeting with President Lula today, was there anything agreed on between the two of you? No, we had a great meeting with the president of Brazil. We're doing a lot of trading and we're going to do some increased trade. We talked about tariffs, we talked about—you know they would like to have some tariff relief. But we had a very good meeting. Good man, he's a smart guy. Mr. President, after these strikes, is the ceasefire with Iran still on? Yeah, it is. They trifled with us today. We blew them away. They trifled, I call that a trifle. I'll let you know when there's no ceasefire, you won't have to know. If there's no ceasefire, you're not going to have to know, you're just going to have to look at one big glow coming out of Iran. And they better sign their agreement fast. Could you give us an update on what is the latest in those talks? You mentioned yesterday they were doing better. No, the talks are going very well, but they have to understand if it doesn't get signed, they're going to have a lot of pain. They're going to have a lot of pain. They want to sign it, I will tell you, they want to sign it a lot more than I do. I think that's what you want to hear fellas, huh? You want to hear a different tone. That's the only thing they understand; they don't understand it. We had three world-class destroyers go through the—the strait today. Any other country under the circumstances wouldn't have done it—they shot missiles at it and drones at it and these stupid boats that came at it. They got blown away in about two minutes. Their tanker got blown—you know what we did with the tanker? We didn't want to create an environment so we shot out the rudder and the tanker's going around spinning around in circles. They should not have done that today. We thought they might, we didn't know, but we were prepared. They shot missiles; every missile was knocked down. Every drone was knocked down and the people that shot it are no longer with us. Mr. President, has the President of Iran officially responded to the one-page offer? Well, it's more than a one-page offer. It's an offer that basically said they will not have nuclear weapons, they're going to hand us the nuclear dust and many other things that we want. But we will have to wait for that. When they agree it doesn't mean much because the next day they forget—they forgot they agreed. And you know we're dealing with different sets of leaders. When you talk about regime change, you know they keep talking about regime change. Well, we got rid of the first regime, we got rid of the second regime, we got rid of most of the third regime and then they say "oh is that regime change?" I think it's the ultimate regime change. How close would you say that you are to a deal right now with Iran? Could happen any day. Could—and it might not happen—but it could happen any day. I believe they want the deal more than I do. Mr. President, are you aware of the FBI raid on Virginia state senator Louise Lucas's house yesterday? Who? Who? Virginia state senator Louise Lucas. She's a Democrat. I'm not aware of that, no. Mr. President, is your trip with China still going ahead and which companies you—you have about your trip? I have a very good relationship with President Xi. We've had a great economic time. China's been great for us economically, but not for other presidents. But we've done very well with China. We're going to have a meeting with President Xi; it's going to be, I think, quite amazing. He's been a—he's been a friend of mine. I got—I've gotten along with him very well over the years. We have had no problem and we've had no problem with China and Iran. You know everybody said "oh China China," but we've had no problem with China. Mr. President, have you spoken with Secretary Rubio since his meeting with Pope Bio and did you ask him to pass on a message? I spoke to him. I just said tell the—tell the Pope very nicely, very respectfully that Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon. So when he comes to their defense, also tell the Pope that Iran killed 42,000 innocent protesters who didn't have guns, who didn't have weapons. Tell that to the Pope. Mr. President, there is a Christian pastor in China that's been imprisoned since October. I know that members of your administration have pushed to highlight the protection of Christians. Are you going to press President Xi on his release? What's his name? His name's Ezra Jin. Are you going to press President Xi on his release? Well, I've heard about it but I will certainly look into it, yeah. I'll look into it, I'll bring it up, yeah. I've gotten a lot of people out. You know, I've gotten a lot of people out of different countries including China—people that were hostages or held—so we'll take a look at that, including Pastor Brunson as you know from—in that case it was Turkey. So we've—we've done a lot of that. I do a lot and I don't pay 6 billion dollars to get somebody out like Biden used to do and Obama; they give 6 billion dollars to get some person out. What's your reaction sir to the Court of International Trade's ruling against your 10 percent across the board tariffs? Well, we were surprised. We had one very positive vote; we had two radical left judges that voted against it. So nothing surprises me with the courts. Nothing surprises me. So we always do it a different way. We get one ruling and we do it a different way. We're taking in hundreds of billions of dollars from tariffs and we're taking—we're taking it away from countries frankly that have ripped us off for years and now they're not ripping us off anymore. What happened to project Vita, Mr. President? Why was that suspended? Administration officials are talking about that as being the next phase of the war. There is project—really wonderful leadership at that place. As you know, Pakistan has been fantastic and their leaders have been fantastic, the field marshal and the prime minister. And they asked us not to do it—we'll go back to it if we have to—they asked us not to do it during the negotiation. Are you considering maybe going back to that now that gas prices are about four dollars? It could—well no, gas prices have come down today. Have you looked? They've come down very substantially today, the gas prices. You know what's happened today? Gas prices are way down and the stock market is way up today. So gas prices have come down and I—I saw today that they broke negatively, in other words to our positive position, they broke our way very substantially. And when the war is over, gas will fall down at levels that you've never seen before. You'll be down to where you were, maybe even lower. We had it down to—I was in Iowa and we were at 85—for about 85—what did you say? Yeah, I think it was lower. We broke—we broke two dollars. We broke it, we broke it. We had numbers that nobody's seen in a long time. So you had two dollars a gallon. We were down—I think you were a dollar 85, dollar 90 in Iowa and a lot of other places. Outside of California where California charges such a high tax on the gasoline, when you take that tax off. But California has a tremendous—I don't know if anybody knows, California was the third largest area for oil. And you were telling me and Chris was telling me before we're talking about California oil. They—California is like an oil—it's rich with oil.
