Yeah, so you know, it's interesting when I spoke to the firm about it the first time when we started American Dynamism and so forth, I said, "Look, you know, like, you can have whatever geopolitical views you want to have, but like the world is what it is." You know, it's not like everybody has agreed on peace worldwide. And it's not as if everybody's agreed not to commit crime ever. So, like, we do have crime, we do have war, we have these things. And then you have to think about, okay, the people that we grew up with who have gone into the military, risked their lives to save our lives: you are making the call because you are better ethically and you know more about geopolitics than the people in the State Department and the people running the Department of War and the people running the intelligence agencies? You're going to decide that those people who are sacrificing their lives, putting their lives on the line to protect us, don't get the best technology? Like, I'm not doing that. I'm not doing that. So, like, you don't have to work here if you want to do that. That would be crazy. Think you know better than the State Department. Yes. Yeah, I mean we have this issue, I mean as we're board members of these companies and, you know, the founder will call us, the CEO will call us and say, "You know, I have some employees that don't think we should do business with this country or this country." And it's like, are you the State Department? Do you know better? It's crazy. And by the way, what a slippery slope to go down. You don't want to be in that position. Yeah, these issues are complicated. I mean, they really are complicated. And I think that these... you know, this dime-store morality that companies come up with... you know, it's like a vibe, vibe, vibe geopolitics. Right, that's right. Okay, so we call it... I want to shift gears a little bit. We call it American Dynamism, but it is more than America. It's about America and her allies. You have spent a lot of time... I know this because sometimes I join you, but I also get a little bit of visibility into your travel schedule... you've spent a lot of time on the road internationally. A lot of foreign leaders come to you and they say, "You know, we want a startup culture, we want American Dynamism, we want to reinvigorate a startup community, do manufacturing, build for defense." You know, how do you think about: how can we export our capabilities, our advantages, our culture to these other countries, especially ones that we may have supply chain dependencies on and who are our allies? Especially ones that are connected to us like Mexico, Canada, Latin America. How can we take American Dynamism abroad? Yeah, so you know, it's an interesting question. I don't have a full answer because we haven't quite entirely figured it out. But I'll say this: you know, we... because America does give everybody a chance, and entrepreneurs can really count on that. I mean, that's a thing. Like, if you're going to basically bet your whole life on an idea and build something, you kind of have to believe that the government isn't just going to arbitrarily take it away from you at any point. And there are very few countries that have that going for it. So we are reasonably unique. There are a couple of places that have great technology entrepreneurship. Sweden is one, Israel is one. But it's pretty rare around the world. Having said that, you know, there are... there is great expertise in things that we need help with, particularly to industrialize. So in Mexico, they are... they do have really good, you know, high-quality manufacturing expertise. In some ways, you know, I think the cars that come out of... the American cars that come out of Mexico are better than kind of the Chinese manufacturing on many fronts. So they do have talent, they are partners, they want to work with us. We need to help secure that country because, you know, it's a border, but it's not a thick border, it's a thin border. You know, we have a lot of people vacationing down there and whatnot. And so that's a real thing. Similarly, Japan... you know, they've always loved robots and they're really good at manufacturing. And, you know, we have a big deficit on the kind of robot supply chain. So, you know, we're working with them at the same time. They just went from 0% of GDP to 3% of GDP spent on defense. We have a lot of defense companies. Their interests and our interests are very aligned when it comes to China. So I think there are some great opportunities, you know, both kind of for us as a firm, our companies, and then the country in working with allies around the world. Yeah, the recent advancements in robotics... we haven't spent a lot of time talking about it today. I'm sure we will next year. But it's really... it feels like we're on the precipice of a robotics revolution. And it's going to be very, very exciting to see that play out and for sure a lot of our companies and a lot of our allies will have a role to play. In a recent deep dive, you know, Packy McCormick did this long deep dive on the firm. Thought it came out pretty well. He described the firm as a power broker using capital and networks to shape markets and influence. How do you view that and balance that power with the need to remain authentic to our founders, to institutions? How do we leverage that power? You know, obviously we're here in DC. How do you think about that responsibility and how do we use it to advance the state of the art? Well, kind of what you lack as an entrepreneur is power. So you have a great invention, you may have built a great technology, but you don't have the power to call anybody, you don't have the power to kind of get the right meeting necessarily with the right people in Congress to kind of help get the regulation correct. You don't have the power to get an audience with the right CEO to buy your product and that kind of thing. So power is sort of a feature of our offering is the way I think about it. And that's very different than the culture that we want to have and want to project internally. And as you know, kind of our very first kind of cultural idea is first-class business and only in a first-class way. And what that means when it comes to entrepreneurs is the ultimate respect for what they're doing. It doesn't mean we're always their best friend or anything like that, but we show up on time, we get back to them in a timely fashion, we're honest, we do all the little things to make sure that our overall behavior isn't "we're the powerful and you're the weak" because, you know, that obviously would be a short ride for our firm at the top. What do you think about, you know, as people watch us raising so much capital? It happened during a period where I think broader the VC industry had slowed down fundraising, but we closed very quickly, we raised a massive amount of capital. Do you think that signals anything about where venture is going as an ecosystem, as an industry? What do you think it looks like in the state of venture capital today? Yeah, so this is something that we've seen coming for a long time and that we kind of prepared for. If you go back to when we started the firm, there was a study that was done that showed that in any given year, there were basically approximately 15 companies that would be started that would ever achieve $100 million a year in revenue. And so the whole idea of venture capital was that you had to get in as many of those 15 as possible, and you really didn't want to be in anything that wasn't one of those. And so the way venture capital firms were constructed was as these partnerships in both economics and control that were small. Because why have, you know, 100 or 500 or 600 people like we do now, if you're only going after 15 companies? It doesn't make sense. But what we saw back then was, well, even though that had been true for decades, it wasn't going to be true anymore because as Marc wrote, software's eating the world. And so what that meant is that there was going to be no industry, no company that wasn't going to be better if there was a great software team at its core. And, you know, that's even evolving now into AI. And so basically every interesting company that gets started is a technology company now. And so there are many, many more interesting companies, and so in order to reach it, you had to be able to scale your organization. Now, here's the problem. If you were one of those venture capital firms that started all those years ago, you were likely set up, as I said, as a partnership with shared economics and shared control. If you have shared control, it's very hard to reorganize. Because, and you know this David as a CEO, to reorganize you need a single decision-maker. There is no way to reorganize if everybody gets a vote. It's going to be bad. I could write a whole book on just reorgs. But reorgs are essentially redistributions of power. When you redistribute power, people are mad. If they get a vote, they're going to foul that reorganization. And you can't scale without reorging. So as a result, like, we've been able to scale because we have centralized control, but you know, most of our kind of competitors have not been able to scale. Like, a very few have. And so I think what we have now is large firms who can cover all the technological areas and then specialized firms that are: "Okay, I'm a specialist in AI infrastructure," or "I'm a specialist in bio," or "I'm a specialist in crypto," or "I'm a specialist in games," but I'm not doing all of that. And the kind of firms in the middle, I think, are getting squeezed out. I think that's right. People sometimes ask me, like, "How do you work at such a massive firm, you know, 600 plus people?" And I say, "Look, well, we're actually a bunch of really small teams that leverage a bunch of shared platform services, much like our portfolio companies." And that allows us to move with urgency but have the might of a very, very large organization behind us. I think one of the other things that I think is interesting that people don't always realize about the firm is there's probably more CEOs as general partners inside the firm than there's got to be than any other firm. And I think all of us recognize: having a hierarchy and a benevolent dictatorship is really appealing. You know, it works. Yeah, it works. And, you know, it makes our life easy. My job: focus on American Dynamism. It's very simple. You know, Martine's job: focus on infra and being the best you can be, figure out what we need. And then it really is a dynamic that I think these death-by-committee kind of venture firms just cannot adapt to. No, definitely. You've talked in a recent podcast about how the media landscape has shifted, how reputation can shape markets. We had Alex Karp here earlier today. He is phenomenal about going direct to his customers. How do you think that shift... it seems like we're seeing that shift in some ways in the government, Emil Michael going on X directly. How would you coach founders and even maybe coach people that are in the government, where narrative and trust is paramount in terms of communicating and thinking about media and communications? Yeah, so look, I think that the media world has changed and it can be confusing because I think that you don't need to think about it in terms of necessarily old media and new media, in that everybody's in the same game, but the rules of the game have changed. And what I mean by that is in the old world, the key to a media strategy was defense. And the reason that the key was defense is: there's a limited number of channels. Those channels have very strict formats. So if you're in the New York Times or the Wall Street Journal, you can get a few quotes in and then you'll be characterized on the rest of what you say. If you're on CNN, you get, like, a short and hostile conversation with a bright light in your face, and so forth. And then because there were a limited number of channels, if you said something wrong, you couldn't erase it. It was kind of like on the permanent record. And there are kind of many cases of these old-school gaffes, like Howard Dean and so forth, where you could never erase it and you could never get over it and so forth. In the new kind of world, there's unlimited channels, there's unlimited formats. And so the key to winning isn't not making a mistake, it's being interesting. Like if you don't say anything that's interesting, it's not going to get any pickup because there are too many other interesting things out there. People like Alex Karp and Donald Trump and so forth who are just like an entertaining show... like Alex was super entertaining out here. I was captivated. I didn't know what he was going to say next. I don't know if he knew what he was going to say next, but it was really... he is very entertaining. And that wins. And then like if you make a mistake, you just do 10 podcasts tomorrow and flood the zone. And it doesn't matter whether the channel is the Wall Street Journal or a podcast or whatever. It's all the same thing now. Because that's the way... you're overwhelmed with media. And so to win you have to be interesting. And then, you know, that doesn't mean you can just be all over the place and not be on message. Alex is always very, very, very consistent, pro-America. Like that's the thing that you can always count on him to be, and that's his main message. And that's great. And he's interesting about it. He's got a great knowledge of history and all these kinds of things and so forth. Awesome. Okay, so we have placed the largest bet in American history on the proposition that this country will win the next century of technology. So what... and I do feel very optimistic. I think you and I are both optimists. I think our firm is broadly very optimistic. In fact, I would say that's ultimately even a core value of the firm is being optimistic about the future. Well, what keeps you up at night? What worries you? What would you ask of the audience, some of whom are founders, some of whom are policymakers, some of whom are implementers? What worries you and keeps you up at night, and what can we do to help? So actually my biggest worry is kind of the perception of technology in America. There was an interesting kind of poll that went out that showed that like, I think over 70% of people in China are optimistic about AI and kind of less than 30% in America or something like that were optimistic about AI. And it's really because we always talk about all the bad things. You know, we're very focused on the dangers of AI here and not on the positive things. And it was interesting, I just spoke to someone from Japan who was like, "You know, the whole Japanese startup ecosystem is restarting because everybody's so fired up about AI. We're all very positive on it. We love robots." You know, we can't... Japanese love robots, by the way. And you know, I think we really need to focus... somebody asked me the other day, "Well, what good can AI do?" and I was like, "Well, we can, we're gonna... not just we can, we're gonna end traffic deaths." We're going to cure cancer. We're gonna end poverty as we know it. Like those are pretty good things. Like we need to think about that as much as we think about, you know, the AI overlords or mass surveillance or any of these other things that we worry about. I think that the positive uses of the technology are extremely positive. The negative ones are there, but we can manage them in the same way we've managed, you know, kind of the negative of every technology starting with fire, which has very bad consequences. It can burn down your village; it's horrible. True. But it does a lot of, you know, it also heats the house and cooks your food and does some very wonderful things. And I think we have to get back to that.

Marc Lore's Blueprint For Scaling Billion-Dollar Startups

Forbes(2.1M)·27:12·625 views·2026-05-08
Marc LoreFounder, Chairman, and CEO, Wonder Group Moira ForbesHost, Forbes
TL;DRMarc Lore is leveraging vertical integration and "physical AI" at Wonder to disrupt the trillion-dollar food industry, utilizing a math-driven scaling framework (VCP) to transition from software-only models to capital-intensive physical infrastructure that creates a defensible market moat.

TL;DR

Marc Lore is leveraging vertical integration and "physical AI" at Wonder to disrupt the trillion-dollar food industry, utilizing a math-driven scaling framework (VCP) to transition from software-only models to capital-intensive physical infrastructure that creates a defensible market moat.

The Wonder Thesis: "Cloud Kitchens" for the Physical World

  • Vertical Integration: Lore argues that food delivery aggregators (e.g., DoorDash, Uber Eats) hit a ceiling because they don't own the production. Wonder owns the restaurants, the cooking process, and the delivery to ensure quality and timing.
  • Kitchen as Software: Wonder is "decoupling the kitchen from the restaurant," similar to how the cloud revolutionized software.
      • Wonder Create: A platform launching late 2024 allowing individuals to launch and scale a digital restaurant across Wonder’s infrastructure for $10/month.
  • Operational Density: A single 2,500-square-foot Wonder kitchen can house 25 different restaurant brands across 20 cuisines (from Bobby Flay steaks to Jose Andres tapas) using electric-only equipment and no open flames.
  • Advanced Logistics: By owning the tech stack, Wonder sequences cooking so that a multi-restaurant family order finishes simultaneously and is timed perfectly with the courier, resulting in 5-minute average delivery times in urban areas.

The High-Stakes Pivot: From Vans to Brick-and-Mortar

  • The "Cook-en-Route" Experiment: Wonder initially utilized 450 Mercedes Sprinter vans to cook food outside customer homes. While customers loved the quality, the model was too capital-intensive and difficult to scale.
  • The Hard Reset: In early 2023, Lore pivoted the entire company to a brick-and-mortar "hub" model after seeing just three weeks of superior data from a test location.
      • Financial Impact: He took a $100M loss and drove revenue to zero to restart with the more scalable model.
      • Current Trajectory: Wonder has opened 120 locations in three years; Lore aims to reach 1,000 locations within the next 3.5 years.

The VCP Framework: Vision, Capital, People

Lore manages his ventures through a proprietary "VCP" system to ensure foundational stability during hyper-growth:

  • Vision: Writing down a 10–15 year vision and building an organizational structure with zero dependencies. Lore hires top-tier leaders and empowers them to run their departments without needing cross-functional approval, which he defines as "true focus."
  • Capital Strategy: Lore uses a "magic number" (66.66) to determine funding needs based on target valuation.
      • The Formula: Divide the target exit valuation by 66.66 to find the necessary seed round. (e.g., To build a $10B company, the seed should be $150M).
      • The 18-Month Rule: The goal is to double the company's valuation and the amount of capital raised every 18 months.
  • People & Performance: Lore emphasizes that investors often over-focus on numbers while ignoring the "foundational systems" of culture and compensation that allow an organization to pivot when things go wrong.

Automation and "Physical AI" as the New Moat

  • Disrupting Labor Costs: While e-commerce fights for basis points of margin, restaurants have 30% labor costs ripe for automation.
  • Robotics Integration: Wonder acquired Spice Robotics (an automated bowl-making machine) from Sweetgreen to automate high-volume concepts.
  • Infrastructure as a Moat: Lore believes capital-intensive businesses are now more attractive than pure software companies because physical infrastructure and proprietary robotics are harder for AI to disintermediate.
  • IPO Goals: Lore plans to take Wonder public in approximately 11 months to gain access to public currency for further brand acquisitions.

Notable Quotes

"We’re doing what the cloud did to software. We’re decoupling the kitchen from the restaurant so that we could scale restaurants like software." — Marc Lore
"I think now with AI... software's become a bad word in the venture community. And here we are... being respected for building a real moat with physical infrastructure and physical AI." — Marc Lore
"Focus is a little bit misunderstood... A lot of it comes down to organizational structure—having the right level person on whatever it is that they're focused on [with no dependencies]." — Marc Lore
"If you have the best people, real clarity around vision, and the right organizational structure that allows people to run and be empowered... magical things will happen." — Marc Lore
I want to know what is the ability of that company to hire the very best people in the world. So you have to be able to identify great talent, you need to convince them to come to the organization, and then you have to get the very best that they have to give. And getting all three of those right is hard. And organizations that have figured that out are the ones that are going to ultimately win. Mark, thanks so much for sitting down with us today. You've built companies across a number of different areas or eras in business: e-commerce in the early 2000s when nobody believed things like diapers could sell online; the marketplace wars of the 2010s with Jet; and now Wonder in this really AI-saturated and capital-tight 2026. From the inside, what feels the same across these eras and what's changed as you're now leaning into Wonder? Yeah, I mean in all three startups I've always, you know, picked capital-intensive businesses that are, I think, able to give us a real moat around the business. I think in the earlier days, the first two startups, that that was a bad thing to actually have a lot of capital required to get to scale. But I think Amazon sort of laid the path for others to follow. And I think now with AI disintermediated software companies, software's become a bad word in the venture community. And here we are sitting here finally where we're being respected for building a real moat with physical infrastructure and physical AI. So it's kind of an exciting time because after 25 years of capital being a headwind, now it seems to be a tailwind. You've called food the last trillion-dollar category without an Amazon. And people have been trying to dominate this category—food delivery—for 15 or so years: DoorDash, Uber Eats, Grubhub and the like. And most often we've seen them scale either into mediocrity or oftentimes hitting a ceiling. Walk us through the actual thesis of Wonder. What have the companies that have tried to tackle the food and restaurant space missed that you're now trying to fulfill on? Yeah, I mean I'm just looking at the customer value proposition as a user of food delivery aggregators. And you know, the customer service, the long delivery times, the high fees and things, and just felt like it was ripe for vertical integration—like not just doing the delivery but also owning the restaurants, doing the cooking, and being able to stitch the cooking and the delivery together in a sequenced way that elevates the customer experience. That was the original thinking. I think over the last couple of years with advancements in AI and robotics, I think we're thinking much bigger now. We're thinking about completely reimagining what it even means to be a kitchen. And at Wonder, we're doing what the cloud did to software. We're decoupling the kitchen from the restaurant so that we could scale restaurants like software. And in fact, we're at the end of the year we're launching something called Wonder Create, which will allow any individual in the world to build a restaurant on the Wonder platform for ten bucks a month and instantaneously scale it across all locations utilizing Wonder's back-end supply chain management, ingredient sourcing, cooking of the food, delivering of the food. So all you need to do is market your restaurant. So we see a future where there are literally going to be millions, if not tens of millions, of restaurant owners that own restaurants only in the digital world, and they're there to market it, and it would all exist on the Wonder infrastructure. How complicated has it been for you creating that vertical infrastructure in this category? Because it seems like Wonder is your biggest bet to date in a lot of different dimensions. Walk us through the complexities and all the different pieces you've had to put together to be able to fulfill this value proposition. Yeah, it has taken seven years and over two billion dollars of capital, so it was much harder I would say than we probably originally thought. It's that entrepreneur naivety that I love—makes you dump into something that people say is impossible. I think the hardest part was, we have now in a single 2500 square foot kitchen we've got 25 different restaurants across 20 different cuisines. So everything from a high-end steakhouse Bobby Flay steak to Jose Andres' Spanish tapas to burgers, barbecue, Chinese, pizza, Italian, Thai, Middle Eastern—just about every cuisine you could imagine all being cooked in a systematic way in 2500 square feet with no gas, no open flames, only electric cooking equipment with very lightly trained labor. And then increasingly more we're adding robotics into the back end. We've completely automated the expo area, which is the toughest part in a restaurant. We just bought Spice Robotics, which is the infinite bowl-making machine; we bought that from Sweetgreen. So in the very near future all the bowl concepts will be completely automated without labor. So yeah, I think that has been the toughest part. I think, you know, acquiring Grubhub Seamless for the delivery piece of it was also critical. And we built all our own tech that stitches it together in between. So we can sequence the cooking. If you order—if you're a family of four and order from four different restaurants—all the food finishes cooking at the same time. But more importantly, it's timed with the courier, so the food doesn't sit in the kitchen and the courier doesn't wait. And it's all integrated and we set a really tight delivery radius so we're able to get it to you in the city—our average delivery time is five minutes—and in the suburbs it's just over 10 minutes. So it's just a faster, more on-time, hotter multi-restaurant order experience that we've been able to create. Many many different pieces that go into it but seamless on the other side as the consumer when they get to experience these. You talked about Wonder being much harder in a lot of different ways than you expected. Can you walk us through a couple of those elements that proved to be more complicated or really also maybe even helped you see the opportunity of this business given that complexity? Yeah, I think the first thing that was super interesting to me was just how much higher the margins are in restaurants than they are in e-commerce. And e-commerce we've got a ton of automation because you're fighting for basis points. And I think in the restaurant world there's so much margin that there really hasn't been this focus on needing to automate. But that really, when you think about it, is ripe for automation because there's 30 points of labor, you know, in every kitchen plus or minus. And so it's really that's where the automation should be focused and it hasn't to date. So I think that that is a really really big opportunity. You have mentioned that early on with Wonder, for example, you had a different business model. A couple of times in your businesses you've had to ultimately pivot. Can you walk us through this evolution in terms of the early concept and where you really saw the opportunity moving forward today? Yeah, so we started with this idea—vertical integration, same vision—but instead of the traditional cook and then deliver the food, we thought what if we deliver then cook? And so we basically built these kitchens in the back of Mercedes sprinter vans and it would drive to your house and then cook the food in six to ten minutes outside your door and deliver it. And we had it down where the driver could cook so there was no extra labor. So you really weren't wasting any time because you have to drive anyway and you have to cook anyway, and the same person was doing it. So the customers loved it. It worked really well. We had 450 trucks on the road. We had an insane Net Promoter Score. But it was very capital intensive. It only worked in the suburban areas and it was definitely harder to scale the labor model. And so some complexities there. At the same time, we had tested a brick-and-mortar and we said what if we—each truck had a restaurant on it and we had now, you know, 25 different restaurants—and we thought what if we take all 25 restaurants and put it in a brick-and-mortar and set a tight delivery radius? Could we sort of replicate the quality? If so, this would work in urban, suburban, exurban, rural. The TAM was much bigger, much less capital, more efficient labor model. So we opened a brick-and-mortar location at the end of 2022. After three weeks of data, I said, "This is it. This is it. That's the future." And you know, we didn't really have the resources to run both models at the same time so we had to make a decision. And so we pulled 450 trucks off the road, sold it, took an 80 million, 100 million dollar loss, took revenue to zero and started over in early '23. And then in the last three years we opened, you know, 120 locations, which is fast to our knowledge ever to do that. And in the next three and a half years we'll have a thousand. So we're just running really fast trying to make up lost ground. It's—I don't know if you've ever missed a turn when you're driving and you miss an exit—you wind up driving faster to get back because you feel like you gotta make up lost time. That's how I've felt the last three years. Like we lost a couple of years and so like we gotta go, you know. We don't have time to do it the way we would have done it if we had unlimited time. When you were first looking at this opportunity and this idea, did you feel at that moment in time just how ambitious it was? I mean with every startup I'm always thinking bigger. You know with Jet, thinking bigger than diapers. I think with diapers.com, you know, I was thinking "Wow, this could be a billion dollar business," right? This was the first real one. And we got off the train at 550 million. And then with Jet, I was like "Okay, I think this actually could be a 10 billion dollar business." And 26 months after we started we got off at 3 billion, right? So in the same order of magnitude but just earlier than the goal. And then I think with Wonder, you know, started and said "I think this could be a 100 billion dollar business." And so but we're not going to get off the train. We're going to take this thing public. And what's interesting—and this hasn't happened before—I think now with the new model and the advancements in AI and robotics, I do think this has the potential now to be a trillion dollar market cap company, which is super exciting as a founder. It's a founder's dream to have that size TAM and market opportunity and be a disruptor. So I'm all in on this. I've dedicated my life to making this happen at this point. You've talked about this IPO for Wonder as would be the biggest entrepreneurial achievement of your career today. What does an IPO represent to you at this moment in time that your other exits didn't? Yeah, I think one is raising access to the capital markets. So this does require billions of dollars of more capital. So access to the public market—we'll be ready to go public in like 11 months, so that's exciting. And also having a public currency to be able to make acquisitions. So a big critical part of our strategy is to acquire the best restaurant brands in the country and buy them and then be able to arb that acquisition by blowing it up on the platform without having to add any new capital or open up any new locations. So very excited about going public for those two reasons. I'd be curious, how has this venture stretched you as an entrepreneur or built different muscles that you may not have expected having already built such strong entrepreneurial muscles in your previous ventures? Yeah, I mean I think the rate at which we're growing is unprecedented in my career—like I haven't done that before. I think the pace of acquisitions as well and some big ones, you know buying Grubhub for example, so that's all new. I think the level of executives that we brought in is a whole other level than anything I've dealt before. So that's also new, you know. Yeah, I think I feel like I'm just learning every day and becoming a better entrepreneur. A lot of the VCP—the Vision Capital People systems that we built at diapers, I've watched it evolve into Jet and then into Wonder. And those systems continue to evolve, you know, the performance management system, compensation systems, and how we think about org structure. And it's just fun to keep learning and evolving every day. When you look at scaling these businesses so quickly and you look at the different phases and what you love, what phase of Wonder are you in right now in a way that sort of really brings out that same entrepreneurial grit that you've had for your entire life? Yeah, I think we're at a really good spot now because I think the last three years like I said, once we went brick-and-mortar, we're trying to put all the pieces together very quickly—buying a delivery network, you know, building the units, buying the restaurants, building restaurants—like we're doing a lot of foundational work. And I feel like we're at a stage now where, and then with the acquisition of the robotics company, I think we're really crystal clear on what the sort of 2040 vision is. And we have all the pieces in place to go execute against that vision. And it's really a matter of like continuing to scale, continuing to keep the culture strong because it's all about execution at this point. And making sure that the food quality continues to improve as we scale, which it has been, which is probably the most exciting and most focused part of where my brain is, which is food quality getting better as we scale. You know, when you talk about this business, particularly with the vertical integration and just the complexity of it, it's really significant and meaningful. When you work with your teams and you're building out your strategy, vision, capital allocation and the like, how do you really exercise the art of focus? How do you ensure that you're continuing to align all the different efforts around the ultimate goals of the business when you do have different complexities, even acquisitions and the like, that may not have existed in your previous businesses? Yeah, I think focus is a little bit misunderstood, you know. I think people look at the number of things you're doing and the more things you're doing, the it's—you know—a lay person would say that's lacking focus. But I think when you look under the hood you have to: one, see how the pieces fit together, you know, in a really compelling strategy and all tied to the vision. I think that's most important. But equally probably as important is building the organizational structure so that you have no dependencies from one piece to another. So you hire a great person that focuses on a particular area and there's no dependencies on any other area, then that person is as focused as they could possibly be. You have other organizations that maybe only doing a few things and they have a upside-down org structure where there's dependencies left, right, and center and there's lacking focus because no one's accountable and no one has the ability to execute without having to rely on somebody else. And so I think a lot of it comes down to organizational structure—having the right level person on whatever it is that they're focused on. You talk about the organizational structure, but what are the things that you think are most underestimated when it comes to what it's taken to build Wonder, what it's taken to get to where you are today to really see the big bold vision that you've set out? What do people not understand has been as complicated that really also is the real value add and the real advantage that you're creating in this category? Yeah, I mean just I think with any business and Wonder's no different, it will always come down to the quality of the people in the organization. And so when I look at a company and judge whether a company's going to be successful, I want to know what is the ability of that company to hire the very best people in the world to do what they need to do. And so it starts with, you know, building a culture that is very attractive, having the vision to sell the individuals into the organization, and then having the right performance management system, compensation systems—the foundational systems—to get the best that they have to give. So you have to be able to identify great talent, you need to convince them to come to the organization and then you have to get the very best that they have to give. And getting all three of those right is hard. And organizations that have figured that out are the ones that are going to ultimately win. And I think people miss that. Like investors certainly miss that. I don't get questions at all about the actual infrastructure required and our ability to attract, retain, get the best out of the best people. Because if you have the best people and you have real clarity around the vision and strategy and you have the right organizational structure that allows people to run and be empowered without having dependencies, then magical things will happen. Why do you think investors don't spend time on that? And obviously you want to hear business and strategy but at the end of the day it's all about people to lead the execution and to build those things. Yeah, I mean I think some of it is just I've just learned human nature. I mean some investors do get it and they do focus on it; most don't. I think it's just human nature to want to look at the numbers. Like "Show me the numbers. I want to—I'm a smart person—I want to independently look at those numbers and I want to make a decision." But if you looked back when we had trucks on the road and you looked at the numbers, well—I don't know—a year later, three years later, it doesn't look anything like it did back then. And so you were looking at the numbers—fine—but you weren't looking at the infrastructure and the organization's ability to recognize that something's not working and to be able to pivot. And in order to be able to pivot and find the opportunity, you need the right people and you need the right structure. So more important than anything I think is people and structure and then your ability to raise capital. That's the other thing. So you could have great people, great vision, clarity, structure and everything but if you can't raise capital then that's also an issue. And so I would also look at, you know, the company's ability to raise capital, their history of raising capital. So... When you talk about capital obviously that's sort of the fuel to be able to grow and scale a business. You have a lot of hard-won wisdom and insights around what founders need to do to raise capital today, to really be able to look at what amount is needed and the dollar figures they actually should be going to raise toward. Can you walk us through that strategy and where you see some lack of alignment today in terms of where and how people are going out to raise money in this current market? Yeah, I mean first I always in every startup had a what I call capital plan. So from the very first day when you start the company, try to understand how much money you want to raise with each round, what you want the valuation to be, how much dilution, you know, ultimately is from each round that you're taking and kind of map that out day one. It all starts from the level of ambition. Because your very first seed round needs to be tied to the level of ambition. And so I have a very simple thing: you know, figure out what size company you ultimately want to create, divide by 66.66—that's the magic number. That tells you basically what the seed round should be. So if you want to create a hundred million dollar company and you divide by 66.66, the seed round's 1.5 million. Billion, it's 15 million. Hundred billion—oh sorry, 10 billion—then it's 150 million, right? And so it's actually and then you start over with that seed round. Let's say 20% dilution on the first round. The goal and the rule of thumb is to try and double the valuation and double the amount raised every 18 months. So that's kind of the way I plan it out. So if you start—you want to create a hundred million dollar business—you raise one and a half million at a four and a half pre, six post, you know that's 25% dilution. And then you say "Okay, now in the next 18 months I want to raise 3 million at a 12 pre" because that was a six post. And you make it in bite-sized chunks, right? And then after you raise that round, then you want to double again and you say "Okay now I want to raise, you know, 6 million at double the valuation and then 12 at double the valuation and 25 at double the valuation." And after six rounds of financing you basically have yourself your hundred million dollar business. And so you just break it down into these chunks. And what it does is when you're building your budget, you sort of know how much capital you have in between raises and you know that you have to double the valuation. And so you just work backwards. And that's the way I've always done it. And at Diapers and Jet it wasn't perfect but every round, you know, every investor made 100% compounded annual return because that's the way it was structured. It was to double the valuation each round. And so if you want—there we did it in sort of 12 months instead of 18—but if you do it in 18 it's a little less than 100% IRR but it's still an amazing IRR. The founder's still left with, you know, probably 15%—I don't remember the exact math, you know, at the end—and so everything works. What I find is sometimes people they undercapitalize it or they raise too much money too soon and it's hard then to make the returns that investors require because every investor—the seed round is different from the A, different from the B, different from the C—they're different investors and they have a different profile that they invest in. And if you stick to this trajectory, you're kind of at the right place at the right valuation for the right investor each round. Mark, I—you know—we've talked about this, how much I think the process and the intention and the foundation has been so instrumental and so unique in terms of how you've been able to do what you do over and over again. When people look at your success, what do you think they underestimate in terms of what's been a driving force? Are there things that people don't understand in terms of what it's really taken or in terms of what your superpowers are as it relates to building again and again at this level? Yeah, I mean there's a lot of pieces to that. I've built this framework called VCP: Vision Capital People. Each has a few different components to it. I spend 80 to 90% of my time thinking about VCP. When you think about Vision it is: what is your 10 to 15 year vision and getting that written down on a piece of paper? What are the core strategies, the 'how' of achieving that vision? What is org—what's the organizational structure that backs the strategy? So again, you have the right people in the right spots driving the most important strategies and metrics without any key dependencies. And how you build that is really important. Having a capital plan under C and having the right pitch deck and the right story and just spending time on that. And then on under the P is, you know, having a clear mission, a clear set of behaviors, a clear compensation system, performance management system. We actually have a room in the office called VCP and we have all these things I just mentioned on whiteboards around the room. And every week our team meets to talk about VCP. It's sort of—the analogy is, you know, a basketball player doing foundational work. So they could be an NBA player but still sitting under the basket and just without even a ball just working, working the form. And that's what I think is missing. A lot of people just running around, you know, putting out fires but not always taking the time to do the foundational work. Because when everyone in the organization understands the nuances of the vision and strategy, in every word—what does 'very' mean? what does 'a lot' mean?—really the nuances of it and that permeates throughout the organization, then everyone is thinking like an owner, everyone's thinking like the founder. And that's leverage. Very much so, if you can create that at scale in a company and really unique to do. Mark, as we wrap up, I'm really struck because you're not someone who's had one success and then is happy just to take a step back and be on vacation for 365 years. You are incredibly driven, you know, all the things I read, incredibly competitive as well. You have to be to do what you've done. When you look at the companies you're building, obviously Wonder super ambitious, have a strategy, a plan in front of you. When you take a step back, what are you building toward? At what point do you feel like you will have achieved success as you define it? Yeah, well first I, you know, I think you have to have a mission that you're really passionate about. It's tough to just say "I'm just building this to make money"—it doesn't, that's not that motivational. But our mission at Wonder is to make great food more accessible. And so we're bringing great food to suburban and exurban areas that don't have access to great food. We're open now till a.m. in the morning in the suburbs and even in exurban areas. Again, where there's only fast food available, so people that are working night shifts at the hospital, they now could have access to better quality food. We're bringing down the price point of great food. We've got a couple of great concepts, salad concept and a fast-casual Mexican that's 30% cheaper than what you'll find in the market for the same quality and portion size. That really fuels me. I love the idea of making food, great food, more accessible to people. And it's such a big mission and it's sort of our north star. We're never going to get there. We're never going to say we checked the box, great food is now accessible. Like you could always do more. And so we're very driven to do that. And so that that's fun. And then as an entrepreneur, you know, you do think about the brands that people talk about from centuries ago. And what was it about those businesses that allowed it to last centuries? I think that's the dream of any entrepreneur, right? To build a business that has that kind of legacy. And so there's some fun in that as well. So I think I would say the combination of that plus the mission is really what fuels me and why I love to get up every morning and do what I do. I love that, yes, to build businesses that outlast you. That's one of the greatest testaments to a successful founder. Well Mark, it's really exciting what you're building with Wonder. I can't wait to see what's ahead, particularly as you look down the IPO route. A really interesting moment for the company but I would imagine for you and your entrepreneurial journey as well. So thanks so much for the insights that you shared and we'll all be watching. Thank you. It was great to be here. Thank you.

A conversation with Manus AI's cofounder and CPO Tao Zhang

Stripe(104.0K)·38:18·1.2K views·2026-05-08
Tao ZhangCofounder and CPO, Manus AI
TL;DRManus AI transitioned from a viral product demo to $100M in ARR within eight months by shifting the AI paradigm from "answer machines" to "autonomous agents" that execute tasks in a cloud-based virtual sandbox.
The "Mens et Manus" Concept: Tao Zhang explains the...

TL;DR

Manus AI transitioned from a viral product demo to $100M in ARR within eight months by shifting the AI paradigm from "answer machines" to "autonomous agents" that execute tasks in a cloud-based virtual sandbox.

The Product Philosophy: "Mind and Hand"

  • The "Mens et Manus" Concept: Tao Zhang explains the company’s name is derived from the MIT motto (Mind and Hand). While LLMs serve as the "mind," Manus provides the "hands" to operate tools and impact the physical world.
  • Virtual Sandbox (VM): Unlike standard chatbots, Manus provides the AI with its own computer/virtual machine.
      • Execution Capabilities: The agent can browse the web, write and run Python code, store files, and retrieve them later without human intervention.
  • Transparency as a Feature: A critical product decision was allowing users to see the agent’s "thinking" process (opening browsers, writing code).
      • Trust Building: Showing the work process helps users understand and trust the autonomous nature of the agent.
  • Asynchronous Workflow: Because the agent operates in a cloud-based VM rather than locally, users can assign a task and close their laptops. Manus notifies them once the outcome is ready, mirroring a human delegating to a colleague.

Rapid Growth and Monetization Strategy

  • Viral Velocity: The product launch went viral through a raw, unedited screen recording of the agent in action.
      • User Acquisition: 2 million signups in the first week; 3.5 million in the first month.
      • High Demand: Invite codes were reportedly reselling for up to $14,000 online.
  • Zero-Dollar Marketing: Growth was entirely organic, driven by influencers in unexpected markets like Egypt and Brazil.
  • Monetization Metrics: The company tracks success through ARR and 1-5 star user ratings.
      • Signal Detection: While the standard top-tier plan is $200/month, some users pay over $5,000/month via usage-based billing, signaling high-value use cases for small-to-medium businesses.
  • PMF Validation: Zhang believes charging for AI products early is essential because AI is expensive; payment is the ultimate proof of Product-Market Fit (PMF).

A New Playbook for Product Development

  • The Flipped Workflow: Manus AI has reversed the traditional software development sequence.

1. Prototype First: Product managers write prompts (the "new PRD") and use Manus to build a functional prototype with front-end, back-end, and AI logic.

2. Engineering: Engineers determine how to make the prototype production-ready.

3. Design: Designers are the final step in the process, refining the interface after the function is proven.

  • Engineering Pool Structure: The team does not use fixed "feature teams."
      • Flexibility: Engineers belong to a general pool and are assigned to projects as needed.
      • AI for Context: To solve the "reading someone else's code" problem, engineers use AI to ingest and explain entire codebases in minutes, allowing for rapid rotation across projects.

Global Operations and Future Vision

  • Global Footprint: Headquartered in Singapore with offices in Tokyo and San Francisco.
  • Cross-Functional Culture: Despite having multiple sites, Zhang insists that researchers, engineers, and product managers sit together to foster "small talk" innovations.
  • The Problem-Definition Bottleneck: Zhang argues that "solving a problem" is no longer the hurdle; the new bottleneck is the human ability to "define a problem" and identify user pain points.
  • The Era of the Manager: Zhang envisions a future where every individual can be a manager, overseeing 10 to 100 autonomous agents to perform tedious work, allowing humans to focus on higher-level creative and strategic tasks.
"Mind we think they are LLMs, they are the models, they are super smart brains. But only with brain, us humans, we can't make real impact... we have to be the one who build hands for these very smart brains then they can operate tools." — Tao Zhang
"Right now, I think the bottleneck is still the capability of defining a problem. Solving a problem is not a problem anymore. But to define a problem is somehow I think most people still lack this capability." — Tao Zhang
"The prompt itself is the interface... the way you write your prompt actually is just like the way you design your interface 10 years ago. It’s a new PRD." — Tao Zhang
AI isn't just moving fast. It's moving at a pace we've never seen before. At Stripe, we're seeing the data firsthand. AI startups are rewriting the playbook and scaling to revenue milestones years faster than the giants that came before them. But how are they doing it? And how do you sustain that kind of momentum? In this series, we sit down with the founders behind this historic rise to find out. This is the AI Boom on Stripe. Episode 3. Tao, thank you so much for being here with us today. Yeah, me too. So, I'd love to take us back to the first time that I learned about Manus, and I think probably the whole world. It's March 2025. We've had AI chatbots for two, three years. Everyone's kind of been interacting with chat, but that's really it. And then a video starts circulating the internet. No flashy stage, no Silicon Valley keynote, just a raw screen recording of your product, Manus. Tell us what this video shows the world. Yeah, I think it's just the seven days before the launch date. When looking at our website, I think it's kind of like too simple. So I was thinking about maybe we should make a video for our launch. I called some video agencies and they all told me that it took them maybe two weeks or three weeks to build a launch video. I told them we only have like six days and they said oh, this is impossible. So we decided to shoot the video by ourselves. I used my own camera. I shot the video and Peak, our chief scientist, is the actor there talking about the product. I shot it and I edited it. Because we are not a very professional launch studio team, we only put Peak on camera for maybe one minute talking about our product. The rest of the time is Peak demoing our product for three use cases. I think that can be the main reason why we went super viral in the first place. We just showed the world what an AI product could be, other than a chatbot. We actually have that video right here, so let's take a look. Hi, I'm Peak from Manus AI. For the past year, we've been quietly building what we believe is the next evolution in AI. Today we're launching an early preview of Manus, the first general AI agent. This isn't just another chatbot or workflow. It's a truly autonomous agent that bridges the gap between conception and execution. While other AI stops at generating ideas, Manus delivers results. We see it as the next paradigm of human-machine collaboration and potentially a glimpse into AGI. Now let me show you Manus in action across three completely different tasks. Demo 1. Resume Screening. Let's start with an easy one. In this example, we'll ask Manus to help screen resumes. I've just sent Manus a zip file containing 10 resume documents. Since each Manus session has its own computer, it can work like a human—first unzipping the file, then browsing through each resume page by page, and recording important information in documents. Manus works asynchronously in the cloud, which means you can close your laptop anytime and Manus will notify you when everything is complete. So, the video shows basically that with Manus you can not just sort of chat, you can actually execute on tasks. It writes Python code, it opens browsers, and— Yeah, do everything. And it goes entirely viral. I think you had 2 million signups for the private preview after that. Oh, yeah, yeah. We had like 2 million in the first week and we got 3.5 million in the first month. Yeah, a lot of people on the waitlist. I think I read somewhere that people were reselling the invite codes for up to $14,000 online. Yeah, at that time, if an influencer got an invitation code to Manus, he would attract much more traffic compared to the price he paid for the invitation code. Unluckily, we didn't get any money from that. No, of course not. No, that's a whole new—we could do a whole different podcast on fraud on the internet now with AI, but we'll leave that for a different time. So what do you think it was in the video or about the product that struck such a nerve with people? Why was—what was the aha moment for people in that time? Yeah, because I think before Manus, people treated AI just as an answer machine. I give you a question and you give me an answer. It's still like telling you how to do things, but in the end, you have to do things by yourself. But in our launch video, we always focus on the outcome, on the deliveries. Maybe it delivers you a site, it delivers you a website, or it delivers the actual code that works. So I think people just get amazed. Another thing is that in the whole process when the AI is doing things, it's totally without human intervention. You just tell Manus what you want and Manus will figure out what the plan is and how to execute the plan step by step just by itself. It's all automatic. So I think that's really like something new and amazing for the whole world to see. Yeah, I remember thinking, oh, this is—it was the first time where I felt oh, this can actually save me time and help me do my job versus it just being a little bit of a thought partner. It's really magical. Did you know it was going to be so successful? Like the night before launch, were you like, yes, we've hit on something really special? Yeah, we were a very small startup at that time. We only had like 40 people there and we thought it was a very good product. When we developed the product, we were amazed by our product ourselves every day. Every day in our office was filled with, "Oh, it's awesome," or "Damn, how can Manus solve this?" So we thought it was going to be successful. But I think nobody expected that we could be at this level of success. Yeah, that's a high bar to clear. This is a good time to talk about the product. From the very beginning of Manus, I feel like you really reinvented what agentic AI products should look like. I wanted to go over some of these product areas with you and talk through some of the thinking behind them. Virtual sandbox—tell us what that is. The virtual sandbox—internally we call it like a virtual machine. It's super important as it's the fundamental part of the whole Manus system. For us, we think in the past why most AI wouldn't work is because they only use the brain. Maybe here is the time I can share why we chose the name Manus for this product. We take this word Manus from MIT's motto, which is Mens et Manus. It's an old Latin word, which means mind and hand. Mind, we think, they are LLMs; they are the models, they are super smart brains. But only with a brain, us humans, we can't make real impact in the physical world. That's why we think we have to be the one who builds hands for these very smart brains, then they can operate tools and they can make impact in the physical world, right? So I think when I say what wouldn't work for other AI tools is because they only have the brain. So what they do is they keep thinking, keep thinking inside the head. But what we do is that we provide a computer to the AI. So anything the AI thinks, okay, maybe I should do something or maybe I should run some code to test my thesis, it can use its own computer to browse the internet, to run the code, to store a file on the file system, or maybe retrieve the document again later. The virtual machine really changes everything because it provides maybe the most powerful tool, the computer, to the AI. It's now you're building tools for the AI to do its job and that's just really incredible. One of the interesting product decisions you made was for the user to be able to see what was happening, like opening the browser and writing the code. What was behind that decision? Oh, yeah, actually, that's kind of like the first important decision we made after we kicked off the project. Because we thought that agent technology is so new and people are not very familiar with this kind of product. So we think it's very important at this stage to show every step, the whole process to the users, to make users understand what is happening in the background so they can trust the product. This is very important. So we build transparency as a feature in our product. That's right. It's an incredible feature. Tell me a little bit about the async workflow. Because you create that trust and now the user can step away and not really worry. Where did that come from? In our founding team, we are all engineers. Even though I run the product team, I have written code for more than 30 years. So we all know coding. We are all Cursor fans; we use Cursor a lot of times. But there's a problem that when you are using Cursor, you have to keep your laptop open because it runs locally on your computer. So you have to keep it open and watch it. In the first maybe four or five months, it needed us humans to click the accept button. Sometimes it'll ask you accept, accept, accept. When we used the product, we thought, why does Cursor need me to click the accept button? It's because it runs locally on my computer. So it needs my permission to run these commands or it has a chance to break the whole computer. But in Manus, the computer is in the cloud. It's not your computer. So it's super safe to perform each step just by the agent itself. So we don't ask the users to keep pressing the accept button. Instead of that, we just let the agent run everything in the cloud. Then it gives us another advantage, which is the async advantage. Everything is running in the cloud, which means after you assign your task to Manus, you can just leave it. You can just close your laptop, you can put your phone back in your pocket and do your own things. After maybe like 10 or 20 minutes when the work is done, we will give you a notification and you will see the outcome. What do you use Manus for? Oh, a lot. Nearly everything. For me, I'm doing prototyping every day with Manus. Whenever I have a new idea, I will not just ask my designers to put the idea alive to me. I will use Manus instead of that. I will just tell Manus everything that is in my head and Manus will build the first prototype for me. And this is not just something we prototype in Figma. This is a fully functional prototype, which means it has the front-end, back-end, and it has the AI capability inside that. In this prototype, actually you can just try and play with it and tweak it. After the prototype is done, I will give the prototype to my designers and engineers and they will instantly understand what I want because this is the real thing. That's putting the fun back in software development, I think. Yeah. Who is your ideal Manus user? Right now, I think we describe them as non-technical users. In the first months when we kicked off the project, at that time we already had Cursor, Windsurf, Devin—all these fancy tools for engineers. We decided that we would not build another fancy tool for engineers because they have enough. But for normal people, for average users, they are underserved. They don't have these fancy tools. They don't have cloud code; they don't have Codex. So the way we built Manus is to build Manus for everyone. So the ideal user is someone who wants to achieve much more with AI but with less technical background. I love that. And there's so many more of them. Yeah, so many more of them. That's a bigger market. You've been coding for 30 years. Do you think it's different building for AI products or do you think really fundamentally software is software? Oh, I think building AI software is totally different. For me, I'm already in this industry for more than 15 years. Before, the way we built a product was somehow like I have an idea. I just build the prototype on Figma. Then I talk to my designer, talk to my engineer, and they will do the design, the engineering, and then write code. We test it, we launch it, and we hear the feedback. And then we just iterate. That's the way we did things before. But now, I think it's totally changed. Previously, I think product managers and designers' outcome was somehow like a PRD or a design. It was about an interface. But in the AI world, the interface doesn't exist anymore. For most AI products, it's just about conversations. So I think the conversation is the new interface, which means the prompt itself is the interface. I know there are many companies today where prompts are still written by engineers or researchers. But I think a better way to do that is the product manager writes the prompt themselves. Because the way you write your prompt actually is just like the way you designed your interface like 10 years ago. It's the new PRD essentially. Yeah, it's a new PRD; it's a new interface. The way we do product here is that whenever we have a new idea, we will just use Manus itself to prototype with our idea. After we feel the function works really well and this is the feel we want, then we will go to our engineer and ask them how to put this prototype in our production environment. Once the engineer tells us this thing can be delivered in our production environment, the last step is to go to the designer. So the whole process is really in the opposite. We have the final experience first. Then we have to make it engineering ready. And the last step is to ask the designer. It's like everything is ready, just do the design. You've completely changed the sequence of that process. Amazing. So at Stripe, we like to paraphrase this quote from Pablo Picasso, which is essentially: when art critics come together, they like to talk about form and function and meaning, and then when artists come together, they like to talk about where to buy the cheapest turpentine. Yeah, definitely. Actually, I put that sentence in my personal profile. Really? Yeah, I really love that sentence. That's amazing. Well, we love it here too. One of the reasons I love doing these types of interviews is that the builders and the co-founders of these companies like you guys are the artists. So I want to talk real tactics now about how you've built Manus and really built the company and gone global. You're headquartered in Singapore. Yeah. You now have teams in Tokyo and in San Francisco. My first question for you: when do you sleep? Oh, yeah, for me it's kind of a harder question because I'm the one doing all the public speeches around the world, so I usually travel. I will sleep whenever I can, on the plane, on a car, and whatever. But I think for most of my team, they will sleep very late in the midnight. Usually, I think our core engineering team will sleep after maybe 3:00 a.m. or 4:00 a.m. That's very normal. Wow. My question was meant in jest, but I'm glad you answered that. You have a very hardworking team. That's very normal. But definitely, they will wake up much later. Usually, our office starts after 10:00 a.m. or 11:00 a.m. because most of us come to the office after 11:00 a.m. because they sleep very late in the midnight. And you were saying you spent 400 hours on a plane last year. Oh, yeah, I spent 400 hours in the air. I'd love to see those travel rewards for you. You say you have about 100 people working at Manus today. Yeah, 100 people. Most companies don't build global teams until they're much bigger. Why did you decide to build out different offices and different locations so early on? Oh, yeah, because after we launched Manus, we went super viral in the whole world. We have a lot of Manus fans in different countries. And we believe it's super important to be there. We don't want to just be someone in the cloud. We just want to be face to face. Even just 15 days after we launched Manus, I came to the US and I traveled from the West Coast to the East Coast and we did more than 15 user meetups in six different cities. We just want to—because Manus is a general AI agent, which means we can do many things. But in the end, even for us, we don't know what users are using us for. So we think it's super important to get to know them, to have face-to-face communication. That's kind of like the initial intention to open international offices. But later we found out it's really important to open sites in different countries because there are different cultures and different ways people do business there. Opening our office there will make us much easier to talk to local markets. You were telling me something interesting right before this conversation that when you guys opened these offices, it was very important to you that you kept the product and engineering and research all together. Tell us a little bit more about that. In our company, we think it's very important to put the product guy, the engineering, and also the researchers sitting together. For example, back in our previous office, I and Peak, our chief scientist, and Bean, who is our full-stack guy—he's a real full-stack guy because he's not a full-stack engineer, he does everything. Our logo is also designed by him. So he's a full-stack guy. So we sit together and every day we have many small talks. It's like, "Okay, I saw something very interesting. Can we try that?" When we sit together, it's very easy to try out some new ideas. I know there are some teams that will separate different teams—the product part and the engineering part. We insist that we should sit together. Right now it's still me, Bean, and Peak sitting together and every day we have many small talks. We believe that those small talks lead to all the innovations we did in the past year. Yeah, especially for a product like Manus, which is so far-reaching and so cross-functional. You said something interesting about how you structure your engineering team. Most engineering teams are structured by product areas or even the customer journey. How do you guys decide on who works on what? Oh, our CTO Panpan has his own philosophy about managing an engineering team. Right now, we have near 40 or 50 engineers and they are in one very big team. Whenever we have a new project, Panpan will just assign someone to working on this project. After that, they will come back to the big pool. No one is specifically appointed to a feature or a project. We think it's very important because it makes us much more flexible. So, a project comes up and you're like "Who here is available?" and people raise their hand and you're like "Great, you two go work on this particular project." They are done with the project, they come back into the general pool and then it's the next project. Who maintains the code and the product after they're done with the project? Oh, yeah, that's a good question. I think the way we run our engineering team also somehow benefits from the AI's capability. Before, we would let the same engineer keep working on the same project because we needed the context. It's really hard to read others' code, right? But right now with the help of AI, it's really easy. If we give an engineer a project and there is some existing code, he can just use the AI to read all the code and explain how the whole project works. So it only takes maybe 10 minutes to understand a very complex project. Before that, it would take days. It's an incredible unlock because now you have everyone, and also people by definition will have more context anyway because they're working on so many different pieces. So I think that's amazing. I hope that's kind of the future of product development and building products. At Stripe, we like to paraphrase this quote from Pablo Picasso, which is essentially: when art critics come together, they like to talk about form and function and meaning, and then when artists come together, they like to talk about where to buy the cheapest turpentine. Yeah, definitely. Actually, I put that sentence in my personal profile. Really? Yeah, I really love that sentence. That's amazing. Well, we love it here too. One of the reasons I love doing these types of interviews is that the builders and the co-founders of these companies like you guys are the artists. So, I want to talk real tactics now about how you've built Manus and really built the company and gone globally. You went from zero to 100 million in ARR in just eight months. How? Oh, okay. Yeah, I know a lot of people are very curious about that. But we are also curious about that too. Before last November, which was in the first eight months, we nearly put zero marketing budget into the whole product, which means most of the traffic was organic traffic from everywhere. For example, back in last June, we were at the top place in the App Store of Egypt. We don't know why. We did some research and found out there was some random guy on Facebook who wrote a post in Arabic talking about Manus, which made us go viral in Egypt. We didn't know that before the post. In Brazil, we have a huge user base there because two influencers on YouTube made videos about us. We didn't know these two influencers until then. For us, a lot of viral waves just came from word of mouth. As you just mentioned, the first time you saw our video, you thought "Oh, this is something new," and you got amazed and told your friends. In the whole first eight months, it was all about the product itself and word of mouth. So, your growth tactic is just build an insanely good product and listen to your feedback. Yeah, that's it. I know in many teams they will treat growth as a separate sector in their company, but for us, we think the best growth is the product itself. After last year, the management team had some review about the past year and we saw there were some important decisions in our product that made us more viral. The first thing is about 20 days before the launch—we decided to build a feature we call session replay. Whenever a task is done and you share the task to your friends, your friends will see the whole session in replay mode. We think that's very important. If your friend only sees the final step, they will not get amazed. They'll just think it's another AI product. But when they see the session in replay mode, they're like "Oh, what is going on?" The AI is controlling the browser, the AI is writing the code, and so on. I think that feature is really important for our growth. You also have the build-first, sign-up-later feature that's obviously using Stripe sandboxes. Has that helped at all with your growth? Oh, yeah, I think that part helped the growth of our Manus 1.5 feature. In the version of Manus 1.5, we introduced a new way to build websites. Before that, Manus could only build static web pages—just static HTML. But in Manus 1.5, we can build fully functional websites with the front-end, back-end databases, and native AI capabilities. Many users were asking us if they could integrate Stripe into the system so they could run their real business on a Manus website. Stripe has the best developer experiences. But because our ideal customer is the non-technical person, it's really hard for them to understand what a token is or many technical things. We think the Stripe sandbox really helped us to make the whole process much simpler. Even for non-technical users, they can build a functional business like an e-commerce website without any knowledge about the technical details. Once he is done with the website, he can just transfer the Stripe sandbox to the real environment. It's an incredible feature. I'm so excited to see all the businesses that are going to happen because of tools like Manus. One of the really cool things about Stripe is that we get some insight into how fast AI cohorts grow, and they have fundamentally different growth curves. In this new world, how do you measure your success when you don't have many benchmarks? I think for us, we care about two metrics. The first is about ARR. It's a traditional metric, but we think it's much more important in this AI era. Right now, AI is still very expensive. You can't just make a free product. In my early years in this industry, we were always talking about not caring about revenue and just bringing the users in, and after you have millions of daily active users, you can find a way to make money. But today, I think things have changed. You must ask the users to pay for your product in the first version. AI is expensive right now, so it's really important to earn money not just for revenue, but to prove your PMF. Someone will pay only if they feel the value is real. Focus on ARR because that means a lot for your PMF. The second metric we are tracking is the user's rating. After every task is done, we will ask the user to rate the task on a scale of one to five stars. That tells us a lot about our quality and performance. You can see these ratings through different segments—by different features, countries, or pricing tiers. That tells us a lot. We are following these two metrics and we are analyzing them every week. Speaking of ARR, how did you think about pricing? Pricing is super important, but we don't know what's the best approach. We learn from each other. If you are looking at today's cutting-edge AI applications from a yearly scope, you will see everyone changes. Sometimes we will learn from others and six months later we find out they learned from us too. Everyone changes their pricing plans. We don't know the best practice here, but we think it's very important to keep in mind that there are some very heavy users and powerful users. They will feel like they get much more value than other users, so they're willing to pay more. Always leave a space there for users to be charged by usage. Usage-based billing is very important. Even if you have a monthly subscription plan, always leave a space there for users to pay more. It seems like it's kind of unbound in some ways; there are users who are willing to pay a whole lot more. And also, I think this is not just about the revenue, it's also about signals. For us, our top plan when we launched was $200. We thought it was a lot. After we launched the plan, we left an option for our users to recharge their account after they hit the limit. Luckily, we found out there are some users who would pay us $5,000 a month. That's a super positive signal to catch. If someone pays $5,000 a month, it means the output of the Manus may be valued 10 times more. So we will do a user interview and find out what they are using us for. Why do they think it's so valuable? That provides us with many insights for the future product roadmap. Any insights you can share from those conversations? Definitely. Why we made Manus 1.5 is because we were looking at users who build web pages in Manus and they spend a lot. We asked them why they want to build these web pages there. They told us that in their country, it's very hard to find engineers to build a business website for small-medium businesses. So for them, spending $1,000 on Manus is super cheap. That gave us some inspiration. We live in a very big country and every day we talk to people from the US which is full of tech people, so we don't feel that pain.
