That's somebody. And he started making computers on his bed in college and selling them because they were better than other computers, and he just—I said, "How did you do that?" He said, "Well I did it and I just never stopped." He just kept going. So go out and buy a Dell, they're great. We're also working to make it easier than ever before to start a family by lowering the price of IVF, cutting regulatory battles and barriers, and fighting to bring down the cost of living so that more Americans can pursue the dream of living just a good life, maybe a great life. We cut drug prices now with the favorite nations—most favored nations. We cut them by 70, 80, 90 percent. You could say 500 percent, 600, depending on the way you phrase the statement. But we now play—so we, when I was here this was a big ambition right at the beginning. I was able to cut them a little bit, but I didn't cut them—I cut them more than anybody else, which was a little bit. For 28 years drug prices and prescription drugs only went in one direction. When I came back, we now did something that everybody said was impossible: favorite nations. We had the highest drug prices in the world by far. Now we have the lowest drug prices in the world, can you imagine? So we cut them by 50, 60, 70 percent. And go out and—you'll see exactly. It's one of the big, I think it's one of the best biggest things that we've done. Drugs that were costing in London $10 for a pill would cost $130 for a pill in the United States. And now if it's $10 or if it's $20, that's what we pay. we pay the lowest price anywhere in the world. So it's great, and the fake news doesn't want to write about it but that's why I talk about it, because we have no choice, we have to. So I just want to again, I want to thank everybody for being here. It's so important we do things like we keep men out of women's sports, a very popular issue. We stopped transgender mutilation of your children for everyone; we don't have that. But I want to thank every single mother here this afternoon and all across our nation for your work every single day. America's moms are raising, really raising the future of our country. It's the most important job there is in America or any place else, and you're doing an incredible job. I just want to wish you all a very happy Mother's Day. We're going to have a great Mother's Day. And for those mothers that lost their child, many of whom are with us today—the Angel Moms and others, Gold Star Moms—we love you especially. We love you maybe even the most. Thank you very much, thank you everybody, appreciate it.

Democrats Vs. Themselves On Gas Prices | FOX Across America

FOX News Podcasts(115.0K)·43:22·3.6K views·2026-05-08
Jimmy FaillaHost, FOX Across America Donald TrumpFormer President of the United States
TL;DRHost Jimmy Failla and guests criticize Democratic leaders and the media for perceived hypocrisy regarding gas prices and Fed independence, arguing that progressive tax-and-spend policies are driving a wealth exodus from cities like New York and Los Angeles to Florida.

TL;DR

Host Jimmy Failla and guests criticize Democratic leaders and the media for perceived hypocrisy regarding gas prices and Fed independence, arguing that progressive tax-and-spend policies are driving a wealth exodus from cities like New York and Los Angeles to Florida.

Energy Policy and Media Narrative Shifts

  • Iran Nuclear Negotiations: Reporter Trey Yingst details a new U.S. proposal requiring Iran to hand over enriched material, accept a 20-year enrichment ban, and dismantle key facilities. Failla suggests the media is preparing to frame these potential diplomatic wins as failures.
  • Gas Price Hypocrisy: Failla highlights a contradiction in political messaging regarding fuel costs.
      • The "Biden Defense": In 2021, Secretary Jennifer Granholm and the White House stated the President does not control gas prices.
      • The "Trump Critique": Failla notes that the same pundits now blame Trump for current gas prices, despite higher domestic production under his administration compared to the "mismatch between supply and demand" caused by Biden-era production cuts.
  • Domestic Production: Failla argues that Biden’s policies—outsourcing production to Venezuela and Iran—directly spiked markets, whereas Trump’s "net exporter" status provided stability.

Economic Policy and Federal Reserve Independence

  • Senator Elizabeth Warren’s Rhetoric: Failla highlights a "montage of hypocrisy" regarding the Federal Reserve.
      • Under Biden: Warren publicly pressured Fed Chair Jerome Powell for "nice big rate cuts" during an election year to bolster the economy.
      • Under Trump: Warren now characterizes presidential calls for rate cuts as the behavior of a "two-bit dictatorship," arguing the Fed must remain independent of politics.
  • Corporate Failures: Failla disputes Warren's claim that the Trump administration caused Spirit Airlines' bankruptcy, noting that Warren herself led the effort to block the airline's merger with JetBlue.

The "Blue City" Exodus and Fiscal Management

  • New York's Tax Burden: Guest Karol Markowicz and Failla discuss the departure of major firms like Citadel (Ken Griffin) from New York to Miami.
      • Spending vs. Revenue: Failla notes the U.S. collects record tax revenue but remains $37 trillion in debt, suggesting the issue is "spending," not a lack of taxation on the wealthy.
      • AOC ("Mom Dani") Critique: Failla mocks Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez’s calls for "fair share" taxation, arguing that increasing taxes on the wealthy in response to them leaving for lower-tax states is counterproductive.
  • Urban Decline: The speakers contrast the "safe and hopeful" New York of the Bloomberg era with current conditions.
      • "AOCs" Red-Light Districts: Failla uses "taxi parlance" to describe new open-air solicitation areas in Elmhurst, Queens, as a result of progressive city management.
      • Suicidal Empathy: Markowicz argues that progressive voters often choose policies that destroy their quality of life because they believe it makes them look "empathetic" to their peers.

California’s Humanitarian Crisis

  • San Francisco and Sunnyvale: Comedian Ryan Reiss and Failla discuss the "disturbing" state of California’s Mission District and the Tenderloin.
      • Drug Policy: They criticize the state for funding drug use through safe injection sites and free needle exchanges, leading to what they describe as "zombie" encampments.
      • Homelessness Statistics: Failla asserts that while the public perceives homelessness as "tough luck," the vast majority of the crisis in California is driven by drug addiction (claiming a 93% rate).
  • The "Line to Get Out": Reiss notes that U-Haul trucks are often free to drive into California because there is such a massive surplus of equipment moving one-way toward states like Nevada and Texas.

Notable Quotes

"Inflation is made in Washington because only Washington can create money... What produces it is too much government spending and too much government creation of money and nothing else." — Milton Friedman (Clip)
"The people who said the president don't control the gas prices are now suddenly concerned about the price of gas. Democrats are so full of crap... it’s the craziest thing in the world that they talk as if we're all Amish and we haven't had the ability to record what was previously said." — Jimmy Failla
"If we understand that... interest rates in the United States are subject to a president who just wants to wave his magic wand, this does not distinguish us then from any other two-bit dictatorship around the world." — Senator Elizabeth Warren (regarding Trump)
"You're not suffering because you’re dumb or because you don’t work hard, you’re suffering because of [the rich guy]. It's a much easier [message]." — Karol Markowicz
This video is a compilation of clips from the radio and television show *Fox Across America with Jimmy Failla*. The show features host Jimmy Failla discussing various political and social issues, often with guests. The following is a verbatim transcription of the audio. **Jimmy Failla:** I wanted to focus with a quick update on the Iranian deal. This is Trey Yingst reporting this morning on the Fox News Channel. There have been no further updates, so let's start here. Clip one. **Trey Yingst:** We are getting new details on the American proposal to end the war with Iran. The *Wall Street Journal* reports the new U.S. proposal includes Iran handing over all enriched material, a 20-year ban on enrichment, and a declaration that Iran doesn't seek nuclear weapons. The proposal also includes Iran dismantling key nuclear facilities, inspections if violations do occur, and a ban on underground nuclear work. Iran's foreign ministry says the regime is still examining the memorandum, but a response is expected in the hours or days ahead. **Jimmy Failla:** Okay, so we are perilously close to what could be some type of a handshake agreement on a permanent ceasefire and the ability to reach a more extensive agreement. This is the part where the media starts telling you it was a complete and total disaster. **Sound Effect Clip:** The media’s a bunch of losers. **Jimmy Failla:** I don't know why that one gets me. But the point is, I was watching this this morning on the way into the building. The new round of talking points is already out there. And what’s funny about this new round is it runs in such a stark contradiction to the last one. You know, right now is very much a "Yeah, but the gas prices. The hell’s going on with the gas prices? Have you seen gas? I’ve never seen gas prices like this!" Now, they were higher under Biden than they are right now under Trump. There wasn’t a war at the time the prices hit the levels that they did under Biden. Okay, but if you remember what the media was telling us back then, was that the president doesn’t control the price of gas. So why are all of these Republican commentators being like "What’s up with the price of gas? It has nothing to do with the president." **Sound Effect Clip:** Come on. Don’t bullshit me. **Jimmy Failla:** But of course it did, because the president at the time cut domestic energy production, changed the supply and demand ratios here in America, and outsourced a lot of our production to other countries like Venezuela and Iran. Yes, it cost us additional money to import the fuel that we could have just been producing here in the first place. Ergo, it was Biden’s policies that directly caused the shortages and directly spiked the market. But at the time, the White House said "Shut up, the president has nothing to do with this." Here’s Jennifer Granholm, right there in 2021 when the fuel was spiking. Clip 52. **Jennifer Granholm:** To be really clear, obviously, the president does not control the price of gasoline. No president does, but what we're seeing right now is this global mismatch between supply and demand. Oil production is lagging behind as the rest of the economy roars back to life. **Jimmy Failla:** It's nothing more than white noise coming from the White House. Straight up, we said it then, we’ll say it now. She's talking about what we're seeing as a global mismatch between supply and demand. Why was there a global mismatch? Because America, which was a net exporter of oil when Trump left office, handed the keys to the car to Joe Biden, and we all know what happened next to domestic energy production. **Sound Effect:** Sound of car tires screeching and a loud crash. **Jimmy Failla:** But again, back then, they were like "the president's got nothing to do with gas prices." You cannot right now, if you throw on a cable news broadcast, look at it like you’re in a rodeo. You know how you gotta see how long you can stay on the bull for? I guarantee you, you can't go eight seconds without a pundit going "Yeah, but the gas prices. Do you believe what Trump did to the gas prices? This is crazy what the president's doing to the gas prices." The people who said the president don't control the gas prices are now suddenly concerned about the price of gas. **Sound Effect Clip:** Democrats are so full of crap. **Jimmy Failla:** About everything. Let me give you another one. This is great. And I want—I make this point, okay? I don’t do a show that wants you mad at Democrats all day because I don’t care. I’m mad at the Republicans right now. The Republicans control the House, the Senate, and the presidency. 84% of us want a voter ID law and they won't pass the freaking law. And John Thune was like "Well, I don’t have the votes. There’s nothing I can do." Yeah, there’s something you can do. It's called get off your ass and go get the votes. Do a little horse trading. Make deals. That’s how legislation has gotten passed in this country since the beginning of time. And they’re like "Well, they don’t want to make deals." Alright, well then nuke the filibuster. The Democrats are telling you the minute they get back into power, they’re nuking the filibuster. They want to stack the Supreme Court, and they want to impeach Trump. They’re gonna do it day one. So it's like, you have—let’s just assume you have a rifle on the kitchen counter. A guy comes into your house and goes for the rifle, okay? He has every intention of shooting you with that rifle. So if the rifle is yours to shoot, as unfortunate as this may sound, you probably need to use the rifle. **Sound Effect Clip:** A stereotypical cartoon cowboy “Yee-haw!” shout. **Jimmy Failla:** Now, I’m not encouraging actual gun violence. I am a conservative. I am not a liberal podcaster. I am not a liberal member of Congress who’s out there speaking in war phrases around the clock and then going "Gee, why do so many people want to kill pedophile Hitler? It’s crazy." You know, time was you could call a guy a Hitler pedophile apologist and nobody got mad at him. I’m not trying to be that guy. But what I want you to understand is just how transactional and shameless politics have become. Okay, here is Elizabeth Warren. I love this one. Elizabeth Warren got ratioed over the weekend because if you remember, when Spirit Airlines went out of business, she was the first one to tweet "I can’t believe the Trump administration let this happen." Except there’s only one small problem: Spirit Airlines would have been in business, but Elizabeth Warren blocked their merger with JetBlue. **Sound Effect Clip:** High-pitched, enthusiastic cheering and shouting. **Jimmy Failla:** How about it? They would still have a Spirit Airlines, except Elizabeth Warren had reservations. **Sound Effect:** A drum “sting” typically used after a joke. **Jimmy Failla:** But here she is, same type of ridiculousness. Okay, this is about rate cuts. So in the run-up to the 2024 election, Elizabeth Warren was crowing that Jerome Powell needed to cut the rates. "Gotta cut the rates here. Come on, man." And a lot of people were like "Wow, she’s pressuring the Fed in an election year because she wants the rate cuts to go down, it’ll help the Democrats as the incumbent party in power." And she just got right on your TV and said cut the rates. I’m gonna play you a montage. Because the minute Trump gets into office, what does Elizabeth Warren do? She goes "How dare he call on the Fed to cut the rates? What kind of a monster, what kind of an existential threat to the integrity of the Fed would tell them to cut the rates?" Oh yeah, you. Here’s the montage. It's a minute of your time. Enjoy this. I loved this. Clip 53. **Elizabeth Warren:** He waited too long. And the best way you can show that you waited too long and that you get it and that you are becoming more responsive to where we are on interest rates is actually go ahead and do a nice big rate cut. Keep in mind that keeping those rates high as he has done, keeping those interest rates so high, actually has contributed to our measure of inflation. The infrastructure that keeps this stock market strong and therefore a big part of our economy strong is the idea that the big pieces move independent of the politics. Somebody is making his, their, her best decisions economically and independently. If we understand that if the New York Stock Exchange and interest rates in the United States are subject to a president who just wants to wave his magic wand, this does not distinguish us then from any other two-bit dictatorship around the world. **Sound Effect Clip:** Native American style chanting. **Jimmy Failla:** Whoa! And she went on to say— **Sound Effect Clip:** Continued Native American style chanting. **Jimmy Failla:** Elizabeth Warren, man. I always say, you know, we're living in the death of shame. I don't think there's a better example. So the first half of that clip is her with Biden in office, going what the Fed needs to do to show they were too late in cutting the rates is go ahead and do a nice big rate cut. She's saying that in an election year with a Democrat in office. And then she comes back to say we can't have a president telling the Fed to cut rates. That makes us a third-world nation. Okay, with a straight face. Democrats, it is the craziest thing in the world that they talk as if we're all Amish and we haven't had the ability to record what was previously said. We don't have the wherewithal to videotape prior statements, press conferences, or TV appearances. It's like a goldfish with no episodic memory. They feel like they can get on TV Tuesday and say anything they want with no regard for what we might have heard them say Monday. And it's so fascinating to me because as we're trying to find a historic world-changing off-ramp in Iran. Okay, if we get nuclear armament off the table permanently in Iran, that stabilizes the Middle East in a way we have not had in over 50 years. Okay, if we liberate their people and yes, juice their economy, because that's how we normalized relations in the Middle East with Israel through the Abraham Accords. A lot of those countries, Arabic nations, didn't love Israel and certainly didn't respect their right to exist, but they liked making money. And President Trump and his negotiating envoy, and I think Jared Kushner probably played one of the biggest hands in that in the first Trump administration, went over there. He was like "Hey, what if instead of blowing each other up all the time, you just made money? You had peaceful stable societies, your economies were a little bit more spunky, and people had a little more money in their pocket." You didn't have to worry about some guy running onto a bus and blowing it up every Tuesday and Thursday and all the senseless death. Okay, what if through these Abraham Accords, we just normalized relations? Look at Israel like a trading partner. Don't look at them like some type of a front to your radical ideology. Look at them like somebody you can make money off of, someone who can help you ensure a more stable future for your people. That's the actual goal with Iran. Okay, that's where they want to see this thing end up. And it's probably going to get there, okay, sooner rather than later. But what you're going to hear in the meantime is this didn't work, it was bad, it's just the Obama nuclear deal, the gas prices, the gas prices, the gas prices. Okay, this coming from the people who said the president don't control the gas prices. Okay, that's the biggest challenge. I spent a lot of time on this yesterday. I'll spend a lot of time on it today. I'll even give you some Republican examples. Okay, there are so many people who put politics and power ahead of the prosperity of this nation. Okay, the good news for you is Trump ain't one of them. That's why your gas prices are high. If Trump was worried about the politics, he would have made a deal three weeks ago, your gas prices would have went down, but Iran would still be on their way to a nuke. The world would be a lot less stable, and oh yeah, we'd wind up in another one of these conflicts and your gas prices would go right back up anyway. Okay, this is a "measure twice, cut once" approach. Okay, and in this instance, the people who are going to get cut the hardest are the ones in the Democrat party that are rooting against America. Why are they going to get cut the hardest? Because Trump is not in here to read the room; he's here to lead the room. President Trump has oranges the size of beach balls. **Jimmy Failla:** This next guest is a big damn deal, and I know that because she said it to me when she walked into the studio. She was like, you know, sit up straight, you got a big damn deal. From the *New York Post* by way of the great state of Florida, Karol Markowicz returns to the show. The crowd goes wild. Good to see you. **Karol Markowicz:** So good to be here. Thank you. **Jimmy Failla:** It’s good for morale. The last time we did this, I was probably on your podcast. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** Before that you were here. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** We're kind of like exchange students, but we missed a semester. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** So you're a senior now. I got left back unfortunately, but we're here, we’re having a nice time is the point. **Karol Markowicz:** I'm loving it. **Jimmy Failla:** How about it? What was it about New York that you missed so much? **Karol Markowicz:** I mean, I—I say this, but you know, it's the Chinese food. **Jimmy Failla:** It is, right? **Karol Markowicz:** It’s all about the Chinese food. Cannot be duplicated elsewhere. **Jimmy Failla:** Is that true? **Karol Markowicz:** Well, they call it New York-style Chinese food at other places, at other restaurants in other states. They’re lying, but they’re trying for it. Okay. **Jimmy Failla:** So they’re trying to do this, they’re just not getting it done. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah, they’re failing, right. **Jimmy Failla:** And what do you think it is, that we just have a better breed of cat? I'm kidding! Don't answer that. Come on, these are jokes. What are we doing? Karol Markowicz. **Karol Markowicz:** But yes. **Jimmy Failla:** So it is funny. It’s so funny. I always wonder, and I think—I don’t think it's a wonder, I think we can go on this as a news network, we could probably confirm this. They probably don’t eat Chinese food in China the way we do in America. **Karol Markowicz:** Oh, that’s for sure. **Jimmy Failla:** Is that for sure? They’re not eating General Tso’s and stuff like that? **Karol Markowicz:** No, they’re not. They’re not, right? No, they’re not. **Jimmy Failla:** I love how we take—we take anyone’s culture in America and Americanize it. **Karol Markowicz:** I make it better. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, yeah, in a weird way we do make it better. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** You know, like, well, what if we fried it? What if we—that looks great, what if we breaded it and fried it? **Karol Markowicz:** Have some extra butter. **Jimmy Failla:** Have some extra butter. Or if it's going the other way, if it’s Italian, we're like, yeah, because when you go to Italy, Italian food’s actually pretty light. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** It’s weird, right? **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah, but I think if you bread it and put some red sauce on it, it gets so much better. **Jimmy Failla:** Hey, can we get a bread bowl with this? So crazy. So, I bring up New York, Florida, the whole shebang-shabo. I was just talking about Mom Dani before we went to break, because Ken Griffin was talking about how the taxes were driving jobs out of New York. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** And Mom Dani came back with, you know, "the rich don’t pay their fair share." Now they do, if we were going to be technical, but this idea of demonizing wealth, I think what they’re ultimately catering to is people who suck at life. **Karol Markowicz:** Fair. **Jimmy Failla:** Because they’re like, "Alright, you don’t want to do the work, you don’t want to pull it together. What if we just make them the bad guy?" **Karol Markowicz:** Exactly. Yeah, "you're not suffering because you’re dumb or because you don’t work hard, you’re suffering because of this guy." Much easier. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, and it is! And that's who it ultimately caters to, but the reason it’s such a dead-end street is because you can’t just go out and tax the rich and the problems will go away. Because all taxing the rich does, if in fact they don’t settle or bring out an accountant and a lawyer, is give more money to the government, and they just spend it too. **Karol Markowicz:** Exactly. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, you know, where do you draw the line? **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** You know, we're dealing with a situation, Karol Markowicz, well you know this, we're collecting a record level of tax revenue as a country right now and we're $37 trillion in debt. At what point is it fair to ask if the problem is spending? **Karol Markowicz:** Right. I mean, I don't think you're allowed to ask that, but yes, that—that might be what the issue is. I love every fraud report lately is like, "Oh, we're misspending it on all of this nonsense." I mean, I think Americans see it. **Jimmy Failla:** I want to believe they see it, and I want to believe that they understand it's not a political issue. Like, when federal tax dollars get absconded, they don't go, "we're only stealing the Republican dollars, don't worry. If you're a liberal, this doesn't affect you." No, we all get ripped off. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** And I—I want to live in that world, where we do have like a common culture politically. We're not there yet. I want to believe we'll get there, but we're not there yet right now. **Karol Markowicz:** You think we will get there? I don't know. **Jimmy Failla:** I'm like, you know, like my—I'm kind of an optimist in that regard. You know how they say necessity is the mother of invention? **Karol Markowicz:** Yes. **Jimmy Failla:** Okay, my optimism is couched by the fact that I believe they’re going to screw this thing up bad enough that we're going to be forced to get there. It doesn't mean we’re going to get there under good terms, it just means we're going to get there, you know. **Karol Markowicz:** They’re going to hit rock bottom and crawl back up together. **Jimmy Failla:** Talking to Karol Markowicz from the *New York Post*. I’m sure like myself and many people listening, you’ve seen some of these debates out in California the past two nights. Governor and Mayor. Very similar in the sense that nothing being espoused on the left makes an American citizen a priority. **Karol Markowicz:** Absolutely. **Jimmy Failla:** Isn't it weird though? **Karol Markowicz:** It’s—it is weird, and yet you kind of see that the left is going to win there. Like that’s—that’s where—I am an optimist too, but California and, you know... **Jimmy Failla:** I know. Think about it. **Karol Markowicz:** It’s hard to be optimistic. **Jimmy Failla:** It—it is, because like—they recalled Gavin Newsom, and he won going away in the recall. So what was the point of the recall? Because they—and this is the part that kills me, and I’ve been harping on this for a while because I'm traveling—you travel so much doing stand-up, you always hear the commercials in other cities. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** And they have nationalized politics. We used to say all politics are local. I don’t believe that anymore because a guy running for county executive in Opelika, Alabama, if he’s a liberal, will be like "He’ll stand up to Donald Trump." And that’s the commercial. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, and you’re like, Donald Trump doesn't know—unless there's a—a McDonald’s, he probably hasn't been to Opelika, okay? And it’s a great county. It’s a wonderful place. **Karol Markowicz:** Is it? **Jimmy Failla:** Killer. Okay, Tommy Daniel, my buddy lives down there. They’ve got a dog Lincoln named after my kid. They know Opelika. **Karol Markowicz:** Wow. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, no, his—his wife Lori is a bit of a hot mess, but Tommy’s aces with me. I go hot and cold on Lori. **Karol Markowicz:** Sorry, Lori. **Jimmy Failla:** But—no, Lori, she’ll get—people are talking. Let’s hope she gets it together. But the point is, okay, Tommy, Lori, whoever we're talking to here, is everybody has tried to outsource political responsibility to some animus people have towards Trump. And it’s lazy, but it’s been effective in some of these places. **Karol Markowicz:** Absolutely. It’s "Do you want to be with us or do you want to be with this monster?" And it works. It works. **Jimmy Failla:** But it’s funny because they kind of made up the monster. **Karol Markowicz:** Well, of course. Right. **Jimmy Failla:** That’s what I find so interesting. And what I find is—you know we got to this place, where we got so petty and partisan that you wanted to beat the other side so badly that people are actually doing a thing. A phrase I used to hate, there was a 20-year-old phrase was like, "You’re voting against your own interests." **Karol Markowicz:** Yes, of course. **Jimmy Failla:** But how are you not voting against your own interests—like if you're in California right now and you’re like, "Yeah, let’s let the Democrats do this for another four years." What is—what’s the deliverable? You want more tents, more heroin? What are you in it for? **Karol Markowicz:** It’s you want to feel good about yourself. You feel like it's part of your personality to be a progressive, and if you vote with the wrong side, then what will your neighbors think? **Jimmy Failla:** That’s a great point. I saw that in Brooklyn. I mean, I saw them continue to vote against their own interests, but to be good people. **Karol Markowicz:** That’s crazy, but it’s funny because they always use that term "suicidal empathy." **Karol Markowicz:** Oh, yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** But it so does parallel with what you just said. A lot of people vote for things that are destroying their quality of life because they think it makes their brand look empathetic. That is my favorite. Lincoln my son, who's—he's a senior in high school now, but I remember him being on this show as a 10-year-old going, "Every single person with a 'Hate Has No Home Here' sign, if they saw you in a MAGA hat, would be like 'I hope you die,'" you know, immediately. And it's true. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah, it's like "Hate has no home here, except for Donald Trump." **Jimmy Failla:** No, "Hate has no home here, and neither do you, if you have a MAGA hat on." So did you see any of Obama making the rounds on Colbert? **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** So silly. **Karol Markowicz:** "Why is he so angry?" **Jimmy Failla:** It's the thing I don't get, but the thing I sort of do. Okay, can I give you my theory on this? First of all, he was president too young. Not just because I don't think he was smart enough in a lot of ways that I could articulate and maybe I'll take the time to get to that in a minute, but also because he now has to spend another 50 years in the public eye not being that relevant. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** You know, he’s—they call him a kingmaker, but he’s one for three in presidential elections. **Karol Markowicz:** Can't actually make any kings. **Jimmy Failla:** You’re a Rodney Kingmaker. You’re getting beat, you’re getting beat up here. It’s not good. And it’s like so many of the things he’s done, I guess his legacy would be Obamacare, but nobody looks at that as a win for anybody unless you run a healthcare company and got a massive subsidy. The Iran nuclear deal hasn't actually held up the best. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** So I don't know what his legacy is, but the reason he was—so propped up is because he kind of was a Buckingham Palace president. In that, you know how people wouldn't badmouth the Queen because they didn't want to lose access? **Karol Markowicz:** Sure. **Jimmy Failla:** So Obama got to bumper bowl as president. Everybody covered him in a favorable manner because they didn't want to lose access. And do you know how they say things like—you laugh at this because you're on Twitter—you know how every time, like every two months or six months, someone will post like "I miss Obama's scandal-free administration." **Karol Markowicz:** Right, the tan suit. **Jimmy Failla:** But we're like, I don't want to be the guy who says "actually," but actually. **Karol Markowicz:** What about the guns they ran into Mexico and lost? **Jimmy Failla:** The Fast and the Furious. How about the fact that they legitimately did wire Trump—Trump Tower? They wiretapped Trump—and do you remember when Trump said that in his transition? He’s like "They bugged Trump Tower!" And everybody was like "What a nutbag this Trump guy is, they'd never do that!" **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah, right. **Jimmy Failla:** They did that and so much more. **Karol Markowicz:** Right, how about, "If you like your doctor you can keep your doctor"? That was the biggest scam ever. **Jimmy Failla:** PolitiFact "Lie of the Year." And what’s funny is, the media didn't really call it out. Even the Iranian nuclear deal, if you remember Ben Rhodes wrote an op-ed going, "Here's how we duped the media into saying this was a..." He like bragged about it! **Karol Markowicz:** Yes, yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** And the media was like, "We don't want any trouble, we just—we want to come to the Easter Egg Roll and have a good spot, so just—go on, give the money to Iran and we'll all get on with our lives." It was crazy. We're talking to Karol Markowicz, she knows things. But the Obama clip that got to me the best is he went in and praised Mom Dani as a generational talent. **Karol Markowicz:** Future. **Jimmy Failla:** But what’s funny is, I agree with that assessment in politics of when you say someone's like "really good communicator" or "they're charismatic," "they're good on social." But that doesn't negate the fact that he's still an empty suit. **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** Which is what Obama was. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah, of course. No accomplishments, nothing to look forward to. I mean, I don't see what he's going to be able to get done from his amazing platform. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, Mom Dani. Nothing. Okay, I mean this will probably end with him having a seven-story library like Obama. **Karol Markowicz:** With no windows. **Jimmy Failla:** With no—but it's true because as we know, there’s nothing Obama—you know, Mom Dani can actually do without either a) the government raising taxes or b) a lot of people who fled this state moving back, which ain't gonna happen. **Karol Markowicz:** No, hell no. **Jimmy Failla:** Do you remember growing up—did you grow up here? I know you lived here. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah, Brooklyn. **Jimmy Failla:** Okay, so I grew up Long Island, and I remember growing up here when I had never left here, truly believing that everyone in this country wished they were us. **Karol Markowicz:** Oh. Wait, I still kind of think that they did at that time. It just not anymore. **Jimmy Failla:** I grew up being like, everyone in the country wishes they were here in New York. **Karol Markowicz:** Right, why wouldn't you? **Jimmy Failla:** And then I don't know, because I traveled and everybody was like, "No, you’re nuts." But that was a better New York. **Karol Markowicz:** It was such a good New York. It was safe, it was awesome, it was amazing. It had hope. **Jimmy Failla:** I say this to people all the time. If you just rewind as far as Michael Bloomberg, okay, who did a disastrous job of running for president, but he was a good mayor. Okay, the city was doing well. And I say this as a cabbie who hated the bike lanes, it was a lot safer. The cops, the morale wasn't what it is now. **Karol Markowicz:** No. **Jimmy Failla:** And you know, he was behind "stop and frisk" which they thought was controversial. But the whole point was they’d be like, "Wow, stop and frisk, you don't even get that many guns." I'm like, maybe because they know they’re going to get stopped. That’s the whole point! **Karol Markowicz:** That could be it. **Jimmy Failla:** So crazy. So, I see a lot of this stuff, these phrases that are bandied about because we have these elections coming and they're like, you know, who’s going to be the person? And it’s always about some type of charismatic thing. But what I think they’ve misread about Trump and his appeal is not that he availed himself so much, it’s that when he was availing himself, people aligned with what he was proposing. And of this whole idea that like, "well, if Kamala would have just gone on Joe Rogan." Kamala would have lost by twice as much if people saw three hours of that! **Karol Markowicz:** Right. **Jimmy Failla:** There's no way. **Karol Markowicz:** She can't talk for three hours. Not make sense. I mean, she doesn't even have anything to say for three hours. **Jimmy Failla:** No. Well, she does though because it takes her that long to answer one question because there's so many redundancies. But then that’s the same thing bandied about with AOC. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah. **Jimmy Failla:** AOC is somebody who, when you look at her actual acumen of deliverables, is she chased Amazon out of her district and replaced them with migrant hookers. That's who's in AOC's district. **Karol Markowicz:** I feel like most people don't like that. **Jimmy Failla:** I mean, if you’re running a small business, not ideal. Okay, you know, I know there's a song called "I Love LA" but not everything about LA. **Karol Markowicz:** Not the migrant hookers. **Jimmy Failla:** It's crazy, you know, it's crazy. Okay, but that's the reality is that in taxi parlance, I’ve never mentioned this on the show before, but I used to be a cab driver. So I'm kind of—these people are hearing it for the first time, bear with me. But JFKs or Laguardias, that's—that’s actually the name of a fare. You know, like there are cabbies who do JFKs, which means they have a relationship with a hotel, you know, a guy that’s either a dispatcher or somebody like that, and they have an ongoing relationship where they park outside the hotel. If someone’s going to JFK, he blows the whistle, gives that cabbie the fare. When the cabbie comes back he tips the guy 10 bucks, okay, and he does a consistent loop of JFKs. Or they do that with Laguardias, okay. There is now, if you drive a p.m. to a.m. night shift in the cab, they do—they’re legitimately called "AOCs" because if guys get in town looking for a red-light district, they're now out in Elmhurst on Jackson Avenue. **Karol Markowicz:** Wow. That’s a shift. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah. The *Post*, your paper, had an article about the Boulevard of Sweethearts where all of these migrant hookers happen to be. **Karol Markowicz:** Yeah, I don't know, I think people didn't vote for migrant hookers. Are they at least trans hookers or... **Jimmy Failla:** It's—it’s—we’re not that progressive yet. We’re trying. I mean, there’s definitely some, I mean, you don't need to go out to Jackson Avenue for that. Go to Times Square Ninth Avenue. They don't call it the Big Apple for nothing. There's a lot of Adam's apples over there. Holy hell, Karol Markowicz. When are you going back? **Karol Markowicz:** Staying through the weekend. **Jimmy Failla:** Ooh, yeah. Well, I’ll be in Omaha. **Karol Markowicz:** Really? **Jimmy Failla:** I’ll bring you back something nice. **Karol Markowicz:** At least extra Chinese food for you. **Jimmy Failla:** Oh, I love that. Do you want—do you want any Omaha Chinese food? I know they’ll have it. No, that's really funny. I’ve never—it's funny. I’ve never eaten—out of state—I don't think in my life have I eaten out of state ethnic cuisine. **Karol Markowicz:** Well, right. Why would you? **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, because we're here. Yeah. And it's like the real deal. **Karol Markowicz:** Yes. **Jimmy Failla:** Like a lot of people don't know, if you go down to Chinatown here, like Baxter Street, like you’re in China. You know, and we're not even saying that in a bad way, but I'm saying culturally, you will see a pronounced difference. **Karol Markowicz:** Absolutely. **Jimmy Failla:** Have you ever seen like chickens running on the streets downtown? It's the craziest thing. I had so funny. There’s a scene in *Napoleon Dynamite* where the school bus is going by as Lloyd shoots the cow. They don't show it, you just hear a gunshot and the school bus full of kids goes, "Aaaah!" So anyway, I was once driving a family down Baxter Street in Chinatown right at the time a woman just like comes running along with a machete, picks up a chicken and beheads it! **Karol Markowicz:** Ooh! **Jimmy Failla:** And the car was like, "Oh my god!" **Karol Markowicz:** And you’re like, "Welcome to New York!" **Jimmy Failla:** Welcome to New York, you guys. You know, if you wait a few years, Mom Dani will have them doing that to people. It’ll be great. You’re gonna love it. It's crazy. The great Karol Markowicz, I miss you already. **Karol Markowicz:** I really loved being here. **Jimmy Failla:** No, no, we're going to do it again. **Karol Markowicz:** Okay. **Jimmy Failla:** More Karol Markowicz is in your future, you heard it here first. **Jimmy Failla:** Well, if you’re listening on KSRO out in Santa Rosa, California, shout out to my man Lawrence Amaturo. You have a golden opportunity to see my next guest right down the highway. Get on the 101; he’s going to be in Sunnyvale, California. The club is called Rooster T. Feathers. The man is called Ryan Reiss. Yo, yo, yo. **Ryan Reiss:** What’s up, brother? How are you? **Jimmy Failla:** When are you out in California slanging these jokes? **Ryan Reiss:** June 6th and 7th. I’m leaving right after your show, on Fox. **Jimmy Failla:** June 6th and 7th. On Friday. **Ryan Reiss:** And we're going to go down to Sunnyvale, California. Rooster T. Feathers. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, you are. What airport do you fly into for a place—you go Frisco? You go a little bit calmer and go into like San Jose? **Ryan Reiss:** Oh god. Have you booked it yet? No? Well, I mean, it depends on where the fentanyl’s better. Hey! **Jimmy Failla:** Well if you like, yeah, Frisco will do you nice. Have you been in—have you driven through the Tenderloin in the last five years? It is, as a guy who drove a taxi in the city and hung out in the city when it was bad, okay, that stretch of the Mission in San Francisco is the most disturbing thing I’ve ever seen. Have you ever seen a guy fall out of a wheelchair and nobody makes any attempt to get him back in it? The only attention he draws is from someone trying to steal the wheelchair because they can panhandle for more money. **Ryan Reiss:** Horrific, man. Well, I mean, I think they're doing a good thing there in that city, they've really got things— **Jimmy Failla:** "Four more years! Give the Democrats another four!" Isn't it crazy? Because we—it’s funny. In all the time we doing—we been doing comedy in the city, New York City. We have seen every version of the city. We were here for the post-9/11 rebound. Things got going, a lot of tourists came and you know, wanted to see Ground Zero and all that. And Bloomberg kind of cleaned it up a little more. Then De Blasio came. And wait, you were running a show at the Gershwin? **Ryan Reiss:** Oh, do you remember that? **Jimmy Failla:** You were running a show at the Gershwin which I believe was just entertaining swingers between orgies. There was a hotel called the Gershwin Hotel, and to co-sign your research, it was next—it’s next to the Museum of Sex. That is real; it is called the Museum of Sex, the hotel right next door. As you’d imagine, the people who stay in a hotel like that— **Ryan Reiss:** I call my bedroom that! **Jimmy Failla:** 80 degrees and sunny, but a lot of raincoats in the audience. It’s crazy. And I remember when we started out, we were doing—that's where we were doing comedy. So we've seen every incarnation of this, and the city's kind of gone up and down. But what they’re doing in California right now is special. They have—I was watching this debate last night, so Spencer Pratt, reality guy— **Ryan Reiss:** Yeah, yeah, I saw that. **Jimmy Failla:** And he was kind of hooting and hollering for good reason about what they currently tolerate. Which is, you know, safe injection sites, homeless encampments in front of elementary schools. Like, could you imagine being a 10-year-old? You know how sophomoric things are and stuff like that and how many dumb things little boys do? And now you're walking through a safe injection site or there’s a daytime hooker? **Ryan Reiss:** Well, I mean, the first thing is shocking. The second thing is just, you know, hands-on training. How does she look? Come on Ryan, they call them sex workers, please. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, these women deserve some dignity. But how did we, as—as young children who grew up in an era of just say no, get to a world where they literally are now funding the drug use? They're like, "Well, the taxpayer's gotta give these people free needles." **Ryan Reiss:** And no one wants to deal with it. Is that what it is? Yeah, the only thing they did was just—just mitigate the disease by giving out needles. It’s—it’s— **Jimmy Failla:** They’re like, "Well, it’ll be safer." But you get more zombies. That’s a point Spencer Pratt was making. He’s like, they think homelessness is somebody ran into some tough luck and they're on the street. And I'm—I'm not denying that reality, but do you know what percentage of the homeless that is? About 2. The actual percentage in California of people that are addicted to drugs and are homeless is over 93%. So giving them a home is essentially giving them an asset they can sell and loot for cash. That’s all you’re doing. It’s not like they’re going to start mowing the lawn and stuff like that. That’s what I think is so—and for cheap, too. I mean like—yes, thank you. But if you want to visit California and see Ryan do comedy, June 6th and 7th, Rooster T. Feathers, Sunnyvale, California. And— **Ryan Reiss:** I'm getting paid in clean needles, so please come. Free crack pipes. **Jimmy Failla:** I’ll be—listen man, I get there a month later. I’m going to be in Monterey the 17th, Napa the 18th. We're at the Grove in Anaheim on July the 25th. Failla is going out west, making our annual California swing. We love California. You do too. **Ryan Reiss:** Yeah, I love going out there. **Jimmy Failla:** California's the prettiest state in the world. Does it have some dicey terrain? Yes. But it doesn't mean like as a country you're supposed to give up on it because you don't like the people running it. **Ryan Reiss:** Good weather just changes people's minds. You know what I mean? It’s so beautiful you're like, "just let 'em be. It’s fine." **Jimmy Failla:** "What's three million dollars for a studio apartment? What are we talking about here?" That is what happens. But think of how badly you have to mismanage a place like that to get the population exodus that they have. You know, because everybody used to say, "Well, of course people move to Florida, look at the weather." But look at the weather in California; no one's moving there anymore. **Ryan Reiss:** So I was in Reno, and I was told that if—if you want to take a truck from Reno to California, it's free. They won't charge you. Because all the trucks are going from California to Reno. So they need the trucks—they need someone to take the trucks back. **Jimmy Failla:** So you're essentially like the Enterprise guy, because they returned the car at the wrong location and you’ve got to get it back to the correct airport. Wow. But think of—listen, if you are running for office, if you’re Spencer Pratt or Steve Hilton who comes on the show all the time, that's really the only thing you should be saying right now. Like, guys, if you really boil it down to it. If there's a line to get in, you got a good restaurant. If there's a line to get out, do you know what those Yelp reviews must look like? There's a line to get out, Ryan. **Ryan Reiss:** Well, that’s why they’re charging to get out. When you leave California or New York, they hit you with a bill. **Jimmy Failla:** It's my favorite thing that Mom Dani’s like, "Alright, we're going to charge people even if they go." I'm like, how can you even do that? I mean, that’s—that’s an old comedy club joke, right? Yeah, it’s free to come in, but to leave it's 40 bucks. You gotta get out of here right now. We're talking to Ryan Reiss. You can see him in Sunnyvale, California. Rooster T. Feathers, June 6th and 7th, because he’s booked for Juneteenth, he’s going to the Obama library opening. Are you attending? **Ryan Reiss:** Do it every year. **Jimmy Failla:** The Obama library, which looks like a toy—a Star Wars toy. Where is it? It's in Chicago. Oh, okay. Yeah. So it’d be nice for them to go there once. No, they ain't hanging out in Chicago. That's my favorite thing in the world. Is everybody’s like, "It's so racist that Trump calls out the inner cities," as they do a podcast from Martha’s Vineyard. **Ryan Reiss:** Yeah. Trump was in the Bronx. Like he goes to these places. **Jimmy Failla:** Yeah, Trump actually shows up. Obama lives in such a white neighborhood that he calls the cops on himself when he goes jogging. Like, literally. He’s like, "No, no, I'm the ex-president!" But it’s a whole—Martha’s Vineyard! And you know where else they have a house? Hawaii. And you know where else they have a house? D.C. In Georgetown. Nobody moves back to the thing, you know. It ain't right. Except Don Mattingly. Don Mattingly still lives in Evansville, Indiana in the off-season. One guy put his money where his mouth is. **Ryan Reiss:** You're still on Long Island. **Jimmy Failla:** Me? Thank you. Okay, my family’s there. I hang out with my mom and stuff like that. And I wouldn't leave, you know. I think the superpower— **Ryan Reiss:** I hope not, because I’m in the backyard. **Jimmy Failla:** That is true. That is so funny. If me and Jenny ever move, we have to put Ryan in the real estate listing. "No, no, he comes with the house. Yeah, yeah, he’s just—he’s grandfathered into the backyard drinking." I owe him so much money for booze, I’m just working it off right now. Talking to Ryan Reiss. Let me give you another one. Because we had a Mom Dani thing happen today. So there’s a guy Ken Griffin, who runs Citadel. Big company, 350 Park Avenue. If—350 Park Avenue. You took an NFL headquarters there. You know what else is there? 346 Park Avenue. The greatest steakhouse in the history of New York City, Bobby Van’s. **Ryan Reiss:** Bobby Van’s! **Jimmy Failla:** Love—nobody likes Bobby Van’s more than this guy. And I’ve been going to Bobby Van’s once a year since I was a cab driver and couldn't afford to go. **Ryan Reiss:** You guys have never seen anything like this. When he walks in, it's like a receiving line at a wedding. All the waiters are "Jimmy! So good to see you! Kiss my granddaughter!" **Jimmy Failla:** But understand, this is because I have been a part of the fabric of that restaurant for 20 years. The fact that I somehow lucked into some TV notoriety is just like an add-on to all of that that was already there. But I truly—the Bobby Van’s bone-in ribeye, I'll put it up against anything anywhere in America. Okay, but again, that’s the Helmsley building. We're talking about big real estate. So if Ken Griffin is one door away from Bobby Van’s, he is running a company just to put it in perspective, ladies and gentlemen. What we're talking about is a Fortune 100 company. Not a Fortune 500 company, a Fortune 50 company. It's one of the biggest companies in America. Okay, they had made a decision to move, okay, from Chicago because it was bad. And they're like, "Alright, we're gonna do a little New York to give you an idea how bad Chicago is." He's like, "Maybe we'll go to the socialist metropolis of New York instead, where they want to defund the police." That’s what Chicago is doing. So they're like, "We'll do a half New York, half Miami." So they get to 350 Park Avenue. He does a press conference the other day and they go, "How does this Mom Dani thing go so far?" And he goes, "Actually, funny you mention that, we're buying more office space in Miami because of Mom Dani." Can I play you this clip? **Ryan Reiss:** Yeah, please. **Jimmy Failla:** It's great. And I want you guys to understand this. Okay, everyone in New York, Paul Glaser was telling me this from East Texas, KTBB. He's like, "You know, they're opening up a stock exchange in Dallas and all of that." I don't think the average New Yorker appreciates just how much Wall Street drives our economy, but how unappealing New York is to the guys who run Wall Street. What I mean by that is Wall Street is predominantly occupied by a lot of coked-up perverts. Okay, have you seen *The Wolf of Wall Street*? That’s just Merrill Lynch. Now imagine you are those perverts and you're in New York, where you freeze your ass off six months out of the year, which means the women are covered up. You're in the Middle East six months of the year. You know what you're not doing in Miami, the other city? You're not covering up the women, Ryan. Oh my—I can’t. So I don't think the average person realizes how perilous this situation is, because of course they'd love to leave New York. I mean, yes, some of them are like us, they got families and stuff. But here he is, saying his Miami office just got bigger because of Mom Dani. Clip 39. **Interviewer:** So where does that leave us at 350 Park? **Ken Griffin:** That leaves us with the fact that we went to Miami and revised our building plan to make it a bigger office building. **Interviewer:** Oh. You're bailing? **Ken Griffin:** So—so what we do at 350 is a still point of discussion internally. But what is no longer a point of discussion is that Miami is now—you know, one of the—when we moved from Chicago, there was a debate between New York and Miami. It's unquestionably true that we made the right choice. I'll leave it at that. It's unquestionably true that we made the right choice. And now, and now what the Mayor of New York has made clear to my partners, and principally my New York partners, my New York partners, is that we need to double down on our bet in Miami. **Jimmy Failla:** Ouch. So you know those one-way U-Hauls you were talking about? Pretty soon they'll be free from Florida to New York, except no one's making that drive. **Ryan Reiss:** Well, sounds like I got another job. Ryan making that drive. But I love it when people are always like, "Oh, but you're not going to get what you have in New York in Miami. Broadway?" I'm like, "Really? Do you—really? Do you really need that? Do you really need to see the remake of 'Cats' Live? Like I don't—have you been to Miami?" **Jimmy Failla:** I know. I know, right? Do you think anyone in—in Miami right now, with all those scantily clad starlets is like, "If only I could see that Luigi Mangione musical." You know, you know Dylan Mulvaney is on Broadway and it’s a shame. **Ryan Reiss:** We're missing the Michael Jackson remake. Oh no. **Jimmy Failla:** But Mom Dani—excuse me, Mom Dani, with a clap back to what he says should happen and what he says—let me find the clip Eric really quick. This is one of my favorites. But he's basically talking about how, you know, he is asked, what do you do, Ken Griffin says he's going to leave, and we get a little gobbledygook about fair share and rich people and blah blah blah. Clip 29. **Mom Dani:** So I'll—I’ll first just start by saying that I want all New Yorkers to succeed. That includes business owners and entrepreneurs who create good-paying jobs, who make this city the economic engine not just of our state, but frankly of our country. And that also includes Ken Griffin, who is a major employer in our city. That does not negate the fact, however, that our tax system is fundamentally broken. It rewards extreme wealth while working people are pushed to the brink. It is a status quo that is unsustainable, that is unjust, and if we want the city to be one that working people can actually afford to live in, then we need meaningful tax reform that includes the wealthiest New Yorkers paying their fair share. **Jimmy Failla:** So imagine that. This guy just said it's too expensive here, we don't get a return on our investment, and we're moving our company to Miami. And Mom Dani yells, "But wait! We'll tax you more!" I mean, think of the insanity of that. **Ryan Reiss:** Well, I mean, if he—if he feels that way, shouldn't he just give his own salary up? Imagine! Shouldn't he just donate that? Aren't his parents rich? They’re loaded. So it sounds like he should have been giving that speech at the dinner table. **Jimmy Failla:** But what’s so fascinating is the answer is never to cut spending. Like, spending policies and spending money is what makes your life unaffordable. If you guys are listening around the country right now, okay, inflation can only be caused by the government. The great Milton Friedman. If you want to hear a Milton Friedman, 30 seconds of your life. This is everything you need to know about inflation. **Milton Friedman:** Inflation is made in Washington because only Washington can create money. And any other attribution to other groups of inflation is wrong. Consumers don't produce it. Producers don't produce it. Trade unions don't produce it. Foreign sheiks don't produce it. Oil imports don't produce it. What produces it is too much government spending and too much government creation of money and nothing else. **Jimmy Failla:** Amen. And understand, okay, this is being compounded in a place like New York because we are a sanctuary city and state. This means you’re spending additional government money to house, feed, and provide healthcare for folks in the country illegally. So I'm just saying, like, this—it’s so stupid. And I don’t think the average person in New York—I’m talking to a WOR audience at the moment—I don’t think the average person realizes how close we could be to losing our biggest economic engine. Because if you are a financial genius, can you think of anything dumber to do than give more money to the people who collect a record amount of money and wound up in debt? You know what I'm saying? Wouldn't you rather give it to the hookers in Miami? **Ryan Reiss:** I was going to say, I mean, like, other than these guys making money and being taxed, they spend a lot of money out. Thank you. They're the ones who have the big parties and they—they buy all the booze and, you know, all the restaurants are them, they’re not cooking. They’re tipping everybody, propping up the strip clubs when the Japanese businessmen come to town. **Ryan Reiss:** I used to say that—I used to tell people like, "Don't go to a strip club in New York City, you’re competing with finance guys. You can't—you don't have the money to compete with those guys. You’re going to get no attention." You're going to want to go out to the suburbs. Because if somebody’s wining and dining Mr. Yamamoto from Japan, they're not looking to give you lap dances in between. A Kansas strip club, oh, they'll give you a lot of attention, but in New York City, I mean... **Jimmy Failla:** Ryan Reiss. If there's not a Cracker Barrel next to the strip club, he ain't going.

Secretary of State Rubio Speaks to Reporters in Rome

C-SPAN(1.8M)·23:10·10.5K views·2026-05-08
Marco RubioSecretary of State, United States Giorgia MeloniPrime Minister, Italy Pope LeoPope
TL;DRSecretary of State Marco Rubio’s visit to Rome focused on aligning with the Vatican and the Italian government on global security threats, specifically Iran’s maritime aggression and the need for humanitarian corridors in Cuba and Sudan.
Rubio reaffirmed that while the...

TL;DR

Secretary of State Marco Rubio’s visit to Rome focused on aligning with the Vatican and the Italian government on global security threats, specifically Iran’s maritime aggression and the need for humanitarian corridors in Cuba and Sudan. Rubio reaffirmed that while the U.S. remains committed to NATO, future troop deployments will be contingent on members' willingness to grant the U.S. logistical power projection during global contingencies.

Diplomatic Engagement with the Vatican

  • The Papal Meeting: Rubio characterized his meeting with Pope Leo as "cordial and important," focusing on the Church’s role as a global spiritual leader and humanitarian facilitator.
      • Strategic Alignment: Discussions covered Christian populations in Africa, the situation in Lebanon, and the Church’s insights into Latin American political dynamics.
      • Addressing Tension: Rubio dismissed media narratives regarding President Trump’s past criticisms of the Pope, stating the President will act in the U.S. interest while maintaining a "productive and fruitful" relationship with the Church.
  • Humanitarian Aid to Cuba: Rubio highlighted that the U.S. has offered $100 million in humanitarian aid to the Cuban people, but the "incompetent regime" is blocking distribution.
      • Role of the Church: The Catholic agency Caritas remains a primary partner for distributing aid, such as recent hurricane relief.

National Security and NATO Strategy

  • NATO and Troop Presence: Rubio addressed potential U.S. troop withdrawals from Europe, noting that while no final decisions have been made by the President, the U.S. is reassessing its footprint.
      • Base Access Challenges: Rubio expressed frustration with NATO allies—specifically citing Spain—who denied the U.S. use of bases during recent "contingencies."
      • Policy Shift: He warned that if NATO membership no longer guarantees the ability to project power from European bases, the U.S. must examine the cost and utility of those deployments.
  • Ukraine and Russia: Rubio described the ongoing conflict as a tragedy and noted that U.S. mediation efforts have "stagnated." He emphasized that the U.S. stands ready to mediate only if there is a realistic opportunity for a diplomatic resolution.

Iranian Aggression and International Waterways

  • Maritime "Red Lines": Rubio warned that Iran is attempting to "normalize" illegal control over international waterways, specifically the Strait of Hormuz.
      • Defensive Posture: Following reports of U.S. destroyers being fired upon, Rubio stated that any threat to American assets would be met with immediate force.
      • Operation Epic Fury: He distinguished current defensive responses from "Operation Epic Fury," which he defined as a completed offensive mission that successfully destroyed Iranian missile launchers and air capabilities.
  • Nuclear Ambitions: Rubio asserted there is global consensus that a nuclear-armed Iran is "unacceptable," but emphasized that the current administration is the first to take "concrete" action to prevent it.

Regional Conflicts and Foreign Policy

  • Lebanon and Hezbollah: The U.S. goal is a strong Lebanese government capable of confronting Hezbollah. Rubio called on Italy to play a role in equipping the Lebanese military and cutting off illicit financing for the Iranian-backed group.
  • Sudan Crisis: Rubio identified Sudan as one of the world's worst humanitarian crises. The U.S. is pushing for a "humanitarian ceasefire" and safe havens for aid distribution through the "quad process."
  • Cuba Sanctions: Rubio clarified that recent sanctions target GAESA, a holding company run by Cuban generals that "steals from the Cuban people" to enrich regime insiders, rather than targeting the populace directly.
  • Taiwan: Ahead of a scheduled trip to China, Rubio reaffirmed that U.S. policy remains unchanged: the U.S. opposes any forced or compelled change to the status quo in the Taiwan Strait.

***

Notable Quotes

"The red line is clear: if they threaten Americans they're going to get blown up... If you fire a missile at the United States and we saw you fire it, we're going to hit you. Of course we are. Who doesn't do that?" — Secretary of State Marco Rubio
"One of the advantages of being in NATO is that it allows us to have forces deployed in Europe... some countries in Europe, like Spain as an example, denied us the use of those bases for a very important contingency... if that's no longer the case... that's a problem and it has to be examined." — Secretary of State Marco Rubio
"We have the most resources, but our resources are not unlimited. We have to always assign resources around the world including military resources on the basis of what serves the national interest." — Secretary of State Marco Rubio
Okay, let's just go to questions. Are you ready? Let's go. Hi, Mr. Secretary. Hi, yeah. Thanks for doing this. I wanted to ask you first about your meeting yesterday with the Pope Leo. President Trump has had very harsh language and words for the Pope against the backdrop of the war in Iran. Did you make amends with the Pope yesterday? No, we had a very good meeting. I mean, ultimately it's important, first of all, we—there's a lot that we work together with, with the church, and we talked about those areas that we're working together on in different parts of the world. I know everyone's interested in, you know, the other aspects of it, but I expressed, you know, updated them on the situation with Iran. I expressed our point of view about why this was an important and the danger that Iran poses to the world, which is largely recognized. So, obviously the, you know, the—the Holy Father is a—a spiritual leader, he's the—first and foremost. I mean, that's—that's his role to play. And obviously the church has always interacted on behalf of a mission for peace and—and a—respect for all of humanity. But at the end it was a very cordial and important meeting, and it's—it's important to share our points of view and—explanation and an understanding of where we're coming from. And I thought it was very positive. Secretary Rubio, you also mentioned talking about the Western Hemisphere with the Pope. And of course, the Catholic Church is instrumental in delivering humanitarian aid to Cuba. I wondered if you discussed that and whether there was any disagreements on US policy towards Cuba. What— Oh, we discussed, I mean, we—there—we've provided six million dollars of humanitarian aid, US humanitarian aid that was distributed by Caritas, the Catholic Church agency. We're prepared to do more. In fact, we've offered the regime there 100 million dollars of humanitarian aid that unfortunately so far they have not agreed to distribute to help the people of Cuba. So we did the—the—hurricane relief, but we're offering more. And it's the regime that's not accepting it. It's the regime that's standing in the way of it. So we discussed that. And we hope we can do it because we do want to help the people of Cuba who are being, you know, hurt by this incompetent regime that's destroyed the country and the—and the economy. Secretary, can I ask about the—the meeting with the Pope? I mean, after this meeting with the Pope, which you described as—as cordial and productive, are you going to recommend to the President that he stops criticizing the Pope in social media? Why would I tell you what I'm going to recommend to the President? But beyond that, the President will always speak clearly about how he feels about the US and US policy. The President of the United States is always going to act in what's in the best interest of the United States. I think we can do that and—and continue to also have a very productive and fruitful and important relationship with the church, because it plays an important role in the world as well. Mr. Secretary, could we talk about whether or not— No, no. I had called her. I'm sorry. Secretary Rubio, were there any themes from your conversation yesterday with Pope Leo that resonated with you personally? Well, if it was personal, why would I tell you? No. I—here's—here's the bottom line on that is that the church is an important global institution, it has a presence all over the world. I know you guys are fixated on who said what about who at what time. I understand all that, okay? Because media, you guys have headlines, you have editors, you have people that want you to post, and these are interesting stories. But at the end of the day, the Pope just returned from Africa, a—very important continent with growing Christian populations, many of whom are threatened or feel threatened by the spread of radical Islamic terrorism. So we care about that. The—the Pope and the church has an interest in Christian communities in Lebanon, a place that we're very involved in trying to establish a peace between Israel and Lebanon. The church obviously has a very important presence in Latin America. The bishops from Venezuela had just been here a few days earlier. So we shared thoughts about those sorts of things. I think it's an opportunity to personally express, you know, our point of view from the standpoint of foreign policy and the areas we're involved in and share insights. But also to gain insights, because the church has a unique role in many of these countries in terms of the insights and information that they're receiving. In some cases in the past, the church has been an important interlocutor, not just with governments but with societies. And then of course on the practical level, the church plays an—an important role in humanitarian efforts in different parts of the world, and in many places has served as a facilitator of US humanitarian assistance such as I highlighted a moment ago when it comes to Cuba. Yes, go ahead. Mr. Secretary, can you say in your meeting with Prime Minister Meloni whether you talked about the possibility of withdrawing US troops from Italy as well as the possibility of the United States withdrawing from NATO altogether? No, we—I—we didn't discuss any specifics like that and that's a decision for the President to make. It's a decision every President makes. The—fact of the matter though is, and I—I've said, you know, publicly and I've said repeatedly: I've been a strong supporter of NATO throughout my career in the Senate and even now, and one of the advantages of being in NATO is that it allows us to have forces deployed in Europe and bases that allow us a logistical ability to project power in—in case of contingencies. Well, we had a contingency. And some countries in Europe, some countries in Europe, like Spain as an example, denied us the use of those bases for a very important contingency that in some ways the denial of those bases actually impeded the mission, not severely, but had a—had a cost and in fact even created some unnecessary dangers. So if the re—if one of the main reasons why the US is in NATO is the ability to have forces deployed in Europe that we could project to other contingencies, and now that's no longer the case, at least when it comes to some NATO members, that's a problem and it has to be examined. But ultimately that's a decision for the President to make. His team and people like myself and others will provide him what those potential options are, but ultimately he'll have to make that decision. He hasn't made those decisions yet. As far as recent news of deployments, like for example the—ones—those were all already ongoing. For example in Germany, the—the—troops that were withdrawn, which represent less than 14 percent of our total troop presence there, that's already pre-programmed. And in fact, all it did was take us back to where we were in 2022. And so there was—there was always a plan to do some shifting within NATO. As far as broader changes, tha—that's—I don't have an announcement for you on that today, but that's a decision for the President to make. Certainly, you know, we'll present both options and perspectives, but we're—you know, that—I don't have anything new to announce on that today. Yes. Mr. Secretary, your former colleagues on the Hill, Republican senators, believe that troops withdrawal will embolden Putin and will benefit Russia. Do you agree with that? Sorry, troops withdrawal will embolden Putin and benefit Russia. Do you agree with that assessment? Well, in the end, the United States is always—the President is going to always do what's in the national interest. We have global obligations all over the world. We have troop deployments all over the world. And those are constantly shifting and changing. For example, we increased troop presence in NATO in the aftermath of the invasion of Ukraine. So—we're always making these decisions and there were some already previewed in—before any of this had happened in terms of the—base denials. But in the end, look—here the bottom line is: we always, you know, while we may be the most powerful country in the world and we may have the most resources, our resources are not unlimited. We have to always assign resources around the world, including military resources, on the basis of what serves the national interest. That's a decision the President has to make, especially within the context of recent developments by some countries within NATO and the stance they've taken towards the United States. But you know, no decision's been made on that yet, that's something the President's still working through. Have you managed to bridge differences on Iran, both with Rome and with the—with the Vatican? Yeah, I mean, I—I don't—I think we—everybody agrees that Iran having a nuclear weapon is unacceptable. Every—I mean, I don't know of anyone who thinks it's a good idea for Iran to have a nuclear weapon except the Ayatollah. So I think the question is what do you do about it? And the President is actually trying to do something about it. And so I mean, that's—I don't know with the—the—I don't think there's any disagreement that they can't have a nuclear weapon. I think the question is what do you do about it? And you know, we've had every American President says 'Oh, they can't have a nuclear weapon'. This is the first American President that's actually trying to do something concrete about it. I don't know who would disagree with that. I mean, it's an important thing that has to be confronted. Can we get an update on the—on the status of negotiations with Iran right now? Well, we should know something today. I mean, we're expecting a response from them. We'll see what the response entails. The hope is that it's something that it can put us into a serious process of negotiation. Obviously we've seen the reporting overnight that Iran is establishing or trying to establish some agency that's going to control traffic in the straits. That would be very problematic. That would actually be unacceptable. We—I mean, we're—the normalization of their controlling of international waterways is both illegal and un—and just something that's unacceptable, and the world has to start asking itself what is it willing to do if Iran tries to normalize a control of an international waterway? I think that's unacceptable. So, but we're—we're expecting a response from them today at some point. We have not received that yet as—you know, the last—in the last hour, but perhaps that will come. Their system is still highly fractured and a bit dysfunctional as well. So that may be serving as an impediment. I hope it's a serious offer. I really do. Thanks. Can you confirm there's a resume of the talks between Israel and Lebanon next week? Do you expect that to go forward? We expect talks. I can't—I'm not sure if we've set the exact date yet, but we expect that there will be additional talks. Look, the—we—it's one of the areas that I think we can work with—with Italy on. They can play a very productive role and a constructive role in providing resources to the Lebanese government. In the end, we all share the same goal, and by all I mean including the Lebanese government and the Israeli government. That we want the relations between Israel and Lebanon, its legitimate government, to be very strong. The impediment to that is Hezbollah. Again, an Iranian agent, Hezbollah, who is victimizing not just Israelis, but Hezbollah's also victimizing Lebanon. The reason why Lebanon faces bombings, the reason why Lebanon faces violence, is because of Hezbollah. It is Hezbollah that's imposing this on them. So I think we all share the same goal which is a strong Lebanese government that doesn't have an armed Hezbollah operating within its national territory imposing a threat to any of its neighbors. And to do that, we have to empower and equip the Lebanese government to be able to confront that threat. And I think Italy and other countries, but Italy in particular, can play an important role in not just helping equip the government but in cutting off the illicit financing that supports—helping them to cut off the financing that supports Hezbollah and the danger they pose. Is it possible to get a peace deal on Lebanon without first solving the—the conflict with Iran? How—how linked are those two conflicts right now? Well, I mean, Hezbollah's an Iranian agent, 100 percent. I mean, Hezbollah wouldn't exist without Iran support. So but I think it's possible. I mean, Hez—Hezbollah's been both weakened but still capable of inflicting damage and doing terrorist activities as we've seen. So, I think the way I would phrase it is: we're not going to negotiate with Iran over Hezbollah because Hezbollah is a dangerous—other than if they're willing to stop funding them and supporting them. I think our negotiations are with Lebanese—our role is with the Lebanese government. Lebanon should be governed by the Lebanese government. It should not have a terrorist group operating within its national territory that poses a threat both to its own people, including the Shia population, and to the government and to Israel and to its other neighbors. So that's what we're going to focus on. Mr. Secretary, can we come back to Cuba a little bit? The—United States, including yesterday, are ramping up sanctions against—against Cuba, the regime and all that. With the exchange with the Pope, did you feel any convergence of—of views on that and on the US policy? Well, let me clarify something for you. Our sanctions are against a company named GAESA. Okay? This is a holding company set up by generals in Cuba that has generated billions of dollars of revenue, none of which benefits the Cuban people. Not one cent of it benefits the Cuban people. You understand this, right? I don't know if you know this. There's the Cuban government and they have a budget, and then there's this private company that has more money than the government does. None of the money in that company goes to build a single road, a single bridge, provide a single grain of rice to a single Cuban, other than the people that are part of GAESA. So that's what we're sanctioning. Is a company that basically is taking anything that makes money in Cuba and illegally putting it into the pockets of a few regime insiders. So that's not sanctions on the Cuban people because the Cuban people don't benefit from GAESA. It's a sanction against this company that is stealing from the Cuban people to the benefit of a few. And we didn't discuss those sanctions yesterday, but we imposed them yesterday and we're going to be doing more, by the way. Mr. Secretary, sorry. Many people thought that your—your was a mission impossible after the Trump's attack—heavy attacks—heavy attacks on the Pope. Which mission, I'm sorry? The visit—the mission. Yes. What's your judgment at the—at the end of the visit? Mission accomplished? We work with the Vatican on a lot of things.
