There's either the stuff we've learned about the early days of OpenAI, and particularly the circumstances under which Elon Musk ends up leaving. We learned a lot about that this week, particularly from Greg Brockman, who is one of the co-founders and is now the president of OpenAI. And then there's also what happened during what everybody refers to as "the blip," the brief period of time where Sam Altman was not the CEO. Should we start with the early days? Because there's a lot of interesting stuff that comes up. There's a bunch of stuff that I didn't know, that maybe I just missed in the course of all of this stuff, including things like, at the very beginning, OpenAI was going to be part of Y Combinator, where Sam was running Y Combinator, this big VC firm for a long time. And like, I think more and more it's becoming clear that a lot of the internal consternation at OpenAI over the years has been all of the stuff that Sam Altman and Greg Brockman did around OpenAI, and the ways in which they were semi-sketchily enriching themselves, all while claiming to have no interest in a nonprofit and just trying to save the world. And if you're Elon Musk, it seems like a really good idea to try to prove as much of that as you possibly can, right? Is that a fair description of what's going on? I think that's a fair characterization of what's going on. You will recall that Sam left his position as CEO of Y Combinator in totally mysterious and extremely sketchy circumstances. Yes. And it is probably because he was double-dealing all over the place, and no one has ever really come out and said it. But a lot of people have basically been like, "If you connect all these dots that I keep leaving everywhere, you will deduce that Sam Altman did a bunch of weird double-dealing stuff and is no longer the CEO of Y Combinator." And I think we're starting to see that in this case as well. Which, by the way, is totally in character with the Sam Altman that is routinely profiled by everybody, including Ronan Farrow. Like, there's a lot of weird double-dealing, talking out of both sides of your mouth, trying to make everyone happy, getting very rich along the way. Yeah, and this ends up being really important in the firing of Sam Altman, but also appears to have existed since the very beginning, right? They're running this nonprofit, they're starting this thing, they're trying to save the world, and Sam Altman like famously didn't take equity in this company, has spent a lot of time talking about how he didn't take equity in this company. And then you start to poke at it and it's like, well, he has equity in that company that is now doing a deal with OpenAI, and he has equity in that company which is doing a deal with OpenAI. You just see there, I think Elon Musk's lawyers have a real vested interest in building a pattern of Sam Altman being unreliable and trying to make himself rich while building a nonprofit on Elon Musk's money. But there are parts of that case that appear to be very straightforwardly compelling from Musk's side of things. There are parts of that case that appear to be very straightforwardly compelling. And I'm going to issue a disclaimer: criticism of one character in this lawsuit is not praise for the other character. Agreed. And both sides get to put on their case. And so, when OpenAI's lawyers are putting on their case, Elon looks maximally bad, and when Elon's lawyers are putting on his case, Sam Altman looks maximally bad, and that's just the way trial coverage goes. And I say this every time because everyone gets very confused about how we feel when we're just talking about the cases the lawyers are putting on. But the out that I have for Sam and Greg, just in this process, yes, the evidence against them is compelling, that they basically did a bunch of shady stuff. They are not sly by any stretch of the imagination. They are just out in the open being like, "So we're going to do some circular financing stuff." And Greg is like leaving himself journal notes. It's like, "Man, I shouldn't, I shouldn't steal a charity." They know what they are doing. They are operating out in the open, they're sending Elon these extremely long notes that all start with this staggering amount of praise because everyone's basically afraid of Elon, it seems, and they have to be really nice to him to get to one decision. And they keep laying everything out. There is, at no point, are they being shady about it, except that the thing that they're doing seems kind of shady. Do you know what I mean? Like, they're like, "We're going to do a shady thing." And Elon's like, "I don't know." And then that proceeds until they get it done. Well, ironically, it's not shady when they're all on the same team, right? And then it just becomes, you know, we all... there's so much money in this that we're all going to get rich and it's going to be fine. And this is like what you see in WeWork, another thing where there was just an outrageous amount of self-dealing. As long as the numbers keep going up and as long as everybody is getting rich, the things people will turn a blind eye to in that realm are outrageous. But then we come to the falling out between Elon Musk and the OpenAI team, and I think this is the part where Elon's case, kind of even in the very best case scenario, looks bad. Because essentially what it seems like is, again, and we said this a little bit last week, there was this big fight over who was going to control OpenAI. Elon Musk demanded everything. He at one point tried to make OpenAI part of Tesla. He said he was going to run OpenAI inside of Tesla in secret and apparently offered Sam Altman a board seat at Tesla. It was like he wanted to subsume this whole thing. He would say his motivations were to save the world; you can feel about that however you want. He lost that fight, basically because the others teamed up and said, "Giving essentially unilateral control to Elon Musk is not the right thing to do." And he took his ball and went home making aggressive threats about starting up a competitor, about how they were going to fail, about how Demis Hassabis, who was running DeepMind, who is like the looming big bad in everybody's mind, which is very funny in retrospect, is going to like ruin the world because OpenAI can't do it. He has such like sore loser energy in this whole split, that it just looks bad. There was also some just extremely weird Elon stuff in the mix. Shivon Zilis, who is the mother of some of his children, is part of OpenAI. Her job is to take notes, basically, on what everyone is talking about. So there's a lot of notes about what Greg Brockman, Sam Altman, Ilya Sutskever, and Elon Musk are talking about. And then, when Elon leaves, she's still there. She's passing intel back to Elon, and then reports come out that she's had his children, and she has to go and tell OpenAI that she's also the mother of Elon Musk's children. Right. And everyone is like, "What are you talking about?" Changes the dynamic slightly. And so it's just like one of the more Elon situations in all of this, that now the mother of his children is on the stand. And even the way that she talks about things, in her affect, is just very Elon. On the stand, instead of saying, "I don't recall" or "I don't remember," she would say, "It's not in my neurons." I hate that, I got to tell you. There's something about that that just makes my skin crawl. That when she said it, our entire Slack room of reporters tracking the trial, they all typed, "It's not in my neurons" like at the same time. Perfect. That's, yeah, it's exactly right. So, yeah, this whole thing is just bizarre. And it really does seem like the further we get into this trial, that the outlines of this split were that Elon Musk wanted control of OpenAI, he lost the fight, and he went away. He says one thing, in 2017, after a discussion about going for-profit, which again is a big piece of this, right? Who wanted to go for-profit and when? Was Musk for this, was Musk against this, did they steal a charity? Like, this is a big part of the whole thing here. This is Brockman writing this, he says at the end of the meeting he said, "You guys are great, but I could start another AI company tomorrow, one tweet is all it takes." Which is, A, five years before Elon Musk bought Twitter. There's the whole reason Elon Musk bought Twitter. And B, just a perfect summation of what a sore loser he is in this particular way. I thought this one is very interesting to me. There is so much in this that is just about who is the talent and where will they work. Sure. This is why they're all afraid of Demis Hassabis. He's got Google's resources, he's got runway, he seems to be attracting the talent. They all think he's very smart. And Elon's like, "I'm going to take Andrej Karpathy. I'm going to take Ilya Sutskever. I've recruited this person." And it's just a handful of people at the bleeding edge of this technology. Especially at this time, it was like a really, really small number of people who were really leading this stuff. Yeah, and they were all basically deciding whether they were going to go out on their own or go work for Demis at Google. Like, these were your choices at the time. Mhm. "One tweet is all it takes" really implies that there's like a bunch of AI people he can like address via tweet. It's a very odd, empty threat at this moment in time, when so much of the drama is basically where is Ilya going to work. Can we keep him away from Demis? Right. And maybe there are more characters in the mix, maybe there are vastly more AI researchers at that time just waiting for a tweet, but it just struck me as such a classic Elon threat, like, "I can tweet this into existence," when every fact that had preceded it indicated that actually you need to hire one of like four people to make this work. Yeah, and then there's the moment where he hires away Andrej Karpathy to Tesla, and he goes back to OpenAI and is like, "Oh, I have an announcement and a confession. I've hired this guy," who is like very important and is now like a celebrity in the AI world, to go work at Tesla, which is a thing that if you care about OpenAI and want it to succeed, you probably don't do. Yeah, at the same time, there's emails in the record now of Sam emailing Elon apologizing for having recruited even one person away for Tesla. Right. Like, "I'm so sorry about this." There's some real... I'm watching 'The Boys,' there's some real Homelander vibes around Elon throughout this entire, you know, everyone's just like afraid of him. Mhm. And instead of lasers from his eyes, he has Twitter. Like, that's basically what this feels like at every turn. Well, it's more than that though, there's the... I wrote down, these texts from Ilya Sutskever came up where he says, "Elon might spend half a day a week with us. I imagined how it will be and I worry that our work environment can become very stressful, and since he'll be bankrolling it, it'll be hard to stop it." Like, you're right, it is fear and flattery in equal measure, and there is this sense of like we have to take his money to do this thing, but we also have to figure out how to keep him out of it as much as possible because he will ruin it. Yeah. And then they're talking about all these conversions and what structure it will be, and the whole time he's like, "Well, I'll just take it, give it to me." Yeah, yeah. And now we're in a lawsuit, which, by the way, he insistently filed. Like, this is... he's trying to kill OpenAI through this. Elon Musk wanted this lawsuit, it's an important thing. This is all happening because he wants it to happen. Yeah, yeah, agreed. So, the other thing which I think is, frankly, vastly more fun in terms of the things we got to learn, is all the stuff that happened during Sam Altman's firing, this very brief couple of days where Sam Altman was fired, unceremoniously and kind of out of nowhere, staged a coup is like a non-generous, but not wrong, way to say it. A lot of people on the team threatened to quit, it became a whole thing, gets reinstated as CEO. We learned a bunch about what happened in the midst of this. What jumped out to you, as somebody who covered this in real time particularly aggressively? Was there new stuff that really jumped out to you? All of these people text like children. Like, just pure children. And I'm not saying I'm like the world's best texter. It's just you read these texts between Mira and Sam, Mira Murati, the CTO who was also the CEO for 5 minutes of all of this. Right. And if you will recall, we broke a lot of news during the time on the Verge. Mhm. Like, Heath and I broke a lot of news throughout all of this, and we were texting and calling everyone all the time. Other great reporters broke a lot of news too, like everyone was talking. Yeah, shout-out to Ashley Vance, now at Bloomberg, who shows up in one of the messages. Yeah, he's like in the screenshots as a notification being like, "There's going to be a new CEO." Uh-huh. Mike Isaac is like, he's doing a victory lap because he was correct that Mira Murati tried to lead some of this charge to push Sam out, but everyone was like talking and everyone was reporting. And I just remember my texts were like very formal. Mhm. You know, I was like, "I'm going to text somebody and see if we can get some news out of this person." "Dear Sir." And it's like, yeah. Pretty much. And then like you're on the back end and you're seeing what they're all saying and they're like, "There's chatter, blah, blah, blah." Like, it's all lowercase. Yeah. No one knows what's going on. There's something about that, you know, it's like it's always interesting to see the other side of a thing you were even loosely a part of. Yeah, I do think the back-and-forths between Mira and Sam as all of this is going on are maybe the best thing that came out and have led to some really like delightful memes. There is, to your point about Mike Isaac, who wrote a story concurrently as all this was happening saying that Mira Murati was one of the people intimately involved with ousting Sam in the first place, got a lot of criticism for that piece, a lot of people who said that wasn't the case. Turns out it functionally was the case. And on the stand, she said over and over, essentially, like, "No, I didn't trust him. No, when he came back, I still didn't trust him." And that's like, that's under oath what she said. And meanwhile, as all of this is going on, she seems to be the person Sam is most consistently messaging trying to get information about what is going on. Because as we know, he basically like... a lot of this comes up in that New Yorker piece you're talking about, he just holed up with a couple of his confidants and lawyers and desperately tried to figure out what was going on. Yeah. Like, spent a weekend basically frantically calling and texting everyone, and Mira Murati is like a source of his in the room. And the one exchange that has come out that is now I think just the most useful meme we've had in a while, is Sam texts, "Can you indicate directionally good or bad? Satya and others anxious." Mira texts, "Directionally very bad." Which is such a brutal, incredible own. Well, no, it keeps going. And then he goes, "Okay, can you wrap up soon? Lots of pressure from Microsoft for an update." And then Mira replies, "Sam, this is very bad." She's like, "No, no, no, you don't understand." There's not an update, like you're fired. Yeah. The background here, and I will say this, again, criticism of one person in the context of this story has nothing like praise for the other people. Everyone screwed all of this up from top to bottom. The board never communicated why they had taken the step of removing Sam as CEO. They never put out a statement, they never said, "Here's all the things that went wrong, here's our evidence for doing this." They had asked Mira to collect evidence of Sam's management style and these challenges, and Mira had done it. She testified she didn't know why she was doing it. It's like, "No, you definitely..." We've all like, you put him on a PIP, like we all know what's going on here, right? "Yeah. 'Oh, you want me to prepare a dossier on my boss? That's probably fine.' Like..." "What did you think this was? You had concerns? You were supposed to document your concerns." And so when the board fired Sam, it was like a shot out of the dark, and no one knew why. And all they said was he was not consistently candid in his conversations. Right. This like totally opaque thing that could mean anything. And so he's texting her, he's legitimately like, "What happened?" And I think he thought there was some middle path. And there's this one set of texts here that is just... and again, they all text like children. Sam says to Mira, "Can you please tell them I just want to resolve this however and would like to join?" And Mira says, "They're convinced about their decision." And I just want everyone to pay attention to this response, which indicates everything about this process and how this company works. Sam says, in response to, "They're convinced about their decision," "For me to be fired? Or some other thing?" And Mira has to confirm, "Yes, for you to be gone." And Sam just says, "Okay." He's like, "So I'm still fired, right?" What decision have they made this time? You can tell no one was communicating successfully at any level of this entire process. No one was communicating. And the, again, the entire process here is they're communicating through Mira. So, both sides are communicating through Mira. Right. And I think at one point she becomes the CEO of OpenAI. Yeah. So, just a lot of back-and-forth confusion here. And we have to talk about Emmett Shear being the CEO for 5 minutes. So, Sam texts Mira, "Can I come in and talk about a path forward with them?" Mira says, "No, they need more time." Sam... "Once again." ... "More time for what?" Which is just a totally reasonable question. And then Mira says, "They walked me through all the reasons and the issues with you and why you can't be the CEO." Again, we still kind of don't know. Right. There's been a lot of reporting, but we still kind of don't know. And then Sam says, "Can you ask why they've been saying all weekend they want me back?" Mira says, "They want a new CEO in place." Sam oddly says, "Can you say he will call back in 10 minutes?" And then Mira says, "They want to have a new CEO in place tonight, not me." Sam says, "Do they know who? Can I tell Satya, is it final?" Mira says, "Trying to add Satya now." Sam says, "Please, still don't want me?" Sam says, "Still don't want me?" Somebody is texting Satya a Zoom link and Satya is going, "What?" "Nope, not happening." And then Mira finally ends all of this with, "New guy is a rando Twitch guy." Which is so good. That rando Twitch guy, by the way, Emmett Shear, who was the CEO of Twitch for a long time. Mhm. I think by most accounts like a good egg in Silicon Valley circles, had a weird moment in the sun as part of this entire story. Yeah, I mean, he's enjoying it. I remember again, during this reporting, when they announced it was Emmett Shear, there was some amount of like, "Oh, we know him." You know? Sure, yeah. Yeah, that's a guy that we were all in like the incubator with. But wait, the texts keep going here. So, Mira says, "New guy is a rando Twitch guy" and then she says, before Sam can even respond, she goes, "They don't want you." And then Sam responds, "Emmett??" with two question marks. And she says, "Yeah, but hold on, I'm pulling Satya in now." Like, it's just bananas. And then Mira is telling Sam that she is trying to bring him back, which may or may not be... like, it seems very clear she is engaging in different ways on both sides of this as this is all going down. It's just bizarre. And then they bring in Satya to try to get him to undo it, because he ends up being like the grownup in the room in a lot of ways throughout this entire thing. It's all just so insane, man. There's a moment where they are going to sell the IP to Anthropic, and Mira says, "They just don't want your hands on AGI," which, very funny, years later we are no closer to AGI with Sam's hands on it or not. By the way, they do finally get Satya Nadella. Sam goes, "Satya get on the call." Mira says, "Yes, with him." Sam says, "Wait, I have an interesting idea." Mira goes, "Still with Satya." Just totally sets him aside. You will recall this is where Nadella decided to react to all of this, which I fully believe he regarded as completely juvenile and nonsensical and very threatening to the valuations of a lot of companies, of a lot of things invested in OpenAI. Including his. He was like, "I'm just going to hire everybody." Yeah. "Microsoft will just hire... you can have whatever shell of OpenAI remains." This is his famous quote about, "We are above them, below them, around them." He's like, "I'll just hire everybody. Sam is going to work here now." And that was a bunch of leverage in the middle of this. I mean, this is all very funny because it is just evidence that these things happen in a much more casual way. Like, this isn't lawyers, this is just two people texting each other, obviously being pulled in five different directions, talking to all the same people who are also pulled in five different directions. I'm pretty sure, you know, Musk's attorneys brought this all up to indicate that Sam was unreliable, untrustworthy. At one point, he was fired from OpenAI. I have no idea if this is going to convince a jury that the structure of OpenAI constituted stealing a charity from Elon Musk, who was again and again presented with this exact scheme and didn't read the paperwork. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, it really seems like the easiest takeaway from all of this is that all of this is just a gigantic mess. And you could make a lot of arguments about how poorly run this company has been throughout this entire process. I will say there are two things that came up in the "why they fired Sam Altman" bits of testimony that I thought were really interesting. One was, like we were talking about, this sort of circular set of deals, that there was a sense that the board, Helen Toner, who was also a board member at the time, testified and talked about some of this, that the board felt like it wasn't being properly made aware of all of Sam and Greg's financial entanglements. They made this deal with a company called Helion, which was a nuclear energy company that both Sam and Greg were invested in. That's a weird thing for OpenAI to be spending its time and resources on, and they felt like they maybe weren't properly notified that one reason to make this deal is that it's going to help make Sam and Greg rich. Another one that came up that I thought was really interesting is, Shivon Zilis said this too, that OpenAI's board was really concerned that it hadn't been notified in advance of ChatGPT being released. Mhm. Which is totally sensible in retrospect, right? Like, the launch of ChatGPT legitimately changed the tech industry forever. Like, it really did. I don't think that's an overstatement at all. I am quite confident that no one at OpenAI, up to and including Sam Altman, knew that was going to happen on the day they shipped ChatGPT. Right, it was a little research preview that they barely announced. It was a research preview. They just put it out into the world in a very sort of academic way. They were like, "Look, we made a thing. Test it out, let us know what you think," and then it caught like wildfire. So, the idea that they should have known ahead of time to warn the board that this earth-shattering thing is coming strikes me as obvious in retrospect but sort of implausible at the time. "Well, I can academically argue the other side of this." "Okay." "I tend to agree with you. Google had something that looked very much like ChatGPT in its labs forever." "As Google will remind you, it invented all of this technology." "The T in ChatGPT is Transformers, which were invented at Google. There, now the Google executive hiding in my bushes to remind me of this..." "Sundar, you can go home now." "...say it all the time." "They had this technology, they had Transformers, they demoed it in a variety of ways, it never made any sense because they always did it in increasingly esoteric ways. We will never forget the I/O where Sundar was like, 'And now I'll have a conversation with Pluto, the planet, about what it's like to be a planet.' And no one knew what was going on." "And if instead he was just like, 'Look at this ChatGPT,' like everyone would have been like, 'Google has invented the future.' You can see that split here in all these texts and emails, because everyone is afraid of Demis, who is building the Transformers at Google." "Right." "Like, they have all seen the actual product and the public is like, 'Sundar's talking to Pluto again.'" "Oh, that's interesting." "Right? And they knew Google knew not to release this product to the public. Their own safety culture kept them away from releasing what was at the time like a GPT-1 or 2 class model to the public." "They're like, 'This is dangerous, there's all this stuff that can go haywire, blah, blah, blah, blah. We're going to do Pluto demos instead.' All of the principles were like, 'This is the future.' Literally the paper is called 'Attention Is All You Need,' right?" "Right." "And so, I can see, again, I'm making the argument academically, because I agree with you that no one knew what would happen. But I can see why a bunch of people immersed in the safety culture of that time were like, 'Everyone knew that you shouldn't do this.' OpenAI exists because Google wouldn't do this." "Mhm." "And you did it anyway without asking us if you should." "That's fair, and especially if you come from a safety-first perspective, which in theory OpenAI at the time did, you would say, 'Oh, this is a potentially problematic thing we're putting out into the world to see what happens when it's out in the world. You should know that it's coming.'" "Right. This is the thing that Google won't do." "Yeah, I suppose I buy that argument. I also still think it's true that they honest to God did not think anybody would care. Like, I really earnestly believe OpenAI shipped ChatGPT... if you think you're releasing a product that's going to change the world, do you call it ChatGPT?" "Like, no." "No, that's what I'm saying." "I agree with you on balance. I think I'm making this argument for the sake of making the argument. I understand, but there was a reason Google did not release this product. And then OpenAI did. Now, OpenAI was nowhere ready to be a company, they were not ready to commercialize it, they were still chasing AGI, like, all this stuff. And now they're a totally different company that, you know, in its heart of hearts wants to be a for-profit enterprise competing with Google." "Yes." "That company is positioned very differently than the OpenAI that was like, 'Look at our research preview.'" "Right." "But I understand why the board, which was 'Our job is to keep everything safe and to like pull the rip cord as we get closer to AGI...'" "Mhm." "...was like, 'Wait, this is the... this is the one thing the big player in the space wouldn't do.'" "Yeah. No, that's fair. I buy that." "Wait, can I... can I just add one more thing?" "Oh, please." "So, I keep forgetting this as well, but Liz is in the courtroom. You should read her live blog and her coverage. It's very funny." "She's so angry." "Everyone appears to be very tiresome." "Yeah." "Also, this is Liz at her finest. Liz being annoyed that she's watching billionaires sue each other is perfect. This is why the Verge exists. Hayden's doing great coverage on the exhibits. But, I keep forgetting myself that Microsoft is a party to this case because they have the big investment in OpenAI." "Mhm." "And Liz keeps mentioning that every single witness ends with Microsoft's lawyers standing up..." "...and saying something like, 'And Microsoft wasn't there?' And then following that up with, 'And Satya Nadella wasn't there either?' And she's like, 'This is literally the only thing they're adding to this trial.'" "So we didn't do that, right?" "They have nothing to do with this." "See you later." "Which is a very funny dynamic in this case that I can't get enough of." "That would be a really fun job as a lawyer. You just stand up and be like, 'But not me though, right?'" "And they're like, 'No, not you.' And you're like..." "See ya." "No further questions." "It's very good." "It's pretty good, yeah." "I really hope at some point we get some Satya texts." "He's too smart for that, man." "He is. Satya wouldn't even be caught on a Zoom call, it appears. So, kudos to Satya. All right, we should take a break and then we're going to come back. We got some gadgets to talk about. We'll be right back." "We'll be right back." Maybe I'm allowed to say that, maybe I'll just lie to you, like who knows. But they can't actually do all the things they're always saying to them, and people run into that really fast. And I think Apple, in particular with the iPhone, wanted to overpromise on the Siri part of it and say it could do everything. The models of the time couldn't do it. And only lately, with all the agentic stuff and the building of harnesses, there's just a method of doing it now that might work and might not work. But I don't think you can build a phone or a new device whole cloth out of LLM chatbots because it's still brittle. It's just brittle in a different way that doesn't immediately appear to be brittle. It confuses everyone into thinking that it actually is resilient to users doing random stuff. But it's actually even more broken, because it can't do the things that it will just confidently tell you it can do. Right. Yeah, it has such an incredibly different kind of structural problem, which is that it fails without telling you it failed, which is very different than the other kinds of failure. Yeah. Let me just play this for you. This is the Bella Ramsey Priority ad. "Siri, what's the name of the guy I had a meeting with a couple of months ago at Cafe Grenel?" "You met Zac Wingate at Cafe Grenel." "Hey! Zac! Oh, wow, I didn't think you'd remember me." "Yeah, of course. Since of course, like, Zac. Nobody works like Zac. Oh." Doesn't work. Fake feature. That was an ad with a celebrity that Apple ran for a feature that didn't and doesn't exist. Yeah. Like, we just shouldn't forget how wild that fact is. I mean, you want to talk about software brain. Yeah, congratulations to everybody on your $25. But this continues to be a bad look for Apple. This is what I mean by Apple did not successfully stand aside the AI capex problems that other companies have. Apple screwed up so badly, that it might actually have turned out okay. Yeah. I mean they sold more Macs than ever because people are running OpenCODA on them. Which Apple also didn't see coming and they have supply issues. So they just concocted one. They never shipped it, to the point where they're paying millions of dollars out, hundreds of millions of dollars out for making fake ads. Yeah. So, it's like fun to wear, it's fun to see that side of things. You know, V and I are constantly talking about the wellness tech complex. I get why Google is putting one of these out. Like, "Oh, you had one Athletic non-alcoholic beer? You're in a deficit." Like, "What? What's happening to me?" I agree with V in that most of these AI coaches are incredibly annoying, and they just like tell you how terrific you are all day, and it's like, "Just, what if you just left me alone? Like, what if you just left me alone, and it would be fine?" What I don't agree with is the Google Health of it all. Let me just read you a quote from... this is from Rishi Chandra, who has been at Google a very long time, has run a bunch of products, was like on the Chromecast team a million years ago. That was when I first met him, was when the Chromecasts were coming out. Yeah. So here's what he says. He says, "I know it will be hard for people," referring to the rebrand, "It was hard for us internally, but as we think about the future, where the health app needs to go, the health app is not going to be specific to Fitbit hardware. To say it's called Google Health, A, signs you up for a bunch of what I would call Google baggage, which is very real, including the design language of the app. Yeah, absolutely, which is, yeah, Google's software design continues to be just horrific. But the idea of Google Health signs you up for people understanding a different kind of data collection that Google is doing. People are going to start to think, "Oh, are you going to use this to show me ads in search results?" And they will promise up and down that the answer is no, and people won't believe them because the thing is called Google Health. I don't know, man. I get why they're doing it. I understand... again, this is every single morning, V and I talk about this, because I try not to Slack our reporters after hours. So, I schedule a lot of insane wellness TikToks to send to V in the morning, and then she's mad at me every morning. I was going to say, I don't know if that's better at 5:00 a.m. than... Everyone's gotten used to it from me. There's a handful of our reporters that get the craziest TikToks from me, like spot on the dot at 5:00, and I always forget to vary it to seem like I'm a real person. It's always at 5:00. But every morning, V and I talk about more crazy wellness stuff, and all of it is quantified self, AI health coaches, data collection, data collection, data collection. And so, of course, it's Google Health, because what they get with the brand is data collection. Sure. Everyone knows what's coming. Fitbit being like, "We're part of the Google family," is like... you get the weird conspiracy theory Facebook posts. Yep. You know, like, "I order Sundar Pichai to not share my Fitbit data with Google." It's like, you don't get that with Google Health. It's just Google. Yeah. That's a fair point. I'm sort of thinking of it next to Meta rebranding all of its apps. Do you remember when it was like WhatsApp by Facebook? Yeah. And everybody was like, "Hold on, Facebook owns WhatsApp?" Yep. And on the one hand, it was like, "Well, yeah, where you been, guys?" But on the other hand, it's like, don't tell them. What are you doing, Mark? People don't know. People like WhatsApp and hate Facebook. Don't shove the Facebook logo into the app. What are you doing? And did he ever take that advice? Yeah, that went great, for sure. He just launched a new—I almost called it a device, but it's not. It's like a weird little robot animal called a Familiar. Sure. I'm going to play the brief intro video here, and then I just... I just need your thoughts on what this is. So, I would describe this thing to you as a robot alien dog. Yeah. That is also a polar bear. Yeah, what if you made like a lamb look ominous? Yeah. They call this thing a Familiar. Again, ominous. Yeah. What do you make of this? They call it basically an embodied AI system. And Colin Angle's whole theory is that these things should be companions, right? This is not like a smart home controller that is also adorable. It is explicitly designed to be adorable and to connect with you and to be your best friend. I want to know what you make of this. I think there's a lot of desire for there to be robots. And the humanoid robots don't work, as we've all seen. And so, can we get a bunch of training data about navigating a house by making a cute fake dog that your kid can knock over and talk to? Boy, I bet we can. Yes. That's what I got for you. I would never let this thing in my house. Zero percent chance I would let this thing in my house. I'm with you. I mean, what's funny to me about this is the funniest lesson of Roomba from 20-plus years ago is that people will fall in love with anything. They put a round vacuum on your floor that bumped into the walls, and people fell in love with it and became best friends with it. So to then be like, "Oh, I have to make it look like a dog," actually feels like you're learning the wrong lesson. It's like, put robots in people's houses. Let them fall in love with them if they want to. This thing creeps me out. If I'm being completely honest with you, even if I were into the theory of this, which I'm not, the specifics of this sort of medium-sized animal just padding around my house... I've been watching a lot of Sonic the Hedgehog recently. My three-year-old is super into Sonic right now. This looks like a knock-off Sonic character, and I do not mean that as a compliment. Yeah, I see that. Again, sinister lamb. Sinister, yeah. Yeah, it's exactly right. Yeah, so I'm torn. I'm fascinated by this to see if it technically can do any of the things it is supposed to do. I just am not at all sold on the thesis behind any of this stuff. Especially because it doesn't actually talk, right? So it's LLM-based, but it meows and purrs. On one of the first Hype Desks we ever did, we talked about the Mario movie and how weird it was that Glen Powell was there to play Fox McCloud, the titular Star Fox character. And then out of nowhere, Nintendo—usually they do like surprise Direct announcements, but they give you a day's heads-up—this was 10 minutes. Literally, all my Slacks, all my messages, all my little work chats were like, "Everyone, drop what you're doing, watch YouTube. They're going to announce a new Star Fox out of nowhere." Yeah, and sure enough they did. And I think you already kind of alluded to it, David. Why break a great thing? It's a remake of Star Fox 64, which is itself a kind of definitive edition remake of the original Star Fox. So this is truly the fifth time they've made this game, but it looks amazing. It's got cinematic quality graphics, which is hit-or-miss depending on what you feel about Fox McCloud looking like an actual fox now. But it's the same game, it's the same gameplay, from what they're saying. It's a little bit of Nintendo Switch. Do you still do a barrel roll? You can still do a barrel roll. Hell yes, you can. That's the only important part of this game. Literally in the Direct, they made sure you knew you could do a barrel roll, you could do a somersault. Peppy Hare and Slippy Toad will still bother you to no end. In the middle of your missions, there's still all that cross-ship chatter, which is very nice. That's classic Star Fox. It's great. We're so excited for that. There's also—okay, wait, but we have to talk about the defining feature of this game, to me, in my opinion, which is... Oh my god, yes. If you use game chat to have your face, like if it's looking at your face, it basically makes like a VTuber version of Star Fox that tracks with your eyebrows and your mouth, and you become Star Fox. Wait, are you mocap-animating the fox, or does it make you a furry? No, no, no. You're mocap-animating Fox McCloud, and then you become Fox McCloud, which is to the delight of furries everywhere. Yeah, it's a virtual fursona onboarding move. It's a VTuber, a fursona VTuber kind of thing. But really, genuinely, the sound that emanated from me, the joy I felt... This is the thing I love about Nintendo. They are so deeply weird, and they just do not care. And they don't care about what the community thinks of these very strange... Like, Alarmo is another really good example of something where nobody was asking for it, and they made this cute little alarm clock, and they were just like, "It's just cool, and we want to do it." This is another example of them just being like, "Hey, we're just going to be Nintendo on our own terms, and this seems really funny and weird, and we're going to just roll with it." Tomodachi Life, also, is another great example of the weirdest thing that could possibly come out of Nintendo, and somehow it works. I've been looking at pictures of the new Star Fox for several minutes. I've been ignoring all of what you just said; I'm sure it was great. I can't decide if this is like... Do you remember when they first showed the new real Sonic, and everybody freaked out, and they had to redo Sonic? Yeah, yeah. This is like—there's a version of this that is that, because there is something slightly horrifying. But this is also the coolest thing I've ever seen in my whole life, because Star Fox is cool. He's got the not-quite-zipped-up jacket, which is so cool. Wait until you see James McCloud. He's got the aviators. Like, oh my god, it's just going to be really... I'm excited about this. It's like uncanny valley, parenthesis, complimentary, do you know what I mean? Yeah, I keep seeing the trend meme like, DLSS on, DLSS off. Right, like, it is just a little too realistic. And I think I'm going to get used to it. It took a while. They just redid Donkey Kong's eyes about a year ago for the movie, for the new Donkey Kong game. It was a big deal because they haven't changed that look in forever. I've gotten used to it; I have to believe it's going to be the same case here. Like, that's the vibe I get here. Oh, he dropped out on purpose. Here's the thing: he dropped out on purpose because he was too good for the Academy. You know what I mean? Like, that's the lore I choose to believe. Okay, so don't Google "Star Fox fursona." Nope, nope. Don't do that. We're finding our stride. This is... this is great news. What else do you have for us today? Gardening. We have gardening. Ashley, you want to talk about this? Just the concept of gardening? No, the man just really likes gardening, and it's like a really wholesome, really wholesome little show on Netflix. I don't understand. I love it, but also I did not peg Zach Galifianakis for a plant girlie. So is this one of those things where Netflix was just like, "Zach Galifianakis, we'd love you to make a show," and he's like, "Cool, I'm only going to do one where I just sit in my garden and talk about plants," and Ted Sarandos is like, "Okay"? Kind of. So, here's kind of the setup. It's directed by Brooke Linder, who also did John Mulaney's "Everybody's in L.A." or whatever, so it has a very similar kind of rough vibe. You think it's going to be Zach Galifianakis being weird, but it's earnest. Every episode is... First of all, every episode is 15 minutes long. Fifteen minutes, super easy watch. There's six of them. You can binge the whole thing in 90 minutes. Every episode has like three parts. One, he talks to actual farmers about the true history of a tomato or a mushroom or an apple; every episode is item-based. And then the other part of it is he just goofs off with kids, like "Kids Say the Darndest Things"-style, where he's just being a clown and having a good time doing what he does. It kind of feels, in a weird way, like a PG Adult Swim show. It feels like something you would see on Nickelodeon in the '90s at like 8:00 p.m. Is this because Netflix is doing vertical scrolling video in its mobile app now, and they're just like, "Make clips, Zach"? That seems like maybe a strategy. Yeah, I would say that would certainly lend itself to that. But it also genuinely feels like this is content for kids. If kids watch it, it's safe content for them, but also keeps them on Netflix. I find stuff like that really fascinating because it's just so odd. All right, Nilay, this means it's time once again for America's favorite podcast within a podcast. We got an email this week calling it the world's favorite podcast within a podcast. So we're going global, buddy. It's time for "Brendan Carr Is a Dummy." What did he do this week, Nilay? He did a lot this week. As always, he did a lot this week. The main thing is that he cheered on the Eighth Circuit overturning the FCC's broadband equity rules that passed during the Biden administration, where broadband companies had to try to provide internet access equitably to people of all kinds of backgrounds. They basically—the court overturned it, and Brendan cheered it and said, "We don't have to do that." Which is just the most Brendan Carr thing in the world. Let's not give people who need internet, internet. Hurray! Yeah, that sucks. You can talk about it lots of ways, but with Brendan, there's nothing he loves more than allowing telecom providers to charge higher rates and provide worse service. You know what I mean? Like, that's his thing; that's where he came from. Like, at his core... Well, and importantly, not do any work at all. It's like Brendan is really trying to protect the work-life balance of carrier executives everywhere. Yeah. Before the FCC under Brendan became all about speech regulations, every single FCC chair—and I knew them all—was all about bridging the digital divide, closing the homework gap, making sure that rural communities had broadband. And Brendan is like, "Screw that." Like, "We don't need to overbuild"—this is one of his favorite phrases—and then he's cheering on these court challenges to forcing what are essentially monopolies to provide coverage equally to people. So, that's just very bad. He's very dumb. This is like... I'm saying it this way without a lot of fire because this is just the most Brendan Carr thing. He's like a cartoon character villain in that sense. Yeah. But like, this is also the thing I'm used to arguing with GOP FCC commissioners. Like, Ajit Pai, we would argue about this sort of thing. At least he's true to form. You know, it's like classic Brendan Carr. Yeah. Then the other thing this week, that I feel the need to issue a disclosure on... Oh? A few weeks ago, we mentioned that Brendan had bullied himself into a discussion of whether the NFL should lose its antitrust exemption. Oh, right. So, big sports leagues have an antitrust exemption because the owners all get together and they collude, basically, on prices for broadcast rights. In any normal market, you would have them all compete. We call those things cartels; they're not generally allowed. But big sports leagues tend to have these antitrust exemptions. The NFL has maybe the most important one, so the owners can come together and the league can go negotiate for broadcast rights. There are lots of structures, and you don't necessarily need that one, but that's the one we have. So because the owners have now started going to streaming and getting deals, and in particular the NFL is so expensive to watch legally, there's been like just a lot of fulmination about breaking the antitrust exemption, and making sure that it's not all on streaming, or if it's going to be on streaming, that the local stations can do whatever they want. So, the DOJ's investigating this. Brendan can't help it because he loves muscling his way into any sort of TV dispute. He decided that he's got the power to, as well. There are going to be Senate hearings about it. Everyone's talking about it, and it is true that fans generally do not like the way that sports broadcast rights are set up right now. Agree. It is expensive, it's complicated. A lot of people are sailing the seven seas, if you know what I mean. Yep. So, this is my disclosure: the Green Bay Packers this week sent a letter to Congress. It's basically a letter that's like, "Hey, just like, we're a small-market team, we're owned by the community. If you upend the economic structure of the NFL, we will be disproportionately impacted." The last sentence says it as clearly as you can: "Put simply, any disruption to the current Sports Broadcasting Act model, eliminating our ability to negotiate as the NFL writ large, would pose an existential threat to the Green Bay Packers and their existence in Green Bay as we know it." This comes the same week as Brendan saying out loud, "Actually, this inquiry might not lead to any action." Because there's a lot of pressure. The NFL is full of lobbyists and billionaires, and they're like, "No, no, no, you're not going to touch us." It is as powerful an organization as we have in America. Yeah, this is a cultural institution. They're like, "You know what? Donald Trump is president? Here's Bad Bunny." They just have the authority to do whatever they want in a way that many other places don't have. So, I'm sure they're lobbying. Brendan said this week, "We're just trying to make sure that we're educated on these issues. It's not clear whether there'll be a regulatory outcome or not." So, you just have the NFL speaking with a low voice being like, "Screw you." As corrupt as it gets in the way that the NFL is corrupt, and then you have the Packers being like, "This might kill the Packers." I am dying to know what your disclosure is here. Is this like Nilay Patel is secretly the backup quarterback for the Green Bay Packers? I don't think anyone would enjoy that. I was raised in Wisconsin by my father, a Brett Favre fan, and my family owns four shares of the Packers. No way. You are the owner of the Green Bay Packers. You know, every now and then, the sports media will run these trolls like, "Packers owner says they're going to trade for Aaron Rodgers." It's always like one guy. Yeah, so the Packers are owned by the community. When they want to raise money to build stadiums, they issue shares which give you nothing, and you buy them, and then they hang them on the wall. And you can get some of the worst merch in history at like a 20% discount from the Pro Shop. You can vote, but it doesn't matter. You can vote on the board of directors of the Packers, which I never do. So, there is your disclosure: my dad and I each own two shares of the Green Bay Packers. I'm now going to start angling for a change to the Verge ethics policy that says you're not allowed to also own any sports teams. If you want to come for me for that one, I will take it on the chin. All right, my first lightning round item: I would just like to alert everyone to the existence of a company called Dreame. If you don't know about Dreame, Jennifer Pattison Tuohy wrote a terrific piece for us about this company. Basically, it's a company out of China that started as a robot vacuum company on kind of a Dyson grind, where they're like, "We know how to make a kind of motor. What can we put motors in?" They started doing stuff, built robot vacuums, built a pretty successful robot vacuum business, and then decided that the next thing to do is to make everything for everyone everywhere throughout the world as we know it. Last week, they had a big, huge U.S. launch event, and have basically begun rolling out some of the most spectacular vaporware the world has ever seen. And I'm like earnestly proud of this company for, with a straight face, saying all of the things that it has been saying over the last week or so. I'm sure one of the things they said was, "We will turn our CEO into Elon Musk." Yeah, just turn him into Elon Musk, just a thing they're saying out loud. They announced, I think, like two dozen smartphones in the Aurora line, a bunch of which have weird ideas about things, including one that is a modular phone where you can attach different modules, including a telephoto camera lens. There's also a satellite communications module. Just, I want to be so clear that none of these things are ever going to exist, and if they do, you won't buy them anyway. But I love so much that a company can get on stage and be like, "Look at all this weird crap we made." They also made a car, I think. No, no, no, it's not just a car, David. It's a rocket car. Oh, right, it is a rocket car. Yeah, it can do 0 to 60 in, they claim, 0.9 seconds. So it literally would have to be a rocket car. It's a rocket car. I don't understand. I mean, our deck is so straightforward. Andy Hawkins is our transportation reporter, and the deck of this piece is: "The Chinese vacuum company has automotive aspirations, but its claims of rocket-boosted acceleration don't add up." It's like, "Yeah, I don't think they add up, dude!" The math ain't mathing, my friends. Yeah. Yeah, they also launched smart rings, smart glasses, and a laundry robot. I would say there is no more classic vaporware genre than a robot that will fold your laundry. Somebody has been shipping that since time immemorial, and I believe it will never actually exist in the real world. But Dreame's making one. They've got a bunch of robot vacuums and lawnmowers—all kinds of wild, every product you could possibly imagine, Dreame believes that it will sell you at some point in the very near future. And it's all a bunch of nonsense, and I just admire the hell out of it. Can I come back to the rocket car? Sure. It's very important that I come back to Dreame announcing a rocket car. It's called the Nex 01 Jet Edition car. It has Batmobile-style fake jet engines on the back. And, again, the claim is it can go from 0 to 60 in 0.9 seconds. You can't do that on regular tires, as our transportation reporter Andy Hawkins points out. You will hit the limits of traction, so you can put all the power to the wheels, but the tires will explode. So they're going to launch this thing briefly into the air as it accelerates. It's like you might have made a top fuel drag car, but you actually just made this Kia-looking thing with jet engines on the back. Fantastic. Unless—unless the reason that it has jet engines is because they're not driving the wheels. This is what I'm saying: they're going to launch the car into the air. The jet engines can just push the car forward. Yeah, the wheels don't have to turn at all. The wheels are just ancillary. And when you talk about the most spectacular vaporware in the history of the world... it honestly has a real claim to it. There's not a lot of better vaporware. Everybody go check it out. We have a great photo essay of all the stuff. Go read Jen's piece; we'll put it all in the show notes. This company is absolutely fascinating. Whether it ships or not, I think Dreame is fascinating and you should go check it out. Nilay, what's your next one? I just want to point out that the Autopian sent an engineer to check out the Nebula Nex 01 jet car, and his assessment was, quote, "feels like horseshit." All right. I like the idea that this person was not an automotive engineer; they're just like an HVAC engineer, where they were just like, "Nope!" Yeah, and wind doesn't work that way. It's really good. It's very good. What's your next one, Nilay? I got one more. It's just one of my favorite stories of the week. We started with Elon, we're going to end with Elon. Elon, out of the blue, just announced that he is further consolidating the SpaceX empire. And xAI, which was its own company, which also contained X—the social platform and everything app that we all use for everything... Right. They started X, then started xAI. xAI swallowed X. Yep. And it all became xAI. It all became xAI, and then SpaceX swallowed xAI. Right. And now, he's formally dissolving xAI as a company. Okay. And it's just going to be part of SpaceX, and they're just going to call it SpaceX AI. Well, so wait, the whole thing is now SpaceX AI? Yes. They did not quite announce it. They put out a press release announcing a compute partnership with Anthropic, in which SpaceX referred to itself as SpaceX AI over and over again in the press release. So, that's what we got. The whole Anthropic thing, by the way, is very weird. They're getting all of this compute from, I guess it's called SpaceX AI, but there's like a clause in it that if Elon Musk decides it's bad for the world, they can pull the compute back. Weird stuff going on. Weird stuff going on. It's also not clear if SpaceX itself is called SpaceX AI, or if just the AI products of SpaceX are under an umbrella brand. None of this makes any sense. X is a feature of xAI, which is a feature of SpaceX AI, which is a branch of SpaceX. Yes. And by the way, X, just to be clear, is the everything app that we all do all of our banking and real estate transactions in. Right. Pretty soon, you might bank with a division within a division within a division within a division of SpaceX. Yes, which is also selling compute to Anthropic. I love it. When you're like, "Oh, this whole episode started with a long trial about corporate structures," and you end with "and it's called SpaceX AI," it's like, well, we've learned nothing. I have no notes. I'm in. I will give you, just to button that, a better example of a company doing a good job. Asha Sharma, who is running the Xbox team now, continues to do good, smart things, which is to say, make obvious decisions to make Xboxes less bad. She announced this week that they're getting rid of the Copilot AI stuff on the Xbox. They're making some big changes on the platform. They started teasing the universal look across all kinds of different devices. Just before we get out of here, just a brief shout-out to Asha Sharma, who has basically shown up and just systematically reversed all of the bad decisions made by people before her, which is actually a pretty good way to endear yourself to fans of Xbox. And it seems to be going very well. There was a lot of skepticism about Asha Sharma when she took over this team, and people are coming around really fast. There's a lot of enthusiasm for what she's been up to recently. I mean, you're just like, "I'm taking the AI out of it," and everyone's like, "You're the best." Yeah. And meanwhile, Microsoft is out here being like, "Oh, do you know all those MSN widgets that show up in your face every time you open the Start menu? We're going to get rid of those too." And everybody's like, "Oh my god, Microsoft gets it! What if we made our products less horrible?" It's like, this is the fate of Windows. I'll take it. All right, we should get out of here. Lots going on on the site. Go read everything. Liz, literally as we speak, is still in the courtroom. There are still shenanigans. This trial has weeks left. We've got a lot left to do, and we'll keep covering it. But we will not be covering it next week because we are off next week. No show Tuesday, no show Friday. We're going to be back with a live show on the 19th after Google I/O. Nilay, you're going to be at I/O. I will be at I/O. TBD on connectivity situations, whether we get you on the show or not, but we have some other folks here that are going to help me run through all the news. We're going to talk about all of it. We're going to go live kind of right-ish after the keynote on Tuesday. So, no Tuesday episode that morning, but we'll be back that afternoon. We're just trying to give everybody a break. There's a lot of stuff coming up. We have some really fun plans that we're going to start to tell you about pretty soon. Lots going on. We just figured we're going to give everybody a week to go stare at trees before the true chaos of developer season gets us all. I'm going to see if I can get my car to go faster than any car has ever gone in the history of the world. You're going to put several rockets on your car and see if the tires even matter anymore. Fake rockets. It's going to be great. I love it. Who's on Decoder this week? Joanna's on very soon talking about new things and her new book, and then I will just hint that there is a wave of big interviews coming that I'm very excited about. It's going to be good. Remember, as always, you can subscribe to The Verge to get this podcast and all of our podcasts ad-free. Theverge.com/subscribe—that is what makes us ungovernable. They can't mess with us as long as you subscribe. The Vergecast is a production of The Verge and the Vox Media Podcast Network. Today's episode is produced by Erik Gomez, Brandon Keefer, and Travis Larchuk. We'll see you in, what, two weeks, Nilay. Rock and roll.

ECB President Christine Lagarde: Stablecoins within a Global Context

Banco de España(24.6K)·36:17·186 views·2026-05-08
Christine LagardePresident, European Central Bank
TL;DRECB President Christine Lagarde argues that Europe should not rush to promote euro-denominated stablecoins as a direct competitor to the US dollar; instead, the focus must be on building public infrastructure that places central bank money at the…
heart of digital...

TL;DR

ECB President Christine Lagarde argues that Europe should not rush to promote euro-denominated stablecoins as a direct competitor to the US dollar; instead, the focus must be on building public infrastructure that places central bank money at the heart of digital settlement to preserve monetary sovereignty and financial stability.

The Global Context and Functional Duality

Lagarde identifies that stablecoins have evolved from a niche crypto tool to a $300 billion market dominated by US dollar-denominated issuers (Tether and Circle). To evaluate their future, she separates their utility into two distinct functions:

  • Monetary Function: The use of stablecoins to extend the reach of reserve currencies cross-border, bypassing traditional correspondent banking and offering a "digital greenback" to savers in countries with weak local currencies.
  • Technological Function: The use of stablecoins as the "native cash" for distributed ledger technology (DLT) and tokenized assets, enabling "atomic settlement" (the simultaneous, instant exchange of assets).
  • Regulatory Divergence:
      • Europe (MiCA): Focused on consumer protection and financial integrity.
      • United States (Genius Act): Explicitly designed to ensure the global dominance of the US dollar and cement demand for US Treasuries.

Monetary Risks and Policy Trade-offs

While euro-denominated stablecoins might seem like a tool to increase the international role of the euro, Lagarde argues the trade-offs are too steep:

  • Financial Stability Concerns: Stablecoins are private liabilities. Lagarde cites the 2023 Silicon Valley Bank collapse, where the USDC stablecoin broke its "peg" (dropping to $0.87), demonstrating that private redemption promises can vanish during market stress.
  • Monetary Policy Transmission: In the bank-centric Eurozone, a mass migration of retail deposits into non-bank stablecoins would weaken the banking system's ability to transmit interest rate changes to firms and households.
  • Sovereignty Issues: Multi-issuer schemes (where a stablecoin is issued by both EU and non-EU entities) create regulatory blind spots, as MiCA protections only reach the EU-based issuer.

The Technological Shift: Tokenization and Infrastructure

Lagarde acknowledges that DLT is "genuinely transformative" for financial markets, particularly in Europe where infrastructure is highly fragmented across 295 trading venues and 32 securities depositories.

  • The Fragility of Private Settlement: Relying on private stablecoins for settlement creates "fragmentation" across competing instruments and lacks the "unconditional finality" provided only by central bank money.
  • The Need for a Common Anchor: To avoid "digital dollarization" of European markets, the public sector must provide a digital settlement asset that ensures the "singleness of money"—the principle that a euro has the same value regardless of its form.

The Strategic Roadmap: Infrastructure Over Instruments

Rather than promoting private stablecoins, the ECB is prioritizing the integration of central bank money into the digital ecosystem:

  • Project APYAPONTES: A wholesale settlement initiative that links DLT platforms to the Eurosystem’s TARGET settlement system.
  • Interoperability Goal: The ECB aims for a fully interoperable European tokenized financial ecosystem by 2028, allowing tokenized commercial bank deposits and central bank money to coexist.
  • Fundamental Foundations: Lagarde concludes that the international role of the euro is best served by deeper capital markets (Savings and Investment Union) and a unified safe asset base, rather than new digital instruments.

Notable Quotes

"The US administration explicitly describes [the Genius Act] as a tool to 'ensure the continued global dominance of the US dollar' and 'to cement demand for US treasuries.' Now the terms of the debate have shifted... It is no longer whether stablecoins should exist, but whether jurisdictions can afford not to have them." — Christine Lagarde
"Ignoranti quem portum petat, nullus suus ventus est. It was Seneca, which translates into: 'To one who does not know to which port one is sailing, no wind is favorable.' ... Once we disentangle these two functions—monetary and technological—the case for promoting euro-denominated stablecoins looks far weaker than it appears." — Christine Lagarde
"Stablecoins are private liabilities... The promise of par redemption depends on the very market confidence that can vanish when financial stability deteriorates, and a mass redemption can accelerate that deterioration." — Christine Lagarde
"We the key question is when stablecoins offer a better solution or with less friction because of some technological advantage or when it's the case that just we see less friction because it is a way to go around the regulatory framework and it's offering some kind of opacity." — Gabriel Galípolo (Governor of the Central Bank of Brazil)
Good morning, ladies and gentlemen. I'll be very brief. I just need to start by thanking my dear friend and partner from the very first minute, Governor José Luis Escrivá. I cannot overstate his work in bringing central banks in Europe and Latin America closer together. So thank you very much, José Luis Escrivá, and in his name, I want to thank all the staff of Banco de España for this great event with this great view. It's very relevant for us in Latin America to be so close and closer to Europe. And also, it's a huge pleasure for me to be here, especially having the chance to share the stage with Christine Lagarde. Some of you may know I'm relatively new to this job. Lagarde is a reference to all of us. But for us that are relatively new to this job, Lagarde is a role model. We are always looking up to her for words of wisdom, especially when we are navigating through troubled waters like the times that we are passing now. And one of the things that I learned during these last 17 months at this job is that something that keeps this community together is that all central banks have the same state of mind and mood. We are constantly concerned. And it's a fact that the news recently has helped us to keep firmly in that state of mind. But before, long before the geopolitical tensions monopolized the debate, the risks and opportunities of stablecoins were on the top of every central bank's mind. I say risks and opportunities because, of course, every one of us in the financial market—supervisors and regulators—are always seeking to boost and welcome technological innovation. Recently, Lagarde said that she has some difficulty seeing the business case for stablecoins. The way I understood that is the key question is: when do stablecoins offer a better solution or one with less friction because of some technological advantage, or when is it the case that we just see less friction because it is a way to go around the regulatory framework and it's offering some kind of opacity to that kind of operation? And if that is the case, we will need something that Madame Lagarde also has a lot of experience with: synchronizing our movements in these troubled waters that we see today. So without further ado, I pass the floor to Madame Lagarde. Thank you very much. Thank you so much, Gabriel, for this lovely introduction and my gratitude to you, José Luis, and to the entire team for having organized this event and taking us to this extraordinary location of yours. So thank you very much. Colleagues, friends, first of all, it's a great privilege to be in your company and to be in this particular location. I have to tell you that José Luis, from the get-go and day one when he joined the Governing Council of the ECB, mentioned to me the fact that we had to reach out to Latin America. He very quickly took his first trip abroad as governor of the Bank of Spain, came back, and said we have to be together. So tribute to you; it's not just a recent development, it's part of your thinking and I really pay tribute to that. I want to pay a second tribute today which might surprise many of you. But you know, you should move over here because you can't see us. Come, you have the... you know it's like the opera seats where you pay half price because you can't see anything. Move over, come, seriously. No, come. You have a screen? No. Come on, move. So I want to pay a second tribute. Many of you who know me have witnessed that ever since I was finance minister for France, then managing director of the IMF, and now president of the ECB, I have carried with me a concern about what we do with our planet and how climate change can impact us and how it is relevant for monetary policy purposes. For those of you in doubt, please read the latest speech by Philip Lane which was delivered a couple of days ago and he really includes all the research that we have done in relation to that. Now, why do I say that today in front of you when I'm going to bore you with stablecoins? Because today is the hundredth anniversary of Sir David Attenborough. And Sir David, who is turning a hundred today, celebrated tonight with a big concert at the Albert Hall in London which will be broadcast on the BBC, has actually done a lot for many scientists around the world. And I hope for some of us in charge of monetary policy to appreciate that wherever we are, whatever we do, whatever the mandate, we have to think about that as well. Because if we don't, you know, our children and grandchildren will not be around to discuss monetary policy and stablecoins, which is the point that I'm coming to. So let me introduce what I want to do with this speech, which will be, I can assure you, a little technical, so those of you who did not get much sleep, please have coffee. Because what I try to do is to debunk a little bit the different functions of stablecoins and I will try, given that I'm president of the European Central Bank, I will try to explain why I'm not so sure that it's indispensable to go in the direction that is predicated by some—in other words, compete with the existing stablecoins that are available around. This is my enterprise of debunking and explaining what functions it has and what technology it is born from in a way. So, few developments in recent years have moved as quickly from the periphery to the center of the policy debate as stablecoins. Stablecoins have grown from less than ten billion dollars six years ago to more than three hundred billion dollars now. They are overwhelmingly denominated in US dollars and nearly ninety percent of the market is controlled by two issuers only: Tether and Circle, based respectively in El Salvador and the United States. As their adoption has expanded and their links to the real financial system are deepening, the risks they pose have come into focus, especially as regards financial stability. And these concerns have been particularly acute in parts of Latin America and parts of Africa. But they are now firmly part of the debate in many advanced economies, and this is certainly the case in Europe. We recognized that early on, not only in respect of stablecoins, but MiCA, which is the abbreviated name for the regulation applicable to crypto assets, has recognized it and we did so back in 2024 ahead of developments that have taken place since elsewhere in the world. And the aim of that regulation was to contain the risks and safeguard the integrity of the financial system. We might have been pioneers in that respect, but the United States followed through, but the approach that they have taken is much broader. The Genius Act is not just a consumer protection and financial stability measure. If you pay attention to the reading of this Genius Act, the US administration explicitly describes it as a tool to, and I quote, "ensure the continued global dominance of the US dollar," end quote, "and to cement demand for US treasuries." Now the terms of the debate have shifted with it. It is no longer whether stablecoins should exist, but whether jurisdictions can afford not to have them. The growing argument as we see it is that Europe must respond by promoting euro-denominated stablecoins of its own. Otherwise, Europe faces a future of digital dollarization and a loss of monetary sovereignty. By the way, that question can translate to many other jurisdictions. I'm looking at it from my window. But I think that what this debate has not asked clearly enough, and this is a question that our good friend Agustin asked once, including to the banks themselves, is: what precisely are stablecoins for? The benefits attributed to them rest on two distinct functions: a monetary function and a technological function. And they are systematically conflated with each other in the current debate. And in my view, to navigate properly, we need to separate those two functions. There was a thinker who was born in this very peninsula who wrote, and I quote in Latin for those of you who of course all speak Latin: "Ignoranti quem portum petat, nullus suus ventus est." It was Seneca, which translates into: "To one who does not know to which port one is sailing, no wind is favorable." So the argument I want to develop today is that once we disentangle these two functions, monetary and technological, the case for promoting euro-denominated stablecoins looks far weaker than it appears. And a more fundamental question comes: do we actually need stablecoins to obtain the benefits that they are alleged to offer? Or are we mistaking the instrument for the outcome? The tree from the forest. When what matters is the architecture underpinning which other instruments can safely emerge. So let me address this issue of one instrument, two functions. Stablecoins were initially designed to solve a narrow problem within the crypto ecosystem, which is price volatility. To make a specific type of crypto assets usable for settlement, the creators of stablecoins anchored them mainly to fiat money, the very system that they had originally sought to bypass by the way. Backing each token, each stablecoin, one-for-one with cash or short-dated government debt. This is the promise. That design choice made stablecoins the internal settlement currency of decentralized finance and the primary bridge between crypto and currencies. And that function and that purpose still accounts for the overwhelming majority of their transaction volume. But as stablecoins move beyond the crypto ecosystem, two distinct functions emerge. The first one, the monetary function. Stablecoins are increasingly seen as a way to extend the global reach of reserve currencies by easing two long-standing constraints of: number one, how those currencies circulate, cross-border in particular; and second, who can hold the assets behind them. Let me tackle the first one: access to cross-border payment infrastructure. Historically, many decades and centuries ago, that access ran through merchant houses; that was in the Renaissance days. Nowadays, it was via the correspondent banking network. Stablecoins allow monetary value to move outside traditional banking channels with fewer intermediaries, exit some of the correspondent banks as a result, allegedly lower costs, possibly, extending the currency's reach into areas where access was not available or had declined. This is most obvious when transactions remain inside the crypto ecosystem, as is increasingly the case in cross-border business-to-business payments, which account already for 60% of stablecoin payment volume. Now that sounds big. But a share of that still only represents 0.01% of global B2B flows. Converting in and out of stablecoins, however, despite promises, sometimes carries costs that can erode those gains. So let me look at the second constraint. It's the ease of holding the currency outside of its home jurisdictions, essentially holding dollars outside the United States. Households abroad in many corners of the world have long held dollars in physical form, the greenback which was available somewhere in a drawer. And institutions have done the same thing through capital markets. But each route carries frictions. With cash being cumbersome, dangerous, and earning no yields, and capital market access remaining the preserve of institutional investors. Stablecoins reduce these frictions as digital access is faster and easier than hard cash. And it reaches savers in countries where weak currencies can erode savings. In economies where access to stable currency has historically been constrained, transaction flows already reach around 7.7% of GDP in Latin America as a whole, and 6.7% in Africa and the Middle East. Now these implications would deepen if stablecoins were remunerated. They are not at the present time. But there are multiple funny tricky ways to circumvent that prohibition. As dollar-pegged stablecoins hold treasury bills as reserves, a yield-bearing stablecoin, if it was allowed, would make its holder indirectly a holder of US government debt. It would be held not only as a store of value, but as an investment asset. And individual portfolio choices aggregate into systemic effects. Our research finds that a 3.5 billion inflow into dollar-backed stablecoins lowers three-month treasury bill yields by around 2.5 to 3.5 basis points under normal conditions, with the effect more than doubling in periods of treasury bill shortages. That's for the monetary function. Let's now look at the technological function. And that's about how transactions are executed and settled within emerging financial infrastructures. Digital innovation in recent years has made it possible for financial assets to migrate onto distributed ledger technology, allowing real-world assets to be tokenized, represented as digital tokens on programmable blockchains. This fancy world of tokenization is nothing more than that: a representation through digital ways of assets, deposits, securities, whatever you can think of, but in digital form. As trading and ownership move onto these platforms, a new requirement emerges. Transactions need a settlement asset that can operate natively within the same environment, that digital environment that I have just described with tokenized assets. Stablecoins fill that role. They have become the default cash leg for so-called atomic settlements. That's another buzzword, which is nothing more than the simultaneous exchange of two assets within a single transaction, where either both sides settle instantly or neither does. This removes settlement risk by design. That function depends on having a stable unit of value on chain. And most crypto assets are too volatile to serve this purpose. By the smart deviation in a way from the initial purpose of that community, the smart anchoring of their value to fiat currencies—the one that you're in charge of, that I'm in charge of—and backing it with liquid reserves, stablecoins are for now the only instrument to perform this role reliably at scale. In effect, they act as the system's native cash. This of course is in stark contrast with existing financial infrastructures as we know them. Today's securities transactions often settle over several days, require reconciliation across fragmented ledgers, and tie up collateral longer than necessary. Distributed ledgers compress this process that I have just described into a single environment, where issuance, trading, settlement and custody can occur continuously. Activities that once required manual operations and coordination—remember the coupon payments, margin calls, collateral movements—can instead be automated. Adoption is already accelerating.
