Tokenized money market funds whose shares are issued as tokens on DLT, deployable as collateral in derivatives and repo markets, have roughly doubled in market capitalization within a year. Not a huge amount—we're talking about 7 billion dollars—but the expansion is rapid. So taken together, these developments reveal a technology that is doing two distinct things at once: reshaping monetary demand and transforming settlement infrastructure. This occurs in ways that blur the boundary between them. And that blurring is precisely what makes the current policy debate so difficult to navigate. Remember Seneca. And that's where Europe might go wrong. So do we need stablecoins? With close to 98% of stablecoins denominated in US dollars, and with the US now moving to entrench that position through legislation, as I said earlier on, the growing argument is we have to move forward, we have to go for these European stablecoins as well to remain competitive. But that framing rests on the confusion I have just described. It treats one instrument as if it performed one function, and it performs two functions. The Tinbergen Principle has to apply at some stage. Because when we examine each function separately, the case for adoption looks a lot less compelling. So let me take each of those two, the monetary function and the technological function in turn. The monetary function first. Euro-denominated stablecoins operating within the framework already established by our regulation, MiCA—that's how the argument goes, not me arguing for it—could generate additional global demand for euro area safe assets. And if that demand grew, driven by buyers outside the euro area, with reserves channeled into safe assets, sovereign yields would compress, financing conditions would ease, and the international reach of the euro would be extended through a new digital channel. In the short term, it looks like a great tailwind, doesn't it? But I think it needs to be assessed against the trade-offs it creates. And I can mention two of them. The first trade-off is financial stability. Stablecoins are private liabilities. It's the promise that I'm issuing a stablecoin to you and I'm keeping in reserve the exact equivalent value. And I'm accountable to you. It's a liability whose stability depends on the credibility and liquidity of their backing. When confidence holds, they function as intended. But when it weakens, the demand for redemption can become sudden and self-reinforcing. This is not hypothetical. Let me take you back to 2023. When Silicon Valley Bank collapsed, a couple of others as well, but SVB was the key one. Circle, one of the two stablecoin issuers, disclosed at that time that 3.3 billion of USDC—that's the Circle stablecoin—so 3.3 billion of reserves were actually held at SVB. Now for a period of time, not too long, USDC, the Circle stablecoin, traded at 0.87. So almost, well actually, more than what was promised. What was promised was one. One stablecoin, one dollar. It went down to 0.87. At scale, such dynamics can transmit stress to the underlying asset markets. The promise of par redemption depends on the very market confidence that can vanish when financial stability deteriorates, and a mass redemption can accelerate that deterioration. As stablecoins' use grows, so too does the potential for feedback loops between redemption and asset markets, particularly when issuers are non-banks. We know how the story ended up, better than it could have been. Thank goodness, the American resolution systems operated well, the Fed and the Treasury worked overnight and the matter was resolved. But had it not been the case, we could have expected something very different. Still on financial stability: the multi-issuer schemes add a further layer of vulnerability where the same stablecoin is issued jointly by a European and a non-European entity. MiCA, our European regulation, safeguards and reaches only the EU issuer. In case of a run, investors will naturally seek to redeem where protections are the strongest, which is likely to be the European issuer where MiCA also prohibits redemption fees. That is not the case in the US. In addition to that, the reserves held in the European Union may not be sufficient to meet concentrated demand. We know the dangers, and I don't think that we need to wait for a crisis to prevent them. I'd like to salute Hélène Rey who is with us and who has written very precisely and acutely on this particular matter. The second trade-off concerns monetary policy transmission. So the first one was financial stability, I'm now going to talk about monetary policy transmission. The ECB's ability to deliver price stability depends on interest rate decisions reaching firms and households through the banking system. When retail deposits migrate into non-bank stablecoins and return to banks as wholesale funding, that transmission channel narrows. Banks lend less, or less efficiently, and the pass-through from policy rates to the real economy weakens as a result. Our research finds that in the euro area, where banks remain the dominant source of credit to the real economy, large-scale deposit substitution would weaken lending to firms and weaken the transmission of monetary policy. It's different in the United States. Firms have broad access to capital markets, and this effect of reduced monetary policy transmission will be less and will matter less. A contraction in bank lending could potentially be more easily absorbed. Now if you take those trade-offs together, they're significant. And in my view, they outweigh the short-term gains in financing conditions and international reach that euro-denominated stablecoins might provide. I think that if we want to strengthen the international role of the euro, stablecoins are not the most efficient way to do so. The best way to do so, to increase the position of the euro as an international currency, is simple. Simple to discuss, simple to put into speeches. Difficult to implement. More integrated capital markets through the saving and investment unions, which is currently in elaboration and will come to the floor of Parliament, I hope in short order. And over time, a safe asset base that matches the scale of our ambitions for the euro's international role. This is also a matter that is highly debated within the European Council which brings together all the leaders of Europe. Some of them are very supportive, others are resisting. I think the movement between the two and the equilibrium is shifting a bit. Let me now look at the technology function. That technology, which is not by the way a latest development—it's been around for at least ten years—is expanding its role and it is transformative, genuinely transformative. DLT makes it possible to build shared cross-jurisdictional financial market infrastructure from the ground up. Issuance, trading, settlement on a single platform, accessible across borders without relying on a maze of legacy intermediaries and burdened by time zones. For Europe, that opportunity is especially compelling. Our financial market infrastructure remains one of the most fragmented in the world. Bear with me. In 2023, there were 295 trading venues in Europe, 14 central clearing counterparties, and 32 central securities depositories across the EU. Compared with two clearing houses and a single CSD in the United States. So DLT offers a real path towards integration. For that reason, the prospect of rapid dollar stablecoin uptake in European tokenized markets is a very legitimate concern. One that risks entrenching dollar dependency. We have a few of those dependencies at the moment, and we are struggling with them. Maybe some of you do as well. But this dollar dependency could actually operate at the level of settlement infrastructure. But the answer does not lie in rejecting technology or discouraging stablecoins altogether or stifling innovation. Not at all. Instead, what we must do is build the public infrastructure that will enable alternative instruments, such as stablecoins and other forms of tokenized money, to operate within a framework that is anchored by central bank money. The stablecoin model on its own has two structural weaknesses as a settlement foundation. First one is fragility, the second one is fragmentation. On fragility, a key benefit of tokenized financial market is atomic settlement. But that guarantee is only as strong as the instrument that serves as cash within the system is solid. As stablecoins can break their peg during times of stress, they do not confer the unconditional finality that central bank money does. At stake is the singleness of money—the principle that a unit of currency has the same value regardless of who issues it because it is guaranteed by central bank money at its core. A settlement layer built on private stablecoins risks weakening that principle. On fragmentation, the promise of tokenized finance is a single interoperable environment. But if settlement relies on stablecoins, that environment with its singleness as alleged, actually fragments across whatever number of competing instruments that market produces. We have two key players at the moment. But this is at the moment. There will be many more of those. So we end up as a result with multiple platforms and no common anchor for convertibility. A Eurosystem survey confirms that the absence of a widely accepted tokenized cash settlement asset has already been a material constraint on tokenization adoption in Europe. While these are weaknesses of the instrument itself, they are not arguments against private tokenized money as part of a broader ecosystem. Tokenized commercial bank deposits, for example, offer another route, as they carry the credit quality of regulated institutions, can circulate on DLT platforms, and meantime prove preferable to stablecoins for many wholesale use cases. So regardless of which instrument will prevail, the public infrastructure must be in place to ensure safety and interoperability across the system. And this is what we are building, and I will finish with that. From September 26, we will offer wholesale settlement through what we have called the APYAPONTES project. Don't ask me why it's called APYAPONTES, but we love these acronyms. APYAPONTES links their distributed ledger platforms to TARGET, which is our settlement system, ensuring that DLT-based transactions can be settled in central bank money from day one. The tests conducted in 2024 that we did showed that this approach works under real-world conditions with transactions across nine jurisdictions being settled on the basis of that technology. And the roadmap for APYAPONTES that we published in March goes even further and sets out the path for a fully interoperable European tokenized financial ecosystem by 2028. Dollar-denominated stablecoins have, through first-mover advantage and network effects, already become the default settlement asset in tokenized finance. When central bank money is available natively on chain and when tokenized deposits and MiCA-compliant euro instruments can operate within the same interoperable environment, market participants will have no reason to rely on a foreign private substitute by default. We are in a race against time, and we're not going to let go. So let me conclude. The question I asked at the outset was whether or not we need stablecoins to obtain the benefits that they are said to provide, or whether we are mistaking the instrument for the outcome. The answer I have argued depends on the function. For the monetary function, the foundations have to come first. Deeper, more integrated capital markets, safe assets. Stablecoins cannot build those foundations for us, and they will not. Without them, euro-denominated stablecoins would risk amplifying the very vulnerability that we are trying to overcome. For the settlement function, the key question is less about which private instrument will prevail—whether stablecoins, tokenized deposits, or yet-to-emerge alternatives—but whether a common anchor is in place. And this is why we are placing central bank money at the heart of this new infrastructure. I started with Seneca. No wind is favorable to one who has no idea about the port he's going to. Well, we know the port. Our task is not to replicate instruments developed elsewhere but to build the foundation and the infrastructure that serves our own objectives. So that we can harness the benefit of innovation and encourage it without importing its fragilities. I thank you so much for your attention.

The Right After Trump (Ft. Benjamin Teitelbaum)

1Dime Radio(19.0K)·80:17·1.2K views·2026-05-08
Benjamin TeitelbaumProfessor
TL;DRThe contemporary far-right is a fractured coalition of "Traditionalists" seeking to restore a structured past and "Futurists" pursuing a borderless, tech-driven hierarchy.
Understanding these movements requires moving beyond the "fascism" label to analyze their specific...

TL;DR

The contemporary far-right is a fractured coalition of "Traditionalists" seeking to restore a structured past and "Futurists" pursuing a borderless, tech-driven hierarchy. Understanding these movements requires moving beyond the "fascism" label to analyze their specific anti-liberal philosophies and the novel strategies they use to challenge mainstream democracy.

Ethnography and the "Deep Hanging Out" Method

  • The Ethnographic Approach: Professor Benjamin Teitelbaum utilizes "deep hanging out"—a form of long-term immersive research—to understand far-right actors as people rather than just studying their texts or music.
      • This method seeks a "knowledge of someone that comes when you know them well" through formal interviews and informal social interaction.
  • Critique of "Activist Scholarship": Teitelbaum distinguishes his work from "activist scholars" who enter research with pre-determined political conclusions.
      • He argues that starting a project with the intent to combat the subject prevents true understanding, as the researcher is no longer open to learning unexpected insights.
  • Mainstreaming of the Fringe: Teitelbaum notes that groups like the Sweden Democrats moved from the radical margins to the center of national politics, meaning that to study the far-right today is to study the state of Western society as a whole.

The Ideology of Steve Bannon and "Traditionalism"

  • Bannon as a Contradiction: Teitelbaum describes Bannon as an "American nationalist anti-liberal."
      • He attempts to fuse the American revolutionary spirit (which is forward-looking and emancipatory) with "Capital T" Traditionalism (which is backward-looking and focuses on inherited identity).
  • Capital "T" Traditionalism: This philosophical school, rooted in the work of René Guénon, posits that a "true religion" existed in the past but has been degraded over time.
      • Cyclic Time: Traditionalists reject linear progress, believing instead in a cycle of decline from a Golden Age to a Dark Age (the Hindu "Kali Yuga").
      • Hierarchy and Borders: They believe a virtuous society requires rigid structure and caste-like hierarchies. A "Dark Age" is defined by borderlessness, intermixing, and the triumph of materialism over spirituality.
  • The "Legacy American" Focus: While Bannon has recently reached out to the Black working class for electoral reasons, his core philosophy prioritizes "legacy Americans"—those whose families participated in the historical construction of the U.S. state.

Geopolitical Tensions: Bannon vs. Alexander Dugin

  • Civilizational Disagreement: While Bannon and Russian philosopher Alexander Dugin both hate globalist liberalism, they disagree on the role of the United States.
      • Dugin views the U.S. as the "purest embodiment" of the disease of liberalism.
      • Bannon argues for a "tribal Americanism" that is inherently Christian and nationalist, suggesting the U.S. and Russia should be allies against non-Christian powers.
  • The China/Iran Divide: The collaboration between Bannon and Dugin collapsed over other nations.
      • Bannon’s View: Wants a global alliance of Christian nations to exclude China and Iran.
      • Dugin’s View: A religious pluralist who values theocracy over democracy; he views Iran as a natural ally because it prioritizes the "spiritual and eternal" over liberal materialism.

The Future: Traditionalist Right vs. Futurist Right

  • The Tech-Right Schism: A major rift exists between Bannon’s "nostalgic reactionary right" and the "futurist right" (represented by figures like Peter Thiel and Elon Musk).
      • Nostalgic Right: Focuses on "Make America Great *Again*," seeking to restore order through historical models.
      • Futurist Right: Seeks a "future arrived early," characterized by the disintegration of inherited social orders and a hierarchy based on technological performance rather than tradition.
  • Disavowal of Democracy: Both factions are united by a skepticism of democracy, though for different reasons. Bannon wants archaic, "non-bureaucratized" communities, while the Tech-Right views democracy as an inefficient process that is "100% dispensable" if it fails to deliver specific products or outcomes.

Re-evaluating the "Fascism" Label

  • The Problem with "Crying Fascism": Teitelbaum argues the term "fascist" is often used as a tool to exclude people from political life rather than to describe them accurately.
      • De-stigmatization: Overusing the label can lead to "ironic" embrace or nihilism among young right-wingers, such as Nick Fuentes, who use taboo-breaking as a communication strategy.
  • Focus on Novelties over Redundancies: Teitelbaum warns that labeling figures like Bannon as "fascists" suggests we already understand them perfectly because we have seen fascism before.
      • He insists that the most important things to learn about the contemporary radical right are its *novelties*—the ways it defies historical rules and anticipates a future that opponents are not prepared for.

***

"It is a lost dream and it should be an abandoned dream of the opponents of the far-right that they're just going to vanquish their enemies... I think that politics have been transformed." — Benjamin Teitelbaum
"Americanism is a tribalism. And that tribalism, Bannon said, not only is- are there links between it and Russia and Russian tribalism, but it's very much as part of this anti-liberal lifeway." — Benjamin Teitelbaum
"I believe that the most important things to learn and to understand about the contemporary radical right are its novelties, not its redundancies... The novelties are what we don't want to face up to." — Benjamin Teitelbaum
We will not get to pick what version of the far-right comes when it comes, when it arrives. That's one reason to be mindful of what is true and what can be right about the sentiment. I very much appreciate that you're thinking along those lines. I think it is a lost dream, and it should be an abandoned dream of the opponents of the far-right, that they're just going to vanquish their enemies. There is this idea that this was just a flash in the pan, a parenthetical movement, or that because Trump was re-elected and it's such a shit show, it's going to implode on itself. The hope is that it will all be gone and we can get back to history-making in the way that we were before. I think that politics have been transformed. You talk about Denmark; that was a political strategy that was condemned by a lot of people because it showed when the Social Democrats in Denmark took over the politics, more or less, of the Dansk Folkeparti, the far-right party. It acknowledged in some sense the permanence or the enduring nature of the far-right's ideals and agendas in Danish politics, at least for the near future. That was really painful for the people who were committed to seeing that movement erased and who were committed to seeing it be parenthetical. The problem is that there are some problems with immigration. There are grievances that might attach themselves to immigration or to a hatred of a certain class of people that you might think are just tragic, self-defeating, or dangerous incarnations of something that needs to be listened to and taken seriously. In the New York Times after the election in Hungary, Ross Douthat was speaking very much in the lines that we're speaking right now. He said it is the left's lullaby that the far-right can be explained by disinformation and by pathetic, dismissible grievances. Instead, what Péter Márki-Zay showed was that if you make an alternative to something that is really corrupt, but you maintain some of the opposition to immigration, you can actually do something quite substantial. You can overthrow a powerful, anti-democratic demagogue with so much force that there's not going to be room for a lot of finagling and tomfoolery after an election result. That is certainly something that I wish the left would do more: to not play a politics of structure and symbolism, but instead say let's look at all these ideas and accept the fact that you need to be a leader for the entirety of your population. You really cannot have no more room for the far-right than for the left and center-left. You're going to have to deal with them. And if you have to deal with them and you have to live together, maybe we can find something that is more productive and some way to find some sense of things. You are listening to 1Dime Radio. Become a patron at patreon.com/1dime to support the show and get access to extra content. "I'm not saying I'm going to rule the world or I'm going to change the world, but I guarantee that I will spark the brain that will change the world, and that's our job. We might not be the one, but let's not be selfish and because we're not going to change the world let's not talk about how we should change it." "By moderately conservative, I mean simply: before you act, think well about all possible consequences." "Philosophers call someone a relativist, by which they mean it's a person that holds that any view is as good as any other view. No one believes that every view is as good as any other view." "You want to do politics, you got to fucking understand the issues. It's not all just emotions and fantasy life." "Remember, my friend, a wise man can learn more from a foolish question than a fool can learn from a wise answer." All right. Welcome to 1Dime Radio. Today I'm here with Professor Benjamin Teitelbaum, ethnographer, a scholar of the far-right and professor at University of Colorado Boulder. He is the author of two incredible books, one of which I was able to get partially through. Both are quite long, featuring very extensive interviews with Steve Bannon, really getting to know the guy and having some revealing insights. And you have another book, which I'm very interested in and I heard really good things about. I heard you talk about it, where you spent a lot of time with these far-right groups. You have a particular approach to studying the far-right that I think gives you a unique perspective on this matter, which has a kind of funny name. What's it called, like "deep hanging out"? "One of the names, yeah." So that's a funny way to talk about it, but maybe that's the first thing: what made your approach to studying the far-right different and what exactly did you do? My approach to studying the far-right has been, as you say Tony, as an ethnographer, and that's changed over the years. I think for a lot of people, the way you could relate to that is to say that I've been kind of like a long-form or a recurring investigative journalist as opposed to a scholar who would just study people from a distance. I don't just study the things that they make, the music they make, or the texts. Instead, I've made a point not only of meeting them for interviews, but the real goal is to get to know them in the colloquial sense of that term. I want to be familiar with their lives, to not only interview them in a formal setting but also to spend a lot of time around them, hence the reference to "deep hanging out" that you talked about. That's the basics of the method. The idea behind it is that there are certain types of knowledge that we can have about another person, but the type of knowledge I was interested in is the knowledge of someone that comes when you know them well. It comes when you can have a conversation with them about their lives, basically. That's a different type of understanding. It's not the only way to know who somebody is or how they see the world, but it's one vital way, I think. It was once upon a time very rare in the study of the far-right, and I still feel like there are relatively few people who do everything that I just mentioned. Yeah, you explicitly said that you don't take the activist approach which is trying to understand for the purposes of combatting it. And that doesn't necessarily mean that you are against combatting it, but rather you avoid this kind of propagandistic sort of approach which is trying to create a narrative that will push people away. Your goal is first and foremost understanding. And correct me if I'm wrong, it's like you spent time mostly with, at least with the book before the Bannon one, Scandinavian far-right groups and white supremacist groups who went from being super fringe and a little bit bad on optics to being very well organized, right? And powerful. I mean, I think if you look at Swedish politics today, for example, it all orbits around the far-right party that managed to change itself enough to really collect a lot of electoral support and then come into the parliament; that's the Sweden Democrats, by the way. So yeah, they moved from the margins to the mainstream. To study them—and maybe we'll get into this: why would someone want to study the far-right?—really encapsulates the benefit of doing it. Because you study the far-right in a lot of Western democracies, you end up studying the society as a whole and where they are right now. Sweden was a big example of that. Can I pick up something that you mentioned though, Tony? You said that I'm not an activist scholar and that's true. You were defining activist scholarship as understanding for the purposes of conducting activism against the people who you're studying. I actually think the problem starts before then. I don't think that one goes into a research project as an activist scholar wanting to understand. I think an activist goes into a project with the answers. They know what they want the outcomes of their research to be, so I would have a lot of arguments with them. I have a lot of friends who are activist scholars and most of my PhD students are activist scholars, so these are not people that I dismiss. But the arguments that I have with them actually begin at the very beginning. I don't think you can both be open to learning potentially anything from someone and be committed to having an outcome to that project that is going to be politically palatable for you if you're wanting your research and your politics to align. Yeah, I've been familiar with the term "activist historian" before. Gordon Wood likes to use that phrase because there's a lot of history books that are written with the conclusion that was there well before they began any research, which is to create a sort of narrative as opposed to just ironing out the details and trying to really accurately portray what happened. Now, what interests me first to get into, because I really want to kind of go through the various factions of the right, because when we talk about the right, this is obviously a huge swath. We're focusing on obviously the radical right, but even that is super diverse. Let's start with Bannon, just because that was obviously one of your biggest projects. This is obviously a huge question, but you spoke to the guy for a very long time: what does he actually believe? What is his political philosophy? I say that because I saw so many contradictory things in the interviews. It seems like he's influenced by American populist movements, to some extent even American labor populist movements, and by very obscure far-right theories like Evola and Traditionalism. It all seems very contradictory and yet this is the guy who supposedly was the mastermind behind Trump's first campaign. Right. What does he believe? What does he actually believe? So I wrote a book on him. I don't know of a simple way to answer that question that is not going to have a lot of qualifiers on it. If you ever hear somebody give you a simple answer to that question, please doubt them. I promise you. And that's not to make him into some sort of superhuman figure, to just say that his complexity means he's great. He's conflicted and hypocritical like everyone else. He is. It's just that I think he's especially noteworthy for wanting to put together a bunch of ideas that don't actually fit together. You could say that he is an American anti-liberal, an American nationalist anti-liberal. That's one way to invite everybody to think about the contradictions that could exist in this particular person. I say that with the base understanding that Americanism, the US revolutionary spirit, is in some way defining of him. That spirit has an alignment with liberalism; that is to say with new beginnings, with an emancipatory cause, to look at the past primarily as a story of oppression and to look to the future to rectify that. It suggests that we will really be able to improve our lives and the lives of those around us if we can tear ourselves up from our inherited identities, places, and things like that. If all of that is Americanism, Bannon is trying to in some way combine that with an entirely opposed set of ideas: that what is inherited is good, that the key insights about our lives and about the universe actually already existed in the past. It suggests that the way to understand them and utilize them is actually to turn our gaze backwards, not forwards. It is not to emancipate ourselves from our history and our context and the circumstances of our birth, but instead to lean into them and to make them our future. Those two things do not combine very well. And all of the latter, all of that lowercase "l" anti-liberal thought and thinking, that's the eccentric stuff. That's Julius Evola, that's Alexander Dugin, that's René Guénon. Those are the European anti-liberals, the Russian anti-liberals, and his attempt to fuse that all together into one coherent ideology is really the subject of the book. Not only that, but also his attempts to coordinate with other people on the basis of some of those ideas. But to get back to your question, what does he actually believe? It's tough to say. It's really hard to say. Within that, I can hear him affirming a lot of different positions. I can hear him saying America's a revolutionary society, and I can hear him saying, no, we are actually a Christian satellite of Western European Christian society, and it is our destiny to maintain that identity at all costs. Hence the borders, hence the preservation of traditional values, hence the opposition to wokeness that was much more pronounced earlier in his career and before he started with the Trump campaign at Breitbart. So I can keep going, but that should just give you a basic overview of what I think the problem is in trying to define his thinking. Yeah, well you mentioned Dugin in there and that was one of the contradictions that I thought was really interesting: the big differences between someone like Alexander Dugin and Bannon, because they get compared a lot. Dugin is often thought to be the Russian equivalent of Bannon to Putin, although his influence on Putin seems far less direct compared to Bannon, who directly worked with Trump and was on talking terms with him for quite some time. But you mention how they have completely different civilizational views. What Bannon sees as the disease that he wants to fight, Dugin sees America as being the cause of that, the purest embodiment of that. So they don't exactly get along—this Russian rightist view and Bannon's American rightist view. Well, let's back up a minute, because they actually could get along. It depends really on the definition of America, essentially. So you said earlier, Tony, that you're interested in understanding what type of political right we're talking about and understanding the divisions within the political right. It's a shock to a lot of American conservatives or Republicans—let's say capital "R" Republicans—that there is such a thing as an anti-capitalist, anti-Western, potentially anti-American right, which also, by the way, shares space with an anti-Christian right. When we tend to think of pro-capitalism, Christian nationalist, American jingoistic attitudes as all just being synonymous with the political right, it really throws us into a tizzy when we start to imagine how a right could germinate in opposition to all those things. But there can be one. Now Alexander Dugin, this Russian philosopher, media personality, rabble-rouser, or whatever you want to call him, is one of the key exponents of that anti-American right. He wouldn't be anti-Christian, but he's flirting with it in a lot of his connections. He's anti-capitalist and anti-liberal. He says, essentially, the world and the nations and the political societies that emerge from, say, the French Revolution, were emancipatory, forward-looking, and progressive. They tried to essentially make culturally and religiously neutral public spheres and states. They tried to move the entire realm of public discourse away from culture and spirituality and into questions of economics and materialism. Such that, let's say in the US in the 1990s and 1980s, our main political battles were over the marginal tax rate, for example. Dugin thinks that all of that actually belongs to the political left. That's all a progressive leftism. If you take someone like that at their word or just follow their thinking, they would claim that it might be capitalism itself that is part of this grand disintegration of society. It's this renegotiation of bonds in terms of flexible contracts, the monetization of society, and the secularization of society that needs to take place in order for different people to trade more easily with each other. It's the loss of ethnic identity and the reformulation of ethnic identity into something symbolic only. You're Irish for a day, you put your T-shirt on, but your actual roots aren't going to impact the people you marry as much or the business dealings that you have. Capitalism can be at the root of all of those things. Some would allege as well that Christianity is at the root of all those things. That was less Alexander Dugin and more French philosopher Alain de Benoist, who said that Christianity is essentially a progressive, emancipatory, liberatory cause and political cause, and therefore that actually belongs in the grand scheme of things in the political left. Anyway, so Dugin looks at all those things and paints them all as being left-wing. He says to boot that the American state itself is an embodiment of all of those processes. It's a manifestation of liberalism, of a sort of anti-conservative, anti-reactionary, anti-deep rightism. And therefore it would never make sense to speak about a conservative or right-wing America, because that's just a contradiction in terms. He lays all this out in his thinking. Bannon comes along and says essentially to Dugin, "I actually think that my thinking can be a part of your world too. I'm also against corporatism, I'm against runaway capitalism, or at least I want capitalism to bend the knee before nationalism. And I think I agree with it all except I will intervene to say I think you got America wrong." This is something that Bannon told me he said to Dugin: that actually there is an essential Americanism that goes beyond, is deeper, is almost invisible, and goes beyond all of our narratives of progress and emancipation and anti-traditionalism. Instead, it is inherently continuous, inherently integrated, and connected to the past. It is Christian, it is nationalist, it is tribalistic, and it belongs to particular people in a particular time. It is not a universal concept of democracy or human rights or capitalism or the Enlightenment that can just purportedly be moved around the world for any group of people. Instead, Americanism is a tribalism. And that tribalism, Bannon said, not only has links between it and Russia and Russian tribalism, but it's very much part of this anti-liberal lifeway and way of being in the world. We can actually find a way to agree together if we label your anti-Americanism properly, which is actually anti-globalist and anti-liberal. So it's in those spaces where you had something that was coming out of Russia that seemed to be so much opposed to the United States, and yet Bannon was trying to find a way to make it fit. But Dugin wasn't buying it. "Yes and no." It's where things started to fall apart in their collaboration. It didn't really have to do with the United States and Russia per se. It had to do with other nations. Iran and China were the key examples. So Bannon was trying to say that, "Hey, look, what makes us friends and actual allies, the reason why there should be some new reunion between the United States and Russia, is that we are both nationalistic and we are both Christian. And we should initiate a new foreign policy that brings Christian nations together, traditional Christian nations together, and that keeps other nations that are not traditional Christian out." In principle among them are Iran and China. Dugin wasn't into that. Part of it is complicated, but he's coming from a religious orientation that a religious scholar would call pluralistic. The many paths up the same mountain. He would call himself a Christian, but also thinks that there is deep validity, for example, in Sufi Islam. Probably in Zoroastrianism as well. In Dugin's case, that is true. And so Dugin said, "Well, it doesn't really matter to me that Iran isn't Christian per se. The fact that they're a theocracy means that they have prioritized the spiritual, the unchanging, the eternal over the fleeting democratic and liberal worldview, and that makes us actual allies. It doesn't matter what religion we're following." So no, an Iran-Russia-China alliance of states actually makes a ton of sense to him. He would add the Eurasianist view, right? The Eurasianist view, although what they were discussing there actually goes beyond that. He was talking about some generic qualities to a nation that made it anti-liberal. That would be spiritual, it would be collectivist, and also immune or hostile to change, and thereby to the narratives of liberalism and progressivism or Marxism, however you would want to cut the pie. And so he happens to think that a lot of the landbound nations of Eurasia, by virtue of their geography, the fact that they're not on the Atlantic or seafaring and thereby susceptible to social forms, narratives, and storytelling that glorifies movement, change, progress, and emancipation, are going to be more predisposed toward continuity and toward holism and collectivism. And that could express itself through a very powerful state, through the centralization of a state, as in the case of North Korea or China. It could also present itself in fealty to inherited spiritual practices and religions, making sure that society was actually not letting itself let go of its previously organizing principle. So there's a lot going on there, but it seems like, to what extent do you take the value systems of someone like Bannon to be very crucial in themselves, or merely just kind of rationalizations for geopolitical strategy? The logic of aligning with Russia against China has geopolitical logic to it from the standpoint of US interest, but from the standpoint of traditionalism it kind of, in my view, doesn't. And I can see why Dugin would see that in many ways Russia and Iran would have things in common. I would even say China too. I think China is a lot more conservative than people really realize. Out of all those countries, the one that I've studied the most is China, and people really underestimate truly how much Confucianism has been actually promoted by the state in the last 10 years, and that never really went away. There is a conservative culture in China. So it's like, to what extent do Bannon's ideologies really matter, or is it just like legitimation narratives, to put it that way? Yeah, it's a totally legitimate question. It's very hard to say. Here's a data point to work with, though, as we consider that question. If you follow his biography from his youth to the present, you actually can be struck by how many costume changes there are. How many times the guy kind of redid himself: wanting to be a politician, being a hippie, joining the military, going to Harvard Business School, becoming an executive, turning to entertainment, turning to media, turning to politics again. So you see this capricious life story and biography defined by difference, defined by almost a lack of authenticity, basically. He's reframing himself so often in order to fit some other agenda. But one thing that has stayed quite still and quite consistent throughout all those costume changes I mentioned was his interest in esoteric philosophy and esoteric religion. That sometimes aligned with the capital "T" Traditionalism that is very much the focus of my research on this world, and sometimes it's just adjacent to it. I'm thinking of the writings of a man named Gurdjieff, for example, or Jacob Needleman, these scholars of religion. Bannon was interested in that from the beginning across his political ebb and flow, across his move from the left to the political right. So now, I don't think it would be at all justified to say that he started being interested in this stuff just when he came into politics and the moment he started to have contact with Alexander Dugin, because there's a deeper history there. Having said that, however, does it mean that he's not in some sense obliged to try and get his political agenda, his geopolitical vision, to work with his philosophy because he knows he really doesn't want to dump the philosophy? He's too attached to it. And somewhere in those moves, there's something inauthentic going on. He's improvising and trying to make all these pieces work as best as he can. I'm hesitant to want to say yes, that it's all just window dressing, that he has his political agendas and strategies and he wants to be fancy about it in part because it lets us off a little bit too easy. Always as a commentator, as a scholar, beware of giving yourself permission to be lazy, and dismissing someone is the way one does that. My suspicion is that he has a number of commitments in his mind at the same time and he's doing his best to make them work together. Where he gets a hit, he runs with it, and where he doesn't, he tries to fudge. We'll return to that shortly on the question of authenticity and so-called grifting. But I think it might be useful for the audience to explain Traditionalism, just because people use that phrase colloquially, obviously, to talk about, "Are my traditionalists in some sense, do you believe in traditional values of whether it be Christian or Buddhist or Muslim or whatsoever?" But Traditionalism by René Guénon is something altogether very different. Can you explain that? Mm-hm. The only clue that we get that we're talking about something specific and distinct is that it's almost always written with a capital "T." This type of Traditionalism I'll refer to is not a political ideology initially, at least. It's a philosophical and religious school that is centered around a French Orientalist—although he did not want to call himself an Orientalist—who was living during the turn of the 20th century in France. He was born René Guénon and died Sheikh Abdel Wahid Yahya in Egypt, having been born a Catholic and died a Sufi sheikh. And what he and his followers were principally interested in was the possibility that once upon a time in the past there was a true and actual religion that got everything right and that was passed through initiation and through generations more or less authentically and correctly. But as time went on—and this is part of the actual content of that religion—as time went on it was gradually forgotten, gradually degraded, gradually splintered. Pieces of it then exist and are preserved in a number of different religious traditions around the world, principally the esoteric, the mystical, the initiatory branches of major religions. Hinduism stands out as being the religion that most of these figures think preserved the most of the original capital "T" Tradition. And so a lot of the work of Traditionalists is comparative religion. It's trying to kind of reconstruct and understand what once was and what the truths and the secrets of the universe are. That's the big picture. The way that this ends up in the hands of people like Bannon and Alexander Dugin has to do with the content of the actual Tradition itself. How is this different from like perennial philosophy, kind of what people like Aldous Huxley were trying to do? Because I do see some—Huxley himself wasn't really like politically on the right in any sense, but he did have this project of wanting to synthesize religious traditions and tended to focus on the Eastern traditions more than others. Yes, by account of their antiquity and continuity of practice. Yeah, Huxley, if you look in his texts, in fact, he cites Traditionalists. He cites René Guénon in some of his seminal work. So it's definitely close to that. Where Traditionalism starts to diverge from, let's say, his particular version of perennialism has to do with some of the actual face-to-face, real-life organizations and groupings that collected around it. And that lived not so much thanks to René Guénon, this figure that I was referring to, but to one of his followers, Frithjof Schuon. Schuon was for the most part a Sufist, although he ended up also trying to synthesize a lot of religions, but he had followers around him who were initiated into his Tariqa, his Sufi grouping. And that's certainly something that we see. It was much more closed, much more initiatory, much closer to the sort of Bhagwan Rajneesh religious cult that really became a sort of fad in the United States in the 1970s and 80s than what we would see among readers of Huxley per se. But there's another distinction when you look at the types of ideas that Traditionalists found in their comparative religious study that then became important to them, and especially important to those who wanted to make political hay out of it all. They wanted to try and derive an actual message and agenda for how politics and society should be shaped today. That's really interesting. I mean I can briefly name the two ideas probably to go through if you could name those. I'll be brief. Yeah, it's really just two concepts when I'm trying to explain how Traditionalism becomes politicized on the right. One of them is cyclic time. If you think most modern political ideologies and a lot of the Judeo-Christian tradition posits that time moves from past to future in a linear motion and we escape the past and we never return to it, Traditionalism asserts something quite different. It says that we're always moving through time in a cycle, and more specifically moving in a cycle of degradation. It says that we move from a golden age toward a silver, a bronze, a dark and back to a golden. And that say for one little minute, that little move from dark to golden, which is cataclysmic and destructive, time is basically withering away at us. We're getting worse and worse, the only solace being that the worst things get for us, the closer we are to a restart and rejuvenation. So that's one part of it. The other thing is a belief that what makes society good, what defines a golden age, is that society is structured and that boundaries and borders between people, an organizational structure, reigns in a golden society. Whereas a dark age society, a society that is depraved and at the furthest reaches of this cycle of decline, is going to be defined by complete borderlessness and a complete intermixing of people. That is captured with the Hindu concept of the caste system and a caste hierarchy, specifically one that borders and prioritizes a priestly caste, the Brahmins in the case of Hinduism, on top of more materially oriented castes like merchants and slaves. But the broader principle is that if you're living in the dark age, you're going to see an age where there is no prioritization or separation or distinction given to spiritual causes. Instead, everybody is going to be commingling in the most likely and most numerous ideas—materialism in particular—are going to reign and everybody is going to be part of a kind of gray mass. That's the dark age. So you get from that a sort of anti-progressivism, and you also get from that an anti-egalitarian ethos that really says inequality and differences between people are good and it is a depraved society that would try to eradicate those differences. Yeah, and the dark ages—I mean I was trying so hard not to get into the Kali Yuga and Evola. But it is worth mentioning I guess, because that kind of completes that sort of trajectory of the politicization of Traditionalism. Yeah, the Kali Yuga, that's a kind of a blanket Hindu term for that era of destruction, that moment when you're at the end of a time cycle and things have gotten so bad and there's actually now going to be a cataclysmic explosion. Everything that's around you is going to be torn apart but in its ashes, in its place, something better is going to rise up. That new society is going to be defined by its prioritization of spirituality over materialism and also by its structuring, by the fact that there are going to be borders there. And so you think of a Trump supporter, doesn't even need to be Bannon. Think of how they might try to see this illustrated in the world today. I don't think it's that far-fetched for them to say globalization, immigration, feminism, the emancipation of previously oppressed minority groups, the intermingling of people from all over the world—that all of those things are instances of border-breaking and the tearing down of structuring boundaries between humanity that could have made us a virtuous society but they're not. And therefore, this must mean that we are at the end of a cycle where our society is tearing apart. No, that kind of has logic to it for sure. There's a left-wing critique of that same thing in some ways with capitalism, globalization, and whatnot. But that brings us to the very blatant contradictions that seem to exist on the right. The Trump right, especially in the second term, has been fully embracing the tech oligarchs of Silicon Valley, who Bannon at least is seeming in rhetoric to be quite at odds with. He's very critical of Elon Musk. But I've seen you talk about this recently, like this new tension between the futurist right and the traditionalist right. It doesn't really seem like the traditionalist right is really that influential in MAGA anymore. Well, they don't have a lot of money. And that's one—there's so many ways of cutting this. This is my next book project, by the way, is essentially about the futurist right, about the ideas of the futurist right. And at face value, I think it's more complicated than I'm going to make it sound right now, but at face value you do see indeed, just like you put it, almost like a full-fledged contradiction of everything I was just talking about coming from the tech right. It's coming from the thinkers. I would really say it's more Peter Thiel who's producing in himself, and also those in his watershed are producing, a more coherent worldview. You could also mention Nick Land who bizarrely Alexander Dugin used to kind of like, although they really end up on complete opposite sides of the spectrum here. And you had in Bannon an idea about how to make a better world, principally by moving us back into the past. It's an idea that's contained in the Make America Great Again slogan, the idea that American greatness is something from our past. But through pure absurdity and through breaking the laws of modern science, we're going to bring the past into the future again. American greatness can be ours again. The last word, "again," is the most important word in that phrase. That captures kind of the Bannon ethos. Let's bring back a society of order and structure by recruiting from the past. The tech right is saying something entirely different. Sometimes it's casual and kind of relatable and other times it's very strange and requires us to be as forgiving in our critical capacities as we would be if we hear someone saying that the past can come alive again. They want to see a future recruited into our present, a future arrived early whose promise and whose benefit to us is not that it's going to restore order and restore boundaries and borders and give us a new hierarchical society with fixed divisions between people. It's the opposite. It is going to be a complete eradication of borders. It is an anti-social and, in most cases, an anti-political vision of complete disintegration of compelled and inherited social order. That's what the tech right, I would argue, is bringing to us. And yeah, it is putting it mildly to say that this is a contradiction. Yeah, and of course that involves Curtis Yarvin being probably more in line with that than Land is. Land seems to be kind of obscure these days but definitely influential on that thinking. If there's anything that really holds these people together onto anything that is right-wing, probably I would say is the hierarchy principle. They both believe in a real hierarchical society. The difference is that the tech right seems just like completely anti-conservative versus the other way around. In some sense, it seems like the tech right sees the traditional in some ways a stabilizing of egalitarianisms that they would oppose. Like they oppose, you know, for example, a tech right person would see that the US has too much welfare or too much of a social safety net and that maybe too much of a commitment to liberalism, like that commitment to the Constitution. Sort of conservative attachments to these sort of structures are bad and you need to break that down. I see that as a Canadian. To me, what's interesting is in Canada, a lot of political traditions are not really like left or right in an explicit way in the sense that there is a "peace, order and good government" which is like the big slogan of our government. On the left there tends to be more of an emphasis on the welfare state and then on the right, more so of the constitutionalism, but both support both to an extent. Both have a commitment to a welfare state. That would make us a conservative country but still, from the eyes of probably a right-wing ideologue, more left-wing than America. So there's a tension between the conservatism and the right and left, in the sense that I think right and left today don't really seem to have any semblance when it comes to order and disorder. It's really the only thing that really is a meaningful distinction between them in my view: hierarchy and equality. Right, right. There's a lot to say about this, Tony. On the one hand, what you have in Canada, in my impression—I'm not a Canadian and I haven't studied Canada that much—but I've always understood it as being somewhat parallel to Scandinavia where you have debates over who the most responsible stewards of the welfare state are. And that is a debate that both the left and the right participate in and therefore you can't actually use that to make meaningful distinctions between the two of them. But if we go back to what you were saying, yes, there is an opposition to equality and a sort of celebration of hierarchy here seen between, let's say, the Bannonites and let's just say Musk for purposes of conversation. It's the nostalgic reactionary right versus the futurist right that we saw manifest in American politics. Indeed, the way that they oppose egalitarianism and the way that they celebrate hierarchy tells the whole story. Is it an inherited hierarchy? Is it a hierarchy based on principles and values that have been passed along to us for generations? If the answer is yes, you're talking about someone on the reactionary right, the nostalgic Bannon, bringing the past back. If you're looking at someone on the futurist right instead, they're going to be saying, no, it shall be through performance. We can have hierarchy, and egalitarianism is our enemy, but this is kind of a sort of meritocracy pushed into overdrive. In that sense, it sounds like a new iteration of a sort of lowercase "l" liberal right that is really connected to the logics of the marketplace and capital guiding things. Another piece of this, though, if we could add and say that it's not only an opposition to equality that unites these forces together in the same political family. It's also varying forms of disavowal of democracy. That's not to say that they are all anti-democratic, but there is a distinction between all of these actors, the Musks and the Bannons and let's say the type of political thinker who sees something almost sacred in democracy, who looks at our democratic process and says that our commitment to democratic process is what gives us our identity, is what is the actual content of our national character and of our political system and our values. They both, the Bannons and the Musks, both have reasons for attacking. Bannon because he wants to see political communities defined by something that is more archaic, less fleeting, less universal, less abstract than something like a bureaucratic process. The Elon Musks because they have some other value, some other measure for success and goodness than democratic elegance, let's say. If they were trying to describe themselves, I think they would say that they were more product than a process-based political ideology, and that they want to see actual people do actual things. If democracy serves us and can get us to a good place and do wonderful things for us, then it's fine, but that's different from saying that democracy is the actual object of our adoration. If it doesn't serve us, it is 100% dispensable. That does seem to definitely tie these opposite sides of the right together in a certain sense. I think that would be tied to the equality question in the sense that democracy—of course we don't have democracy in the direct sense—but in so far as what modern democracy does, it is the distribution of political power. For the economy, I view socialism—in so far as it's not just a statist project, because then what distinguishes Bismarckian socialism from National Socialism from Soviet socialism? Obviously those are different political projects, but I view socialism as being about the distribution of economic power, so that's still on the equality angle. Democracy is the distribution of political power, so it's still on the left side of the spectrum to some extent. Even no matter how right-wing of a government you have, if there's a commitment to a separation of powers and multi-party elections, that is still to the left politically than someone who believes in some kind of autocratic rule, whether it's theocratic or how Yarvin sees it, which is like the CEO monarch. Or Bannon who—that is actually a good question. So like, is Bannon fully committed against democracy? No. No, but this is really helpful everything that you just said because both of those sides are there with him. This is the divided Bannon. This is the contradiction that we talked about. He wants to see distribution of power among the people. And the key aspect of this is: who are the people? And it turns out that category "the people" aligns partially but not entirely with the electorate of the United States. And there are certain people within this sphere that matter more than others. Now, I've never heard him say something as blatant as, for example, "I don't think West and East Coast elites should be able to vote." But he has said the identity of the nation, the identity of our political community, is defined by the white working class, especially what someone might call legacy Americans. I've never actually heard him use that term, but I've heard him describe this population in his own terms. He arrives at it descriptively. That is to say people who have been in the United States for multiple generations whose family participated in the construction of the American state, in mainstream American society and our institutions in ways that other people would not. That would push out, of course, a lot of Americans today, a lot of working-class Americans, and a lot of white Americans. I don't have many so-called legacy American ancestors, for example. But he has said that their interests need to be the actual priority for the country, for the state. If he is speaking about nefarious Chinese actions against the United States, his principal soapbox topic is to talk about the ways that he thinks the Chinese are targeting the American white working class. In particular, he thinks they are targeting them with opioids and painkillers and addictive drugs that he blames China for funneling into the very social groups, the very demographics where true American spirit and true American identity are being housed—the white working class. So you don't see this actually manifesting in electoral politics, but you do see a distinction between people and people within the demos of the United States. It's a claim that the interests of some ought to matter more than the interests of others. And it is a claim that this should be so indefinitely, by the way. This is not about rectifying an injustice. This is about indefinitely. I've seen Bannon include black people as part of the people that MAGA wants to help, which—and I've seen him reiterate it a lot, so it's hard for me to say he's not being authentic. What it seems to me is like for him, it's really people who have been in America for a certain amount of time, at the very minimum legally migrated. And he certainly uses left-wing rhetoric when it comes to some of his reasons for opposing immigration. There are these little moments that tend to happen where he overlaps with Bernie Sanders, for instance, on opposing H1B visas. So there's that. But of course, this brings us to the "F-word," which is fascism. Fascism has a history of using left-wing rhetoric. Obviously, in the case of the National Socialists, some might say that's something separate from fascism. They are very explicit about who the people are. For the Italian fascists, it's kind of post-racial but still an Italian polity are the people, and they use left-wing rhetoric. Whenever people see any kind of mesh of left-wing rhetoric with sort of authoritarian and culturally right-wing views, they tend to press the fascism button. How useful do you think fascism is as a word today? That's the word we've been hearing for a very long time. It gets applied to people like Bannon, people like Trump, Fuentes, and even Charlie Kirk, right? So it's quite widely used. But in the case of Bannon, I wouldn't dismiss someone for calling him that even though I'm not sure if it's a useful label. Do you think this label is useful because it permeates so much of both media and academic analysis of this subject? Yeah, briefly just on the topic before we get to the question of fascism, on the topic of Bannon championing Black Americans: that becomes much more prominent in his communications after Trump's first term. It was entirely absent before then, but it starts to rise in anticipation of 2024. I think it was partially because he knew that that was a demographic that Trump could have reached into, and he was right, of course. So- but do have that as- as you're- you know, processing or evaluating my arguments about- about what- about how he looks at Americans, true Americans, non-you know, fake Americans. That- that commentary on- on the Black working class is a slightly more recent phenomenon. Okay, but to the question of- of fascism, I don't make a lot of friends in- in the circles that I move in because I do not think that it is a useful term. Part of that has to do with the actual definitions. You began, Tony, by talking about a sort of combination of a consideration of holism of the nation with with a sort of reactionary, militant, anti-liberal, anti-law and order sort of- of methodology. And some other- other- other scholars would elaborate some of that, you know, they would say that you would have to have some sort of extreme form of nationalism, you would probably need to have some concept of a domestic other, of a domestic parasite that needs to be excised in some way. Sometimes that you can have a foreign other, but many cases some of the hallmarks of historic fascism have to do with- you know, with cleansing the pool and conducting purges in various ways. And and yet there are- there are entirely other definitions of fascism. There are some that are much more ideological. Roger Griffin is the famous one who says that fascism, one of the hallmarks is palingenesis, or a palingenetic understanding of history, which is to say that fascism wants to see a rebirth of a lost potentiality from its past and that what it plans to do is to peer into the past, find something that was good but that was corrupted and stunted and breathe new life into it again and allow it to live. The problem with that is there are- there are all these definitions. It's not entirely clear to what extent any of them can really triumph over the other. It's not clear also to what extent some of them are- are diachronic, that is to say not historical, to extent to which some of them are actually just isolating general ideas, behaviors, words, programs that could be moved from one historical context to another, they're not actually bound to a time and a place. So someone could make a very good case that- that fascism is impossible outside of early 20th-century Europe. And then if you're looking at something, it might be fascist-like, but it's not going to be the actual thing. There's a lot of mud thrown into- into this question. It's very difficult to- to come out of it with a lot of certainty. So then the question becomes to me, okay, if I'm not as a scholar feeling like I'm standing on really solid ground if I call someone a fascist, is there some other benefit to it? And the best I can come up with these days myself is that if I'm lucky it's meaningless, because the word it is used quite a bit. Most often I think that the actual intention of using the term fascism is to push someone out of the- out of political consideration. Right. When you call someone a fascist, what you're saying is that our democratic system, our community, our society cannot tolerate their ideas being in power. And we have to draw a line somewhere and so we're going to identify someone who stands outside that we're going to call them a fascist, put them out. That in and of itself is- I don't think an- an inherently invalid thing to be doing and thinking, necessarily. But where it is troubling is when we are in a situation where there are a bunch of people are in power, they're I think winning cultural battles in ways that they haven't before, they're part of our immediate future. They're acting in new and different ways and by calling them fascist, what we're essentially also saying is that we know what they are. We understand this thing. I've seen it before. The reason I can tell you that we need to exclude this from our political life is because I get this thing, I understand it. And that is the exact wrong message for us today when we're speaking about someone like, let's say, Steve Bannon, who orchestrated one of the most- I don't- I shouldn't say he orchestrated, I'm not- I'm not sold on that, but at least participated in and has supported and been a contributor to a political cause that is remarkably resilient, that has defied all of the rules, is operating according to a set of laws that we did not anticipate and- and I think its opponents were not prepared for. The last thing we need to do in that situation is to prevent ourselves from not learning to give ourselves an excuse for not paying attention to it. I believe that the most important things to learn and to understand about the contemporary radical right are its novelties, not its redundancies. The novelties are what we don't want to face up to and- and also I think the pathway to- to dealing with it and to finding- finding a better future for ourselves. So that at least should give you some- some of the background into why- why I'm not convinced by the label fascism per se as a scholar, as an academic, and I'm full-on opposed to it as a sort of social critic and social commentator. I fully agree. I wrote a- an essay a while ago on my substack called "Crying Fascism," and it began immediately with how I think we are seeing a bit of a zeitgeist shift and also a little glimpse into the consequences of abusing the word fascist because, you know, I don't think it was a coincidence that- you know, it was- it was sort of like to me symbolic almost that after when Charlie Kirk died everyone was- there was so many people on the liberal and left side who were celebrating that and saying like, "Oh this is a fascist, one fascist gone." And then I mean really who's now the big person in- in the right in terms of like who's got the influence, who are people watching? It's Nick Fuentes, a guy who kind of at least like whether he's serious or not, we can get into that, openly will, you know, praise Hitler and will, you know, much more explicitly echo like white nationalist neo-Nazi talking points even if he's more recently tried to distance himself a little bit from some of that. But definitely like more than Kirk is because Kirk, you know, I remember in a debate was talking about how race was a social construct against a kind of left-wing activist, interestingly, in one of his points. And Kirk, you know, I can say he's definitely like a MAGA person, a Trump right-winger, but definitely not, you know, in the Nazi milieu and I don't really think even in the in- in any kind of like Mussolini fascist milieu at all. But yeah, it goes to show that like I don't think people care about the word fascist. That's what my- my point is. I think it not only is it meaningless, I think it in some way encouraged some- some people to be like, "Well fuck it, you know." And people like- jokingly will, you know, do the Nazi salute or whatever. But that brings us to that question that, you know, I bookmarked which is that question of seriousness. But it seems like you want to say something before we get into that. Oh no. No, I mean it- it by saying like fuck it, we mean someone says, "Okay you're going to call me a fascist? Well either I'm going to take the costume off or- or I'm just going to embrace nihilism and say say anything now." What's- I'm curious what- what you're thinking is. Well I think to some extent that if you associate ideas that are really just radically or I would say definitively right-wing ideas, because you could say that politics was dominated by a sort of liberal- like left-liberals and right-liberals for quite a while, vaguely. But that the resurgence of like truly right-wing ideas is more recent. And so equating all just right-wing ideas with fascism makes people who gravitate to those ideas think, "Well maybe this fascism isn't so bad." In some ways, you know, this is how I got into socialist politics. When I was very young, I- I used to just flock kind of to the Social Democratic party. I tended to like the Social Democratic party in Canada at the time and it was my high school teacher who saying, "Oh yeah, he's the socialist in the class." And I didn't really- the party didn't really consider itself socialist at the time. And I remember thinking, "Well, what's this socialism stuff? It doesn't seem so bad." I remember they called Bernie Sanders a communist. And I was like, "Okay, well, you know." And then they say, "Oh Bernie Sanders, he had his honeymoon in the Soviet Union." And I'm sitting thinking, "Oh, interesting. So maybe there was a reason people gravitated to that sort of thing." And it kind of for a while made me much more like communist-sympathetic, right? So I wonder if that can have that effect for people on the radical right. I see what you- I know of one case, I'm not going to be able to name the name, but not someone who is- who is not prominent. Where I think something like that happened where they were just called a fascist so many times that they actually became curious. I know of other cases where where someone- this- I'm thinking of a person from Scandinavia who said that it was merely a media strategy or communication strategy that he just decided, "I'm just going to start calling myself a fascist so that no one else can call me that." But, you know, that's slightly different from someone saying, "You know what, actually I am a fascist." Although I- I happen to think these people certainly do sympathize with someone like Mussolini more than your average person in a- in a Western democracy. So there are pieces of that, no doubt. Yeah, it's the- de-stigmatization of it, the indirect de-stigmatization of it. It's like when you abuse the word that much it- it literally loses its stigma. That's kind of how I see it. Because I think Piers Morgan getting 6 million views interviewing Nick Fuentes and trying to get him with all these questions and Fuentes was completely unfazed. I mean, he didn't back down on anything, he just stood his ground and there's a lot of people watching that who, you know, saw themselves in Fuentes, which I mean says a lot. And this brings us to the question of serious, right? Because on the one hand, some might say, okay well the people who embrace fascistic talking points will, you know, praise Hitler here and there, say he has Aura whatever, like there's obviously an ironic flavor to that. But the question is how seriously should we take people like Fuentes? And you've talked to a lot of people who are white nationalists, like in the whole- the Scandinavian right-wing crowds. So like, you know, in your experience, do you believe that people should be taken seriously at their word for their beliefs and where do you draw the line? No. It- it's hard though. I'm not sure that I can give you a- a simple theory because clearly we were just talking about Bannon. You ask me this question, should he be taken seriously and- and obviously I think he should be taken more seriously than some commentators think he should be taken seriously. Working against that are some other cases, you really do I think need to know the biography of the person in- in question. There- there were plenty of people, especially at the rank and file level, far-right organizations in Scandinavia, in Sweden in particular, who I just saw through the life that they were living, the words they were speaking, that they were really hypocritical. And they were claiming to be something that they were not. They were claiming to be white nationalists, they were claiming to be anti-immigrant activists and they were hiding, participating in and hiding a refugee in their- in their hometown from Syria, following the Syrian- the 2015 refugee crisis. So- so if you see someone doing that and then you- and you know that that's true about them and- and- and then you hear them saying, "Oh, I'm this and that and this, I'm a white nationalist." You clearly cannot take them at- at face value. The challenge is- is I don't think that you can just dismiss them either. That obviously from one perspective obviously doesn't mean that their other words and actions cannot harm anybody, they obviously- they obviously can. They are putting words out into- into a conversation that has real- oftentimes has real stakes. So you can't dismiss it in that sense, but you also can't dismiss the possibility that with the language, with the references, with the self-identifying, they aren't saying something else about themselves that's real. And that helps us if we take the case of Nick Fuentes. Do I- I do not think that he's an actual Nazi. And if he says he's an actual Nazi, I'm still not obliged to agree with him. I almost want- want him to prove it, because I doubt that he'll actually live up to that. Does- does he really actually like Hitler and think Hitler was that great? No. But is he saying something real with all that? He is saying that he is going to do away with all of the sacred cows of- of modern postwar Western liberal political discourse. And that he's going to say the things that you're not allowed to say and maybe his- his opposition to that decorum is real and should be taken for granted and that- that sounds like a- that sounds like a euphemistic account of him, that sounds like a sweet way to take- to start offering apologetics for him. It does not actually need to be- a less- a more benign way of conducting politics. Someone who is insincere in their Nazism but sincere in their opposition to something else could in fact still be as dangerous an ideologue as you thought in the first place. Lurking behind all that, I would encourage people to consider the possibility also that we might have gotten things wrong when we think that fascism was the absolute historical pinnacle of human depravity and that it's completely impossible for anyone to occupy any political position, any ideological position that's worse than fascism. Such that if you say someone's not a fascist, I'm paying them a compliment. That is not true. I think that's not true and in the case of Fuentes, you are looking at someone who has real power and- and maybe really pose a real threat to- to a lot of our political life and- and that doesn't- we don't- they don't need to be a fascist or a Nazi for that to be true. That's a good point. Fascism shouldn't be a synonym of like just being bad and- and negative. Yeah, I fully agree. I- do you see Fuentes's rise as part of this broader trajectory of like the far-right or something kind of unique and distinct? Because my quick take is I actually think Fuentes is a little bit distinct from the other right-wing figures and that I think his rise is just as connected to the rise of inceldom more broadly than just popularity of right-wing ideology. Like I think a big part of Fuentes's popularity is not just like his ideology or his, you know, white nationalism, because he seems to kind of be very even contradictory on that. It's really the inceldom, I think it's the anti-woman stuff that is more like the source of his appeal. I agree. And it's- it's sometimes not even so much his words as his look and his style and his pose. I think it's a lot of the extra-linguistic features of him that- that are playing in there. Yeah, if we- if we assume that the contemporary right is composed to some large extent by the grievances of young white men, certainly someone like Nick Fuentes is going to figure into that in ways more than just the plain and the obvious. And- and he is voicing frustration with mainstream society, he's voicing frustration with mainstream sexuality.
