He's voicing frustration with the codes and with the direction of history that a lot of young white men feel is decidedly against them and their future. And they're rebelling against it in all ways possible and making oneself a social pariah according to polite society is a great way to do that. Now another component of what makes Fuentes very different obviously is that he represents this kind of new- I mean, he is in some sense separate from it, but I would say there is like this new part of the American right, at least in so far as what I've been paying attention to, that is breaking from MAGA. In some very crucial ways, in some cases totally breaking from MAGA. I would say at this point Fuentes has completely broken from MAGA. Tucker Carlson who is, you know, more moderate compared to Fuentes, at least in terms of the rhetoric, he's more or less I would say like broken with MAGA although he tried to be closer to it certainly. There seems to be like a bloc that is- an anti- or a post-MAGA right I guess you could say. And a lot of it seems to stem from Israel really like- about Israel. That's what it seems like, but maybe even bigger than that. That like Trump isn't really committed to let's say like the- the stuff like the deportations because when Trump obviously did his anti-migrant discourses there's different audiences there, like there's some people who might genuinely care about the jobs and the- the safety component, and then there's the whole demographics debate. And the people really who care about the demographics debate are obviously very not satisfied with what he's done now and- with his immigration policy. So there seems to be like a post-Trump right and I'm curious where you see that going and- you know to what extent like maybe- maybe maybe this post-Trump right was always there and that like the only thing that held an illusion of the right altogether was the demagoguery of- of Trump himself. I think yes. There's a post-Trump right. The charisma, I think to use a little bit more of a dispassionate term, of Trump absolutely brought together a coalition that is meant to break apart in- in his movement. But I think we can learn something from looking at the causes you name, Israel. And for certainly for a portion of the right, opposition to Israel is a definitive question and it's able to express itself in ways today that it couldn't in the past and that has created new- new fissures. Another- we could fold that into something else, not so much for the people who have a specific opposition to Israel but for those who are opposed to, let's say, foreign partnerships and military intervention, which is I think a larger- a larger part of the MAGA base. For those who expected that the America First agenda that Trump had been elected on, they- they expected that that was going to define his foreign policy, they- they are left out and they're alienated by his actions. Israel is just an example of that, but it's not the only one. And- so if we think in those terms, what we're seeing- what we would see instead is not- not Israel as the defining problem, but instead ideology. Instead the- the idea that Trump and Trump's movement was actually based on principles and ideas as opposed to a personality. And for those who were really attached to the principles and ideas, that's- they're the ones who I think are having the trouble right now. Now there's- there's a lot that could be said about this, Tony. I happen to think if that's correct and that's what's going on, I happen to think this is entirely predictable. And I think that to understand this, we need to be speaking less about fascism or the far-right or reactionary politics and more about the other word that we haven't- we haven't talked about that much up into this point, which is populism. Because there are reasons why a populist movement is going to end up being focused at once on ideas and principles and on a person who may or may not follow those ideas and principles. And- and if there's some incoherence there you're going to have factions break out infighting and- and maybe even the dissolution of the movement. Okay. All right. So you mentioned that your next book is on the divisions on the right and the future of the right. I'm very curious to, you know, get your analysis and also prediction as to what is potentially going to happen after Trump, because I think that is really one of the most interesting developments that could happen. Is that Trump's aging, what is MAGA without Trump? If you enjoyed this podcast episode, then you can listen to the second half of it on Patreon in the Backroom. In fact, for every episode I do publicly, I have another one I do for patrons only. It's not just the second half of a conversation, it's usually set up as a separate episode of its own that you can listen to from front to back without having to watch the public one, but it generally functions as a follow-up with the guest who I had on the public episode. It's one of the benefits that patrons get by supporting me, so it's really patrons that pretty much keep this whole thing going. And you get a lot in return. At this point, there is well over a hundred exclusive episodes on Patreon, so by becoming a patron you can get access to all of them. If you want to listen to many of the exclusive episodes, I recommend becoming a patron for a year. It's actually cheaper, you get like 10 to 20% off by paying for the year instead of by month. And you also get access to the private Discord server if you want to chat with me and other open-minded people in the 1Dime community. So what are you waiting for? See you in the backroom. I am sick and tired of revolutionaries or protest movements which focus on those ecstatic moments. Oh my God, one million people on Syntagma Square, on Tahrir, in Istanbul, and so on. We were also so enthusiastic there and so on and so on. I am not impressed by that at all. I think this is even relatively easy to do. The true test of a revolution or radical social change is: how do ordinary people experience the change when the ecstatic moment is over, when things return to normal? That's the most difficult task. When nothing happens, when just everyday life is here, how is this everyday life different from the previous everyday life?

Global Markets, World Uncertainties and the $2 Trillion Fund | Jens Stoltenberg & David Solomon

Norges Bank Investment Management(108.0K)·27:49·5.8K views·2026-05-08
Nicolai TangenCEO, NBIM Jens StoltenbergFormer Secretary General, NATO David SolomonCEO, Goldman Sachs
TL;DRWhile geopolitical instability in the Middle East and structural stagnation in Europe present significant short-term risks, long-term economic optimism remains high due to the transformative productivity potential of AI and the continued compounding power of global equity markets.

TL;DR

While geopolitical instability in the Middle East and structural stagnation in Europe present significant short-term risks, long-term economic optimism remains high due to the transformative productivity potential of AI and the continued compounding power of global equity markets.

Geopolitics and Market Volatility

  • Middle East Conflict: Jens Stoltenberg characterized the current situation as "serious and dangerous," noting that while the primary tragedy is human suffering, the economic fallout—reduced energy supplies, higher prices, and inflationary pressure—is inevitable.
  • Market Disconnection: David Solomon expressed surprise that equity markets have not "derisked" more in response to geopolitical tension.
      • Underpriced Risks: While rate and commodity markets have reacted, equity risk premia remain low. Solomon suggests the market is discounting the high probability of a "longer conflict" that could leave "sand in the gears" of global growth.
  • Private Credit Resilience: Despite concerns about higher rates, Solomon argues that the private credit asset class (approx. $1.7 trillion) is manageable.
      • Historical Context: During the Financial Crisis, leveraged lending saw a 10% default rate but a 50% recovery rate; with current coupons at 9-10%, the net loss remains sustainable for diversified portfolios.

The Growth Gap: Europe vs. the United States

  • Shifting Allocations: The Norwegian Sovereign Wealth Fund has halved its European exposure (from 42% to 21%) over the last decade while increasing US exposure to 55%, driven largely by the divergence in earnings growth.
  • Structural Impediments: Solomon identified a widening gap between US trend growth (~2%) and European trend growth (~0.7%).
      • Fragmentation: Europe functions as 27 independent nations rather than a single economic bloc, inhibiting the creation of "European champions" in sectors like banking.
      • Bureaucracy: Excessive regulation and a failure to implement reforms (such as those suggested in the Draghi report) act as headwinds.
  • Risk Culture: Solomon argued that a "risk-taking culture" is notably absent in Europe compared to the US. He noted that the "risk-taking genes" largely migrated to America, leaving Europe with a more cautious, status-quo-oriented population.
  • Investment Strategy: Stoltenberg defended the fund’s "conservative" index-based approach, owning roughly 1.5% of 7,200 companies globally as the most effective risk management tool for a democratic nation.

AI and the Productivity Revolution

  • Enterprise Transformation: Solomon views AI as a "powerful technology acceleration" that allows firms to move beyond simple automation to "remaking operating processes" from scratch.
      • Capacity for Growth: Productivity gains from AI allow enterprises to reinvest saved capital into growth areas where they were previously resource-constrained.
  • The Ford Analogy: Solomon compared the AI revolution to Henry Ford’s implementation of the assembly line, which allowed for a shift from a six-day to a five-day work week without losing productivity.
  • Norway’s Digital Exception: Stoltenberg noted that while Europe lags generally, Norway maintains high productivity per hour worked due to early and aggressive adoption of digital solutions in both the public and private sectors.

The China Outlook

  • Shifting Predictions: Solomon noted that in 2014, economists predicted China would be the world's largest economy by now. While China hit its GDP targets, the US outperformed expectations so significantly that China’s path to the top spot is no longer "cast in stone."
  • Capital Allocation: Solomon expressed skepticism that a centrally controlled economy can compete indefinitely with a market-driven one.
      • Interdependence: Despite friction, Solomon emphasized that "we need China, and China needs us," suggesting a dynamic of "gives and gets" rather than total decoupling.

Notable Quotes

"In 1996, [the Sovereign Wealth Fund] was zero. Now it is two trillion... 27% of every krone we spend on the Norwegian budget is financed by the return from the fund." — Jens Stoltenberg
"A lot of the risk-taking genes left with the Mayflower. The risk-takers per definition said, 'Let’s take some risks, let’s go to America.' The people who are left in Europe are the people who don’t want to change." — David Solomon
"Find me a 25-year period of time where the world was not better 25 years in the future from where it was 25 years in the past. You can’t find one for the last thousands of years." — David Solomon
Okay, well, big welcome, big welcome, David and Jens. It's just incredible to have you both here. Because Stoltenberg, you invented the spending rule so that we don't spend, so that the politicians don't spend the whole fund, only 3% a year. Despite that, it's now accounting for more than a quarter of the Norwegian state budget. So, really great to have you here. And David Solomon, a very important person for the fund because you help us move the fund around and we interface with you in so many different ways. You help us trade equities. And actually, Stoltenberg made a trade earlier today, which went to Goldman Sachs. They accepted it. It went quite well, I think. It was a very small one, they said. Quite. Anywhere. They help us trade the bonds, the corporate credit. They are doing a lot of the IPOs and we had a little chat here beforehand. Basically, David says that we are in all the IPOs because that's the way to make sure that it is a successful IPO. They have the deepest experience of any firm. So we have a great relationship with you on the ECM side. We are big shareholders in Goldman Sachs. That's been a pretty good deal. So, absolutely. So, it's a very, very deep relationship. So, thanks for that. Now, first question is going to Stoltenberg. Just, you've been a politician, trying to... I'm still a politician. I know. But first you were a politician. Then you went to NATO, trying to keep all these various countries working together. Now you are back in a government with a lot of different parties. What's more difficult? Is it to kind of sort out the parties or the countries? Well, I know that the correct answer is to say that it's more difficult to manage the world than to manage a minority government in Norway, but to be honest, I'm a bit in doubt. Because the reality is that when you head an organization like NATO, there are some rules, some kind of stability in at least the institution. But in politics, it's much more, what shall I say, unpredictable from day to day. But before I say anything more about that, let me just thank you, Nikolai, for what you do, for leading the Sovereign Wealth Fund in such an excellent way, and thank all the people in this room. Because what you do is of great importance, of vital importance for Norway. And as Nikolai alluded to, now it's 27% of every krone we spend on the Norwegian budget is financed by the return from the Sovereign Wealth Fund. I remember back in 1996, it was zero. And it has gone from zero to now two trillion. And half of it, actually a bit more than half of it, is invested in the United States. And again, most people move forward in their life. I have started to move backwards. Because I was Minister of Finance 30 years ago, and it's extremely great to be back and not least work again with the pension fund. And one of the most controversial decisions we made back in '97, in the year after we had the first installment, was actually to decide that we should not only invest in fixed income, which was the plan, but also start to invest in equity. And now the pension fund has earned more money from the equity investments than from oil and gas. So I thank you all, and I thank Goldman Sachs for everything you have given me, and we spend it with, what shall I say, great joy every day in Norway. Thank you. David, you meet more CEOs globally than pretty much any other person. What's on the CEOs' minds these days? Sure. First of all, thank you for having me. And deeply appreciative for our partnership, which is long-standing and very significant for Goldman Sachs, and for your support and sponsorship. It's an interesting time. I, you know, we had earnings today and on our earnings call, a lot of the focus... we had a really good quarter. We had the second-best revenue the firm's ever had. We had the second-best earnings the firm's ever had, the second-best EPS the firm's ever had. By the way, the stock's down $30 on that. But it's down, I think, because there's an expectation that at the moment with what's going on geopolitically and what's going on in the Middle East, the forward doesn't look quite the same as what the last three months looked. And I tried on the call to in a very balanced way say of course, I don't know what's coming, nobody knows what's coming, but I think for the most part when you think about the things that drive our business, they're going on despite what's going on geopolitically in the Middle East. Of course, if oil prices remain high and elevated for a period of time, it will filter into the economy more and will have an impact and cause inflationary pressures and growth impacts. But CEOs broadly, and particularly here in the United States, feel like there's an opportunity to do things to drive scale because the regulatory environment is allowing scale consolidation. And there is so much change coming with the acceleration of technology that CEOs are very, very front-footed and very, very aggressive and they're not letting the moment distract them from what they want to accomplish in the next three to five years. And that's very good for our business. And I actually think that's going to continue through '26 and '27 even if we have in the short term some sand in the gears from what's going on in the Middle East. And so CEOs are quite forward-leaning at the moment and really realize that technology is just accelerating the pace of change and they know that if they spend time being cautious, they're not really investing in participating in that acceleration and they're not really gaining scale, that they risk weakening their competitive position. And they don't want that to happen and so they're kind of blocking out a bunch of the noise. Yeah. I thought we should do just one interesting thing and just touch on the Middle East, because we have two incredible angles here. One, of course, from the security side, but then on the financial side too. So Jens first, just what are you, how do you read the situation in the Middle East just now? Well, I read it as a very serious and dangerous situation. And of course the war which is going on there is first and foremost about people who are killed, homes which are destroyed and all the human suffering. But as we have seen so many times before, wars have also economic consequences. And we have seen the increase or the reduced supply of energy, the increase in energy prices, and of course also that many central banks and others also now predict higher general inflation and actually also some lower growth. And that impacts the global economy and it impacts also the Norwegian economy. Of course, how serious that will be depends on how long this war will last and for how long time there will be reduced supplies of oil, gas and other commodities from the Middle East. As a politician but also as an economist by... I worked for many years ago as an economist. I'm a bit surprised that the markets have not reacted more. And David, why is that? Why haven't they reacted more? I would share the perspective that I'm surprised that equity markets haven't derisked more. You know, I do think markets broadly, when you think about rate markets and the level of volatility increase in rate markets, that's been pretty significant over the course of the last month. You know, obviously commodity markets have reacted. But equity risk premia has not really reacted. And I don't have a good answer. I think what the market is telling you is the market is telling you that it thinks there'll be a resolution to status quo. I think most of us that are observers, either as politicians or as business people that are really thinking about the situation, think the risk of this being a longer conflict that leaves sand in the gears of economic activity for a more extended period of time is higher than what the market's discounting at the moment. And so I don't have a crystal ball. The one thing I know is I don't know. But I do know that in the distribution of outcomes, the outcome that this takes a lot longer to resolve and it leaves sand in the gears that slow economic growth or put more inflation into the economy is not an insignificant small little tail. It's actually a reasonable possibility. And if it continues, you will see equity prices repricing over time. Do you think it could amplify the certain signs we are seeing in private credit through higher rates, more difficult environment? I think when you're talking about private credit, you really have to recognize a handful of things. But one of the things I'd just say is credit broadly is an asset class and private credit is a small part of the overall asset class of credit broadly. It's direct lending, which is what's getting all the attention, is 1.6, 1.7 trillion dollars of assets, about 230 billion of which is kind of retail-focused. If we have an economic slowdown, losses will increase. But I think, and I said this on our earnings call this morning, I think the thing that people aren't putting in perspective, what does a cycle look like? Well, if you take a very difficult cycle, the financial crisis, all leveraged lending through the financial crisis, the loss rate was, the default rate was about 10% and the recovery rate was 50%, which means the net loss rate was about 5 to 6%. And the coupons are 9 to 10%. So you have to keep that in perspective, both on the size of the asset class and the cycle. It doesn't have to be a systemic thing, probably won't for that asset class. And even though losses go up because we have an economic slowdown, it doesn't mean through the life of the portfolio that the performance hasn't been reasonable. If you're collecting 10%, we've had a long cycle, if you collect 10% for 15 years and then you go through a cycle where you have a 5 or 6% loss, your returns are actually going to be okay. Over the last 10 years in this fund, the investments in Europe have gone from 42% to 21%. So it's halved. Those in the US have gone from 37 to 55. And it's to a very large extent driven by difference in earnings growth. Jens, any reflection on just the relative power of Europe and the US? Well, it reflects a reality that the economy in the US has grown much more than the economy and markets in Europe. And since we are indexed, or at least mainly an index fund, of course that is reflected in the way the Norwegian Pension Fund is invested. I think for a politician who is responsible for setting those indexes, the big issue is should we change? Should we do something different? And we actually asked some experts to evaluate that. And the main conclusion from that group is actually that we should not change. And that the main idea we developed and agreed back in 1997, that we should follow the markets, we should spread across the world in almost all listed companies. As you know, we own roughly 1.5% of almost all listed companies, of more than 7,200 companies in total. That is the best way for a country, a democratic country to manage risk. I say this also partly because I remember again when we made the decision that I had meetings with the Qatari investment fund and with Kuwait investment office and so on, and they had a totally different approach. We wanted to learn from them but we decided to do the opposite, to be very conservative, to spread the risks. And also tell the Norwegian voters and the media and the journalists when they ask about obvious risks, for instance to be so big in the United States, is that there's no way to hide 20,000 billion Norwegian kroner or two trillion. The alternative was to keep the value, the national wealth in oil and gas in the ground. That would have been even more risky. So this long answer has one conclusion and that is that we strongly believe from the Norwegian government that we should continue to be diversified, small in many companies, and then we are extremely grateful for what you do also with the active management, but that's within limits that doesn't really change that main approach. And David, any reflection on the differential between Europe and, not necessarily share price performance but more the environment, the operating environment for companies? What are companies telling you about investing in Europe? Sure. And the thing I'd highlight because you're talking about it as an indexation against earnings growth, 10 years from now it's going to continue because Europe is 0.7 trend growth and the US is 2% trend growth. And so what you're seeing and what you've seen, what's created this widening differential is one's growing meaningfully faster than the other and that compounds. It's going to keep compounding unless Europe changes the way it operates, which it doesn't seem that Europe has the collective ability to materially change the way it operates to get that growth level up to a different place. I mean, you just have to step back and look at the size and the scale of Europe and its growth rate against the size and the scale of the US and its growth rate and it looks like that's going to continue to compound unless something were to change that doesn't seem likely at the moment. What could they change? Well, I think the thing that I've talked publicly about is that the theory of the European Union was to harness the collective economic power of all the European nations. But that's not happened. You know, we can talk about the great experiment of the European Union and there have been benefits, obviously, but the real benefit was to harness the economic might of 450 million people and get people to work collectively. But what we really have is 27 nations operating relatively independently, a big bureaucracy that sits in the middle that actually inhibits growth and investment. And look, I think Mario Draghi's paper have a lot of good ideas but only about 11% of them have been implemented. So one thing you can do is implement 50% of them. But given the way Europe operates, I think the chance of that is low. And that will continue to be a headwind for European growth and will continue to shift the weighting of where a fund like yours, if you're indexing against earnings around the world, when you look forward 10 years, unless there's a shift in that, that will widen. I totally agree. I think it's in one way quite obvious what Europe should do and that is to actually create a real single market. In the 90s, they were quite successful to establish the internal market, to establish a common currency. I think many people believed that was not possible, they did it, and it triggered some growth. Then it has slowed down and just to be able to create a capital union, savings union, and to really have common regulation would be a huge step forward, would help them a lot. And when you meet political leaders in Europe, they all see it. But so far they've not been able to really implement that. But at some stage, I really hope and believe they will do it. Then remember, the European Union, it's 450 million people. But if you look at Europe, for instance, defined by NATO allies, which are European countries but not members of the EU, like Norway, and add them to the 450, then we have 600 million people. So it's a huge economy and a lot of people and a great potential. But then we need to overcome the fragmentation and all the national regulations which still exist in Europe. Two things that I think need to happen to do that. One, you've got to create European champions. So for example, you need a couple of European champion financial institutions that can compete with the leading financial institutions in the US. But to do that, you can't be worried about whether they're located in France or Germany or somewhere else. They just have to be located in Europe. It has to be in the collective interest of Europe to create banking consolidation across borders so that you have a scaled institution that has the ability to compete globally. You have to have centralized capital markets, not fragmented capital markets, so you can have capital formation. The other thing that's really lacking in Europe broadly speaking, present company excluded, is there is not a risk-taking culture in Europe. One of the great things about the United States is people want to take risk, and taking risk spurs investment, which drives innovation and drives economic growth. A lot of the risk-taking genes left with the Mayflower, you know. And so, because the risk-takers per definition is just 'Hey, let's take some risks, let's go to America.' And so the people who are left in Europe are the people who don't want to change, we want to be at home, you know, with mummy. So that's a bit different. Like you and me. Well, at least I studied here. Okay, neither. Okay. David, one of the reasons for the trend growth, the difference in trend growth is technology. And of course, you're seeing it in, you know, the large concentration in stock markets and so on. How does AI change the way you work at Goldman's? How we're working at Goldman, how we're working... I mean, I'm super excited about AI technology and what it's going to do over the next decade or two to accelerate growth and productivity in the world. This is a powerful technology acceleration that the best enterprises will figure out how to use it to really remake operating processes in their business so that they have more capacity to invest in growth in their business. And so at Goldman Sachs, there are two ways that we think about this. The first is our business because it's a professional services business, has a lot of smart people. And so one of the things we obviously do is get these tools, these technology tools into the hands of smart people so the smart people can find ways to do more, do things more productively. That's easy, we've done that for ages with technology. But the more powerful thing here is that this technology is allowing big enterprises to remake operating processes, really fundamentally take out a white sheet of paper and say the way we've always done this, this particular process, we can do it differently. The result of that is enormous efficiency and productivity gains. And you know, the benefit of that is not just that you can bring your cost down, the benefit for most enterprises is it gives you more capacity to invest in places where you want to invest to grow that you've been constrained in terms of how much you can invest. And so we're extremely focused on that combination of re-underwriting certain operating processes to create efficiency and create more capacity to invest in growth in places where we feel constrained. And most CEOs that I talk to are really focused around that thought process. A paradox I know about Norway on this is that when you look at Europe, they have not invested as much in digital solutions and AI as the United States. But Norway is an exception for some reason. It's quite high. And I think that demonstrates that we don't have the big tech companies but we are able to use, to implement the solutions they are providing at a higher scale than most other European countries. And for again for a politician, the issue is not only to understand the potential of AI, which is obviously great, but how can we as politicians create a framework so actually the society is able to take benefit and to use that technology. And I think that's at least a lesson from Norway is that high level education helps and also high level digital solutions, for instance in the public sector. I lived for some 10 years in Belgium and it's a nightmare when it comes to, you know, basic issues like tax forms or all the financial transactions compared to in Norway where you do it on your cell phone, most of those transactions. And the fact that we like to spend some quality time at our cabins in the mountains. Yeah, that's not wrong in itself. Should be a driver for using AI. That's correct. But I just saw an overview in the Economist, they compared productivity and Norway again, they're quite high when it comes to overall productivity, but in particular when it comes to productivity per hour worked. So when we don't work so much, but we are very productive when we work. The joking aside on hours worked, just a lens for people to think about when people talk about this. You know, when you think through history, you have technology change and it destroys jobs and it changes jobs and things shift. If you go back 115 years when Henry Ford was starting to mass produce cars, people think about Henry Ford as being famous for creating the assembly line to build cars. But another thing that Henry Ford was famous for is he was the first CEO of a big US company to say the company was going to go to a five-day work week from a six-day work week. And he thought because the assembly line was increasing productivity, he could in a five-day work week with that assembly line get as much productivity or more than he was getting in a six-day work week and it would be better for his employees. It's a lens to look at in the world we live in today. We don't have to be locked in a five-day work week. It doesn't mean that everybody works hard, but productivity can go up in many ways and things that look so certain today might not look so certain 10 years from now with the productivity leverage that we have. And it's just interesting to go back in history and think about the shift from 95% of jobs coming from farming to an industrial revolution to now a technological revolution, and there'll be things that mirror in different ways. But we are increasing productivity massively, and that doesn't mean that it doesn't come from the same metrics that we've always looked at. But we are now up against China where they don't really talk about four-day week, it's more like six-day week, right? And they are applying AI even more than we do, even though on the models they may be slightly behind, the application is very strong. Earlier today I did a podcast with the CEO of Pfizer. He thinks that within one to two years they'll be ahead of us also in cancer medicine. So just how do you see the Chinese competition when you talk to your clients? China's a very, very significant economy and they might be ahead of us in certain things. But I think anyone that's so certain as to how this plays out should just look at history as a lens because it's not so simple. Jim O'Neill was a very, very famous Goldman Sachs economist. He's the Goldman Sachs economist who coined the phrase BRICs. In 2014, Jim O'Neill predicted that China would be the largest economy in the world and in 2027 it would be a 21 trillion dollar economy. Well, he got the 21 trillion, right? It looks like China's going to be a 22 trillion dollar economy in 2027. But what he got massively wrong was how the US would grow competitively and how the US's position would change. And so if we were sitting here in 2018, all three of us would agree China would be the largest economy in the world sometime in this decade. Now we all sit here and everyone would agree there's no way China will be the largest economy anytime that we can see in the future. So the world is competitive. They're going to be a very large economy. We have to figure out how to work with and integrate with China. There's a lot of friction there. But it's not cast in stone. And I would just say that in the history of the world, it is very, very hard to compete in a globally competitive world when all the capital allocation is centrally controlled. That doesn't mean that this time it won't work. But I think it'll be very dynamic. I think we need China, I think China needs us. There'll be places where they run ahead, there'll be places where we run ahead. But you know, we have to, if we stick to the knitting of really driving growth and freedom and combining those two things, we'll do just fine. But there'll be gives and gets. Absolutely. Before we open up for questions, last thing, David, what advice would you give to the Norwegian Minister of Finance when it comes to winning elections? No, when it comes to, when it comes to the Sovereign Wealth Fund. Well, one thing for sure, I am not a politician. I mean, I've got such enormous admiration for what you've done. Because I think that you intuitively or academically or otherwise really understood the power of believing in growth in the world and of compounding. And I'm still a big believer. You know, the world's not perfect, things are going to be wrong. But I'd bet you a lot that if we were sitting here 25 years from now, the world will continue to be better. And if the world continues to be better, that means it's growing. And if it's growing and you're investing and you're participating, you're compounding. Okay. We all know that 7% for 10 years, you double your resources. And so I don't think I need to give you advice. I think you get it. The world's going to grow, you're going to participate in a very powerful way, and you've got to stick to keeping that participation where the growth is in the world. And if you're aligned with the growth in earnings in the world, you're going to do just fine. I really believe that. And I'm a huge optimist. You've got to step out of the moment, you know, find me a 25-year period of time where the world was not better 25 years in the future from where it was 25 years in the past. You can't find one for the last thousands of years.

President Trump Delivers Remarks in the Rose Garden

C-SPAN(1.8M)·20:05·1.8K views·2026-05-08
Donald TrumpPresident, United States
TL;DRPresident Trump utilized a Rose Garden Mother’s Day event to honor "Angel Moms" and "Gold Star Moms," while detailing administration achievements in border security, drug interdiction, and new economic incentives for American families.
Border Enforcement Claims: The...

TL;DR

President Trump utilized a Rose Garden Mother’s Day event to honor "Angel Moms" and "Gold Star Moms," while detailing administration achievements in border security, drug interdiction, and new economic incentives for American families.

Border Security and Recognition of "Angel Moms"

  • Border Enforcement Claims: The President asserted that the administration has transformed the southern border from the most dangerous in history to the strongest, claiming "zero" illegal crossings over the last 11 months and the removal of thousands of criminal aliens.
  • Angel Moms Recognition: Trump specifically honored "Angel Moms"—mothers who lost children to crimes committed by undocumented immigrants—praising their resilience in the face of "open border policies."
      • Named Honorees: Mary Ann Mendoza, Tammy Nobles, and Jacqueline Medina were highlighted for their advocacy and personal sacrifice.
  • Criticism of Previous Policies: The President characterized past border management as "stupid borders" run by "stupid people," specifically citing the influx of criminals and the "emptying of prisons" from foreign countries like the Congo.

Anti-Narcotics and Drug Pricing Initiatives

  • Drug Interdiction Statistics: Significant progress was reported in stopping the flow of illegal substances into the U.S.
      • Fentanyl: The President claimed a 59% decrease in the flow of deadly fentanyl across the border.
      • Maritime Interdiction: Reported a 97% reduction in drugs entering the country by sea.
  • Pharmaceutical Cost Reductions: Trump touted the "most favored nations" policy, which he claimed has reduced prescription drug prices by 50% to 90% by ensuring the U.S. pays the lowest price available globally.
  • Legislative Action: Mentioned the signing of the HALT Fentanyl Act as a cornerstone of the mission to end the drug epidemic.

Family Support and Economic Policy

  • Financial Incentives for Parents: The briefing highlighted several initiatives aimed at lowering the cost of raising children:
      • Child Tax Credit: Expanded to $2,200 per child.
      • Newborn Savings: Announced a program to give every American newborn $1,000 automatically to invest for their future.
      • Trump Accounts: Tax-advantaged savings accounts designed to help families set aside money for children.
  • Education and Healthcare:
      • School Choice: Claimed the delivery of the largest expansion of school choice in American history.
      • IVF and Living Costs: Pledged to lower the price of IVF and cut regulatory barriers to make starting a family more affordable.
  • Private Sector Partnership: Thanked the Dell family for contributing over $6.2 billion toward family-centric initiatives.

National Security and Gold Star Tributes

  • Gold Star Families: The President honored mothers of fallen service members, including Janice Chance, mother of Marine Captain Jesse Melton, who died in Afghanistan in 2008.
  • Abbey Gate Families: Specific recognition was given to mothers who lost children during the 2021 withdrawal from Afghanistan at Abbey Gate, which Trump attributed to the "gross incompetence" of the Biden administration.
  • Key Administration Women: Trump recognized Brooke Rollins (Secretary of Agriculture) and Susie Wiles (White House Chief of Staff) for their leadership roles.

White House Infrastructure: The Rose Garden Renovation

  • Functional Upgrades: Trump detailed the replacement of the Rose Garden’s grass with white stone to prevent drainage issues and "muddy shoes" during events, noting the stone’s ability to reflect sunlight and keep the area cool.
  • The Presidential Walk of Fame: The addition of new black granite features and portraits of U.S. presidents from George Washington to the current administration was highlighted as a well-received improvement to the grounds.

***

Notable Quotes

"In their honor we’ve rapidly turned the worst and most dangerous border in the history of our country into the strongest border in American history. We have no people come in during the last 11 months—we have zero, zero."

— President Donald Trump

"We had the highest drug prices in the world by far. Now we have the lowest drug prices in the world. Can you imagine? So we cut them by 50, 60, 70 percent... the fake news doesn't want to write about it, but that's why I talk about it."

— President Donald Trump

"America’s moms are raising the future of our country. You have the most important job there is in America or anyplace else, and you’re doing an incredible job."

— President Donald Trump

And, I hope you like what we’re doing. We’re—did everybody get to see the Presidential Walk of Fame? Right? And you have some of your favorite presidents there, some of your least favorite presidents. Some were good, some were great, some were terrible, but that’s the way life goes, right? And you notice the roses. This is a rose garden and we added a lot of roses, I will tell you. This was a rose garden that didn’t have too many roses and if you look now we, we cornered the market on roses. And you deserve them. So thank you for being with us in this beautiful spring, this gorgeous spring day at the very famous Rose Garden. It’s been famous for a long time. They let it get a little bit out of control but, now it’s brought back to the highest level. And you know where you’re standing, or sitting right now, is white stone and that was grass. The problem with it is you couldn’t walk on it cause this was built on a wetland and the grass was always soaking wet. It didn’t matter, it was always soaking wet, absolutely. And so, when Brooke Rollins would get up and start making a magnificent speech and she’d be standing in mud and she’d say, "I ruined my shoes," her speeches would never be as good as they could have been cause she was always concerned about ruining her shoes. But people would come here, women would come with the high heels, and it wasn’t a pretty picture. So after about 150 years, we gave up on the grass. We said, "Let’s build it so we can use it." And now we have lunches here, we have dinners here, and it’s nice and it’s cool. The white reflects the sun so you don’t—although it does go in my eye right now I notice. I didn’t, I didn’t anticipate that, but, it’s, it’s a great place. It’s a great place and we do news conferences, we do everything. And right up here you probably noticed the new black granite and it’s, it’s been so well received. People can’t even believe it cause the stones were all broken and really in bad disrepair and it’s, it’s really become great. And we added the pictures of the presidents from George Washington to a guy named Trump. People really, they’ve loved it. We call it the Presidential Wall of Fame or Walk of Fame. We say you could call it either. Do you like Walk or Wall? Ready? Presidential Walk of Fame. Presidential Wall of Fame. Same. It’s the same. I would call that, I would call that a tie. But I want to thank you all for being here. It’s amazing, the weekend we celebrate Mother’s Day honoring the women who raised us and all of the wonderful mothers in our lives. For millions of American families, Sunday will be filled with love and gratitude and joy. Great, I had a great mother by the way. And today we’re honored to be joined by many strong and truly heroic moms who have stood up for their children both in life and in a very profound loss. Very, very terrible, in many just terrible loss many cases. And we have a lot of the greatest people on earth, Angel Moms, that are with us today. They’re great people. They’ve been through so much and I got to know so many of you during my first, during my first days of running. I got to know you and I got to love you. But we want to thank you for being here Mother’s Day. It’s one of the great days. I always think so fondly of my mother, I had a wonderful mother. Among those with us this afternoon are a number of the Angel Moms who saw their precious children stolen from them by the open border policies of the radical left. Open borders—I call them stupid borders, not open. I call them stupid borders by stupid people that put them there. And the migrant criminals that let, they let them pour into our country and, bad things happened. Bad things happened. 25 million people came into our country. Many of them were criminals, many of them were murderers. They allowed them in with their open border policy of stupidity. These incredibly brave and resilient women have turned terrible tragedy into unyielding resolve, becoming advocates so that their mothers do not have to endure the same heartbreak that they’ve felt all their lives, they’ve felt for so long. They’ve raised the most beautiful child and it’s taken and they’ve spent so many years. In many cases, I see some of you, for I’ve known you for years and others are unfortunately new members of that club. And I say unfortunately, it’s a sad thing. Should have never happened, should have never happened. In their honor we’ve rapidly turned the worst and most dangerous border in the history of our country into the strongest border in American history. We have no people come in during the last, last 11 months we have zero, zero. Nobody even tries to come up. You know, you used to read about the thousands of people that would pour in and they’d just come up in caravans. I came, I think it was my name I came up with, it looked like a caravan. 25, 30,000 people pouring up, they walk through Mexico they come from all different locations, come from all over the world actually. But they came in through our southern border and they’d come up and, just let them walk in. They’d walk in with nobody had any idea who they were. They were murderers. 11,888 murderers they were allowed to come into our country. We’ve gotten a lot of them out. Many of them murdered more than one person. What a shame, what a disgusting shame that people can even think that this is okay is pretty incredible. But for the past 12 months we’ve had that’s been our number and these numbers are done by people that are—the numbers when I say zero, I mean, I actually mean zero. It’s hard to believe actually, but they’re done by people that are, they tend to be somewhat on the left so they’re not going to give us a fair shake. And they say that, you know, people don’t come anymore because they know they’re not going to get through. So when they know they’re not going to get through they just don’t come and just on that we save a lot of lives. Because some of that journey up from Honduras and El Salvador and lots of other countries, or from the Congo where they drop them there and they walk up and they come through our country. They emptied the prisons of the Congo into the area of the southern border and then told them to just walk in because stupid Americans are going to accept you beautifully. And these were hard, mean, vicious criminals they allowed into our country. How anybody can vote for these people is hard to believe, okay, it’s hard for believe. But to a friend of mine, Mary Ann Mendoza. Mary Ann where is Mary Ann, where are you Mary Ann? I got to know you right from the beginning, right? And I, I won’t ask her that but I, I would often ask is it easier with time because time has gone by Mary Ann, no? It’s not. Sadly it’s not. You know you always hear with time and time and but a lot of people say it’s not, it’s not easier. But, you're special woman, I’ve gotten to be a friend of yours. You’re special woman. What you have to go through is disgraceful. Tammy Nobles, Tammy are you here, Tammy Nobles? Right. Does time help Tammy? No. It’s a joke. No. It’s a joke. It used to be, you know time heals all wounds, right? But it doesn’t, doesn’t when you love somebody that much it doesn’t, doesn’t help like it should. Jacqueline Medina, a great person, where is Jacqueline? Jacqueline, where are you? Thank you darling. Thank you. And so many others that I want to thank you for coming and, I don’t think you’ve been treated properly. The, it’s hard to believe but the Angel Moms have not been treated properly but this president treats you properly. I can’t speak for others but this one treats you properly. And we love you all and, all I can say is, our hearts are out to you on Mother’s Day and you have a good Mother’s Day. Go and have a good Mother’s Day. In securing our border we’ve also made tremendous strides in stopping the flow of illegal drugs into our country. The flood of deadly fentanyl across our border is down by 59 percent and the drugs coming in by sea are down by 97 percent. 97 percent and we’re trying to figure out who the 3 percent are because they are the bravest people on earth. You see how we stop them by sea. You know, for years they’d go and catch the boat, bring them to court, they’d go in, they’d go out. By the time the evening they’d be back in another boat bringing drugs in. So we gave them warning and we don’t do that anymore; we do it much more, much more efficiently. But, think of that. Down by, by sea, a lot of them came in, about 50 percent it’s down 97. Now we’ve started a very strong—the sea was more difficult and now we’ve started land. And it’s—you’ll see it coming down too. It's come down a lot but you’ll see it coming down and they come through Mexico for the most part. We have a, a problem because the cartels rule Mexico and, nobody else. The cartels, they just rule it and, you know we lose 200,000 people a year through this poison that pours into our country. So we have the sea done, meaning the ocean, waterways are pretty much done 97 percent and we’re going to have the land done very soon. I want to thank the brave moms fighting the epidemic of drug addiction here this afternoon including Anne Fundner. Anne, you here someplace? Anne Fundner. Does a great job, wow, good. That’s a great job you do Anne. Stephanie Roe, Stephanie. Thank you. Thank you Stephanie, thank you behind the cameras there Stephanie. Thank thank you very much. And a friend of mine Jackie Siegel who is a great, great mom, great mother. Thank you Jackie. I’ve known her for a long time she looks better now than she did 20 years ago, right? Thank you very much Jackie. Last summer it was my great honor to sign the HALT Fentanyl Act into law and we’re never giving up on that mission for ending the drug epidemic in America. We’ve come a long way though; we’ve, we’ve stopped massive amounts of drugs from coming in. This afternoon we’re also joined by some of the most extraordinary mothers our nation has ever produced, our cherished Gold Star Moms. These are Gold Star Moms. These mothers raised up children so selfless and so devoted that they volunteered to wear the uniform of our nation and lay down their lives for all of us. One of those mothers is Janice Chance, Janice. Where is Janice? Thank you, thank you Janice. Hi, you look great. That’s a beautiful hat. Thank you Janice. Who lost her son Marine Captain Jesse Melton. And I heard he was an outstanding—they, they give me lists and they tell me about people, you know, they said that Jesse was outstanding, Janice. They have the word outstanding. Thank you for being here. Just before he went to Afghanistan in 2008, Jesse told his mother, "Mom I want to go change the world and make it an even better place. I want to make it a better place mom." Just before his 30th birthday, even though he had the day off, Jesse volunteered to take the place of a fellow Marine and that of his brother in arms could be present for the birth of a daughter. That day Jesse gave his life for our country. Jesse was struck down that day by helping somebody else. And Janice I want to say America is a strong and free nation today because of people like your great son and we want to thank you for being here. Thank you very much darling. I also want to express my love for the moms with us whose extraordinary heroes gave their lives in the terrorist attack at Abbey Gate in Afghanistan. I got to know you very well, very well. And these are phenomenal people. We went to a club I own in Bedminster, New Jersey, which is beautiful. They played music. They said, "You actually think he’s coming out?" They said yes and I was going to do just a stop by and say hello and we ended up listening to music for four hours on that beautiful, that beautiful night, looking at the sky, thinking about those wonderful people. Abbey Gate, very well known. Should have never happened. That should have never happened. That was incompetence by the Biden administration. So to Alice Lopez, you know that, right? Yeah we know that. To Alicia Lopez and Paula Knauss-Selph, Coral Briceño and to all of the Abbey Gate moms we’re thinking of you in this Mother’s Day and, again we had you to the White House. We had you to—I was over at a very beautiful cemetery as beautiful as they can be but, you know, it’s a sad occasion. We went over and we paid our respects but we lost great people that day. We shouldn’t have lost any people that day, gross incompetence. This Mother’s Day we’re thinking of all of those incredible moms and we’re doing everything in our power to support them and millions of other mothers all across America. And we’re blessed to have many wonderful mothers serving in this administration including Secretary of Agriculture Brooke Rollins who’s doing a phenomenal job. Thank you very much. And the first woman ever to serve as a White House Chief of Staff—she’s become a legend, Susie Wiles. She’s amazing. To help American families and mothers we passed the great big beautiful bill, the largest tax cuts in American history. We expanded the child tax credit to $2,200 from a much lower number per child and delivered the largest expansion of school choice in American history. We have school choice all over now. We didn’t have, we had virtually none. Everybody loves school choice. With accounts we have a very special thing, the Trump Accounts, where we’re helping families to set aside tax advantage savings for their children’s future and we are giving every American newborn child $1,000 automatically to invest and to grow over the course of their life. And I want to thank the Dell family. It’s a great family he is, amazing, she is amazing. They, they’ve done such a job, such a job on that. They put up a lot of money too; they put up 6 billion 250 million. That’s somebody, and he started making computers on his bed in college and selling them because they were better than other computers and he just—I said, "How did you do that?" He said, "Well I did it and I just never stopped, it just kept going." So go out and buy a Dell, they’re great. We’re also working to make it easier than ever before to start a family by lowering the price of IVF, cutting regulatory battles and and barriers and fighting to bring down the cost of living so that more Americans can pursue the dream of—and it’s the dream of living just a good life, maybe a great life. We cut drug prices now with the favored nations, most favored nations, we cut them by 50, 60, 70, 80, 90 percent. You could say 500 percent, 600 depending on the way you phrase the statement. But we now play—so we, when I was here this was a big ambition right at the beginning. I was able to cut them a little bit but I didn’t cut them—I cut them more than anybody else which was a little bit. Because for 28 years drug prices and prescription drugs only went in one direction and when I came back we now did something that everybody said was impossible: favored nations. We had the highest drug prices in the world by far. Now we have the lowest drug prices in the world. Can you imagine? So we cut them by 50, 60, 70 percent and, go out and you’ll see exactly it’s one of the big, I think it’s one of the best biggest things that we’ve done. Drugs that were costing in London $10 for a pill would cost $130 for a pill in the United States. And now if it’s $10 or if it’s $20, that’s what we pay. We pay the lowest price anywhere in the world. So it’s great. And the fake news doesn’t want to write about it but that’s why I talk about it because we have no choice, we have to. So I just want to again, I want to thank everybody for being here. It’s so important we do things like we keep men out of women’s sports, very popular issue. We stopped transgender mutilation of your children for everyone, we don’t have that. But I want to thank every single mother here this afternoon and all across our nation for your work every single day. America’s moms are raising, really raising the future of our country. The most important—you have the most—we don’t call it a job because when you love it it’s not a job, but let’s for this purpose, you have the most important job there is in America or anyplace else and you’re doing an incredible job. I just want to wish you all a very happy Mother’s Day. We’re going to have a great Mother’s Day and for those mothers that lost their child many of whom are with us today, the Angel Moms and others, Gold Star Moms, we love you especially. We love you maybe even the most. Thank you very much, thank you everybody. Appreciate it. Thank you everybody. Let’s get some music. We need music. Let’s go. Get some music. Where are we sitting? I don’t know. This way please guys grab your stool. Guys move, move. Still time. You’re watching C-SPAN, democracy unfiltered. America celebrates 250 years and C-SPAN is there to mark every moment.
